text: Quite preserved place is toured in a couple of hours.
Market for bad clothes, lousy quality, knickknacks and so on. If it's stingier, it'll fall flat.A disappointment with capital letters.
We love the experience, lots of pechinchas, wool blouses, shoes, etc, it's chaotic but very interesting
Waste of time!! I love street fairs, but this is not worth 5 minutes!! Products of very low quality, I lost my time!!

Terrible - unsafe vibe, absolute tat for sale on the many repeated stalls. Once you are inside the street of stalls there is no way out until you get to the end and will the unsafe vibe was horrible morning.

Nightmare!!!!  My wife and I have been to street markets / antique fairs all over the world but this experience at the Porta Portese market was the worst.  If you are seeking anything but second hand clothing, there is absolutely nothing there for you.  The market is in the confines of a very long narrow way with high walls on each side.  In other words, there is no way out but the start and the end.  The space between each side of the vendors is extremely narrow and with the crowds will make anyone feel claustrophobic.  It's actually very unsafe should a panic rush begin.  Unless you are desperate for a deal on second hand clothing, I would very strongly suggest avoiding this market.

My wife and I went to this Market on Sunday Nov . 23 2025. It was our first time and we probably only went through 1/3 of the market, which was plenty. It was extremely crowded and many items were overpriced compared to the flea markets in the USA. There were a few vendors that had very good deals and there were some that actually had everything for $1, (just like the Dollar Tree used to be in the US). For the most part, the market was mostly new and used clothes and shoes. There are several places to buy used tools and household items. The market is huge and almost overwhelming. It was hard for us to relax and take it all in because of the constant fear of the pickpockets that you are warned about and something I experienced first hand when $300 was taken from me. I have always been very careful and aware,no matter what county we have visited but they got me. It p!ssed me off and I wanted to beat the guys a$$ if I could have found him again but to be honest, I would almost like to shake his hand and let him keep the $300 if he would show me how he did it. He was definitely a professional! I had always assumed that careless people who don't pay attention to their surroundings were the only people that pickpockets prayed on but I can assure you that even the most vigilant can fall pray to these predators. So in closing, we would probably never go back to the market but it is something to experience and something I would recommend checking out at least once.
It was an amazing experience, I loved this market very much. You have to bring cash, because there are so many cool and vintage pieces. I bought many clothes and each cost 50cents

Porta Portese has lost its ancient charm.I hadn't been back in many years and I had no idea he'd been like this. I was practically the only Roman to walk the historic market: around me only tourists who arrived in fleets from every corner of the city.Rome, once again, has bowed to the will of hit-and-run tourism: expanses of useless stalls, manned mostly by Bangladeshis - the same ones you find in the local markets of the various of the city in the middle of the week - with the hope of grabbing some French or German by the throat with garments that have nothing vintage.For the Roman citizen now there is nothing left, even Porta Portese have taken us away.Poor Rome and poor us.

We were recommended by our property owner but honestly I wouldn't bother unless you are looking for 5 euro shirts from north African stall holders. Maybe in the past it had more interesting fair but now its a km of tat interspersed by a few more interesting antique stalls. It's like any market anywhere.
It is worth the trip if you want to make a cheap purchase, however, it takes a lot of energy and time to separate mass production from actual vintage. There are some hidden diamonds there.
They have a lot of souvenirs, clothes, cashmere, vinyl, DVD, CD, antiques, wool, sells everything. It is a very cool, yet economical tour to buy souvenirs from Rome.

Porta Portese , this has been on my For you page since the first time I visited Rome.They sell everything and anything.Very cheap.I would say , go to Porta Portese prior to do any shopping in Rome first and then whatever you haven’t seen there then you can go to the shop.Its really good for teenagers who like a different style everyday .Prices range from 0.50cents  to €100. There is public transport available close by.Meter taxi charged us €15 to get there and €12 to get back to our hotel next to Trevi Fountain.Go early in the morning as by 10am it’s already packed.
like all large street markets you have to wade through tons of crap to find the jewels but they are there. Some decent clothing and antique fixtures amid a sea of Chinese made junk

Very large but much of the same thing over and over....second hand clothes just dumped in piles on the stall tables. Mainly over priced asking for 5 euros and sometimes the quality and state is very poor and stains, very used and worn looking. You can buy the same in Primark but brand new. There were very little stalls that sold new or unusual things and most that are there  you can find them in central Rome. Same cheap magnets, same shorts, t-shirts and trousers. Nothing original or special about this market. No bargains. In the UK there are 2nd hand shops everywhere that you can shop in a more orderly fashion and pay the same. Nothing Italian, special or fun about this market. All the stalls are the same and its overrated and isn't cheapfor what it is. Just very very large full of used tat.

Second-hand clothes, cheap jewelry, and various items sold on street corners are what you’ll find at Porta Portese. There are no antiques—just a few overpriced old LPs, a couple of tables with vintage tools, and some old books, mostly in Italian. It's extremely crowded and not worth the effort.

Famous Sunday market, it has lost over the years its peculiarity as a market for quality second-hand items, antiques or vintage. They are almost exclusively stalls selling fourth-rate clothes and the sellers are all foreigners. Crowded to the hilt, I wasn't thrilled. Beware of pickpockets, very present.
The typical market found in every city, low quality clothing and counterfeit goods. You can take a ride but it doesn't deserve.

We went to the Sunday market. Lots of people and sellers, nothing special to buy. I was expecting to find nice antiques and vintage, but there were very few. No need to go again. Keep your bag and phone tight.
Piles of second-hand clothes, ugly 1 Euro jewelery and household staff. Only go there if you need socks, pajama and home pants - you can by all these things here at once.
Famous Rome flea market, easily accessible by bus, very large market, very poor product level, attention to pockets and wallets
The traditional Porta Portese market has all the credentials to be a historic site of the city.There are many offers and many opportunities to do small “business”

Visiting Rome we wanted to make a jump to this historic Domenicale.Ci market is immersed in a thick bolgia of people and stalls with the usual things .Si every hundred stalls maybe there is one with some different piece but it is not worth it.After an hour I ran away. Perhaps the early hours of the morning with fewer people and a good sounding speed you can find something but to date I think it is indicated only for those who want to dress and has little ability to spend.

My dad wanted to go antique hunting but instead all we found was a mile long line of people with no spatial awareness going slower than a tortoise with a boulder attached to its legs thinking that they are getting a good bargain but what they are actually getting are cheap and tatty secondhand clothes. My mum and I on the other hand are slightly claustrophobic and there was no exit for several hundred meters.DO NOT GO UNLESS YOU WANT A CLAUSTROPHOBIC HOT 1 WAY IN 1 WAY OUT CRAP CHEAP MARKET!!!

Incredible experience. We were surprised in every city we visited, including: Milan, Lago di Garda, Verona, Venice, Assisi, Florence and Rome. Rome is a true open-air museum. Beautiful, beautiful and beautiful city.
All very beautiful, very nice views and the super cool market.. don't stop visiting.. very good offers from 0.50 .. would definitely go again

Porta Portese was the characteristic market of Rome, a place that could not fail to be visited by tourists and frequented by the Romans. Today it has become a shabby place where you can no longer breathe the air you used to. In 90% of cases there are no longer Roman street vendors. You will find some stalls with good books, very low quality clothing and if you are looking for some good vintage vinyl you will come across perfect improvisers or pseudo connoisseurs, who are not such. In addition, it is incomprehensible why you cannot make purchases by credit card. Finally, for a bottle of water I was asked for 2 erects. Un vero furto! Saying I'm disappointed in what I saw is too little

If you are into second-hand clothes. I'm sure there are treasures buried in some of the piles of clothes, but that's not my thing. Of the "antiques," there were some stalls, mostly yard sale garabage items.  There were one or two stalls with furniture.  Otherwise, it is mostly the same linen dresses that are sold on about every street corner tent in Rome. Not worth the 45-minute walk, it took us to get there.  This does not need to be on your bucket list for Rome.

I decided to go for a pleasant walk and visit the flea market once again after many years and perhaps find a bargain. As it started raining, my need for a bargain became a neccesity as my shoes were soaked and nothing back at the hotel. The shoe selection was excellent and I found a decent pair of runners for 12€. It was fun exploring the flea market even in the steady rainfall.
A city that manages to bring together culture, architecture, religion and history in the same place in a vivid way. It is worth going for several days to get to know it.
Don't waste your time. Everyone has the same stuff for miles. Nothing worth the trip. Go spend your time doing something else

Our visit to the Porta Portese market was a major disappointment for us. The goods on offer were mostly junk and poor quality for 1-5 euros... It was obvious that many of the products were cheap mass-produced items that offered little value, hence the low prices. The market was extremely crowded and you had to squeeze through the crowds. It was not for us at all. If you like junk and cheap goods, then you can go there. If not, then you can save your time, because it was far from the romantic ideas of a lively and cultural market. Disappointed by the quality of the goods and the crowded atmosphere. In addition, the streets where the market was held were in a neglected state. Garbage was lying around and the unsanitary conditions reinforced the impression of neglect and disinterest on the part of the organizers.Our recommendation would be to explore other markets in Rome that may offer a more pleasant shopping experience.

On May 5, 2024, around 8 a.m., I was scammed at a restaurant in the Porta Portese market.1. The male employee in the photo said it was 2 euros per skewer and paid for it. (But I came back later and saw that it was being sold to other people for 1 euro.) 2. When I handed 10 euros to the woman on the left in the photo, she gave me 7 euros instead of 8 euros. (If you subtract 2 euros from 10 euros, doesn't that make 8 euros? 8 euros is one 5-euro bill + 2 euros + 1 euro. For 7 euros, you need 1 5 euro bill + 2 euros, which means they gave you one less coin. This isn't stupid, it's a scam against tourists.) The mistake started when the female employee handed out the wrong coin. I felt that it wasn't so. Because I felt she was checking to see if I noticed. This restaurant is a very bad restaurant that scams tourists visiting Rome. How many tourists have they scammed so far? I fought and yelled at the employee about the fraud. Therefore, all merchants and people next to the above restaurant are aware of the above restaurant's fraudulent activities.I can prove that my experience is not a lie.
Terrible place, full of people, very crowded and hot. Full of trinkets and used things. Lots of people and a chaotic atmosphere.

Perhaps I should have read more reviews before going to what turned out to be one of the longest queues with nothing to see. It's an endless amount of stalls with 95% selling the same poor quality cheap clothing and accessories over and over. I wouldn't describe it as a flea market but just clothes market. There were just two refreshment stalls. We had a cheap coffee which was nearly all frothy milk and virtually no coffee. It was absolutely heaving with people which meant you couldn't even walk through it quickly.  Certainly the most disappointing thing of our whole trip to Rome. I strongly recommend you find another market experience elsewhere.
Giant fair with super cheap products (Flea Market)It happens on Sundays.The Fair is crowded.It is close to the Pirâmide Metro.Super worth the visit.
Cool place, has somewhere to rest, take photos, several ice cream shops, cafes, the best shopping streets around. It's definitely worth the visit! I recommend

I am often in Rome and it has been 10 years since I went to the market as it had become just a chinoiserie bazaar. Instead I found hundreds of stalls selling clothing, objects, books as old as it was 30 years ago. That market is coming back. Let's hope...
Only cheap clothes from China. Nothing to do with a flea market. It's a shame if you waste time there. Avoid at all costs.

Highly inadvisable experience for anyone coming to Rome to visit. Only stands selling low-priced goods, very rare - two or three - those with some retro records: the Sunday market has lost all its charm.

Amazing Gluten free pasta, focaccia. clams and pasta for my kids. Home made burrata. Amatriciana, dessert, wine. And the staff is so welcoming. outdoorseating is cozy and fun. Highly recommend for a great meal.

A mile long dump, chock full of stalls selling absolute tat.If you’re in the market for a pair of cheap shoes made in a Chinese factory, or a cardigan in the middle of summer then this might be for you but if you expected to see an authentic Italian market bustling with interesting finds then you’ll be sorely disappointed.It’s one big straight line with no exits so once you’re half way in you’ve got to go the whole distance to get out. Why it’s so crowded makes no sense as it’s rubbish.There was one food stall, a hot dog van like you’d find at a terrible fun fair.If I could give it zero stars I would. An absolute waste of an hour of my life.

I always seek out markets when I travel. This one was a disappointment. There is nothing local or authentic about it. There are piles of used clothes and purses and bunch of trinkets and shoes made in China. In this vast market, I saw two stalls selling “nicer” vintage clothing and a few stalls with vintage leather coats. I would never go back there.

For a taste of the local culture, this was one of the best places I had visited during my stay in Rome. If you intend to go, you should try to visit on a Sunday morning.You need to bear in mind that this is not a pristine shopping mall where you can walk in and do your thing. :) This is a market with many small-time vendors, selling their food, used books, household amenities, clothes / shoes (some of which are used), etc. And at times, you need to haggle (better if you speak the language I guess, which I did a little). If you have the right expectations, this place can be super fun to walk around, if not buy something.I arrived in Rome on a Saturday, to my AirBnb and discovered late at night that my travel adapter wasn't working. I found new ones at Porta Portese the next morning. I also bought some pasta making tools, handbags, etc.
What a disappointment! Low quality, poor selection, questionable salespeople - better stay away from this venue.

If you're searching for a huge market, it's here! At Porta Portese find clothes, bags, shoes..all kind of items and in case of hungry find also panini italiani alla porchetta (pork)The whole area is situared near to area Testaccio  along the river Tiber and it's about 2 km long . There are both used and new items for sale.

There were only two booths with any vintage or even remotely antique goods.   Unless your looking for discounted cheap clothing or thrift goods avoid this market.   It wasn’t worth the cab fare over there. I was looking for antiques and found nothing in that category.   I did find a fabric vendor who had wonderful fabric that had Monet and Van Gogh paintings printed on them.  So it wasn’t a total loss but avoid it if your looking for real antiques

I was looking for more antiques and perhaps  better vintage vendors and what I found was table after table of very low quality bundles of clothing. I guess if you have seen everything in Rome and you have no other plans on a Sunday then it’s worth it.

It had been maybe five years since we last went there.Over the years this flee market has declined a lot, once you could also find new items  identical to those sold in shops at lower prices. Currently there is a lot of poor quality new things or used stuff.Porta Portese, in any case, is a unique experience.The typical Roman characters of the stalls are no longer there, there are not even those who used to sell cheap lighters or garlic braids. Now they are mostly foreigners trying to make a living, hired by unscrupulous employers who pay them the minimum percent of their sales.This 17th-century gate, built with the expansion of the Janiculum ramparts, is in good condition and is very beautiful and impressive from the side of Via Portuense.It is one of the gates of Rome, built in 1644 to replace the older Porta Portuensis. It was built in conjunction with the construction of the ramparts protecting the Janiculm hill, an extension of the Leonine Walls to defend the Janiculum, commissioned by Pope Urban VIII of the Barberini family.When the gate was opened, however, pope Urban VIII was dead, and for this reason the coat of arms of the new pope Innocent X of the Pamphilj family was placed over the gate. It is the historic Roman Sunday market, where you can look for and perhaps buy everything, fashion street clothes with little money, find a sought-after vinyl LP, a rare stamp.You cannot pass through Rome and not browse the immense market and the Porta Portese stalls!Go for a look!
Cheap, tacky modern stuff for the most part.A handful of good stalls but anything worth even looking at was overpriced for vintage.
Please don’t waste your time unless you are into junk- I mean low quality clothing and tacky worthless objects. We ruined our morning just to get there.Avoid!
Market full of rags, nothing special and waste of time for tourists.There hundreds of stands that sell cheap clothes for cheap price

We took a taxi out to here cost 9 euro. Our plan was to perhaps spend a hour looking around and then leave. We spent 3 to 4 hours wandering around. It's a huge market perhaps not great quality and a lot of similar stalls but we enjoyed it. We talked and laughed and enjoyed looking at all the bits. It was busy. It was enjoyable and a nice way to spend a December Sunday morning in Rome.

We went completely out of our way to go to Porta Portese based on reviews and normally love markets. This was a huge disappointment. Difficult to get to and impossible to leave, cost a fortune in taxis. The product was all the same, like trainer city! No variety, very poor quality nothing like any of the reviews. A total waste of time!

We hired e bikes and cycled here easily to see a bit of out of town Rome. The Sunday market was great of u love a rummage and a bargain. We got all our holiday tat and some bags and clothes for €1-€2. It was worth the ride although not a necessary in Rome it was nice to have the time to do something different. We found a great local Italian place full of families near by that did the best carbonara.

This wins as the worst market I’ve been to anywhere in the world. It took us forever to find it but I almost wish we’d just stayed lost. This is a long, crowded row of worthless, low quality clothing, shoes, and other junk. Once you start walking there’s no way to get out except to turn around and retrace your steps.  It has absolutely zero charm. Do not waste your time!

Porta Portese market on early Sunday morning is a lively place. There are lot of clothes, shoes, handbags, food and even antiques on sale. If you can bargain, try your skills. Even though prices are very low.There are not too many places to get a cold drink. Please remember to have a water bottle with you.

We went in August.  It was very hot. Perhaps the heat kept some of the vendors away.  It was not as vast as the reviews suggested.  A few good bargains but nothing extraordinary.  Two food vendors.  Again, perhaps many were on holiday.

Walked round the whole market from clothes bags and shoes section which wasn’t that interesting The main area where you can find interesting items is in the middle section where some stalls sells old coins and currency as well as watches and clocks Spend a good two hours and to properly go round you will be walking close to 7km plus Happy hunting !

Really? Don’t bother. A few kilometres ofChinese-made rubbish, fake shoes and piles of aeons hand clothes. The same as any other market in Rome (such as the small one by Ostiense Station every day of the week). There are much better, real flea / antique markets in Rome than this.
Lovely little marketplace with various vintage clothes, trinkets and collectables. If you're looking for souvenir then places like this are much more tasteful.

Loved this market. I caught the bus from Termini. I bought a bus map so was simple to find correct bus. The bus dropped me off over the bridge, then it is a short walk to the market. Hundreds of clothes souvenir and antique stalls with good prices. Take cash and wear comfortable shoes.

I make a point to go here whenever I can-- been about 10 times in the last few years.  There are a lot of things here however I suggest you get there early in the morning as the crowds by late morning/ early afternoon are hard to negotiate.  There are several areas to find antiques and depending on your entrance to the market(I used to approach it from the main entrance however prefer to go right to the antique area off of Circonvallazione Gianicolense)--it is a little further to walk (I stay in Trastevere) however I find it easier to negotiate from this entrance.  The vendors are a lot of fun (just be nice) and there are some real treasures to find just take your time-- it is sensory overload.

My wife and I made this I must stop during a recent trip to Rome. It has similar things to other flea markets but there are large displays of leather coats and shoes. It is massive, at least 3/4 of a mile long. Just when you think that you're done you see that it branches off to other streets. It was a little disappointing because we were hoping to get some very different older decorative pieces with an Italian theme. Did not have to be expensive or rare, just different. This market did not seem to have that. Many vendors had the same thing and an occasional table of older stuff. Despite all of this, it was a great time but be aware of your wallet all the time. That applies to all of Europe but especially to this venue. Just use common sense

Mostly new cheap and tacky stuff. Not antiques, crafts or local merchandise. Don’t think you’re going to find interesting old European things to buy. Lots of t-shirts etc -  stuff that can be found on street corners.
Huge place , full of everything. I loved it. it is a must. Everyone can find something to buy. Very crowded but still if you love markets it is a must.
Not a very interesting flea market almost exclusively new and used clothing.  It IS big.  I was expecting antiques, local food along with the clothing. My mistake....

It is only on Sundays, you can walk here or take a bus, to Porta Portese, from here the Flea Market starts from there, lots of clothing, it used to be different, but still a few items worth buying....your choice if you have nothing else to do.

Located in the Trastevere district, this is a huge flea market where you can find everything from antiques to knick-knacks, from gastronomy to clothing both new and used. With the right attention and a little luck you can find good deals.

This place is huge! If you are looking for antiques and bric a brac it's here but in one smaller section near the far right side of the front of the market. It used to be all antiques before. I found a great antique Sicilian puppet that I bargained from 20 euro to 15. Don't forget to bargain. The clothing is eh in price and a lot of secondhand stuff, the touristy shirts here are a great deal at 5 euro each. They sell furs and leather that are secondhand, food is available (it's better towards the end if you can find the end). You can spend hours here...

If you are in the Trastevere area of Rome on a Sunday afternoon you must visit the weekly flee market. The seller booths go on forever and while I did not buy anything of any consequence, I am sure there are real bargains to be had for shoes and women clothing and little trinkets and the like. And you may find a true treasure. Regardless, it was fun simply to walk around and browse.

This is a Sunday market selling pretty much anything you can think of(clothing, jewellery, furniture, second hand, bags, homeware, etc). I went at 9am and it was starting to get busy, by 10 it was very busy. A good place to pick up souvenirs and cheap clothing. My favourite was the scarves, what they sell in central Rome for 5-10 euros, you can get here for 2. Don't forget to haggle though, got a handbag for 15, starting at 35. Rule of the thumb is how much you are willing to pay for it and lets see how it goes. It is a selection of fakes and the real thing, priced accordingly. Tourists and locals visit likewise.  A few food stalls too so you can grab a bite. Police is visible so it is safe(obviously watch your valuables). I personally loved the hustle and bustle of the place! And every stall holder i spoke to had a reasonable level of English(my Italian is non-existent). Worth a visit!
The worst market I’ve ever been to . If your looking for secondhand collectables don’t go here what was here was over priced and poor quality unfriendly sellers a wasted morning
This is a really big market visited by locals. They sell cheap shoes and clothing, nothing special, but the athmospere is nice. The sandwiches and pizza’s are good and not expensive.

very large market. did not go to buy stuff, however I enjoyed the atmosphere and bought scarves, watches and costume jewellery.  there are hundreds of stalls.  it is fun to walk around the market. we walked for two hours and enjoyed the antique section.  the place is crowded and in summer it is hot. beware of pinpockets.  also note that there are various entrances to the market.  we entered from the arch.  it was crowded and found stalls selling clothes, accessories, bags, etc.  continued walking and at the very end of the market there is an antique section.  the market sprawls into some parallel streets.  i lost my bearings and therefore it tool longer than expected to exit again from the arch. overall quite a nice experience.
This market is only good to find very cheap souvenirs, nothing more. There is nothing Italian about it, it’s just full of tourists and immigrants.

This market is incredibly big! We loved it as it contained the usual things - shoes, clothes, keyrings, bags etc. Also has a bit of food, although we did not have any. Prices are cheap and there is certainly something for everyone. It took us over two hours to get through it and have a look around. Outside there is parking (an elderly man takes €2 to mind your car less then a minute walk from the start of the market) it is very handy. I would recommend popping by to have a look if you are around.

Came here for the benefit of my 3 teenagers and ended up buying 4 super lightweight summer shirts for €10 - bargain! The girls bought stuff too. As others say, it is very very big - the stalls all stretch out along one very long road. They’re all rather similar, selling mainly cheap clothing, not much else in terms of bric a beach and certainly no antique type stuff that you get in other flea markets. Some food stalls along the way and further in some cafes kind of ‘behind’ the main market. Although shaded, it was incredibly hot when we went - hot even for Rome in August - so take some water. We were careful about thieves having been targeted unsuccessfully at the Forum, but I didn’t notice anything obvious. Worth a visit if you just want some cheap clothes bargains but not if you’re expecting to be entertained by curios or to find something you didn’t expect. And great for men’s summer shirts, in all sizes.

170 bus gets you straight to Porta Portese. Sunday market is bustling. Even in the middle of Summer, hot as it is it's worth the Sunday visit. It's really  a bit of everything. Lots of clothes to ruffle through at very cheap prices and good bargains to be had. Shoe prices good but check carefully. There are lots of the same same stalls and prices vary so do some comparisons. Worth a trip.

Cheap and nasty shoes and clothes... was hoping to find souvenirs, local produce and baked goods, and maybe some interesting antiques/vintage stuff.  One long road in and through, and through, and through.  It’s an endless row of the same items.  In addition, it’s a waste of a 10+ Euro taxi ride to get there.  The best part of the day was a performance by a “one-man band” playing old Bowie and Depeche Mode covers, but that wasn’t worth the time/money to go!  Give this one a miss and enjoy wandering through some of the back streets instead!

I am an avid charity shop fan/market goer and this is one of the best places I’ve been too with the best and lowest prices I’ve seen compared to a lot of places. Most of my clothes are brought from these type of places and I resell profitable clothes if they don’t fit me on my online shop. You need a lot of time and dedication like with every market and charity shops to find the best pieces here. The market is huge and goes on forever, luckily there is a McDonald’s near the other end if you start from Porta Portese team stop to feed the famished by lunch time. I bought some winged sun glasses, a champion sweater top and a Very nice Nike tshirt all for under a tenner. There are some horrible fakes, knockoffs and some useless stuff but thats what makes a flea market all the more fun! You can definitely haggle if you know how! 100% worth a visit if you a market fan like me.

We had a couple of hours free and our friends wanted to go, so why not.It’s a huge market frequented by locals and tourists alike. You have to look carefully, know your prices and bargain.  Most of the shoe stalls were selling end of season, seconds or surplus stocks. One stall had nice shoes going for €20 however the majority of shoe stalls were so so (can you tell my focus was on shoes ?) and in some cases selling outright damaged stock.I bought some nice ‘made in Italy’ silk ties for €1 and some good quality Italian pants.Wouldn’t go again as there are lots of shopping opportunities around Rome with prices not that much more, but better quality.
Terrible market if you can even call it that... more like a really bad good will... everyone selling second hand clothes and no way in or out once you start ... DO NOT BE FOOLED DONT GO!!

I have been two or three times but only left with stuff today. This is probably because it's a large market and it just so happens that today the cheap clothing (50 cents to 2 euros, lots of choice too) was at the front of the market (by tram 8's Porta Portese stop). I have trouble finding jeans I'm willing to pay the high street price for but found some levis and a pair from a different brand today for a total of 2.50EUR. Not bad :)There's also a well placed cash machine near the start of the market too, just to the right as you enter from the Porta Portese tram stop mentioned above.

I love Italian street markets and this one did not disappoint.  It goes on and on and actually I do not think we saw it all.  Prices were very reasonable and merchants were mostly willing to negotiate.  There wasn't much in terms of high end items but if you are looking for bargains by all means check it out.

This flea market is quite uniquely European!  The predominant objects for sale are vintage clothing, but one should keep in mind the quality of Italian wools and knits, and how these items are unaffordable today, and really last!  There are also many really interesting household objects one cannot find in the US.  In addition, one entire section has quality new items, such as cashmere sweaters, and locally sourced handbags and luggage.  This is not your typical Chinese product flea market, although there a few of those tables.  Rather, it is an immersion into the historical, material culture of Italy, and it is vast!

This was a great flea market, but don’t go expecting anything but a flea market.  There are booths with cheap Chinese stuff, but also vintage clothing, antiques, Peruvian clothing, Indian jewelry, shoes, purses, souvenirs, and lots of fur coats.  It seems that the best area for antiques was well into the center which took us a good hour to get to, albeit we were shopping. (did I mention HUGE) We gave up after seeing the market extended past the major cross street.  Even turning back we logged a good two miles on the Fitbit.We came prepared for pickpockets, stowing anything valuable safely away, and keeping bags in front and had no problems.

In the lonely planet guide book to Rome this was rated as the best market in Rome, however in reality this was not the case, we were expecting to find souvenirs and some decent leather goods, but 95% of the vast number of stalls were all jumble sale type stalls of very cheap clothing household goods and cheap and nasty handbags and shoes.If you are inclined to spend numerous hours searching for the odd decent quality handbag or purse this maybe for you, for me definitely not one for the tourist, more for the less well off local.

If you are a tourist you may never learn about this street bazaar. It is the market of the rare eternal collectors and the locals who know it's there every Sunday. It is located in an area that escapes from the classic touristic Rome, so if you are a visitor in the city and you haven't read this post, you may never hear about the Bazaar. The Romans don't advertise it like the Colosseum, Pantheon or Piazza de Spagna. Everyone knows what they need. Besides bazaars were never for everyoneThere are a lot of clothes but unfortunately they are more expensive for tourists, if you are a native you have great offers because speaking italian is a great way to doing an offer :( I have write some more at my blog EuframeCheck it!

After reading the reviews we were a little hesitant about going to this flea market, but we decided to take a chance. We went early Sunday morning to avoid the crowds. It wasn’t that bad! No one tried to pickpocket us, we were fine. It was a little cheesy but that’s what a flea market is. We found a couple of things and got good bargains after haggling a bit with the merchants. Why not check it out?

It’s huge but it’s a lot of imported Chinese crap. We only found a few booths that were unique items and it was a bit frustrating in the end. It does look like there are many parts to it but by the time we found more what we were looking for we were spent and they were shutting down for the day. I wish we went to the other flea at the piazza da popolo

There is a really great lively buzz to this market. First, this is a place for the poorly paid Romans to come and buy their necessities. It is not a tourist market. Sure there is a lot of what I call rubbish. However there are a few parts of this market that do sell some interesting, beautiful things of quality. You just have to be patient to find them. This market starts early, get their early, by 11am it is just jam packed and full of pick-pockets looking for gullible tourists.Bottom line, I bought some great souvenirs.
And the market has pickpockets.  You can actually see them working the crowd.  Better to just go when there is no market and no thieves and just look at the gate.

We arrived about 10.30 when the market wasn't overcrowded.  We enjoyed browsing and rummaging although many of the stalls were similar to those found elsewhere in Rome. The stalls were mainly leather goods, 2nd hand clothes including fur coats for 35 euro, hats, shoes, cheap new clothes and kitchen ware. We walked for about an hour, saw no end to the market and so retraced our steps.  We bought quite a few items, bargaining was fun but whether they were a great buy I'm not so sure.We'd been warned about pickpockets and couldn't believe it when my husband realised his glasses and case had disappeared from the leg pocket of his cargo trousers even though it was closed by presstuds.

A combination of "made in Italy" stuff found everywhere else in the world and a range of  secondhand gear - furniture, bric brac etc.Quite a large market, with lots of the same stuff, stallholders will negotiate and prices vary wildly for the same stuff as you progress through the "new stuff" stands in the markets.To get to the secondhand section you need to go about half way down then turn right.The cafes dotted along the street to the market are reasonable.It depends on your expectations whether this is a worthwhile deviation from your time in Rome.  I probably wouldn't bother next time - although we had a spare day so it was just something to do.

This market only opens on Sundays and it is very interesting. Many of the merchants are Arabic. You can find almost anything from shoes to clothes for men and women. They also sell food products.The place is full of people wandering or shopping. Prices are really good specially for clothes. Cash only.

We always plan our trip to begin or end in Trastevere section of Rome to visit this huge flea markets every Sunday morning, The market is huge and is 50% new and 50% old merchandise. You can get a lot of good buys here.

This was my second visit in 18 months.  I just love the atmosphere and no matter where I travel Sunday mornings rummaging and wandering through all the stalls  is most enjoyable.  Junk or not it never fails to make my day!  I rarely go shopping at markets for specifics, rather just the fun of what will be.I also love a good haggle on prices and the fun that goes with it.  Porta Portese gives me all of thatThis time I Took my 14 year old granddaughter who completely immersed herself with glee in the makeup counters where she would gladly have spent the whole morning.  I rummaged and got myself a beautiful pink linen table runner for  1 euro - happy as.This time we entered from the Ports Portese entrance 2 hours didnt even get us around a quarter of the market.  Lashing rain mixed with bursts of sunshine couldn't stop us.We were staying near the Spanish steps and took a local Taxi to and from the markets at 13euro each way.  Taxi is metered.  There are a few eateries and bars which offer toilet facilities you may need to just buy a cake or drink for the opportunity to use them.
Very large flea market. Watch your bags. Some bargains. Clothes for a $1.00euro. Good assortment of items. Would only buy a leather bag from a reputable store, not here.

I arrived in bus from Termini and started the market from the upper side and then finished in Porta Portese door. At the beginning there were antiques which looked like trash, but some were cool. You can buy brand stuff cheaper (e.g. All Star shoes for 45€) and also brand sneakers for 15€ which look obviously wornBe careful with thieves

We didnt get to the market until 11.30 but that still gave us just over 2 hours of "looky looky, nice price". I assume we had missed the crowds as it was relatively easy to look at what you wanted to and move about from stall to many stalls.. it is large. With cheap clothing - tops from 50 cents and shorts/skirts from about €3 there is something for everyone, you just have to be prepared to dive in and rummage. Prices seemed very reasonable and almost negated the need to haggle.

In Rome for a week.It was Sunday. What to do? Didn't fancy the crowds in St Peter's. Did that the following Monday.So. Up relatively early and off to the Flea Market located at Porta Portese in the south of Trastevere.Partner and I are always partial to a bargain. We spend some of our spare time rooting around Charity Shops in the UK. Thought this might be similar...Arrived at Porta Portese.I guess I was looking for an old school slightly edgy "proper" flea market. You know. Boxes upon boxes of rubbish then suddenly you hit the Mother Lode.Not to be alas.An apparently endless mirage of market stalls. Loads of similar goods.Loads of €1, €2, € you name it "table scrums". I guess mostly second hand; sorry "pre-owned" to use the current usage.Some, though not as many food stalls as I had expected.Lots of similar stuff...Guess it all depends on what you are looking for.I got some Italian seeds for next year at a good price. Some unusual variants to try next year.Partner picked up some nice linen clothes.Worth a visit to while away a Sunday morning for sure.Unless of course The Vatican calls you...

We visit this place two or three times per year. Open on Sunday,  very crowded, quite safe (although others think differently), every time we find something to buy. Dresses, bags, electronics... everything cheaper than elsewhere. Mostly "use and throw away" stuff but for us it is ok. Many strangers as sellers (mainly Indians) but some Romans too. It deserves a visit anyway.

After reading both good and bad we made the trek early Sunday morning.   We decided to walk from our hotel near the Trevi fountain and it was a pleasant 45 minute walk.   The entrance was a bit hard to find using google maps as it was off by about 50 yards.    Once inside the place was really interesting. We spent a few hours wandering and did find some great deals.    It is rows and rows of clothing and shiny crap. The vendors overall were polite and of course we did have a few more aggressive ones with the typical lines "for you a special price" but that just made it more fun. The hockers around trevi and the other tourist traps are 100% more annoying than anyone we experienced at this market.   The antiques were decent and properly priced, clothing and shoes ranged from €1.00 to probably €65.00I suggest going around 7:30-8:00am before the crowds. Even a few hours in it was bearable except for the smokers which seem to be everywhere.

From Los Angeles I go to flea markets and estate sales all of the time. Rose bowl flea market in LA is big with lots of vintage finds. I thought this place would be similar but found it to be small only a handful of vendors selling vintage items. Was hoping to find something Italian unique and vintage to take home from my trip. Very disappointed. So many swap meet kind of vendors with poor quality with nothing good to sell. Been to a few street markets in France that were amazing. Not sure how this is Romes biggest flea market. More of a junk market. Where were the vintage finds? Would not go back.  A big letdown. As for the pic pocketers I went early in the morning and it was fine no problem.

This was like Petticoat Lane meets Brick lane with sections of new items , antique stalls and junk stalls mixed together.Mostly new clothes / shoes with some second hand clothes and house hold items. There is a small quantity of bric a brac / antique stalls. Prices vary hugely between stalls and you need to haggle. Very interesting to watch the locals and see what is for sale. Look out for pickpockets, especially when crowded.
We went there for a few hours. It was quite fun and we found some great bargains. You have to haggle.

While I don't like these flea markets this one is a bit better than others my wife has made me go along with her to. Generally, I would prefer to be waterboarded than to go to these places.  However, on the sunday that we went in May it was not so crowded as most markets and the quality of the goods seem better. It is mostly clothes, leather goods, and shoes. These seem to be very good and my wife made some good buys. You will get tired of hearing the hawkers yell Prego! Prego@ Prego!.   I wish I had known in Italian how to say Shut Up.  Too much.  If you can stand a long walk this market would be good exercise.
This is a large space seling the cheapest of cheap rubbish  - mainly clothes, footwear, accessories and lugaage. No redeeming features.

Disregard the descriptions of this as Romes largest and most interesting markets. It is a boring flea market, albeit a very long one. Unless you are looking for cheap clothing and footwear, avoid this.

I was truely disappointed from our visit to puerta portese. Its was all about clothes shoes and bags... and unique goods were rarely found.We preferred to cut our visit short and to spend our sunday morning walking the street around the basilica di santa maria in trastevere.

Warning: the travelling to this market via tube + foot may be longer than expected. Wear the correct kegs and footies. We arrived at Piramide station, gasping for air and gagging for water and then trudged 20 mins to this BOG STANDARD market. A taxi would be suggested.Upon arrival at the market you are greeted with many a seller, selling many a crap, for many a Euro. You're swarme by sellers trying to begin a haggle as though it is a sport. They hover around you like wasps to do ketchup on a hot summers day. Recalling from memory - it was basically fake bags, cheap necklaces and jewellery, and stink clothes (where they source this stuff from baffles me to this day).The sellers however, are up for a good haggle, which we get serious kicks from. They are so desperate to make a sale that you could buy their own arm off them for a 5 euro offer. I like the jewellery and managed to get myself some earrings for 1 euro. My husband bagged a couple of jerseys for what he thought was a good haggle of 9 euros (I disagree, he has enough already).The our dismay the same man, selling YOU GUESSED IT, the same tack, was at it again with his lighter on the necklace trick. The numnuts burnt his finger yet again, for what?! All for a puny offer of 3 euros for the necklace, down from 25, which he rudely declined, with a look that could burn off satin's skin. Another seller when browsing their junk store, repulsed us when he squirted mucus out of his gob on the piping hot concrete -- yes we did hear a sizzle as the mucus cooked in the baking sun. The sellers lack etiquette, but if you don't mind this and want a bargain, it is worth the travel on a Sunday morning.To answer an FAQ; it is INDEED open on Easter Sunday, we are the proof within the pudding. Come rain, come shine, come sellers street spit, this place don't close. Even with every army in the world on hand, these sellers are not going ANYWHERE. They ain't budgin!Thanks,Papa Francesco
I like a good flea market .  It's big but nothing  special .  Alot of clothes . Beware of pick pockets  they are not shy here.

Great market.  If you want souvenirs or a re-supply of things forgotten on your trip (shoes, clothing, shampoo) this is a great place.  Fun to wander around this very large site. Supposedly a haven for pick pockets, be careful but we had no problems. Awesome food stalls and really no end to the type of goods available.  A better version of the Orange County marketplace.

We caught s taxi from the Trevino fountain area - €9. Great for a mouth around - lots of fake goods , clothes and bricks brick a brak area. We then walked down the riverbank to St. Peter's square for the 12o'clock blessing - took about 45 minutes to walk .

The Sunday market consists of mostly Chinese knock offs and 1 Euro stalls and was not really my cup of tea. However, the bicycle stalls on the "first floor" were really interesting and had some real gems in there (although most of the bikes were in line with the cheap and nasty theme of the Sunday market). Cool spot if you are into vintage bikes!

If you live in Rome, are staying there for a while or have visited so often you have done everything else there is to do in the city, then you can pass an interesting morning looking at all the cheap and/or weird stuff you can buy. If you are the average tourist, don't go, there are plenty of other things to do - 95% of the stuff you can find in your own local market. As has been pointed out by other reviews the 'flea market ' part is by Trastevere/Ippolito Nuevo, but don't expect bargains. Otherwise there are lots of stalls with piles of second hand clothes and knock-off branded trainers etc. I bought a second-hand stove-top cafetiere and an old skirt which I'm making into a cushion; I would probably go back for bags and leather belts but not much else.It was fairly busy. The stall-holders started to pack up at 1.30pm.

If you're in Rome on a Sunday and have some free time, I would recommend this market. You can buy the same souvenirs for less than the touristy places and also find some good shoes, bags but should negotiate the price on higher priced items. Nice to be shopping in the mix with locals. Be mindful that the market is really long...a couple of miles.

Good market, lots of crap but something to fill in a free Sunday morning. Bought some things but not special. All leather is fake so don't go if you are looking for that. Full of locals and not many tourists so a more authentic experience.

These markets are so large that you could get lost, or worse still,  miss the best part. For me, I'm not interested in all the chinese junk being sold by the Bangladeshi, I want to visit these markets to find cool vintage stuff. So if you want to bypass all the junk and get to the real original section of the markets, DO NOT enter through the large gate near the lungotevere. Catch the tram from piazza Venezia and get off just before Via lppolito Nievo. This is also the best place to enter the markets. Here you will find all the cool vintage furniture and bric o brac. I have filled so much of my house with retro furniture. Also don't forget to haggle on price. I have found that they also are more willing to drop the price just before closing.

I'd been to Rome many times, but never been to Porta Portese. I was with a friend who'd never been to Rome, and she was keen on going to PP. I was reluctant, but our morning there turned out to be one of the highlights of our trip. Utterly delightful. Yes, there's a lot of junk for sale, but also great stuff, including inexpensive cotton dresses and nice shoes. My friend who lives in Rome buys all of his shirts there for about a Euro each.

This is the oldest street market in Rome and, by far, the largest.  With thousands of stalls, one can find almost everything: clothes, purses, new and antique furnishings, bikes, old cameras, kitchen utensils, posters, books, stamps, etc.  etc.  It is a huge market, open only on Sunday, extending over several kilometres and it can be very crowded.    It is not necessarily a "flea market" nor it is a market of  only used items or arts and crafts.  It has never been one.  Just like at Targets or Zellers or Homesense in NA, there are lots of cheap imitations or knock offs of major brands and cheap imported items and electronic junk. But there are also many stands that sell quality Italian made items, clothes, etc. and bargains to be had.   There is a reason why lots of Romans shop here or on stands all over the city.  Not many can afford the high end shops that dot the high tourist areas.  With patience, in the Porta Portese market one can find decent deals, items that in NA would  be at least twice as expensive.  We purchased for 12 euro a very nice daypack (a comparable one at home would cost 4 times as much), Italian made wool shirts, Italian made lounge top and pants, etc.  If you are in Rome for just a few days, we would definitely not recommend you visit it.  There are way better places to go.  But, if you have time, it is worth the very local experience as long as you don't have too high expectations and don't mind the crowds.  For that it is worth a 4 stars.  Every time we have come to Rome, we have made a point to spend a little time here.

We were told the flea market held at Porta Portese on Sundays was a great shopping opportunity. With experience with amazing flea markets around the world, we were looking for something special. This...was not. It was more like a big yard sale, with used shoes, clothes, and household good strewn across tables alongside cheap knockoff cosmetics, DVDs/CDs, and jewelry. There are a couple of good purse vendors if you are looking for that (they will try to sell you a "genuine Italian leather purse" for 50 euros each, but just know what you want to pay and they will eventually take it - we bought 2 bags for about $20 - better than Target). Don't make this a destination unless you will be walking near Colosseum and want to stroll along the Tiber.

This is like the largest version of the cheapest rubbish market you have ever been to. Only one single stall with craft items actually made by someone as opposed to knock-off rubbish clothes. Too much rubbish and too many people in too big a location.

Yes, it has lost its peculiarity, but it's still fun walking around the market.You can find a bit of everything, if you come here make sure you buy some stuff, otherwise you'll remember only the crowd.

Wonderful......Sunday morning only ...we got there at 9 and it was in full swing. There are 2 main streets...one has a lot of new typical market stuff the other has genuine flea market gear...need to spend and least 4 hours here...so much to see....take cash and be prepared to haggle.

My husband and I stumbled on this flea market during our recent stay in Rome. We were here for at least an hour just browsing all the different stalls and bargaining for different things. We walked out with a scarf and a couple of (very touristy) teeshirts. There is a lot of repetition throughout the stalls so I'd say you could probably walk a quarter of it and see most of the goods, but we decided to walk the whole thing and found better deals for it. Anyway, definitely a fun way to see a lively side of Rome!

So, apparently if you can tolerate the crowds and heaps of garbage, there are treasures to me found at this Sunday market. For me - I wouldn't waste my time. We walked there from the Trevi fountain to enjoy the nice day, and when we arrived I found myself living my worst nightmare. Piles of €1 tshirts with old ladies picking through them. Men yelling about their "deals" for €10 fake leather purses. It wasn't even fun. We walked in about 10 minutes and I couldn't wait to get out (which unfortunately was the same way we entered). If you're patient and really want a true flea market experience - where the only thing you might leave with are fleas - then this is for you. Otherwise - sleep in.

We went to the flea market to have a look on the way to traverste I think that everything could of been on about 8 stalls, a lot of repetition. But it's a good walk through and interesting They sell:- BagsBeltsClothes(Cheap but good) Tourist gifts A lot of kitchen equipmentSunglassesas seen on tv products I did like my visit here, the smell of leather from some of the stalls is incredible.

I headed there early on a Sunday morning to be the crowds. While I succeeded in beating the crowds, was disappointed that it was lined with stalls and stalls of, it reminded me of a dollar store in the U.S. Really not worth the time.

First, we had difficulty finding this. The map sent us a couple of blocks away from the actual market. We went early and it was still unpleasantly crowded. It seemed most of the items were of lesser quality, similar to items sold on various streets around Rome. Definitely not worth the hike from our hotel, by the Vatican.
Some are great finds. I found that most of the shopkeepers were friendly but some Italian shopkeepers were very racist and rude. Ruined the experience. Beware of preworn stuff.
This is a very big and crowded market. People trying to sell the same thing at different prices. We also noticed that there are some stalls that sell second hand clothing without advising you.

The market is indeed very long, but the first 100 meters pretty much cover 99% percent of the goods - "leather" bags and wallets, 1€ shirts and bags and 2€ electronics. Don't understand me wrong - you might have a good chance to find a nice shirt for a very cheap price, but you will have to go through a pile of hundreds of clothes before you'll find the right size and color. Towards the end of the market, they sell some second hand/antiques which for me was the most interesting part of the market.

We went to the Porta Portese Market on our first day in Rome. It is a big market, easy to find. Here you can buy everything from toothpaste and sunglasses to antique furniture. Some stalls are cheap, others are more expensive. My advice is to check prices on different market stalls before you buy

My wife and I went looking for vintage goods.  We found that vintage items were mostly clustered at the far end of the market (and off of the main aisle away from the archway entrance and further from the bridge).  Once there, we found some great records and a few fun vintage items. Pricing by the vendors is aggressive, so be prepared to walk away.
Pushing shoving, bumping, and possibly some pick pocketing. It is a fun trip, however.  Spent Sundays there, just looking.
Try to visit it early in the morning, before hundreds of visitors come. You can really find anything here, from cheap Chinese fake goods, to antique furniture.
An extremely long market!  Absolutely loved it - lots of bargains, people and live music! Definatly felt like a local!

Porta Portese is a giant flea market that stretches across a couple blocks.  The vendor stalls sell a lot of the same stuff: fake leather goods, luggage, shoes, clothing, household goods, and printed artwork.  There are only a few food stands so I would recommend to eat beforehand.  The vendors are very eager to sell so don't be afraid to haggle.  I would also recommend to keep your personal belongings secure as it can be very crowded and pickpockets are present.  I think there are other street markets in Rome that are more interesting as I suspect the majority of items here are made in China.  But if you're looking for cheap stuff, this is the place!

This is one of the main flea market in Rome. A little bit away from the center but highly accessible using public transport. You can buy cheap things here as what a usual flea market has but majority are scarves, little souvenirs (keychains/magnets) not much on the intricate stuff. Overall if you want to buy cheap, this place is OK.
You're not going to find high quality items here, (think Wembley market Italian style) but, if you're in the area its a fun way to pass an hour.

The market is open at sundays from early at the morning till whenever. When we were there they started to close the shops at 13:00, when it started to rain. And that's when the prices started to drop. The market is very long, but after a while you start to see the same $hit everywhere. If you want to get the really special things, go to the parallel street. Thats where all the african and indian shops are.You cannot be at Rome and miss this market!
It's not, by any criteria in which a flea market can be judged, a flea market. It's nothing more than a school jumble sale with second-hand clothes, terrible fakes and iPhone chargers.
The Porta Portese market includes primarily clothes, bags, some inexpensive jewelry, etc. If you are seeking a true flea or antique market - this is not the place!

If you think it's all crappy fake stuff, then you haven't walked through far enough. There is more than one entrance to the flea market. At one entrance, it *is* full of junk – fake jewelry, underwear, socks, all the stuff you might see at souvenir kiosks… But if you keep going past that, or better yet, if you enter at one of the other points, you will find a treasure trove of vintage clothing, antiques, and vintage merchandise of all kinds… And you will surely come away with a treasure as a souvenir.  I happen to be a collector of space-age plastics, and I found three wonderful pieces for my collection.  (I also bought my son a 1€ Italian silk tie, and my husband an Irish style wool tweed cap for 5€ that they both loved!)  So, even the crappy end of the flea market might have something you like. Be sure to go in the morning and on Sundays only… It closes at some point in the afternoon. Oh, and parts of it can be very crowded - I was warned by locals to mind my purse. But at no point did I feel threatened or unsafe.
Kind of like a flea market anywhere but did purchase a couple of little things, nothing amazing though and prices were normal rather than a bargain.Glad we did it but not sure I would recommend.

From piazza Porta Portese to Piazza Ipolito Nievo, (about 3km) every sunday fom 7 a.m. to 2.30 p.m.  it's the largest and most famous flea market in Rome. Here you can find unimmaginable things at a very cheap price; to go there it's very fun. To discouver Porta Portese is incredible..
Full with fake stuff.I expected some fruits and vegetables but there is only just fake jewellerys and doggy cloths which are quite expensive.I would not recommend.

I went to Porta Portese for the flea market. I got there by taxi as there is not a Metro station close enough. Also when I left the Taxi, the Taxi driver  SPECIFICALLY advised me to be careful of pick pockets and to keep my money in my front pocket. Aside from all this I had a great time! The market is absolutely HUGE. not a little big, but HUGE!!! You can find bargains, you can find garbage and everything in between. I found a pair of Lotto shoes for 15 euro, Touristy t-shirts for 5 Euro a piece, and a INCREDIBLY warm Enos Jeans jacket for 30 Euro. I brought along an empty large cloth bag in anticipation of acquiring a few things and was glad that I did.There are food vendors here, but I just did not trust them. There were also restaurants on side streets, but I skipped those also. I grabbed a taxi from where I started and had a great time at the Market. No troubles, just good shopping & people watching.

Had a great time walking around with my wife and teenage daughters.  After spending 15 minutes finding the elusive "Popeners" the vendor refused to budge one red Euro on the price.  I can't blame him as we spent so much time searching his table.  Deals on stuff can be found, but the real value is having an enjoyable, non-tourist experience.  It's worth walking the entire market- it took about 2 hours.  The market can be segmented by type of offering - kitchen stuff in one area, etc.  We avoided the food, per Rick Steves' advice.

I liked this place. You can buy beautiful fashion jewelary. Everything is inexpensive. You can even find good and quality stuff there.  Only thing you have to walk a  lot.You shouldn't miss this flea market.

Compared to many years ago, when we first visited Rome, the Porta Portese Sunday market has been gentrified considerably. No more beggars and shell games, but serious stalls with merchandise worth a look. The only hazard to be aware of are the pickpockets. A notable observation was, that most stalls were managed by people from the fare east, and mostly Bangladeshi. Employment of people from Bangladesh was also notable in restaurants in Rome, by the way. The Sunday market was also more extensive than some years ago. Many stalls, mostly flea market merchandise, spilled over in the neighboring streets. Even on the Viale Ippolito Nievo thoroughfare. A visit to this market is recommended. Bus line 75 stops in front of the Porta Portese.

Full of junks, low quality fake designer purse/wallets, cheap clothing and leather goods, some "antiques", I counted 3 flower stalls.  Crowded.  There's a lady selling used designer ties, 30 euro for an Hermes tie?  I was tempted but decided against buying.  Things are cheap here, croissant and cappuccino only 1.90 euro versus 4 euro in town.

We ventured off to this flea market for an afternoon of browsing and shopping. It was a crazy day of vendors hawking their wares, promising to give the best prices and deals, etc. We bought some beautiful scarves there which were very nice but similar in cost to many other places we went outside of the flea market. The market is at least a mile long so wear comfortable shoes and bring a canvas bag to carry your finds. I would recommend this activity for a day of entertainment. We had fun and afterwards, ate at a FABULOUS nearby restaurant called La Piazzatta, which is worth going to, even if you skip the shopping!!

We were really looking forward to go to this flee marked i read so much about. in stead we got so disappointed. where was all the old stuff ? small treasures ? original clothes? all we could see was street sellers selling fake clothes and cheap underwear ..

As previous reviewers have stated, there is a lot of stuff here that you would see at any Flea Market in the world.  However, it is in Rome!  There are good deals on leather goods (jackets and purses) and other trinkets that you might want to bring home to your friend.  It is crowded and you must bargain.  Be prepared to walk away and they will reduce the price.  It is so big, we didn't even reach the end!  We took the Piramide Metro and walked from there.  Wonderful way to experience Rome!
Best shoping in rome for young travellers with no money:)) i liked it a lot..............שופינג מצויין לדלי תקציב:) וגם למי שאינו דל תקציב יש גם דברים יקרים יותר

This is another view of Rome, as you walk through Trasvatere (one of our favourite parts of Rome).  The market on a Sunday is great, get up really early and get there for about 8 or 9, it only goes on until 2 and lots of the better vendors leave about 12.  Some amazing stalls in the antique section, we saw a very large Mercury on a stand that I wanted to bring home.
A hotch potch of rubbish spread over scores of roads, with stalls manned by immigrants or by the rudest Roman dropouts Who hate tourists and some.. Not all,  are extremely rude
Go for the Sunday flea market. Rows upon rows of treasures to be found. Some old, some new. Lots of great people watching too!

The area is not attractive and the markets are terrible. We couldn't work out if they were for locals or tourists. Full of crap! No Italian produce, just random junk and very cheap tourist products. Do not waste your precious time in Rome going to these markets.

We arrived at 7:30 am as we read on trip advisor to start early to avoid De crowds. Due to travelling with an "Irish Mammy" we were still there at 15:30 as she insisted in looking for more bargains!!!. Advise for travellers is to decide what your definitely not interested in and then do a walk by of these stalls, this will speed things up considerably. Lovely cafes along the way with very reasonable prices. Queues for the loos very long so try go early before crowds arrive. All in all a lovely way to spend our last day.

We bought a rolling case and a cheap Chinese straw hat.  It was hot, very hot and there was block after block of all the same stuff. Probably would have had a better review if we'd visited in a cooler month

From the plaza Venezia take the tram and basically 15 minutes later basically step out where most people do. That's Porta Portese.  Most people will go there for the flea market. It is quite eclectic but do not expect to add anything to your collection of valuables. Go for the inexpensive souvenir and the pleasure of enjoying bargain shopping. If that's your thing you'll feel right at home.

It is indeed a huge market, with plenty of 1euro clothes stalls and tourist tat.The real flea market bits (furniture, phones, household bric a brac etc) are in the side streets to the right as you come in via the arch.You still have to walk a bit of the main market strip before you can branch off.But as everyone says do haggle, it's very diffulct to disguise the fact you're a tourist so prepare to be ripped off a bit.But it's huge fun anyway. If only I could figure how to get a stone credible fountain on my luggage I'd be a very lucky person.

if you’re feeding up with Rome’s museums, buildings, and endless religious stuffs, Porta Portese is a very nice place to visit. at first I was reluctant to visit after hearing many of my friends talked about it. So I gave a try without having an expectation, just getting to know. if you already visited some countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa, or other European's traditional markets, this market just pretty closed  to them but with different taste. Porta Portese gives us an idea of the other side of society (people, culture, history, and desire). TIPS AND INFO:1. Open on Sundays morning2. Taxi: I’m pretty sure all Rome taxi-drivers know this place. Metro & Bus: metro (line B) to Marconi station and then bus 170. From Piramide station and then take the bus 280 or 23. Tram: one of the easiest one, from Piazza Venezia than take tram no. 8 3. Buying: if you decide to buy things, make sure you know how to haggle 4. Safety: be aware of pickpockets

As flea markets go, there isn't a lot of older or antique stuff.  Instead, it is a good place for cheap souvenirs, shoes, clothes (lots of clothes), luggage, and household goods.  It is fun to people watch and haggle - but make sure to haggle!  Don't take the first price, ever!  If you recognize it for what it is, it is a fun Sunday morning. There was a police presence and we did not feel unsafe, as many of the other reviews talked about pickpockets.  Just don't be dumb!

This outdoor flea market is not unlike others found in the US, except for incredible lack of support facilities!!!  Most of the vendors have similar tourist "stuff" with a large supply of used clothing thrown in the mix.  Only a couple of restaurants with toilets along the route and NO public facilities.  Some shade, but a warm experience in the summertime.

Upon advice from our tour guide, we took a cab there on a Sunday morning from our hotel to avoid crowd and also to catch the worms : p.  From the guide to the front desk and even our cab driver, they told us to take care of our wallets.  I heard so much of this place and would like to visit given the opportunity and time despite being notorious for pickpockets and the interesting story that one will find their stolen stuff there.   There is something for everyone, from cheap clothes, jewelleries, some old postcards and stamps, old furniture, and everyday stuff to pricey and good quality jackets/winter wear.  We were happy to buy the latter at extremely good deal starting from less than EUR5!!!   Thereafter, we took tram no. 8 to stop at Tiber River to have a photo stop and enjoy the rest of our day in Rome.

something for everyone here. There's lots of car boot style stuff and junk and general market things like fruit and veg and cheap clothes but there are also expensive antiques, antique Italian  furniture, great bargains and gems to find and lots of retro as well as new stuff too so it is worth a look around. It is an interior designer's dream ace to pick up a bargain, but you must haggle. Also as a tip, decide the max amount you will pay for something and stick to it and get the money out and ready to count it out in from of the traders as they are more likely to sell things at a bargain when they actually see the cash in from of them. The market is usually open from dawn to 4pm ish every Sunday and is in the Trastevere area, over the bridge the Vatican side of the water, just south of St. Peter's  on the map. About 10 to 15 mins walk from St. Peter's. Keep your money, bags and valuables close to you though as it's renowned for pick pockets. Also take some bags with you in case you buy anything as often the sellers don't have carriers to put your purchases in.

I love a market and have seen quite a few in my time.. If you really expect to go to any market nowadays and uncover genuine bargains, antiques and treasures worth passing on to your children, you will be sadly disappointed.  I often think I can imagine people working till the early hours of the morning before the market, attaching dust and grime on to the "antiques", or scratching them to make them look genuine.On a Sunday morning, you can take a long walk to Trastevere, over the Tiber River, or take a number 8 tram to reach this market.  I thought it was much better than Campo de Fiori, also in Rome, El Encants in Barcelona or El Rastro in Madrid and spent a couple of happy hours here.You can buy new and used clothing, bags and leather goods, costume jewellery, piles of junk pretending to be antiques and curios, shoes, shoes and shoes, furniture, computer and smartphone accessories.  The market is vast and there is a big crush of people.  Heed the usual warnings about pickpockets.In general, the traders are good natured and some are roguish.  One of them spoke to me in French and offered to reduce a pair of pants labelled at 48 euros to 40 euros, "because you are French", he said.  I had already seen very similar pants on sale in downtown Rome for 25 euros and anyway, I'm not French.  But he was so convincing, that it almost bought them.  I couldn't be sure if any of the smartphone products, such as portable chargers, were duds or not, so was wary of buying any.  Afterwards, I strolled the long walk in to the city, stopping for a lunch of grilled calamari at one of the many food outlets in this district.I thought it was a morning well spent.

Some people say "Trash" and other people say "Treasure" about the Porta Portese market, right?There's a good reason for that, and it's geograpgy rather than taste>1.  The north end of the market (the first part you reach if you've crossed over the bridge from central Rome) is an unceasing street of cheap clothes, shoes and bags - much of it used and very cheap (and therefore welcome to the poor people who shop there, but not very interesting).2.  The south end (plus the street heading from Porta Portese up the hill into Travestere neighborhood) is what puts the "flea" in this "flea market".  No real treasures, but interesting stuff (including some creepy Mussolini era books, posters and photos).TIP:  Go early or don't goTIP:  This is an easy tram/bus ride from central tourism area or a looong walk.

This market just goes on and on, it's amazingFull of great bargains , Lots of cafes and places to eat and drink just behind the stallsDon't believe the old reviews, there is something for everyoneWe walked to it from hotel , about 3 km and taxi back as so exhausted Well worth a look

Sunday mornings marketplace opens early in the morning. There you can buy very cheap clothing, bags etc. Of course most of the products come from China, India and Thailand so don´t expect to find any luxorus products. There´s a small cafeteria that sell drinks, coffee, toast etc. Cheap prices and tasty.

I've seen reviews saying that this flea market is food of cheap new merchandise and I've seen reviews that see it's a nice authentic flea market. The answer is that it is both:  if you enter from the ancient port you will see endless new merchandise, however if you continue to another entry of the market - it's a beautiful authoetic flea market.

Was excited by the prospect of Roman vintage finds however the stalls were mostly usual market stuff eg knock off bags etc. The antique stalls that were there were really expensive, you could haggle but starting prices were higher than expected.

I've read a few negative reviews on here - not sure what you're expecting from a market if you hated this place. It is exactly what a market should be.Yes, there's cheap stuff.Yes, there's fake stuff.Yes, vendors will always try to get a few extra dollars out of you. They're trying to make money!As in all marketplaces, be prepared to walk away and the price will come down. I had fun, found exactly what I wanted - pair of shorts, some scarves, a hat and a shirt for my wife, and a cap for me. All up, three hours there cost approx. €15 and we left happy with our purchases.It's a market - a big market, not a boutique mall. Be prepared.

It is everything that other reviewers have said.  We visited the market today and have found it to be one of the highlights of our current trip.  We went there out of interest, not intending to shop, but in filtering through the "stuff", there were fantastic bargains to be had and we went home with lots of amazing things that were bought at outrageous prices compared to Australia (even at the outlet shopping centres).  There are lots of stands with the same things, but there are also stands where you can find bargains.  Be prepared to bargain a little, or a lot, but it does seem that anything with a price on it will not be bargained on - still worth asking though.  With handbags, there are lots of stands that have similar products, but then there are the gems that have real leather bags, in different styles, for around 50 euros.  My tip is: Be patient and enjoy the experience AND wear comfortable walking shoes!

Was here recently and saw exactly two tables of "antiques." The rest is cheap clothes, motorcycle helmets, key chains....you get the picture. Nothing but junk and garbage. Stay away (unless you're a pickpocket).

I am so very sorry to strongly do not recommend this experience while in RomeSo much to see in this wonderful time that really is a pity to waste a morning here. Noisy, full of cheap things. Nothing is left of the old times. Pay attention to your pockets. It can a times be also unsafe.

This is the place where you can find anything, from clothes to toys, shoes and bags... everything with a very cheap price! The market closes around 13:00 so if you want to find something you have to go early in the morning. Don't forget home your patience!! There is a lot of people !! Be aware of the bag thieves!
I just wanted to take aloof, but it's a waste of time, if you wanna go just pass by early so you could avoid the crowds. Nothing special in there.

the sunday market is a must if you stay over sunday in Rome. easy way to get there is going on the A metro to Valle aurelia and switch to FR3 to Transtevre station. than you are at the end of the market and you can walk for hours until you each the beginning (the Port Portese gate). the market if full with fakes but you can find many good things. if you like markets - it is a must. if you are afraid of crowed - do not go.

When in Rome and that too on the Sunday then its time to visit this place.Cheap clothes , hand bags, shoes , paintings etc..however while buying make sure to chk these properly as the hand bags are covered in plastic bags indicating that they are brand new.Prices are quite reasonable therefore i did not find the need to haggle in the market.
