Hand bags ranged between 5 euors to 10 euros...shoes were in the range from 5 euros to 20 euros...Lovely and cheap place in entire rome for picking up gifts etc ..however may get duped if not checked properly while it has been bought.The market shuts by 1 pm so be there well before time and take time to explore it.

I thought there was only one flea market in Rome, so I did a Google map search for Flea Market and it took me here.  Apparently there is a nice one (Campo de Fiori) but this one was full of garbage.  Seriously - it was all second hand clothes (basically the same stuff everywhere) and plastic crap from China.  Do not waste your time.

At Porta Portese you can buy almost everything, from Made in China-style cheap stuff, scarfs, shoes, clothes (used from 50 cents and up, and new), vintage items, bags, makeup, fake and real jewelry and much more. A lot of stands have the same stuff, and the market is BIG!Make sure to bargain (you don't want to find the same item at a lower price later), hold on to your valuables, don't bring money/phone ecc if you really don't need them, do bring coins... Here, cash is king. Don't forget that you can take the train to the Trastevere station and then walk 5 minutes to the market. Most of the old, cool, vintage stuff are at the via Ippolito Nievo/Largo Francesco Anziani/via Ergisto Bezzi intersection. If you want vintage or used stuff, come early. Like 6-7 AM.Tip: Bring a big shopping bag because the ones they give you rip easily. And you'll end up carrying 5 ripped plastic bags.

There is a lot of junk here.....BUT if you have the time and the patience you can find some deals, although like another reviewer posted, they are far and few between. I did buy some olive wood utensils along with some very inexpensive sweaters that I bought the last time I was here as they surprising wore very well. The market is HUGE and most of it is redundant and it gets incredibly crowded as the day goes on so get there early. I believe it starts at 9:00 and ends at 2:00. Spend the rest of your day walking around the lovely neighborhood of Trastevere. Grazie and Graziella for lunch is great.
Interesting and beautiful landmark, however, nothing many things to see or do around it. I stayed in a hotel with a walking distance to Porta Portese, I couldn't lose the opportunity to visit.

If you like markets, this is the one for you.  It's the biggest I've ever been to.  It's only on on Sundays so get there early as it's HUGE.  You probably can't get round it all in the time it's open. It's in Trastevere but the no.170 bus goes there from the bus station which is right next to the train and metro station.  It takes about 30 minutes to get there.  All kinds of goods on sale. We found a toilet which was inside a supermarket but you have to look for it as there are no signs. The market is so big that on Sundays the buses have to be diverted to get round it so be sure you get to the right bus stop when returning to Rome centre.

Was dragged here by the Mrs who had visions of an Italian version of Chatuchack market in Bangkok.Got there and it was just like a bad weekend market in the UK selling tat and a few olive oils.Dont go, go to Campo Fiori, at least theres some authentic goods and its in a nice square.

What a waste of time.  Long winding path of crowds. Nothing but junk. Not even funny junk for joke gifts.  Out of style, dated looks.  But if you need men's underwear or plastic dolls in bags this is the place for you.  Couldn't get out fast enough.  And you literally couldn't because it's one long, unending path so you pretty much have to endure the whole thing before you can escape. I tried to give this a negative rating.

Don't visit Rome and not make a visit to Porta Portese Market,on a sunday it is a incredible place,with miles and miles of everything..,New and Secondhand areas ,where ,there are lots  of secondhand clothes and designer leather handbags, many silver jewellery stalls, Italian shoes and bags at cheap prices....New top Italian  designer labels at 10 eurs  .....There is also  old area with antique items, this market has everything for sale, and if you are a charity shop or a boot sale addict, you will love Porta Portese.it is a very large,long  road,and a early start is needed to get from one end to the other......

If you enjoy browsing through flea markets, than this is your cup of tea. Rome has so many fabulous sights ready to be explored that in my opinion it's a waste of time visiting such places moreover since all the goods are fakes!!!!!

4 of our 6 traveling to Rome spent about 2 hours traipsing through the hundreds of vendors.Very much like most American flea markets this one featured inexpensive clothes, both new and used, cheap electronics and a lot of inexpensive souvenirs.  There ARE a few dealers selling higher end items, especially books.  They are few and far between.  Most interesting was the 3 card monte con game being run in plain view of the several police patrolling the flea market.The market is probably close to a mile long so be prepared for a long hike if you want to explore everything

We got tipped on this market by a local guide. You can probably buy almost everything here and most prices can be bargained down (to our experience). Dont expect too much from the goods sold here, but take it as an experience. Due to the sheer size of the market, you will probably not have time to see it all.

We had a couple of hours on Sunday before our flight back home so we thought it could be a nice experience. Wrong. A total waste of time. Nothing authentic or distinguishly Italian. No antiques at ,all. Mainly cheap clothes and toys, fake designer bags and i-phone cases all made in the far-east. Haggling is a must.

We came early. About 8:30 AM. It was perfect timing. The sun wasn't that bad until 10 o'clock. It wasn't crowded yet, so we could walk freely. Porta Portese is huge. Sells all kinds of things: new and used; modern electronics and antique stuff; stolen phones and original brand perfumes; souvenirs and home essentials. If you forgot your charger at home or the SD card in your camera is full, 5 euro and you're fine.My advice: don't buy that new and cheap Chinese stuff (clothes, shoes, underwear). They have a lot of it around the city and it's all the same. You can find something used instead and it's gonna be unique. Try looking for a nice fabric like silk or linen.

Very cheap flea market run by professionals, beware. That said, you can buy almost anything here, from clothes to toys to cords for electronics. It's easy to bargain down prices, if you can do it with confidence.

This market is huge and has items of varying quality.  Depending on what you like, you can find a bargain. I found some stylish and extremely comfortable shoes and lovely jewellery.  All at prices I would never find in the UK for the quality.  There was a lot of repetition of stalls and some were far better than others.  It is a flea market so adjust your expectations accordingly and you may be pleasantly surprised.

we took a early sunday morning ride out to porta portese  market,it was a huge market/flea market,it was a interesting morning out,on sale was a mind blowing range of everything possible for sale well worth a look round for the interest factor

Little worth buying and hardly any antiques stores. Mostly jumble sale stores of clothing and shoes or the same type of stores you get on an English market with cheap, low quality goods. If you only have a short holiday in Rome, this will disappoint as it is not in the attractive, older part of Trestavere.

I was expecting flea market with authentic items. But the wast majority is low quality, "made in china" clothes that I wouldn't dare to wear. Many toys for little ones ( I bought one but unfortunately my LO didn't appreciate it at all).If your main target is HM and Primark - you can find something in there too.But if you are looking for higher quality or unique designs - don't go there.And YES we did found some authentic stalls - a few from the other entrance to the market (we entered through Porta Portese arch. But that was lik 5-6 sellers :) out of ...300 may be? You've got the point ;)

I'm not even sure we got half way through it to be honest. In saying that anyone that knows me knows that I take shopping very seriously (more sport than hobby) but it was exhausting!  There was lots of tables with junk items (made in china, poor quality items that weren't of much interest) but I definitely bought some of my best bargains here.  Picked up a couple of italian jackets for an absolute steal and lots of little gifts for friends back home. Definitely worth it but I know my husband would have hated every minute of it.

If you like the flea market scene this is the place, This huge market stretched from arch entry to the neighborhood and inside the neighborhood, many stalls selling almost any junk you could think of, clothes, bags, souvenirs, second hand etc' prices are reasonable and you should negotiate the price. keep your bags and wallets secure, this was the main local advice made for us while asking about the market.
It is known for its flea market, however this is also nice to walk around in the beautiful Trastavere neighborhood. Stop in and have a look at any of the churches in this area.
if you have some free time, it's very big and there is a big selection of second hand items. You need patience in order to find something nice and useful for you.

It is only open on Sunday, but it is enormous.  Stalls stretch on both sides of the street for over a mile.  Not all of the vendors speak English, but with a bit of effort, you will be able to communicate with all of them.  Restaurants are also part of the area.  We took a public bus alongside the river to the main entrance.   On the way back, we discovered a street car line less than 2 blocks away.  After a while, many of the stalls will begin to look alike, but it is a fascinating look into non-touristy Rome.  Bargaining with the vendors is part of the fun.  My son wears the 5X Italian leather jacket we bought there on special occasions.

this sunday morning i visited 9-10am when the foot traffic was nearly entirely locals. there were more tourists coming in as i left.the entry from the main transport street, viale de trastavere, had a great set of old treasures - used leather bags from morocco, linen nightgowns, replacement pieces for your crystal chandelier!. in other spots there were hundreds of stalls for locals eg socks, light bulbs, reading glasses, crystal chandeliers.its a cash system, but up on that main street opposite the entry there's an ATM, i got directions from the patrolling policemen.prices were great. i bought a hand woven mat, used snake skin boots and an antique weekend leather bag, each around 30 euro.after an hour i'd only gone around a little area but like any flea market it was certainly starting to repeat itself. in the area i covered there was only one hot food stall, and a couple of cold food to take. home.i took bus H from termini to get there and back. the tram from piazza venezia also goes there
Went there this sunday morning.Spent 3 hours diging staff!!!Clean marketHugeNiceEven good coffee .

I love this market, it is the second time we have visited (last time was in 2010) and it was just as good as I remembered .There are some areas that are really crowded, like the clothing section, but personally I think that is the least interesting part.  It is a fabulous place to find old treasures big and small.  I love all the old books, letters and photos that have ended up here.  There is old furniture, jewellery, crockery, leather goods old and new in fact there is a bit of everything if you have the patience to keep looking.  You are expected to bargain politely but do not get too carried away as these people are not making their fortune here. If you are not claustrophobic and love to look for a bargain don't miss it...

It just went on for miles!!, I don't think I got to the end of it. It seems to be divided into sections with an interesting (proper flea market) second hand antique section around the Viale Trastevere. The rest is what you find in all markets around Europe, clothes, stalls that sell kitchen stuff etc. There are a couple of cafes en route which are good for a sit down and a coffee, both have lavatories which judging by the queues are rather essential!...It seemed a friendly, safe market, as with all crowded places, you have to keep your hand on your handbag, but apart from that it was a good way to spend a Sunday morning.

As shown in all the reviews, this market is a  " must see " - ONCE !!  It is overly large because there are multiple stalls selling the same imported goods .  Not representative of the wonderful Italian handicrafts that are available at many of the provincial villages .

I have been living in Italy for two years and did not think to go , but I had a friend who was visiting and was interested to go . She concluded that it did not worth wasting half a day.The driving direction which is written on the site was not the most accurate . It would be better to highlight that it is within the trastevere area .

This is an absolutely huge flea market.  There is a lot of repetition of items, and it's more populated with locals than tourists, but that's a good thing.There is a section in the middle that focuses on "antique" items, and there are some bargains to be had.For the most part, there are great deals on costume jewelry and inexpensive clothing, and the experience is  entertaining if nothing else.  If you have plenty of time in Roma, you might include this, but if you're in town for less than 2 weeks, you should probably be elsewhere, like Villa d'Este & Villa Adriana, or Ostia Antica.  Starts early, (6A?) and starts wrapping up by 2P if not earlier.  Sundays only.
As a tourist don't waste your time... Too many other good things to do in Roma. It was a long walk with lots of landfill at the end.
This market is open only at sundays.lot of things to buy and in cheap prices you can buy all your souvenirs to your family at home.
Another market conquered by boring stands with cheap clothes and Rome souvenirs. We were expecting for a more Italian unique experience and were very disappointed.

I agree with some comments which say that almost all the market are clothes stands like the other markets in Rome during the week. However, if you want to see and buy old things (what is supposed to be in a flea market), go to Parboni Napoleone and Angelo Bargoni streets, just next to the Ippolito Nievo square (and tramway station).

I have seen Flea markets in Milan,Italy they call it Sabato-market. Sabato means saturday. This time went to Porta Porse similar flea market in Roma. Nearest metro stop is Piramide in blue line. Market starts around 7AM in morning till 2pm, 2 to 3 hours is enough for porta portese (of course it depends from person to person) If you are looking for some bargain stuff, or any antique items u can visit the place. For me one should visit the place. You can get some used goods at cheap price !!!

I overheard a person comment "where do they get all these worthless items". Yes, there are lots of worthless items buy lots of good items as well. You really have to take your time searching. If purchasing clothing.. Most of the clothing tables are a mess. I searched a few for a couple of minutes each and always found something nice in different sizes. I did not purchase much but I managed to get a very nice vintage purse for 10 euros. I walked away from it the first time because the price was not right. I purchased it at the lower price almost at closing time and on my way out. Don't hesitate to make an offer. The flea markets where I live in NJ are the same so I was not expecting to find much more than I did even in such a huge place. Flea markets are just that and not high end superstores.

In this large flea market you can find many stock or just cheap clothes, shoes, bags, toys, even antiques and things for your car. We went there just because it was very close to our appartment. If you don't go, you don't miss anything. If it's close, take a look and you may find something cheap and useful to buy.

Lots of clothes, purses, jewelry, knick knacks, shoes, kitchen goods.  Pretty much the same things you see for sale in street stalls all over the city.  Some ready-to-eat food.  Very little old stuff or anything interesting.  The most interesting part was watching people sort through tables mounded with piles of clothing.

This Sunday flea market in Rome at Porta Portese is a fun event and a great place to pick up some good deals.  Go early as it is VERY crowded in the afternoon.  There are lots of clothes, purses, shoes, as well as garage sale items (although they really don't have too many garage sales there) Math is the international language so it is OK to bargain!

This market is pretty junky, but if you have patience, and a knack for bargaining, you can pick up some young "in" fashions at incredible prices.  These are not designer or high quality fashions, just hip daywear.  I enjoyed it very much.

We went here on Sunday thinking it was an antique/ flea market. Instead, there were rows and rows of stands, such as the ones you find by metro stops, selling cheap souvenirs, purses and clothes. Do not take a trip here if you have a limited amount of time in Rome.

Seriously, unless you have done everything else that Rome has to offer, I would strongly recommend skipping this market. Go to Campo Fiori instead and save yourself some time, money for a cab or bus and precious hours of walking. The only thing we saw was row after row of crap. The entire time I was concerned that someone would try to pick my pocket. This place has cheaply accessories, cheap knock of handbags and cheap  crap clothes. I was very disappointed and was so sorry I missed the other reviews before I wasted my morning. Please do not make my mistake. Pass on this one.

This is not really a flea market, but a cheap jewelry, cheap accessories, clothing items and souvenirs market.There are a few shops that sell lamps and other flea market stuff but they are few and some are not on the main street.This place is huge and it takes a good 2 hours to walk all the way to the end of the stands. We had the time and the girls wanted to get some souvenirs for their friends. All in all we had a nice time just walking around, haggling and buying some junk. Go if you like this type of pass-time; Don't bother if you are on a tight schedule or if you are looking for antiques.

Absolute load of trash, if you want to waste a few hours of your time then wonder this market of stall after stall of crap, repeated stall after stall of the same stuff, overpriced and low quality, wait till you get back home and go to a car boot

Great if you are looking for tat jewellery and souveiners dont expect to find good quality handbags watches or clothes, quite a way to travel worth a visit if you have an hour to spare but i wouldnt make a special visit as there isnt much else around, on the up side went to a cafe and had one of the best fresh sandwiches ever!!!
New Cheap chinese stuff (clothes & Shoes) you can also get at your shops @ you Main Train station. If you only have a short Time i strongly reconmend to Skip this flea market!!!!

If you have a few hours to kill and are in the area I would recommend coming to Porta Portese.Yes there are cheap knock offs and cheap jewelry but there are also other good stalls suitable for everyone, infact there are hundreds ! To walk from one end of the market to the other will take you a good two hours so bring sensible shoes!
Was looking forward to visiting this market, but left hugely disappointed. Only cheap and fake stuff, doesn't have that unique feeling that you expect from Italian market.

We were recommended to go to the market here which is held every Sunday. Very disappointing unless you want to by cheap sports wear and obviously fake goods. No sign of anything unique or interesting.

A mixed bag. Yes, there are a few 'flea market' stands but you really have to work to find them. It is mostly clothing, inexpensive underwear, kitchen utensils, and reading glasses. Lots of food stands. Colorful slice of life. Big crowds. Watch for pick-pockets here. It was fun for a couple hours.

Take bus #75 from termini station and it drops you off directly in front of the gate. Be prepared for crowds. Mostly clothes, handbags, costume jewellery and the odd bits and pieces. Goes on and on for what seems like miles and miles. Arrived at 12.00 and left when closing at 14.30. Only need one visit. Lots of local Italians were shopping however strangely 80% of the vendors were not local at all. Came away with a few bits and pieces for the children. If you go remember to bargain and walk away if the price is not to your liking - not worth the aggravation. Was fun but very very busy. Note - we did not see any toilet facilities.

This market is nice; if you are looking for cheap clothes, shoes, bags, (ehh well...everything) you will find it. Can't say anything about quality though. It is nice to walk around, have a look, have a talk, watch your pockets....and wander away when you're tired of it.

so crowded, but we've bought souvenirs for 1 euro, which was sold in other places at 3 euro. Also there is a range of vintage bags from 3 euro till 50 (for the designers bags, as celine, & burberry), the books where nice, just ask for Marco Polo who sells great arts books for just 2 euro the book.
huge market with all kinds of stuff, quite cheap, very busy and beware of pickpockets. ALways good to buy some presents.

As I know, all vendors from smaller mercatos are going to Porta Portese on Sundays.  I usually buy there only Italian leather shoes. They are from the last year collections but still leather and superb quality with prices up to 30 euros (when their initial prices are 60 to 150 euros in the shops). Is not it a find? I have never encountered fraud about these shoes – if it is written on the shoe – vera pelle, then it is a leather. The market is huge and in the end you will be very tired but hopefully very happy. Good luck!!!

I've walked the hopeless, energetic, frustruating, suffocating and fun crows of Porta Portese four times. I might not have returned quite so many times had I not had new friends or relatives with me each time. As there were first-timers on each visit, we just had to go to Porta Portese. Remember that it is only open on Sunday mornings!This humongous flea market is comprised by a couple of main streets and an assortment of small side-streets stock full of sellers. Before going there, it would actually be a good idea to purchase a good enough map of Romeand to memorize the layout of the place. The only decent one I know is the 1:12500 map by Touring Editore – a priceless companion to any traveler in the city, and it's dead cheap! (My original copy is from 2003, a battered loyal guide that gave way to the new expanded edition that I bought in September 2012.)  This will make it much easier to walk around and not miss the most interesting stuff on the small streets. The main avenues are full of cheap textiles, gizmos familiar from shopping channel, stolen hygienic products and cigarettes resold as supposedly legit et cetera. The antiques and miscellanea are elsewhere.I'm actually grateful to a friend who had prepared thus and showed me a few streets I hadn't seen before, even though I had been there three times! There's no decent food sold at the market as far as I know, which is a shame. Decent falafel, but one doesn't come to Rome for falafel.

This market is worth a visit to grab cheap clothes, scarves and shoes and also to see curios and antique furniture.  Much like any flea market in the world, there is a lot of rubbish and some hidden treasures.  Beware of pickpockets, the crowds are quite big and it we were warned to keep hands on wallets in pockets and also the zips to bags done up as the pickpockets there are quite adept! Luckily we managed to avoid having our pockets picked. Porta Portese is South of the river and is within easy walking distance of Trastevere area, the nearest station is a walk away but everywhere in Rome is not to greatly distanced apart.

Visit if you are looking for cheap shoes,bags,clothes & accesories. Bargain possible....beware of the used shoes and jackets which are refurbished and sold.GO ONLY IF YOU HAVE LOADS OF TIME otherwise avoidable. Open only on Sundays untill 2 pm.

Last time we were in Rome, we scoured the city for a Roma soccer shirt that wasn't see-through polyester.  I think we ended up paying EU25 for it.  This time, we paid EU5 at Porta Portese for a reasonably nice cotton one with embroidered letters and crest.  This is most definitely a flea market and pickpockets abound but if you know what you're in for, there are some nice deals to be had.

If you really want to visit the sundaymarket at Via Portuense, use the buss and not the subway (the stop 'Piramide' is still quite a long way from the market since you have to find the bridge over the water first). But then again: I found the market not worth visiting at all. You could get cheap fake watches or some shorts and that's about it, really. Maybe I should have walked further but after searching for it for a while (and I must admit: it was far too hot to search for anything) I was so disappointed that I returned back to Rome centre. Still, it was an experience to see the suburbs of Rome. Graffiti, men in broken chairs trying to sell gasoline, weed growing everywhere etc. etc. So: take a risk and find out what I couldn't find, or just stay in Rome centre.

the rest of it includes vendors you can find on streets throughout roma, selling hats, t-shirts, women's clothing, purses, etc. you have to slog through a lot of this stuff to get to the personal selling.some of the regular vendors will bargain, some will not -- and some of the ones that will not actually seem offended by trying to bargain. maybe it was just the day we were there!

Opens at about 9am, shuts down at around 2pm. I caught the number 3 bus (stops Viale Trastevere) from/to Manzoni. The bus also goes past the Colosseum.I had a lot of fun at the market. But then I also really like open air markets. Got a leather belt, hat and sunnies for â¬15 all up. Snacked on a panini for â¬3. It's nothing special considering everything there is to do in Rome, but I had a free Sunday morning and it was worth it for me to check out.You have to haggle with the shop keepers, and that was a lot of fun. English is doable, but I happen to know Bengali (most of the people working there are from Bangladesh) so that made it easier since I can't speak Italian.I say check it out if you've got the time and if you like buying things cheap.

As one of our days in Rome falls on Sunday, we are recommended to visit the flea market which was held every Sunday in Porta Portese. It was a disappointed as most of the vendors sold junk goods instead of the usual handicraft and antique goods. We did not give up after an hour walk hoping to find some vendors which sell unique goods. All we found are full of junk clothes, things made by PRC and third countries, not ITALY. If you have no time, it's fine to skip this place. There's other plenty of places to spend a Sunday in Rome.

This used to be a decent market where you could find some good quality clothing (socks,& sweaters especially). However it is now full of a lot of junk. There's plenty of better ways to spend a Sunday in Rome

I was unsure what I expect after mixed reviews but thought we'd hope on the bus and take a look.It's a massive boot fair full of junk. I was not impressed at all and came away with nothing.In my opinion, not worth the trip.

we went there on a sunday just to see what was all the fuss about!the market itself is big.there are many shops and is packed with people. you can bargain with the owners.. if u have time in rome you can go and see.if not you havent missed alot..

Other than its enormous size and possibly the only day (Sunday) you can find so many thousands of people in Rome in one place.The stands and stalls are also along all the sidestreets.,We were there for over 2 hours but did not find anything of value to buy other than a few souveniers, and not seeing all...... even if we had spent another 2 hours.The atmosphere ,the haggling, and the colourful experience was nice .

It took a little finding, but if you take the No 8 tram from Largo Argentina ask someone when you are say 4 stops past the Tiber: "Porta Portese??" [POR TAR POR TAY SAY?]. There is a stop right at one edge of the market. Those reviewers disappointed by the market may have taken the wrong street, there are a couple of less crowded less interesting areas.AND GO ON A SUNDAY! Not open other days.There are, in addition to such new items as top Levis with labels upside down or wrong size, for next to nothing, new leather bags, and a wondrous array of used garments (great light-weight gifts to take home) there are some serious old or antique items including books, prints, maps, glassware, etc. As the items get more 'antique' and as the end of the market morning, haggle hard (preferably numbers in Italian) expect vociferous and lightning fast exchanges, and if you reach a conclusion on price, do be a good person and buy the thing. Nothing ruder than chiseling tourists who walk away after the bargaining, spoils the process for others.The market is very crowded, wheelchairs only for the brave with company!There is food available from stalls in the market. Or cross the viale Trastevere (tram route) and go a little further uphill to find interesting eating places.OR, exiting the market at Porta Portese itself, upstream end, by the river, cross the bridge and ask your way to the Testaccio market, excellent food market off the tourist routes. And from Testaccio an excellent walk back to the centre via the Circus Maximus, etc.

Trastevere's Porta Portese market is a very unique Sunday morning experience; it is much larger than San Giovanni or even St.Ouen in Paris - easily accessible with Tram #8. You can get garments on grab tables for 0,50 or 1 €. Many stalls for clothing, shoes, leggings, kitchen ware, tools, wallets, some antiques and some 'made in china' items...  The market gets very crowded and it becomes a challenge to move between stalls. Got many people bumping into us - not knowing if it was willingly or unwillingly - but we were sure to hang on to our wallet / purse as this is pickpocket paradise - however, it is worth the experience!

This is a market in Trastevere, Roma that has been in business for hundreds of years. It was a superb place to find the unique, and  if 'market chic' clothing was your thing or for those who couldn't afford haute couture, then this was the place for you. Alas, it has lost so much of its originality through the years as greed took root after the older Roman dealers sold up and retired. There is little haggling, few personalities and street after street sell the same made in China junk. The few stalls still selling treasures and gems have priced their goods in a stratosphere that many customers find out of sight. A few pieces I was interested in turned out to be fakes,although the dealer priced them as originals. Then there is the bag snatching merchants to contend with so as always, be aware of your surrounding. I wish I could write a better review, but better you know before going what it's like, rather than end up being taken for a ride by the new stallholders of this once brilliant market..

We visited the market at Porta Portese on our last day in Rome. The market itself start at 5 of 6 o'clock in the morning and when we left around half past one in the afternoon, it was still very crowded. This market is certainly worth coming to! You can buy clothes at very cheap prices (1 euro for a T-shirt!). But clothes aren't the only things you can buy at the market. There are also jewels, wallets, sunglasses, shoes (lots of shoes!), hats, cd's, dvd's and so on. And if your suitcase is getting too small, you can just buy a new one there!
When in Porta Portese don't forget to try the local restaurant, the name is Brò Porta portese, top roman and italian food with a lot of wines and great value

Don't forget to experience Porta Portese market. Every sunday, from 8 am to 2 pm.  There are two PP markets: one, the biggest and most famous, in Viale Trastevere; the second, little but surely amazing, in  viale Togliatti corner via Prenestina. You can find everything at optimum prices and spend some hours in absolute relax.

Every Sunday in the trendy Trastevere area is Rome's flea market.  I was quite disappointed - too much plastic made in China LV handbags and shoes amongst other poor quality goods.  I did see leather jackets, clothes from 1-10Euros, old makeup, but nothing of interest.  I did purchase a small stove top expresso coffee maker made in Italy for 3Euros - made of aluminum not stainless steel.  There were a few kitchen items of interest.  I was hoping for dead stock - last year's goods - but no luck this time.  Roughtly worth about an hour's visit.The walk along the river is beautiful, passing the Isle in the midst of the River.  Combine this trip with a walk to the old Jewish Ghetto and area ruins - now a trendy area full of restaurants..  All just a short walk from Capital Hill - walk down the stairs and head toward the old Theatre that looks like the Colossuem.  Or, from the Colosseum, head toward the Arch of Constantine and walk around the perimeter of the Palantine ruins until you reach the old Jewish Ghetto.

A huge market covering everything - leather goods, clothes, jewelry, kitchenware, furniture and much more, with a section specializing in vintage & antiques;Prices are significantly reduced than the main shopping streets while quality remains very good. Some great antiques finds if you are into that.Bargaining is a must ! bring along your walking shoes and beware of pickpockets. Try to get there early to avoid the crowds.

My sister and I visted Rome Sept 18-22 2008. We like to shop, we like to bargain, we like outdoor markets - we thought Porta Portese would be just what we were looking for! Alas, we were quite disappointed. Oh, it was indeed a huge outdoor market, but the merchandise available was not of any interest to us, tourists. It was your basic local flea market with items that would appeal to locals: discounted DVDs and CDs, 1e bins of clothes, cheap leather shoes, handbags and jackets, household goods, tableclothes & linens - lots of flea market junk! No antiques, no rare, old roman finds, no cheap souvenir items.  Don't waste your time.

Every Sunday from 7am to 2pm, hundreds and hundreds of stalls, selling everything from dolls to clothes to antiques to coins to fake designer purses to jewelry to electronics and everything in between.  Bartering is a given, get ready to sift through a bunch of crap to take home a couple awesome treasures.

I went to a couple of these flea markets while living in Rome. Mostly they are filled with junk. The better markets are the smaller local ones held in various places around Rome. I bought all my kitchen gadgets at these markets for 1 or 2 for a dollar.I bought some things that I have loved and have displayed in my house now.  Rome apts have huge keys and I found a few keys for sale in the market that I bought for almost no money ($3.00 U.S. for several).  They are displayed on my wall of travel memories now.  I also found lots of old postcards of places in Italy -- old black and white ones with fabulous styles of writing (ornate).  They had old stamps and date marks which were fabulous.  I found frames that allowed me to tear the edges of the postcards (to look older) and I put a postcard in the middle of each frame, for a set of 5 frames. I've had many compliments on the arrangement and the antique subject matter.  I have extras that I plan to use for my scrapbooking -- both the fronts and the backs of the postcards are very interesting.Then once I made a mistake and bought a small weekender suitcase. It looked nice on the outside but then, after I took it back home, I realized it had a faint odor of gasoline. It took much airing out and filling with different deodorizers to get rid of the smell.  So buyer beware.

My wife and I just returned from a very hot and humid week in Rome. As Porta Portese is only open on Sundays, on the early morning day following our arrival, we went. We were advised that it opens at 6:30 AM and should plan on being there by 8 AM, in order to have the best selections. We were also told not to wear any jewelry and to be very much aware of the gypsies (men, women and children), for they are expert pickpockets. With this in mind, I even left my watch in the hotel (glad I did). As it happened, shortly after we arrived at the market, I was approached by a mother and child. The mother carries a piece of cardboard and pushes it against you covering the hands of the child. Let's just say that niether will ever forget this American raised in NY.We arrived at 7:30 AM and began walking the numerous aisles of vendors. The most prominent items for sale were shoes and shirts. There were many fake copies of brand name pocketbooks and wallets for sale. The market has everything from used items to automotive to all types of clothing and food. As the Euro is the form of payment, forget the negotiations of number of Lira to the Dollar and with the Euro being worth about 18-20% higher than the US$, bargains may not always be a bargain. While we did see people negotiating, it was all in Italian.We stayed about 2 hours going up and down most, not all, of the aisles and while we did not see anything that we wanted to buy, it was very interesting to see. While niether of us would probably ever go back, it is worth seeing once.

After waiting in line for over an hour under the sun, we were suddenly required to provide our full names to access the Pantheon.We were told this is to prevent ticket resale, which feels excessive given that entry is already time-based and controlled at the monument. This requirement was not clearly communicated beforehand.We were then asked to show an ID matching the full name on the ticket. When we explained that we didn’t have it with us and questioned the requirement, the staff’s attitude was extremely rude. Instead of offering any kind of solution, we were simply denied entry and asked to leave.Overall, the process felt disproportionate and unnecessarily strict, and I seriously question whether requesting and verifying personal data in this way is appropriate. After such a long wait, the experience was deeply disappointing.Despite being one of my favourite monuments in Rome and an undeniably magical place, I would not recommend visiting under these conditions.

We bought tickets with audio guide on line about 75 days in advance of our visit . We bought  direct from the Pantheon di Roma.  The cost was 15 Euros each. You pick up the ticket at a nearby tour office. Then you walk over to the Pantheon and get in the pre-paid ticket line. We bought for the first time slot at 9AM ; we arrived at the pre-paid line at 8:45AM and there were about 20 people ahead of us in that time slot.  Within a few minutes there were many more behind us. The audio guide is very good.  It clearly explained the highlights of this important site. Plan to be in there for about 2 hours to cover the whole experience.  By the end of our vist the place was crowded but still OK to walk about to admire the entirety of this scacred place. Upon leaving the building I noticed that the line for those without pre-paid tickets was very, very long.  So, buy ahead and save a ton of time.
I know the MONUMENT .. visited already ... even before the ticket .. inside rich in history and beyond works as a Church .. I turned and turned inside....

It's amazing how it stays there after like two thousand years! Talk about the history. They have started to take a 50 SEK entrance but I think that's quite ok. A very special building that takes like 20 minutes to see. Worth a visit!

Wow, what a beautiful, serene place to visit.We had already purchased our tickets online, prior to our visit, but you can buy tickets on the day. There were large queues, but as a disabled person, we presented ourselves to the staff and we were taken straight through, so no queuing. To buy tickets on the day, just join the smaller queue to the left of the Pantheon, don’t engage with ticket touts outside.We sat down inside and were awestruck by the sheer size and beauty. Lovely coffee shops around the Pantheon too.

So I didnt actually go in the pantheon but I am writing this rewiew because I visited it from the outside, even if you dont go in it is worth your time to go and look at this beutiful structure, I had a great experience even just admireing a wonderful temple. I expected less than what it was alot bigger than it looks on that photo. There is a big crowd around the entrance but getting a photo is doable. If you want to get in would reccomend to reserve online as the line to get a ticket is unwaitable but if not I reccomend to still go visit it from the outside for 5/10 minutes its worth the time. I reccomend this to people who are at least a little interested in history.

A lovely chap met us, we scanned a QR code, downloaded an app, logged in with the details he provided and then he walked us to the “skip the line” with our preprinted tickets that he provided. Easy to locate the meeting point and the audio is clear.

Just like every major attraction in Rome the Pantheon is insanely crowded. It's hard to enjoy and absorb any of them with a quality visit. If I had known this beforehand I would not have bothered. Be warned.

Visit itself rather short, less than an hour is enough; structure still in perfect condition and wonderful from an architectural point of view.You waste time especially waiting in line to buy a ticket for € 5 at the totems near the entrance: a lot of crowds, to which is added the one always present in the square.

Great experience, well worth visiting inside - the queue without tickets took about 10 minutes to go down. Friendly staff and security. €5 per person entry, although this is increasing to €7 in July 2026. Not too overcrowded and surprisingly spectacular.
Ticket does not reduce waiting timeNo guideLittle to see
With or without pre-booked ticket, same waiting timeAudioguide to download for a feeNot much to see

This was an interesting tour narrated by Rick Steves, which could have been downloaded regardless of paying for the tour. However, given the ticket line, it was good to have purchased the tickets in advance so we didn’t have to wait.
This architectural wonder must be seen . If you are there before 10 am, you can simply buy a ticket on site.

It's a very stylish building, even though it's an old building, like the story of Raphael, who loved it and was buried here. Amazingly, it hasn't been destroyed in long history. I don't have any knowledge of architecture, but I'm afraid of how this was made a long time ago.

Pantheon is not just merely a church, it describes the belief system of ancient rome was not as simple as described today. An ancient temple with no statues, where sunlight appears through an open oculus, describing the powerful source of life in a beaitiful way. The ancient temple is now a church. Not only an architectural marvel, a monument which describes an ancient civilization. Make sure to buy skip the line tickets as it can get crowded. One can spend aroud 20 to 30 minutes inside the church.

We arrived at 7:30am and there was no one around, great for external pics. Had to wait for opening. Do not eat or drink in the places in the piazza  we did food was horrible and expensive  bit needed the toilet.Going inside judt crazy, loads of people, but enjoyed the audio guide - an incredible buiding.
Located in the historic center of Rome, this is one of the main monuments in the history of the city.It is one of the best preserved structures of ancient Rome and deserves a visit.
Amazing tour,it felt peaceful even though it was busy.Our time slot was changed and we were put with a guide Claudia,she was very witty and knowledgeable.It was around 11 euros.
This was amazing! It is amazingly preserved and beautiful. I think it is worth getting a ticket in advance though
Engineering marvel.  Crazy to think it is 2000 years old.

Visited the Pantheon with a guided tour and it truly enhanced the experience. Skipping the long lines made everything smooth and stress-free, while the knowledgeable guide brought the monument’s rich history and stunning architecture to life. Seeing the magnificent dome and oculus in person was unforgettable, and the insightful commentary made the visit even more meaningful. A well-organized and worthwhile experience for anyone exploring Rome.

Originally built as a temple dedicated to all gods by Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa in 27 BC and then rebuilt by Hadrian, the Pantheon is a masterpiece of architecture and engineering. The imposing pronaos, with its sixteen monolithic Corinthian columns of grey and pink granite, overlooks the square directly, creating a spectacular visual impact. The distance from the floor to the top of the dome is identical to the diameter: practically the interior space consists of a perfect sphere. The vault is decorated with 5 orders of drawers and has in the center the bronze-edged eye, the only source of natural light in the building. The marble floor, slightly convex and with an almost invisible slope, conveys water (in case of rain) to 22 hidden drainage holes, preventing the formation of puddles. The niches are overflowing with works of art, including the fresco of the Annunciation by Melozzo da Forlì, the tomb of the sovereigns Vittorio Emanuele II and Umberto I, the tomb of Raphael Sanzio (obtained from an ancient Greek marble sarcophagus and surmounted by the statue of the Madonna del Sasso by Lorenzetto). Leaning against the back of the “Rotunda” are, instead, the conspicuous remains of the Basilica Neptuni (raised by Agrippa, at the same time as the Pantheon, but later rebuilt by Hadrian. Wonderful!!

This magnificent building is one that many visitors to Rome quite rightly want to see, but the queues in April were not too long and while the building is certainly busy, it is not oppressively so. Originally built in the 2nd century AD, it became a Roman Catholic basilica in the 7th century and its continuous use explains why it is still intact and so well preserved. Entombed inside are the Renaissance master Raphael and the first kings of the united Italy, Victor Emmanuel II and Umberto I. The star of the show is the incredible cupola with its occulus, still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome. To visit Rome and not see it would be a crime of omission.
The Pantheon fantastic of course, but the time to observe it guided is very little and inside the guide can not speak, better to do it alone.

After buying the tickets, we received an email stating we had to pay more to keep the time slot we had chosen. We lost the tour and requested a refund as we had to buy our own tickets to enter the Pantheon.
The most perfectly preserved ancient building in all of Rome

I was lucky enough to explore Rome with Mohammad and it was genuinely one of the highlights of my entire trip to Italy.From the moment the tour began it was clear that Mr. J has a deep and genuine love for Roman history and culture. His storytelling is exceptional — he has a rare gift for making ancient history feel immediate, personal and thrilling.He took us through streets and corners of Rome that most tourists completely miss, sharing stories and historical context that you simply cannot find in any guidebook.His multilingual skills also made our mixed group feel completely comfortable and included throughout the entire experience.Journey with Mr. J is not just a tour — it is a real journey through time. Absolutely unforgettable!

The Panteon is an absolutely marvelous monument, but for me it is not understandable why one wants to wait an hour in the queue and pay 10€ at an agency to see the interiour and leave after 15 minutes.If you want the experience, come at 9 in the morning and buy a ticket directly at the entrance. Then the queue is short and no agency doubles the price.
Beautiful! To do absolutely with an audio guide in order to know the remarkable history of this site. You will be surprised to learn the richness of this place.

Rome has so many architectural gems throughout the city and the Pantheon did not disappoint.  It is so beautiful inside and the structure of the building itself is wonderful.  Even though it is small inside, it is packed with lots of great history and a spectacular dome.  Also, it is a church.   It only takes less than an hour to walk around the inside of the Pantheon.  I was able to purchase my tickets onsite and the wait time was only 10 minutes.   They have multiple kiosks outside where you can purchase your tickets.

Visited the Pantheon in Rome and it lived up to the hype. The scale hits you the second you walk in, and that open oculus in the dome is even more impressive in person. It’s free to enter, which makes it feel even more special compared to other landmarks nearby. It does get crowded, but if you go early, it’s worth it just to stand there and take it all in. Definitely a must-see

No visit to Rome is complete without a visit to the Pantheon, an overwhelming building twenty centuries old. Built in the times of the Roman empire it was designated as a church some centuries ago. In the Pantheon are buried Italian kings and also the unique artist Rafael. The Romans knew a thing or two about sustainable building, as proven by the Pantheon. Even now scientists are poring over the possible recipe of the concrete the Romans used to construct their buildings.

A small building, only one room but there are quite a few things to see in there. We had an audio guide which definitely helped. The gift shop had run out of guide books which was frustrating as we would have liked to learn more about the history of the building. We bought skip the line tickets which was a good idea as the queue to buy tickets was very long.

It is an absolute architectural marvel, unique in the world, coupled with an unimaginable technical feat that also explains this integrity of structural conservation unprecedented in view of its 1900 years.The Pantheon of Rome, which inspired so many other monuments such as the Capitol in Washington and its French counterpart, embodies the pinnacle of genius of the Roman builders who nevertheless make the greatest masters of this art of all time. That is to say the feat, especially of this dome which would seem of divine essence as its designers wanted. The bronze doors that still work are nearly 2000 years old and the columns of the 15-meter-high entrance gantry (pronaos) are carved in one block!

We paid a trip to the pantheon. We bought our skip the line tickets off get your guide. We went at 11 and there wasn’t much of a a queue for the normal pay on the day line. I think it would be super busy during the summer months. It’s very beautiful inside with the hole in the roof . When it rains it cascades down and is drained away. It looks quite spectacular. You would only need half an hour max to visit here.
We walked around Rome and passed the Pantheon, but there was such a queue to the entrance so we skipped going in. We walked around and looked from outside.

Booked the ticket online, and although the line was still long, it moved quickly. One room, but filled with rich history and pure architectural magic. The Pantheon in Rome is simple, powerful, and unforgettable.
entry had 5.- pp. Very nice but we're going around fast. Pretty from the outside, too. There can be people so plan time.

Absolutely beautiful & stunning piece of history!Would definitely recommend visiting- with either an audio or real guide to understand the history & beauty of what you are seeing.Rome is so full of beauty & history & the Pantheon is one of its jewels.Wish we had experienced the dropping of the petals through the roof as sounds stunning10/10

It is one of the best preserved ancient Roman buildings in the world and a masterpiece in the history of human architecture. Its name comes from the Greek meaning place dedicated to all gods. Known for its huge domes and magnificent porticoes, the building remains one of Rome's most popular tourist destinations.HistoryInitial construction (27 BC)Originally built under the auspices of Octavian's son-in-law, consul Marcus Agrippa, the Pantheon was dedicated to the Roman gods around 27 BC. Above the main entrance, there is still an inscription “M.AGRIPPA·L.F.COS·TERTIUM·FECIT” (which was built during Agrippa's three reigns), which has long led scholars to believe that the existing building was built by Agrippa.Fire and reconstructionAgrippa's original building was destroyed in a fire in 80 AD, which was repaired by Emperor Tumisan, but soon burned by lightning.Extant buildings (c. 118–125 AD)The existing Pantheon was rebuilt by order of Emperor Hadrian and completed around 125 AD. Hadrian kept the original inscription without signing himself out of respect for Agrippa, a practice that was rare in ancient times.Medieval: changed to churchIn 609 AD, Byzantine Emperor Phocas gave the Pantheon to Pope Boniface IV, who converted it into a Christian church dedicated to the Virgin Mary and the martyrs. It was this “conversion” that allowed the building to survive the massive demolition and destruction of pagan temples in the Middle Ages.Renaissance to modern timesThe Pantheon became an important royal mausoleum, where Vittorio Emmanuel II, King of the Italian Reunification, and his son Umberto I are buried. The famous painter Raphael also slept there (1520).Architectural featuresThe Pantheon's dome is 43.3 metres in diameter and indoor net height, and is perfectly proportioned - if a complete sphere is placed under the dome, it can reach the ground and the dome. There is a circular sky-eye about 8.9 meters in diameter in the center of the dome; it is the only source of light indoors and serves as a vent. Rainwater falls from the eye of the sky to the ground, channelled by an elaborate drainage system.The dome remained the largest reinforced concrete-free dome in the world for more than 1,300 years, until it was surpassed during the Renaissance (Cathedral of Our Lady of the Flowers, Florence, 1436).AnecdotesRaphael's last wish: Raphael, who wished to be buried in the Pantheon, is said to have said with affection: "As long as I rest here after I die, I have no regrets in this life. “His epitaph was written by Cardinal Peterbo Bembo and reads in Latin to the effect that: “The natural fear of living was transcended by him, and when he died, he feared his own demise.”Copper was misappropriated: in the 17th century, Pope Urban VIII ordered the removal of bronze materials from the Pantheon portico for the construction of the Huagai (Bernini design) of St. Peter's Basilica and the canons of the Vatican Castle. The move drew strong criticism, with the Romans mocking: “The Barbarynis (i.e. the barbarians) did what even the barbarians did.”The magical light effect of the heavenly eye: every year at noon on April 21 (the anniversary of Rome's founding), sunlight shines directly from the heavenly eye on the entrance gate, illuminating the porch and creating spectacular columns of light, which many scholars believe Hadrian designed on purpose.Concrete Mystery: The ash concrete formula used by the ancient Romans was lost in the Middle Ages. The study found that the concrete is not only extremely strong, but even strengthens over time - a property that stuns modern engineers when it comes into contact with seawater to create new mineral crystals.The Pantheon spans nearly 2,000 years, from pagan temples to Christian churches, from royal mausoleums to tourist resorts, and is almost the best symbol of the vitality of Roman civilization.

Creative aspects of the design of the Pantheon include:* Concrete Innovation: The Pantheon's dome is the largest unreinforced concrete dome ever built. The Romans developed high-quality concrete and used it to create the dome, arches, and vaults. * Differential Concrete Mix: The concrete mix wasn't uniform throughout the dome. Heavier materials like brick and tufa were used at the base, while lighter materials like pumice were incorporated above. * The Oculus: The oculus, a circular opening at the top of the dome, reduced the overall weight and provided a source of natural light. This also allowed for a unique lighting effect on certain days of the year, with the sun illuminating the interior in a dramatic way. * Step-Rings: Step-rings were incorporated into the construction, which helped to distribute the weight of the dome and counteract hoop stressSome prominent examples of buildings that are considered copies or are heavily influenced by the Pantheon's construction include: * The Jefferson Memorial in Washington, D.C., designed by John Russell Pope, is a notable example, often considered a direct copy of the Pantheon's dome and portico.* The University of Virginia Rotunda, designed by Thomas Jefferson, is a direct homage to the Pantheon and served as the university's library.* The U.S. Capitol Building in Washington, D.C. incorporates a massive dome and a grand entrance influenced by the Pantheon's design.

The Pantheon oculus is a circular opening at the top of the Pantheon's dome in Rome that serves as the building's sole source of natural light. This 25-foot-wide aperture, meaning "eye" in Latin, is an architectural marvel that acts as a giant sundial, casting shifting beams of sunlight inside the rotunda. The oculus also allows for ventilation, lets rain in, and its design helps distribute the dome's weight.

Visiting the Pantheon in Rome felt like stepping into a space where time refuses to move forward. I had seen countless photographs of the building before arriving, but nothing compared to standing in front of it on a warm afternoon with the noise of the city swirling around its ancient columns. The moment I walked through the massive bronze doors, the sound softened and the atmosphere changed. Light poured through the oculus at the top of the dome, forming a bright circle on the marble floor that slowly shifted as the sun moved. Watching that gentle movement made the entire structure feel alive.The interior felt both vast and intimate. Even with other visitors standing around me, the space had a quiet presence that encouraged you to slow down and absorb every detail. The marble walls, the intricate chapels, and the perfect symmetry of the dome created a sense of balance that is hard to describe until you are inside it. I found myself looking up repeatedly, drawn to the way the light shaped the dome’s coffers and how the sky seemed close enough to reach. At one point a bird flew across the opening, and its silhouette against the sunlight became one of those unexpected moments that stay with you long after you leave.What struck me the most was how the Pantheon is still used as a living building. It is not just an ancient monument but also a church, so the quiet hum of devotion mixes with the curious energy of travelers. I liked lingering near the tombs of Italian kings and listening to a local guide explain how the structure has remained standing for nearly two thousand years without losing its harmony. There was something reassuring in that longevity, as if the building itself were asserting that beauty and purpose can endure through centuries of change.Stepping back outside felt like emerging from another world. The plaza was lively, filled with people taking photos or sitting on the edge of the fountain while musicians played nearby. The contrast between the calm of the interior and the buzz of the square made me appreciate the experience even more. The Pantheon left me with a sense of awe that was both peaceful and energizing, and it became one of those rare places where history feels immediate and personal.

The Pantheon in Rome was built by Emperor Hadrian around 2,000 years ago, around 126 AD. Before it was converted into a Christian church, it served as a temple dedicated to all the pagan gods of ancient Rome. Today, the Pantheon also houses the tombs of several iconic historical figures. Later, it was consecrated as a Christian basilica and dedicated to the Virgin Mary.Raphael (Raffaello Sanzio) (d. 1520) – The world-renowned Renaissance painter and architect. The dome of the Pantheon isn't even the largest dome in Rome. But the building that was constructed in 27-25 BC is the only ancient Roman building that has remained practically intact through the centuries and is described as "the most beautiful relic of ancient Rome" and an"unrivaled artifact of Roman antiquity." Built on the site of an earlier temple by Marcus Agrippa during the reign of Augustus to honor their military victory at Actium in 31 BC, the present building is a result of a radical reconstruction by the Emperor Hadrian between 118 and 125 AD. It includes a Greek-style portico with 40-foot-tall Corinthian columns made of Egyptian granite, an enormous portal with bronze doors, walls made of brick-faced concrete and a rotunda with statues of Roman emperors occupying niches in the walls The open window at the top of the dome was the interior's only source of direct light. The dome is an architectural masterpiece, a true stroke of genius by Hadrian. It is almost a perfect sphere because it's height is equal to its diameter, 43 meters and 44 centimeters by 43 meters and 44 centimeters. Go figure. The dome is made entirely of concrete that doesn't have a frame to stand on. To build it, Hadrian invented a grandiose solution because he had to built it in such a way that it became lighter as it rose in height, thus reducing the thickness of the wall, which goes from six meters at the base to two meters near the central hole. Lighter materials were mixed with the concrete from travertine marble at the base to pumice stone at the top. And so it stands today.

The Pantheon is a must see in Rome.  We visited on our first day in the city after walking from our hotel to Piazza Navona and the Trevi Fountain.   It was a hot July day but we did not wait long in line to purchase the 5 euro entry ticket.  We did not bother with the timed ticket because it was afternoon and the lines were short.  The building is a marvel of engineering.  How could it withstand the elements and still be standing?   Built almost two thousand years ago, it has been a temple to pagan Gods and is currently a church.  In previous visits (this was my third) you could stand in the center of the building directly under the round opening in the ceiling.  Today there is a large sculpture in that space.  Visit to appreciate the building’s longevity and history, but you don’t need more than 45 minutes.  Other sites are a short walk away, making this a great stop on a walking tour of the old city.

The inside actually felt small and simple compared to many of the other churches in Rome. I'm glad I visited inside once, but if I were in Rome again, I take a selfie outside and skip the inside visit.

Inside you'll marvel at its beauty from the ceiling (with an open hole on it, called the "oculus") to its marble flooring. Some famous personalities also have their tombs here, like the painter Rafael and some kings of Italy. Mass is still celebrated here. Check out its schedule in case you want to attend it.

The Pantheon, Trevi Fountain and Spanish Steps are all within a reasonable walk of each other. My ranking is 1 Pantheon, 2 Trevi Fountain, 3 Spanish steps.  I will review them in descending order starting with the Spanish Steps. The Spanish Steps are --- steps. Perhaps there is an elegance to the curve and the balustrades but you will be hard pressed to see it because of the crowds. To me this was the Kardashians of landmarks, famous for being famous, a picture of it, the real thing, neither is really going to make that much impact on anyone who has half a life. People wax poetic about the sound the fountain makes. Well, maybe at 6AM but you will not be able to hear or fully see the fountain around all the people posing for selfies. The streets around the steps are filled with high end lux brand stores that were crawling with private security outside. I guess they were supposed to be anonymous in their tailored suits but they really need some Mod Squad training if they are going to fit in with all the tourists. Admittedly there was a heightened security threat announced that day and I am sure every one in Italy immediately yelled 'Protect the Gucci bags at all costs!' So, The Spanish Steps. they make a scenic backdrop for shopping in stores that don't have clearance racks.The Trevi Fountain is a magnificent and massive thing. It looks like it is growing out of the building or that the building is growing out of it. It is incredibly dramatic in all its baroque style. There was a barricade around it when we were there while a crew used very specific vacuum cleaners to suck all the coins out that people throw in daily. The vacuuming was supposed to be finished by 10AM and people could get closer to the fountain, so keep that in mind if you interested in throwing money your around. It is actually easier get a senes of the entire fountain from above. This is another site famous for all the films it has been featured in so it was quite crowded, even before 10AM on a weekday, and while people are encouraged to reenact Three Coins in the Fountain, you cannot reenact La Dolce Vita. Saving the best for last the Pantheon is an other worldly experience. It is a space impossible to get a sense of from a photograph. We walked by it on a Saturday around noon and the crowds were so dense we decided to try again early on a weekday. You can get a timed ticket online, but this is the one time we didn't do it in case I drank the entire bottle of wine the night before and we had to delay it another day. We managed to get there around 8:45 and oddly the timed ticket line was significant while there was no one in the line to buy tickets. It was 5 euro (cash only) and we walked right in. The building is singular. It starts with the approach. There are massive doors and you can see a coffered dome with a mystical light that is revealed slowly as you approach the doorway. Inside, words, like pictures, cannot describe the experience of this colossal volume with an opening to the sky in the center. James Turrell has nothing on the ancients. A perfect circle of dense blue, occasionally a cloud drifts by, the sense of immense space, it literally transports you. This building is old and there is nothing that compares to it. Even with the amount of people in it, you can still feel alone with it and that is a rare thing. I am not going to go into the history of the Pantheon, that's what Wikipedia is for, but it has been in use since the 2nd century so it has been through a lot. While the inside has been pasted with images of catholicism the outside really shows the changes and abuse it has withstood in its life, so it is worth looking at more than just the front facade. Walking around the building past the back left corner there is a small plaza with a fantastic monument of an elephant with an Egyptian obelisk on its back. The easter egg hunt of finding Egyptian obelisks in Rome is its own thing, but this one, it turns out, was designed by Bernini. The elephant is so alive you can tell it is by a major artist, but who would expect a Bernini that has been out in the elements for almost 400 years virtually overshadowed by the Pantheon. If you are taking a leisurely walk and continue down that road you will come to the Theatre of Pompey, where Julius Caesar was murdered, which is how that democracy ended.

Visiting the Pantheon in Rome with my family was one of the most memorable experiences of our trip to Italy. Walking through the cobbled streets of the Eternal City, we knew we were approaching something special—but nothing prepared us for the moment we stepped into the Piazza della Rotonda and saw the Pantheon in all its grandeur.First ImpressionsThe Pantheon is absolutely breathtaking from the outside. Its massive Corinthian columns and imposing facade are instantly awe-inspiring. Even my kids, who can usually only be impressed by gelato and gladiators, stopped in their tracks. The sense of history was palpable—you can feel the centuries in the worn stone and the quiet reverence of the place.Stepping InsideAs we walked through the enormous bronze doors into the rotunda, a collective “wow” escaped from all of us. The interior is a perfect circle, with marble floors, statues of saints, and beautiful niches lining the walls. But the true highlight? The oculus—a circular opening in the center of the dome that lets sunlight pour in. It was around midday, and the sunbeam shining through the oculus felt almost spiritual. The light moved across the walls like a sundial, and it was mesmerizing to watch.Even our youngest was intrigued by the idea that this hole in the ceiling has been open to the sky for nearly 2,000 years—and that it’s the only source of natural light in the building.A Living MonumentWhat struck me most was how alive the Pantheon still feels. It's not just a relic—it's a working church, known as the Basilica of St. Mary and the Martyrs. There were people praying, lighting candles, and taking in the moment in silence. We explained to our kids that we were standing in a building that had been a Roman temple before becoming a Christian church, and that it has been continuously used since around 126 AD. That timeline was mind-blowing.Family-Friendly ExperienceThe visit was surprisingly family-friendly. There’s no entrance fee (which is great for a family group), and the site isn’t overwhelmingly large, so you can take your time without feeling rushed or exhausted. There are benches where you can sit and take it all in, and just outside are plenty of cafes and gelaterias where we grabbed a treat afterwards.We also appreciated the subtle educational opportunity—history, architecture, and art all in one spot, without it feeling like a museum.Final ThoughtsThe Pantheon is more than just a must-see site—it's a powerful experience that bridges the ancient and modern world. Whether you’re a history buff or just want to feel a deeper connection to human civilization, it delivers in a quiet, awe-inspiring way.Our family left feeling humbled, inspired, and surprisingly peaceful. If you're in Rome with your loved ones, don’t miss it—it’s not just a monument, it's a moment you’ll remember together.

The Pantheon is not only one of the most iconic monuments in Rome: it is an experience that leaves you speechless, even if you thought you already knew everything. As soon as you enter, time seems to stand still. The light filtering through the oculus – a perfect 9-metre opening in the centre of the dome – creates an almost mystical atmosphere. There are no windows, just that circle of sky that illuminates the interior as if it had been designed by a Renaissance artist with today’s technology. And instead it is there, intact for over 1900 years. The dome is the largest in the world in unreinforced concrete. Even today, engineers and architects study it to understand how it is possible. And then there is that sense of harmony: everything is proportionate, simple and perfect. You walk and you realize that you are stepping on the same floor that the Romans of the second century stepped on. Inside, in addition to the imposing architecture, there are also the tombs of great Italians, including Raphael Sanzio and the kings Vittorio Emanuele II and Umberto I. An ancient temple turned church, became a mausoleum, became a legend. The Pantheon is one of those places that never disappoints you. It's simple, solemn, great. A monument that combines technique and spirituality in an absolutely unique way.

Timeless Wonder of Rome: PantheonI was really captivated when I visited the Pantheon, which is high on my attraction list in Rome! Rising right in the heart of the city, in the heart of Piazza della Rotonda, this structure impresses with its architecture and atmosphere.First Impression & IntroductionWhen you approach the Pantheon, you see a majestic structure welcoming you with its massive columns. It’s simple but just as impressive from the outside. Entry was completely free, which is a big advantage over other sights in Rome. The moment you step in, you’re entering a different world.Architecture & AtmosphereThe massive dome inside is truly breathtaking. The natural light flows in, especially through the oculus (open round window) in its middle, adding a mystical touch. This structure has been here for centuries, so people feel like they’re on a journey through history. Inside, there''s a quiet atmosphere, so visitors often spend time looking up in awe.Dates and DetailsThe history of the Pantheon, M.S. It dates back to 126 and is most impressive because it’s largely preserved from the Roman Empire to the present day. There’s also the tomb of the famous Italian artist, Raffaello. Strolling around historic details makes the structure more meaningful.Square and EnvironmentPiazza della Rotonda square in front of the Pantheon is also special beauty. There are plenty of cafes to sit around for a coffee. We sat in a cafe after a visit, savored an Italian coffee, and savored the moment with repeat views of this amazing structure.As ResultOne of the most impressive structures to see in Rome is definitely the Pantheon! With both architecture and history, it takes you on a journey through time. If you’re on your way to Rome, be sure to stop by and be enchanted by that massive dome!👉 Tip: Visit early in the morning or in the evening, and you’ll find fewer crowds and a more peaceful atmosphere.

The Pantheon is the best-preserved structure from ancient Rome and is a marvel of engineering considering it was built nearly 2000 years ago in AD 120. It was a temple dedicated to all of the Roman gods. It remains intact to this day as it was transformed into a Christian church after the Fall of Rome guaranteeing its preservation. Inside is found the tombs of King Victor Emmanuel II, the first king of Italy after unification and of his son Umberto I who was assassinated in 1900 along with the great Renaissance artist Raphael. In truth, there really isn't a lot to see inside. However, the Pantheon is a must-see highlight of Rome. Unlike the Roman Forum which requires much imagination to picture what the ruins of the great temples must have looked like, the Pantheon remains intact to this day. Visiting the Pantheon is no longer free, and it is best to book in advance online to get a specific time slot for entry. The 15-euro charge includes an audio guide. It is best to visit as early in the day as possible as it can get quite crowded. Opening time is 9am. It is also worthwhile to stop by at night to get photos as the piazza in front of the Pantheon is quite beautiful when lit after dark and it is more serene when less crowds are around.

One of the most famous monuments of the capital as well as one of the most significant and best preserved in ancient Rome. Made by Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa, son-in-law of Octavian Augustus, what we see today is the reconstruction made under the emperor Hadrian, and subsequently restored by both Septimius Severus and Caracalla. The magnificent state of preservation is probably due to the fact that since 609 AD it has been used as a Christian church (and still is!), dedicated to Santa Maria ad Martyres. Externally, the majestic pronaos of 16 granite columns 14 meters high will already impress, but the greatest charm is inside for the famous dome, a masterpiece of Roman engineering. Made entirely of Roman concrete it is the largest dome in the world made with this technique and probably the widest ever. From the bottom you can not understand the real enormous dimensions: the central hole, the only point of light of the interior, is 9 meters wide and the entire diameter is 43 meters and 44 cm (as well as the height from the floor, so that the interior of the Pantheon could contain a perfect sphere of 43.44 meters in diameter); to support its enormous weight (thousands of tons) expedients have been adopted, such as the decrease in the thickness of the wall (from 6 meters of the base to 2 meters near the oculus) and the use of lighter and lighter concrete as you climb in height (from the pieces of travertine and bricks at the base, to then continue with tufa and finish with lapilli and pumice). The interior has obviously been modified over the centuries since, as mentioned, from a Roman temple it was transformed into a church, so today the perimeter of the cylinder has chapels, niches, aedicules and works from various eras, but like the dome, the large bronze door and the floor in polychrome marble is from the Roman period, a floor that, although not noticeable, is slightly concave in the center to collect water that falls from the oculus on rainy days and that through some holes is then collected in underground pipes. Among the works present you can see valuable sculptures of the sixteenth century and two remarkable paintings from the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, as well as the tomb of Raphael and the tombs of the first two kings of Italy: Victor Emmanuel II and Umberto I (the latter together with his wife, Queen Margherita). I will not dwell on other aspects, curiosities and legends concerning this extraordinary monument, which I invite you to discover in advance to make the visit even more interesting.
