The Pantheon is one of the iconic landmarks of Rome and with good reason. In simple terms described as a temple dedicated to all of the gods, the first structure to appear on this site was built during the reign of the Emperor Augustus. It was subsequently destroyed by two different fires over the next 100 years until the Emperor Hadrian commissioned the current structure, which is widely renowned for having the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome, making it an architectural marvel. The Pantheon has been in continuous use since its construction, initially as a Roman temple and subsequently as a Christian church dedicated to St. Mary and the Martyrs. It currently contains the tombs of the renowned Renaissance artist Raphael and the first King of unified Italy, Victor Emmanuel II. One of the oldest artefacts in the building is a 7th-century Byzantine icon given by Emperor Phocas II to then Pope Boniface IV.The Pantheon is a truly spectacular structure and it should be on anyone's top 5 list of sites to see in Rome. You can easily combine a visit with a walk to the beautiful Piazza Navona, which is less than 5 minutes away.
A wonderful experience, places full of so much history and so much culture. How not to fall in love with Rome!!!!

The place is small, the dome huge, impressive. It is worth putting it on your list and making time to visit. The Pantheon was the inspiration for the Duomo of Florence, who in turn inspired that of St. Peter and the White House in Washington, D.C. No need to buy tickets online (we went in October at 5:30pm and only did 10 min). Outside there are many people in the square, be careful with pickpockets and take the children by the hand if they are boys because in the crowd they can lose sight of you. Inside there is room to sit because it is a church. You need to dress with knees and shoulders covered.If you need to go to the bathroom, buy a coffee. One of the best gelatories we tried in Italy is on the side. The coffee "La Casa del Caffe Tazza D'oro" we liked, they have a great variety, good quality in coffee and has bathrooms. They also have coffee grounds or beans to go. METER: Children under 10 years old do not pay. If you buy tickets you have a certain number of hours to use them. If they expire DO NOT THROW THEM AWAY because it is cheaper to "reload" them on the same machine than to buy a new one.  Learn this word: USCITA, it means "exit."  Subway doors won't open if you stick your hand or foot in, be careful. There's a yellow line on the floor, stay back.STRIKES: We stayed 4 days in Rome and we got 2 strikes and a march in front of the hotel. There are many strikes all over Italy. Strikes are announced a day or two in advance and there is always a schedule or some line that does guarantee service, but you have to be aware. Check the news a day before your walk or ask at your hotel.TAXIS: Taxis in Rome can only be taken from certain places and there are huge queues. You find them on google maps, they are usually close to very tourist places like the Pantheon or the Colosseum. There is no UBER but limousine service, very expensive. Don't trust me to be able to take a taxi.

The Pantheon is a must see! We paid $5 euro and the line moved us in quickly. The Pantheon's dome is still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome. At the top of the dome sits an opening, or oculus, 27 feet in width. The oculus, which has no covering, lets light—as well as rain and other weather—into the Pantheon. The bronze doors are 24.7 feet high, the oldest in Rome. It also houses the tombs of Vittorio Emanuele II, the first king of Italy; his son, Umberto I, and Umberto’s wife, Queen Margherita. The Pantheon is a catholic church and services are still held so dress appropriately with knees and shoulders covered.

The Pantheon is one of the best preserved buildings of antiquity. For us it was a highlight of our trip to Rome. The massive dome, measuring around 43 metres in diameter, is a masterpiece of Roman architecture and has been considered the largest dome ever made of unreinforced concrete for almost 2,000 years. For this reason alone, you should definitely visit the Pantheon from the inside. In addition, there is the magnificent marble interior decoration, the beautiful altarpieces (as it is now a church) and the tombs of world-famous personalities (among others Raphael, two Italian kings). The official audio guide is highly recommended for interesting background information. Time required: 45 - 60 minutes.

Come scriveva Stendhal: "The Pantheon is the most perfect thing we have left of Roman architecture." It was originally a temple dedicated to all gods, built by urban planner Agrippa in 27 BC. Inside is the tomb of Raphael Sanzio, between the fifth and sixth chapels. Noteworthy is the dome, which has a huge opening in the center, the only source of light. Admission costs € 5 for adults, minors enter for free.

The views and inside are just amazing we took a lot of photos. Mandatory monument to visit if you pass by Rome and you have some time to lose in line the Pantheon for me has never been anything exciting (in fact I have never rushed to go and see it when in the eternal city), but surely it is necessary to make it known to the Family to give a fuller picture of Rome and its history.Outside the line, as in these times, it is really stressful: up to two hours at certain times just to view a circular building with little inside to see (apart from the tomb of Raphael, some Kings and little more).I noticed instead that many tourists, forgetting that this is more like a real church, enter to protect themselves from the weather or the warm weather, and bivouac sitting on the pews for hours thanks to the very low entrance fee.Even in unsuitable clothes (and in fact the guardians inside have a lot to do to dress semi-naked women and little men with panzone in plain sight!).When we went, late afternoon, but with over 30 degrees temperature, for example tourists noticed inside more than interested in any things to see were competing to sit and rest a little '.And maybe nibble and drink the things bought from the stupid stalls and dingy little bars around.That said, however, for those who have not had a visit to the Pantheon must be done, if only to observe the curious roof "with the hole" ("oculus"), a hole that nowadays does not really serve any purpose, but that at the time was believed to allow the gods to "spy" on visitors and astronomers of that time to better scrutinize the sky.Nice to observe when it rains: inside, in fact, the water does not enter even in the presence of heavy rainfall, except in very modest quantities (something that the gratings inside can very well handle, as they have done for centuries and centuries).

When in Rome you have to visit the Pantheon. Earlier last year (Feb. 2023) it was still free, now they charge entry fees...Join a good tour, you will get more out of it, especially if you can avoid long chaotic tourist lines.The Pantheon ( from Greek Πάνθειον Pantheion, "[temple] of all the gods") is a former Roman temple and, since AD 609, a Catholic church (Basilica Santa Maria ad Martyres or Basilica of St. Mary and the Martyrs) in Rome, Italy. It was built on the site of an earlier temple commissioned by Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa during the reign of Augustus (27 BC – AD 14); then, after the original burnt down, the present building was ordered by the emperor Hadrian and probably dedicated c. AD 126. Its date of construction is uncertain, because Hadrian chose not to inscribe the new temple but rather to retain the inscription of Agrippa's older temple.The building is round in plan, except for the portico with large granite Corinthian columns (eight in the first rank and two groups of four behind) under a pediment. A rectangular vestibule links the porch to the rotunda, which is under a coffered concrete dome, with a central opening (oculus) to the sky. Almost two thousand years after it was built, the Pantheon's dome is still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome !The height to the oculus and the diameter of the interior circle are the same, 43 metres (142 ft).It is one of the best-preserved of all Ancient Roman buildings, in large part because it has been in continuous use throughout its history. Since the 7th century, it has been a church dedicated to St. Mary and the Martyrs (Latin: Sancta Maria ad Martyres), known as "Santa Maria Rotonda". The square in front of the Pantheon is called Piazza della Rotonda. The Pantheon is a state property, managed by Italy's Ministry of Cultural Heritage and Activities and Tourism through the Polo Museale del Lazio. In 2013, it was visited by over six million people.The Pantheon's large circular domed cella, with a conventional temple portico front, was unique in Roman architecture. Nevertheless, it became a standard exemplar when classical styles were revived, and has been copied many times by later architects

Built by the Roman emperors Agrippa and Hadrian around 2000 years ago, it’s incredible that the Pantheon has survived intact for so long. It’s beaten barbarian invaders, wars, fire, looting and the elements. You can’t help but be struck by a sense of awe. Having been inside before, we were put off by the very long queues that snaked across the piazza and we didn’t want to wait in the sun so we didn’t go inside this time. That was probably a mistake. The dome is quite amazing - the largest unreinforced concrete dome in the world. The record still stands. Never been beaten. To pull off this incredible engineering feat, they built the flower walls out of heavy stone, then as they built upwards, they made the concrete thinner and added pumice so that it was quite lightweight. Sunlight streams through the ‘eye’ of the dome. It looks small but it’s actually 8.2m across. I’d recommend going inside and in hindsight, I’m sorry that we didn’t bite the bullet and join the queue.If you have the chance to walk past the Pantheon at night, do it. The facade is beautifully illuminated and it just looks magical.

The Pantheon was built by Agrippa, friend and son-in-law of Augustus, in 27 BC, damaged in 80 AD. and in 110 AD. from a fire, it was restored by the emperor Hadrian around 123 AD, it is a temple dedicated to past, present and future divinities. Temple with the largest Roman-era dome in the world, there is a central hole, oculus, where on April 21st (Rome's birthday) at 12 the sun hits the main door from where the emperor ritually entered. The bronze door dates back to that era. The kings of Italy are buried here. Take a look at the floor, they were like this in the great Roman buildings. Now it is a church and it is precisely due to the fact that it almost immediately became a church with the advent of Christianity as the religion of the empire that it did not suffer spoliation as happened to all the other Roman monuments. Here too, under 18s don't pay, there are totems where you have to get tickets for both adults and minors, the cost per adult is only 5 euros, it closes at 6.30pm. Fabulous.

This is one of the iconic landmark buildings in Rome. The Pantheon that you see today was built between 118-125 AD during the time of Emperor Hadrian and is located at Piazza della Rotonda in the heart of the city centre. It is a masterpiece of architectural skill from Ancient Rome and one of the most popular attractions in the city. Opening hours are now 8.30am to 7.30pm (Mon to Sat) and 9am to 6pm (Sun). Note there is now an entry fee of €5 per person if you want to visit the inside. Highlights of the Pantheon start with the dome structure which was the largest in the world until finally being surpassed by Brunelleschi's Dome at Basilica Santa Maria del Fiore in Florence in the 15th-century. This is an enormous concrete roof with no support that has remained standing for nearly two millennium. It is best viewed from inside where you can look directly upward and appreciate its width and scale. Originally constructed to be a temple, the Pantheon was converted into a Christian Church in 609. Today, you will find small altars and tombs lining the interior circular layout of the building. The marble flooring is impressive as well. Outside, it is worth a few minutes viewing the front portico structure and large Corinthian style columns that support it. You will walk through here to enter and exit the building. The portico actually belongs to an earlier Augustian period Pantheon which was mostly destroyed by the Great Fire of Rome in 64 AD. There is lastly an interesting fountain (1575) and obelisk (added in 1711) that are worth seeing at the Pantheon as well. In the end, this is must-do sightseeing for first time visitors to Rome. You can purchase entry tickets at the Pantheon directly or online prior to your visit. Allow 15-30 minutes to view the interior and a bit more time to view the exterior as well.

Our visit to the Pantheon with my family was a transcendent journey through time and space, immersing us in the awe-inspiring beauty and timeless elegance of one of Rome's most iconic monuments. From its majestic dome to its ancient mysteries, the Pantheon captivated our senses and left an indelible mark on our hearts.A Marvel of Ancient Engineering:As we approached the Pantheon, we were struck by its imposing facade and grandeur. Built over 2,000 years ago, this architectural marvel stands as a testament to the ingenuity and craftsmanship of ancient Rome. The sheer scale of the structure, with its towering columns and monumental portico, evoked a sense of wonder and reverence.Stepping Inside History:Entering the Pantheon's hallowed halls, we were transported back in time to the glory days of ancient Rome. The vast interior space, bathed in soft light filtering through the oculus, felt like a sanctuary of serenity and contemplation. We gazed in awe at the majestic dome, marveling at its perfect proportions and elegant design.Discovering Ancient Mysteries:Our visit to the Pantheon was a journey of discovery, as we unraveled the mysteries and legends surrounding this ancient monument. We learned about the history of the Pantheon, from its origins as a temple dedicated to all the gods to its transformation into a Christian church in the Middle Ages.We marveled at the engineering feats that enabled the construction of the dome, with its innovative use of concrete and cleverly designed coffers. Each detail, from the intricate marble floors to the celestial motifs adorning the interior, spoke to the genius of the ancient architects who created this masterpiece.Awe-Inspiring Moments and Shared Memories:As we explored the Pantheon together, we shared moments of wonder and reflection that will stay with us forever. We stood in silent awe beneath the oculus, watching sunlight stream through the opening like a divine spotlight illuminating the sacred space below. We whispered secrets to each other, marveling at the acoustic marvels of the dome that carried our voices to the heavens.Outside, we lingered in the Piazza della Rotonda, soaking in the vibrant atmosphere and basking in the beauty of the Pantheon's majestic presence. We snapped photos to capture the memories, but no camera could fully capture the magic of our shared experience.Our visit to the Pantheon was a journey of discovery, enlightenment, and shared wonder that transcended time and space. As we bid farewell to this ancient marvel, we carried with us not only memories of its beauty and grandeur but also a profound appreciation for the enduring legacy of ancient Rome.

The Pantheon is truly a structure that has stood the test of time. It is one of the best preserved monuments of Ancient Rome, in large part because it has been in continuous use since its completion around 128 AD, first as a temple for Roman gods before being converted to a church. The rotunda building is connected to the granite-columned portico via a rectangular vestibule. The star attraction is undoubtedly the coffered concrete dome ceiling with an oculus (central opening) to the sky. Amazingly, it was still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome 2,000 years after it was built. As the expression of Rome's glorious past which has gone on to influence countless buildings across Europe and the Americas throughout history, the Pantheon is worth every Euro (only €5) and every waiting time that you have to go through just to enter the building and appreciate its magnificence and beauty.

Built in 27 BC as a temple dedicated to all the Olympian gods, the Pantheon is an extraordinary example of Roman architecture. The Pantheon's dome is one of the largest ever built in antiquity, and its central oculus, a large circular opening, allowed natural light and, according to some, symbolizes the connection between the temple and the sky. After initially being a pagan temple, it was transformed into a Christian church in the 7th century. This change in function helped preserve the structure for future generations. The interior houses the tombs of important historical personalities, including Raffaello Sanzio, the famous Renaissance painter. From July this year the entrance fee is 5 euros for adults and 3 euros for those aged 18 to 25, previously it was free

We visited the Pantheon mid-week around mid-afternoon.  From my advance reading, I was under the impression that this was free to enter. However, at some point they have begun charging 5 Euros to get in.  We had a city pass that purported to cover the Pantheon, so I asked a staff member about it. She said I would need to exchange the voucher for a ticket between two shops in the same plaza.  I walked over there but all that existed between the shops was an alley with plywood over the entrance.  It reminded me of a scene from one of the later Harry Potter movies, where there is a row house that is enchanted so that it is invisible to most people. I was still puzzling over this when my wife showed up, having read some fine print on the pass' site description. It referenced a company rep with an orange umbrella. We found her and got our tickets for the Pantheon (and a reservation for Castel Sant'Angelo, which we planned to visit afterwards). With that, we were able to enter the Pantheon at no additional charge. The interior is a bit dark, lit mostly by the doorway plus light from the oculus in the roof.  Architecturally, it was stunning.  We spent a while strolling the perimeter and seeing who was buried here (the artist Raphael, Queen Margherita for whom the pizza is named, the first King of reunified Italy, etc.). We also took a lot of photos.  It was also crowded inside.  I'm sure it would be even more so in the summertime. They had the center part roped off for some reason (it would likely be wet if there had been recent rain, given the oculus). We also had to dodge people trying odd photo techniques (putting cameras on the floor to take a vertical selfie with the roof, etc.). Also, this is a functioning church, so there could be times when worship services are being conducted.I was very glad we made time to see the Pantheon.  There are so few structures that remain basically intact from ~ 2000 years ago.  Marveling at the dome's architecture and the overall sense of history was great.

The Pantheon was praised by Michelangelo as "the design of an angel". In 609 AD, the Byzantine Emperor dedicated the Pantheon to Pope Boniface IV, who renamed it the Church of Our Lady and Martyrs. Santa Maria ad Martyres﹞, which is also the official name of the Pantheon today. The Pantheon, the "Temple of the Gods" in Rome, is the crystallization of ancient Roman architectural art. Its majesty and dome interior have become a symbol of Rome itself. So you have to visit inside to appreciate its true size and beauty. The hole above the dome is the only source of light. This hole is also the only lighting point of the Pantheon, so that the light leaks from the top and moves with the position of the sun. And changing the angle of the light gives people a sacred and solemn feeling.

The Pantheon in Rome is a priceless treasure of architecture and history. It is an ancient Roman temple dedicated to all the gods. Its impressive dome is the largest unreinforced concrete construction in the world. Inside, the majestic rotunda is bathed in light thanks to the celestial opening of the oculus. Precious marbles and works of art adorn its walls, testifying to the artistic genius of Roman times. The solemn and sacred atmosphere of the place invites visitors to feel the history and contemplate the grandeur of the Roman Empire. A visit to the Pantheon is a must during a stay in Rome.

The Pantheon makes the strongest impression if you approach it from behind or from the side. And then, instead of a neat facade that closes the picture area, you will see powerful walls that seem to be rooted in the ground, covered with holes and scars. It is here that you begin to believe in the two-thousand-year age of this stocky structure. Augustus's son-in-law Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa founded the temple of all the gods here in 27 BC. True, the first building was square. And the Pantheon became round already under Hadrian (118-126). Since no one fully understood how to build a roof over such a huge structure, the inside of the building was filled with earth, to which the emperor ordered gold coins to be mixed. When the work was completed and the inscription “M.” remaining from the original version was installed on the façade. AGRIPPA I.F. COS. TERTIUM FECIT” (“Marcus Agrippa, son of Lucius, three times consul, made”), Hadrian announced that everyone who found the coin could take it for themselves - and in a matter of minutes the inside of the temple became empty and clean. However, this legend could have been put into circulation by Renaissance architects, who spent weeks on end taking measurements in the hope of understanding the secret of the dome. The Pantheon managed to survive until the beginning of the Renaissance in almost intact form thanks to Emperor Phocas, who in 609 donated the building to Pope Boniface IV. The Pope immediately rededicated the temple of all the gods to the Madonna and all the martyrs, transporting 28 carts with the remains of the first Christians from the catacombs, and in gratitude to the emperor he placed a column in the Forum. Later pontiffs tried in every possible way to tame the pagan building - Urban VIII even ordered Bernini to add a pair of bell towers along the edges of the pediment, but the idea turned out to be extremely unsuccessful, the structure was called “donkey ears” and was scolded until it was finally demolished at the end of the 19th century. The same Urban ordered bronze decorations of the pediment to be placed on the canopy in San Pietro. The church is still active here. The cool thing is that, if you wish, you can even get married here with a view of the modest grave of Raphael and the magnificent tombstones of the first Italian kings - Victor Emmanuel and his son Umberto. Previously, there were more monuments; almost all the artists of Rome wanted to rest next to Raphael. At some point, Pope Pius VII even had to clear the place, sending some of the busts to the museum on the Capitol (they left Baldassare Peruzzi, Annibale Caracci and the composer Corelli). But the most interesting thing here is not the monuments, no matter how many there are, but the dome.☝️ Its height is equal to the diameter - 43.44 meters and you can fit an ideal sphere into the interior of the temple. From the inside you can see that the dome does not have a lantern, like in later Christian churches. In the very center there is an uncovered hole, under which a rather impressive puddle accumulates when it rains. But at any other time, the lighting effects are simply amazing. The spectacle of snowflakes spinning in a whirlpool of light is especially mesmerizing - if you are suddenly caught in the snow in Rome❄️❄️❄️, grab your camera and head straight to the Pantheon. An interesting fact, there is a legend that in the original version there was no hole in the dome and it was allegedly pierced by evil spirits who were rushing around in search of a way out at the sounds of the first mass. And lastly, the most reliable way to get to the Pantheon without wandering through the alleys for too long, this is to turn off Via Corso, 305 behind Palazzo Doria Pamphilj, and then straight ahead, you won’t miss it.☝️

The Pantheon is my number 3 in Rome after the Colosseum and the Roman Forum. This is a must see. The 2nd century building commissioned by Emperor Hadrian is one of the best-preserved buildings from ancient Rome. Its size is impressive. The portico with columns seems to be glued to the round building and does not fit in well with it. The Pantheon was once the largest structure covered with a concrete dome in the world. The building is simple and made of good materials, giving the entire interior a unique character. You have to queue for tickets but it doesn't take too long. While standing in line, we admire the huge columns supporting the portico, which was commissioned by Marcus Agrippa. The dome with a hole in the middle makes an amazing impression. The light comes in. The interior is a mix of architectural styles. There are grave chapels in semicircular niches, including: kings Victor Emmanuel II and Humbert I and Raphael. I recommend this facility

The visit to the Pantheon, to discover the hidden mysteries of its history, attracts visitors from all over the world for the magnificence of its proportions, the majesty of the dome and the architectural peculiarity. Did not include skip the line. We paid for but did not do the tour because we did not understand until after we paid that it was not a skip the line experience.
A must visit, better with a guide and skip-the-line tickets. You have to buy the ticket online to avoid queuesSpectacular.

Firstly, if you can cope with the booking system online, try your best to get advance tickets, because the queues are long. Also, try and get tickets for as early as possible too. I liked this former Roman-temple-turned-church. The construction is a wonder of Roman engineering. I liked the doric columns and the sloped floor (to cope with rain that falls through the hole in the roof). To think think structure had mostly survived since Roman times is a wonder.I was much more invested in the building as a Roman structure than it's current use as an RC church. The (very long) description panels generally don't say much about the Pantheon's pre-Christian roots, indeed, they just refer to that time as "pagan". That in itself made me a little sad, but I acknowledge that the Roman Catholic church has a long association with the site and so feel their history trumps a dead civilization of 2000 years ago.Don't miss Raphael's tomb to the left of the entrance. Also the impressive tomb to Emmanuel II is the ultimate in Italian overstatement. When I say big, the Italians say, no, bigger!
One of the greatest experiences we had in Rome. Such an amazing place that we had to go in there again. It was my nephew's (18 years old) favorite place in Rome. And on top of that it was free

Having been inside on many visits I was stunned by the line of tourists waiting to enter the Pantheon and was thankful I had done so previously! Wonderful building that dates back to 27BC and rebuilt by Emperor Hadrian in 118AD, it was finally dedicated as a Church in 609AD.  To me the stunning feature is the Oculus (eye) that provides the only lighting to the interior. The whole structure is a truly amazing piece of ancient engineering and fully understandable why people line up to enter & view.
We passed by and decided to enter the old temple and it was well worth it. The queue was less than 10 minutes and as in all museums in Italy, they offered a great discount for European students.

The building is an incredible dive back into the past and is absolutely beautiful to look at. There were some huge crowds outside but that is only to be expecting. I would definitely recommend taking a look if you are in Rome.

We knew this place was worth the detour, but this visit exceeded our expectations. Really very interesting, without being too long. Many of the information obtained were useful for visits we made later, such as the Colosseum or the Vatican. Truly a must in our opinion.
If you are into history or architecture this is one of the places I would recommend to begin with in Rome.

The Pantheon is a recommended must-see site in Rome due to its long history, beautiful construction, the level of preservation, and its continued use.We were one of the first to reserve and pay for our entrance tickets online right after the new fee started, probably because we were visiting on a weekend.  When we arrived early at the site before opening, there were already multiple lines.  After asking the line monitors, our tickets were scanned by a hand-held device and we entered through the shortest line quite quickly.We had also decided to do an audiotour, for which one must have identification to leave as security.  I found the tour a bit lacking in complexity and detail, as it mainly just pointed out each of the items to see in the building without going into as much of the history as I would have preferred.The Pantheon continues its long religious tradition as first a temple and now a church, and its mixture of decor inside reflect both of those eras.  However, the magnificent architecture is timeless, and a true representation of the might and artistry of the Roman empire.

It is only fairly recently that there has been a charge to visit The Pantheon. It is free entry for some, which is clearly stated on the information boards. We did not have tickets, so we joined a queue, arriving around 10am. We queued for about half an hour. Not having arranged a tour, we walked around looking at the architecture and decoration of the building. We spent about half an hour looking around.
Very beautiful attraction in Rome. It is well-preserved and very impressive architecture. On the square there is a coffee shop where you can sit and admire it. It's worth going.

The tour was wonderful. The only minus was that the entry ticket to Pantheon was not included and we had to stay in line to get in. In the description it said that we should arrive around 1 hour ahead of time because it is not a skip the line ticket. We did. We were lucky and the line moved quickly and we got in 40 minutes before the tour, this is how long we had to wait inside. But it is better than the alternative of missing the tour. We looked around and end up sitting on the benches while waiting. I would have paid extra to skip the line and safe time I’m Tome for other things to do. Otherwise it was a wonderful experience when the tour started. We have learned a lot of interesting facts. I believe it it essential to have a guided tour in the Pantheon and this one was very good

Very nice visit! Application has 2€ only on the phone to have an audio guide. Very interesting and very nice place. Just because we went in July, a lot of people and queue to get in but this is the season that does it!
Dont miss it when in rome.The most beautiful place to visit.we loved the sun weather.we only paid 5euro per person

Definitely to see among the monuments of Rome. By the way you can buy tickets online on the official website without going through agencies, and if you wish with audio guide. The entrance is regulated and allows you to live the experience without being in the crowd and allowing you to enjoy the beauty present.

We arrived in Piazza della Rotunda to see the Pantheon with a long line of tourists waiting in the entrance line.  It was well organized with zig-zag ropes and the line moved quickly until we were inside.  So glad we made the effort.  The design of the interior is exquisite and belies its age, almost like it was built yesterday.  So much beautiful detail in all the alcoves.  The coffered dome with oculus is spectacular.  Won't ever forget this visit.

My family and I visited just returned from a trip to Rome and Florence. The Pantheon was well worth the time we spent waiting in a fairly fast moving line.  Apparently they started charging for tickets around July 3, 2023, as we thought it was free, or otherwise we would have paid for tickets in advance. With that being said, the line moved quickly, the crowd was well controlled, and once inside History was right before your eyes.   The Painter Raphael is buried there, and his life story is amazing.If you like History or are familiar with Raphael, I believe spending some time in the Pantheon is well worth it.We didn't purchase tickets in advance, and it didn't seem like you needed to, however either way, I believe if you are in Rome, you should visit the Pantheon.

Been to the Pantheon twice before.  Never seen crowds as much as this time.  Long line to access all standing directly in the sun.Impressive Building.    Interesting interior and offers historical context.Suggestion avoid summer near noon thru mid afternoon.On a plaza with quite a few restaurants

It's a beautiful place and in amazingly good shape for such an old building.  The largest unsupported dome still standing is here, and the inside is quite beautiful.  Definitely worth seeing during a stay in Rome!

We met with Elaine today  She  is very experienced, knowledgeable, and fun who delivered an amazing experience at all three main venues at the Vatican, Vatican Museums, Sistine Chapel, and St Peters. She obviously studied the history of so many works of art and side facts that enriched the tour. She kept checking with our small group for affirmation to move to other levels of our interest. Truly a great tour! Highly recommend Elaine! David

Buy the tickets online and breeze past the crowd.. it’s not worth standing in the sun in summer.. we Googled it and found a great “ how to buy” site that talked us through it.. the payment system can be tricky as it’s in Italian but persist as we literally walked right in past all the tours and the people waiting … the site in my option is THE place to see for Roman antiquity, even though it has been slightly hijacked by being made into a church. The sheer size and materials used, the mathematical genius to build it.. unmatched in my option.. I like the collosuem but I will always love the pantheon
Definitely a must while in Rome.very amazing and magical place.very well maintained.they only charge a small fee of 5 euro

I am so glad to have visited the Pantheon. It is a true historical and architectural marvel of antiquity. The audio tour is well worthwhile and gives wonderful background information on this site.The only issue was that this is a site requiring modest dress and no photography and yet there were plenty af people waking around with bare shoulders and short shorts taking photos and video everywhere! The supposed guards who should be enforcing this seemed oblivious!

The well preserved Pantheon, in the center of Rome, is essentially a 2 component circular building featuring a rotunda with a colossal domed ceiling rising from it's walls and a front porch with supporting Corinthian columns that dramatically greets visitors and gives access to the main building through the monster doors.I found the structure of the Pantheon remarkable: for it's size, design and engineering construction. One of the architectural feats revealed in it's interior is dome. The dome mathematics are: a 142' diameter, a rise of 71' above the base and yet there is no evidence of arch support .....free form.It is thought that the quality of the mortar used in the concrete for the dome contributed to the success of it's stability (still around to-day). At the top of the dome sits an opening (oculus) referred to as the "Eye of the Pantheon" which has no cover....it lets light in.....as well as the weather.Presently the Pantheon generally exists in its original form and is today a source of architectural inspiration, know-how and is still a modern working church.
Pantheon is good from history point of view but nothing spectacular inside. It was too crowded when we visited - during the second half the day.

You now have to pay (free under 18’s) it is only €5 but I wouldn’t queue when you get there you can buy a ticket online on the website, wait a few minutes and then go straight through. The queue to buy tickets was pretty big! There are also lots of touts saying you need to buy shawls and can’t go in if your shoulders are uncovered. THIS IS A SCAM! You can go in with bare shoulders so don’t buy any scarfs unless you actually want one.It’s really pretty inside and although popular and busy it’s not too crowded. A must see when in Rome

Planning to visit Rome for few days then the Pantheon is a place to visit. The beauty of the hall is worth the visit. Seeing the largest unsupported dome in the world is enough to queue  outside in the heat.
We arrived early. There were no queues but it was very busy later in day. Arrive early! We also got a guided tour so we could appreciate the building and its history

Tickets now required as of the beginning of July 2023. The website was not working to buy direct but may be possible to buy through a broker with up charge. We had to arrange through the hotel and then at the door they made us buy new tickets. If I went back, I'd investigate further and make sure you have tickets in addition to a tour!

Worth a visit as the inside of the dome is spectacular and because of its influence on ancient and modern architecture. However, there is not much to see inside other than that. Pre-book timed tickets online or you will spend more time queuing to get in than you will actually inside. You can book tickets on the Musei Italiani website (search for musei italiani pantheon).

Alas, admission to the Pantheon is no longer free as from 3 July 2023. Even if there was a queue, you were inside very quickly.Now there is an admission fee, it had made the queue system worse, so unless you are able to get there early or buy a skip the line ticket, please avoid during the rest of the day. Therefore my images are only external shots of the building.

Highly recommend weekdays. The line moves quickly and entry is free. However, please remember to wear clothes that covers knees and shoulders, we saw quite a few people denied entry purchasing scarves from street vendors to be able to get in.

I recommend seeing it, but it might be better to skip entering. The line to get in can be quite long. By the time you finally get to buy tickets, you might realize that you've already seen everything there is to see.

Just being there in the Pantheon amidst the history and architecture was amazing. Booked on the website directly and had no waiting time outside. Arrived an hour earlier anticipating waiting in the line (queue) but was allowed inside upon showing the print out of the ticket. The audio guide was definitely worth it. The staff makes an effort to make visitors feel welcome and valued. They also appreciate the sanctity of the place and will advise maintaining a quiet ambiance when necessary (which is a good thing in my opinion).

The last time I was in Rome I found the Pantheon by accident but that was 35 years ago. So we followed our map and found the Pantheon but this time you had to queue to go in there were hundreds of people queuing groups and individuals. I was not prepared to waste an hour queuing as I had seen it before.

Who in their right mind would implement a new entrance fee and procedure in peak season? Only in Rome. What was the hurry? This monument has been there over two thousand years, I can’t believe a few months delay would have made much of a difference. The Cultural Minister should address these concerns as his website, the one which issues tickets to the Pantheon , was down all day long yesterday. When it finally came back up it said there were no more tickets. Totally disappointing as I had already purchased a tour for 10 on the official Pantheon site. then realized entrance tickets weren’t included. Total disorganization. Dear Cultural Minister, if you’re going to turn Rome into Disney World , at least do it right .
What an absolute waste of money. Not worth it at all. We waited over an hour to get inside and then had to wait in another line to get the audio equipment. Very disorganized!

We ordered the entrance (free) online, about a week or two before arrival, we didn't want any surprises.When we arrived there, it looked like the most popular club in Rome - plenty of people (without tickets) trying to get in.The Pantheon itself is stunning piece of ancient genius architecture with later art, including paintings, sculptures, and tombs of some of Italy's most famed persons.

Took less than 2 minutes to get in during peak tourist season. It's free. Takes around 10-15 min inside. Incredible to think this building has been standing for that long! Make sure you dress modestly or the security will not let you in.

When we visited my niece who was studying abroad in Rome, the first place she showed us was the Pantheon.  This ancient temple/church is a sacred space and an architectural marvel.  Touring this peaceful and inspiring site was a grand way to begin our Roman adventure in February 2014.

We visited Pantheon independantly(Booked online) not with a tour Guide. It was Fabulous but if you have booked an Audio Tour online do not stand in the queue show your tickets at the middle door and be admitted imediately
Amazing historic place, worth a quick stop; interesting that sometimes in the morning there are no lines at all but typically in the afternoon the line to enter can get quite long

The 3 star rating is not of the Pantheon itself but the buying of a ticket, or in our case not the buying of a ticket.Went online to buy, searched for Pantheon tickets, bought an audio tour which did not state was not including the entrance ticket.  HD to select a specific time.  What lunatic would sell an audio tour without the entrance ticket!You have to turn up at least 90 minutes before your audio tour booking to get an entrance ticket.Shambolic process which someone has not thought through.Hopefully the process changes so when you go to buy the audio tour it prompts you to also buy an entrance ticket.Guys this is not rocket science!

They led you to believe during online booking that audio guide include the entrance ticket. It doesn't. The 10 euro audio guide is way overpriced. And we had to wait in the sun for about 45 minutes. The stuff was rude and unpleasant.

You do not want to miss going inside. On my last trip to Rome many years ago I decided not to go in and only saw it from the outside. Major mistake on my part. The Pantheon needs to be on your list of places to visit in Rome.

I am fully aware that The Pantheon has only this week started ticketing, but it should be noted that the edition of tickets made the experience a bit worse for wear. We stood in a queue for almost 45 minutes and upon reaching the front, got overtaken by three groups of people being ushered to the front because they had booked the audio guide and didn’t need to queue. Despite asking directly if I needed to queue, I was directed to join the queue and wait despite also having pre-booked the audio guide. Staff were sharp, rude and generally projecting stress onto visitors. It’s not advised to shout in someone’s face that they’re blocking the line, after they’ve queued for no reason, and have paid to be there!Ruined what would have been a lovely experience really.
This was great - definitely a must if you are going to Rome. I would suggest the audio tour, it was great.

We visited the Pantheon today (5th July 2023), which is just the third day that you now have to buy a ticket to enter (previously it was free). Beforehand I bought tickets to the guided tour on the official pantheon website (not the audio guide, an actual tour with a person) and also bought entry tickets online. These were provided to me in two separate ticket PDFs. However even if you buy tickets online, we were still told to wait in the line for the tickets. Please note when facing the pantheon there are two lines. At the time we were there, the line on the right was for buying tickets with a credit card. The one on the left was for paying with cash.We started waiting in line at 2:05pm ahead of our 3pm guided tour time. When we were close to the entry doors a staff member checked our tickets and then escorted us to the front of the line, saving us maybe 10 minutes more waiting.  Another staff member scanned our entry tickets again and then gave us a paper ticket that a guard then ripped off. Not sure what the point of that was. The system seemed a bit chaotic and they clearly are still ironing out issues. We were directed to the audio guide counter to show our ticket for the guided tour. At this point it was 2:45pm.After all that we were told to wait by the audio guide counter and then our guide met us at exactly 3pm. We were the only ones in the tour so we had a private tour! Our guide was fantastic and clearly very passionate. She also had great historical diagrams and photos on her ipad that were very interesting to me as an engineer.Overall the experience was great and the Pantheon is well worth visiting. However I've taken a star off because of the hectic ticketing system and that we still had to wait 40 minutes in the line even though we'd bought 'reserved' tickets online.

Beautiful building, which is a must to visit. We went on Friday evening, not long before it closed. Queue was not too long and we were in within a few minutes. They were quite strict on the dress code so glad we researched before we went. Knees and shoulders must be covered. Lots of lovely bards and restauarnts in the area too and prices were very reasonable!
With the switch to paying for entry things are very disorganized. They purposely make it difficult to purchase tickets on line so you pay more for a visit.
No one to tell us where to go so very confusing. Get in the general admission line then get your audio equipment. Not sure we really needed to pay for a reservation.

The Pantheon is always impressive with its carved ceiling, thinking about the craftsmanship to build such a structure 2000 years ago is just mind blowing. We enjoyed looking around both the inside and the exterior, though the interior was incredibly crowded. There are now metal corrals for tourists to queue in (weren't there when we visited 10 years ago) which help somewhat but also detract from the front steps of the building where you used to be able to stand or sit between the pillars while admiring the giant front doors.

The building is stunning, but the organisation is a complete shambles. Having just changed their booking system, it appeared that no one had a clue what was going on.  Having queued for an hour for my allotted time, I had my pass scanned on my phone to be made to wait until they printed me a paper ticket which was immediately taken by a man standing next to where they were printing tickets - the purpose the entirely unclear. I was then told to go to queue for the audio guide, which they wouldn’t give me to unless I left my identity documents with them. In no way would I ever leave my identity documents with a random person…. I went without, so all in all, the worst experience in Rome which was a shame as I’m sure it’s an interesting place.

We visited today, which was the day after the paid ticketing system was introduced. It is a complete mess. Obviously the Pantheon itself is incredible, but I have given it a 1 star review because as of today, this new system is a shambles. We queued for an hour & then got told we were in the line for the audio guide. I told them numerous times we didn’t want the audio guide & that I had bought tickets online, even showing them the tickets. They laughed at us (& others in the queue in the same situation) & told us we had the audio guide tickets (we didn’t) & to stay in the line. Eventually we just walked in because no one had a clue what they were doing & people who hadn’t bought tickets were walking past us, buying tickets & gaining entry. They really need to sort it out, the queue was ridiculous by the time we left on account of the disorganization.
Booking the audio tour in advance made it much quicker on the day. Good info, the narrators were kind of dull.  Amazing building, every nook and cranny.
The Pantheon is an amazing, ancient, architectural structure that is still wholly intact. I recommend the audio tour. It’s very informative. The Pantheon is a definite must-see.

The Pantheon was free to enter and was absolutely amazing. Huge cathedral highlighting Romes transition from Paganism to Christianity. Highlights;The resting place for Rafael do Santi. Beautiful church with enormous dome.

Incredible feeling sitting in the oldest building in the world which is still in use! Few tips for visiting this place:1. Avoid visiting on weekends or public holidays. While there is no ticket but there is pre-booking required on weekends or public holidays. This can be done on their website at no charge.2. Be prepared to stand in the queue for upto an hour to enter, but the wait is worth it!3. Do sit there inside for getting a complete perspective and the environment to soak in.The place where Julius Caesar was killed is nearby too, don't miss visiting that.
The Pantheon is not to be missed! It was amazing and awe inspiring! The self guided audio tour was informative and engaging to an adult. I highly recommend!

The Pantheon in Rome is truly a sight to behold! From the grandeur of its impressive architecture to the ancient artifacts and breathtaking views, it is a must-see for all visitors to the Eternal City. Its grandeur and splendor will leave you in awe, and its ancient artifacts will give you a glimpse into the past. The Pantheon is an incredible testament to the grandeur of Rome and its history, and a visit to this incredible site is sure to be an unforgettable experience.

I have to first admit, being in Rome for the first time, I was unaware that the Pantheon would have limited accessibility on Christian Mass days, so anyone looking to go in should plan to go on a day other than Sunday or Wednesday. My fiancé and I booked a tour because we wanted to see the inside of the Pantheon during our short stay in the city and as I mentioned before, access was unavailable on Sunday when we first went. We booked the tour through viator for about 25 euros a person and it was well worth it. Our guide Giulia (hopefully I'm spelling it right) was informative and provided a lot of information we wouldn't have gotten touring on our own. She even let me take a picture of one of her iPad photos! I'm so glad we were able to fit in this experience during our short stay in Rome.

Friendly staff and easy to understand audio tour. I didn't give it 5 stars because the audio tour didn't follow a path around the building. So we heard info on one area then had to turn around to another area.

My husband I walked over from our hotel one evening. Not far from Trevi Fountain which was like a zoo it was so crowded. Lines were short, free entry. Just get in the free line. It moves quickly. Quiet and contemplative. Cool relief from the heat. Not overly crowded in the evening.

We went on our own.  Don't miss the opportunity to walk into this incredible building that was build/re-built a good 1900 years ago!  It's crazy how stunning it is and how people that long ago created such a beautiful and amazing place.

We visited on our own during the week and were able to get in to walk around for free after about a 10 minute wait in a fast moving line.  We returned on Saturday 6/10 and were told at the front of the line all tickets had been sold.  A guide offered for us to join his group, which we did and they provided a headset for each and shawls for ladies with bare shoulders. The tour was amazing, and our guide was very informative, showing pictures on her phone of solar events on certain dates, and reviewing the architecture, history, constructions theories, materials and more.  I highly recommend but don’t have the contact information as we paid cash euro afterwards (significantly cheaper than the Venmo price).

Nicely to walk around is the spacious & grand circular floor which is likely "one of marble" (although not to walk through the floor for some reason) but really not much time is needed I would say with just "pretty plain" altar & small alcoves in the wall among which is the tomb of Victor Emmanuel II the "1st king" of Italy (all plain with only statues of saints & such and with nothing else of additional decorations).  And apart from looking around is also to look up to the interior of the "sizeable dome" above with some elaborate designs & such, and actually also pretty impressive to look up not inside but just before entering this temple are these "great pillars" of nice lengths & sizes (with "some time" to view those pillars in my case since there was a line-up to enter starting from the small square outside then through the pillars to get to the temple entrance).Located at just about a 15-minute walk north from the Victor Emmanuel II Monument building (with "2 chariot sculptures" on its top & a number of statues around the building), the visit to this temple was followed by those to: the Loyola Church (with a beautiful "ceiling mural" which can also be viewed in the "slanted mirror" on the floor); the Trevi Fountain (with a set of great statues at a little higher up and water flowing from under them to the pool a little below); and the Spanish Steps (a "pretty wide" staircase between buildings along both sides and with a small church all the way at the top).  And yes, the area overall is also "quite a crowded one" with many narrow streets which are also lined with plenty of souvenir shops (kind of an "old town" area I would say with many colorful heritage buildings but among them also some grand-stately ones with front pillars & such).

This is so worth it! Even though the Pantheon is free (for now) there is a good chance you'll be stuck in line in the hot sun for a hour or so just to get in. Because this tour is also affiliated with the Pantheon, if there's a long line to get in (like there was when we arrived), you can just walk past all those suckers and go right on in. There were a lot of people walking around with their ears to their phones listening to self-guides, but I could tell they were jelly that we had an actual historian as our guide. Our guide was very enthusiastic and full of so much information - we asked so many questions and she had an answer for all of them. You could tell how much passion she has for the Pantheon and Rome in general.

The visit was great. Brief, concise, enlightened. Here the audio guide is enough, unlike the Vatican where the audio is long and boring, favor a guide. And just remember to book your visits to avoid queuing in full sun.

What appears as merely a boring building on the exterior transforms into a scintillating masterpiece on the inside. Entry is free, and history/religion buffs will be delighted right away. I learned so much from my visit that I never knew I would know. Dress appropriately and use a quiet voice to maintain a pleasant atmosphere for everyone.A top-three attraction in Rome!

A decent audio tour of the Pantheon. On arrival you take your booking details to the audio tour desk and receive your audio device and earphones.  Just to note that we were asked for some ID to act as security to make sure you hand back the device at the end. We were fine with this and used a driver's license, but just a note to anyone considering the tour to make sure you have some form of ID on you.The audio tour was informative with a number of points of interest together with a map to show you where you should be looking.  There was a lot of detail on the Pantheon itself and its history as well as a number of sculptures and the interesting design points of the building, particularly the dome and the oculus. The only disappointing element was that 2 of the points of interest were outside the main doors, however we were told that we couldn't exit with the audio devices so you just have to try and get a decent view from inside by the doors (which isn't easy with the number of people entering and exiting) or commit the detail to memory and try to look at it on the way out.  The information provided ranges from 2 or 3 minutes on an item to 6 or 7 minutes on some of the more detailed points of interest. Personally I would have liked there to be a few more points of interest on the tour (if I recall there were around 15 in total) and there were a number of elements that could have had more detail included, but there seemed to be just enough information and detail to make the tour worthwhile, particularly if you've not been to the Pantheon before or know much about its history. The tour does allow you to take things in at your own pace rather than follow a guide so you can take in the superb detail and design slowly and quietly and give the Pantheon the reverence it deserves.
It's amazing, but I didn't realize we needed tickets to get in, unlike previous years when I have visitied!

Amazing architecture, this iconic building should not be missed. It is located in a beautiful piazza with a lovely fountain. Check out their website for when you need reservations.  We stood in line one afternoon - it moved quickly. Lots of neat restaurants surrounding the Pantheon that have great views of it.

The Pantheon is an architectural marvel, definitely worth standing in line to see. It is magnificent and, as the largest freestanding dome in the world,  something you will enjoy visiting. Don’t be deterred by the extremely long line — it moves very quickly.  You can spend as little as 5 minutes or as long as an hour in the dome. Absolutely beautiful!
The queue was not too bad on a Saturday. The audio guide made it much more interesting.  The building is magnificent and well worth the visit.

Unfortunately, my daughter and I could only see the outside. We were waiting in line and a woman told us that reservations were needed on Saturdays and Sundays.  It was also Pentecoste that added to the lines. I'm glad that she mentioned it, since we would have been turned away after waiting in line.  Had I known reservations were needed, I would have made one before we left the US.  I had read it was free and there was no mention of weekend reservations.

Took a private tour with a local guide.  It was spectacular, not only did we see inside, we felt like our guide brought us back in time to fully understand the pantheon.  No cost to enter, so worth the visit

Where else can you step inside a massive and beautiful building that is nearly 2000 years old for free (I say this as there is talk about charging entrance – but who knows how long that may take)?  Probably not many options.  The lines can be long, but step in anyway.  They move very fast since there are no tickets, etc. to worry about.  In its original design, it would have been covered in marble in many other places but that was removed a few centuries ago to build St. Peter’s Basilica.  It is still is phenomenal sight to see and is fantastically well preserved based on its age.

An example of exquisite ancient engineering built 2000 years ago. A marvelous dome.  This is a must-see site for any visitor to Rome. Admission is free but there is a line to get in which moves quickly. Though previously  this was a temple to the ancient pagan gods, it is currently a working Catholic Church and the visitor’s dress code is required and enforced. This cannot be missed.
Love this building but unfortunately so too do lots of tourists. Never disappoints particularly the ocular

BEWARE…This tour was falsely advertised as “Skip the Line”. That was NOT TRUE as you must join the one line for everyone until you enter the building and check in at the inside  Audio Tour Desk. We tried to visit on Sunday June 4th which unfortunately was a holiday with everyone out. The line was many blocks longs and we just skipped the tour and forfeited out ticket cost as a bad experience. I would like to get a refund as it totally false to show this as skip the line, but doubt that ever happens.
Don't miss this.  It surprised me that the inside is so well preserved and beautiful.  Great staff.  Good audio guide.  The organisers did everything right.  Best part of my trip to Rome.

This is one of the oldest buildings in the world & a MUST visit for every visitor to Rome; we were no exception. The first time we went was on a Sunday afternoon, to find out that at weekends, you have to book a time slot on line, and everything was full. We went back the following day early afternoon, bit of a queue outside, but well organised and soon inside. The place is breathtaking & a true wonder. The final resting place of 2 former Kings of Italy, and the painter Raphael. Take your time going round and enjoy
Just walk in.  It is AMAZING.  I've been there many times.  Right in the heart of town.  Look up to the ceiling and in every crevice.  Take amazing photos.

Pantheon Rome is a must-see for anyone looking to explore the rich history of Italy. Its grandeur and awe-inspiring interior have made it a popular tourist destination for centuries. The ancient artifacts and architecture of this magnificent structure are a testament to the grandeur of the Roman Empire. From the impressive dome to the intricate marble floors, visitors can't help but be in awe of this ancient wonder. Not only does it offer a unique glimpse into the past, but its interior is filled with ancient artifacts, including statues, frescoes, and paintings. It's no wonder why Pantheon Rome has been a popular destination for centuries. Its rich history and grandeur make it a must-see for anyone looking to explore the history and culture of Italy.

Built by (or more accurately “ordered to be built by”) the Emperor Hadrian, and preceded by a similar temple commissioned over a century earlier during the rule of Augustus, the Pantheon was finished around 126 AD and has impressed millions of people ever since.  While it was roughly meant to pay homage to the pagan gods, it was subsequently given to Pope Boniface IV in 609 by the Byzantine emperor Phocas and consecrated as a church soon thereafter.  It remains an active place of Christian worship as well as an even-more-active magnet for tourists from around the world.  Numerous dignitaries are buried inside, and the structure is simply and visually ethereal.  Entry is free but ticketed on the weekends as of this review, although I did hear murmurings of fees being charged in the future.I visited twice during my week in the city.  The first was on a Saturday with a timed ticket and walking tour previously purchased for 3:00PM.  I was allowed to enter early although unfortunately the tour never materialized, and I wasn’t given a reason why.  I spent maybe an hour inside viewing everything in a clockwise fashion, jockeying around static storyboards (the line for audio guides was prohibitively long) and trying to make sense out of the details of the interior.  I returned two days later on a Monday at opening; there were surprisingly even more people present, the audio guide counter was closed, the app didn’t download, and my time on site was even more brief.  I walked around the exterior of the building, admired the fountain and portico, and departed.In summary, I was equally impressed and frustrated by my two visits to the Pantheon.  I’m certainly realistic about organization in one of the Europe’s most frequented buildings in one of the world’s most visited cities, but this was just plain messy.  Disorganization in this case led to a subpar overall experience and I learned more about the structure on the flight home while I was typing this review.  Worth a visit?  Of course, it is a stunning structure.  Room for improvement?  Most definitely… here’s hoping that the powers-that-be make some adjustments as the world opens back up to tourism to make this a more fulfilling experience.

Well worth the money, absolutely superb to be guided through, I think this might be my favourite place in Rome out of many favourites, just go.

One of the "musts" for any U.VA alum visiting Rome is to "check out" the Pantheon - after all, it so impressed Mr. Jefferson that he used its design as a model for The Rotunda, the centerpiece of his "academical village".  So, I was expecting to see an older version of The Rotunda.WRONG!  The volume of the open space of the Pantheon is nearly six times that of The Rotunda.  So, I was stunned by its sheer size.  No wonder Mr. Jefferson, like me, got a little weak in the knees when he first beheld it.At the time of its construction the Pantheon embodied advances in architecture and construction techniques that would not be matched for a millennium.  As it stands today, nearly 2,000 years after its construction, it is the only building from its era still serving its basic function.  Simply amazing; an eternal part of The Eternal City.

This is a former Roman temple and, since 609 AD, a Catholic church (Basilica di Santa Maria ad Martyres or Basilica of St. Mary and the Martyrs) in Rome. Originally  on the site of an earlier temple commissioned by Marcus Agrippa during the reign of Augustus, It was rebuilt by the emperor Hadrian and probably dedicated c. 126 AD. The building is cylindrical with a portico of large impressive granite Corinthian columns.  It is regarded as probably the best-preserved of all Ancient Roman buildings, mainly due to it being kept in continuous use. The Pantheon is free to visit, but you may have to stand in line as they control the amount of people inside at any one time. You can hire an audio guide for about 8.5 euro's or have the full guided tour for about 25 euro's. It is open every day 9.00 am – 7 pm (last entrance at 6.45 pm).

Arguably the engineering marvel of ancient Rome, this Roman temple is now a Catholic church and attracts tourists throughout the day. Seeing its construction helps you appreciate and understand its inspiration for subsequent millennia of architects and builders -- not least the construction of St. Peter's Basilica and the Duomo of Florence.
