We were fortunate to be able to walk straight in on Friday afternoon - amazing to think this is more than 1,000 years older than Westminster Abbey!
This is a free attraction. You walk through the building The inside is beautiful and the architecture is amazing for its age.

The Pantheon is worth visiting because it is a Roman temple still standing, not in ruins.  Inside the building, one can pay their respects for Victor Emmanuel the first king of unified Italy and his wife, Marguerite of Savoy. The tour is free.  Also take some time to admire one of the many Egyptian obelisks in Rome just outside this building.
It was very short but nice tour. Even it is free you need to pre boom your visit . Inside you spent 10-15 min

Anything about the building is much better explained in many sites that what I could ever say. I would only like to mention the  feelings that, as a classic Rome lover, invaded me just starting when facing the building from the outside. Once in the interior, just try to isolate from crowds of visitors and you will hear the building talking directly to you about what and how was the life in Rome 2000 years ago.

Free to enter. Impressive building beautifully preserved inside too. Wouldn’t bother with the audio tours, just walk inside and do a loop around to see the different statues. Queue did not take too long either.
Good to have booked the audio tour in advance so no waiting in line outside. Very good explanation of the dome, statues and paintings. Worth it!
Visited with my folks and my husband.  It's a beautiful building, with gorgeous iconographic imagery.

There's no point paying 10 dollars for this. The entry to the Pantheon is free for everyone. There's literature in front of every artefact and a 2 euro audio guide inside.I don't see any reason to pay 10$ for getting a device which reads out Wikipedia articles about the saints and the stories behind itI would advise the guests to just go to the Pantheon enter, roam around, read around and come out. No point of this tour

...in construction but is the oldest in continuous use... always long lines during dayhours finally after 4 days (late afternoon) we saw a short line so we queue to see this wonder...Built as a pagan temple when converted into a Christian church in the early Medieval period saved the building from complete destruction and spoliation, but still some materials were taken from it to build elsewhere (external marble and bronze from the ceiling portico were recycled as cannons for St. Angelo castle).  The interior materials largely survived, as a sacred place people would had respect of it, though (and logical) it went under extensive restoration in recent years.Needless to say, the inside is awe inspiring.  Past times artists and kings are buried there; the first kings of Italy, Queen Margherite of Savoy and the Renaissance painter Raphael just to mention a few.  If can visit and attend a Sunday mass that could be quite an experience.P.S. the temple of Mercury located in Baiae (near Naples) is predated to this but the use was abandoned since centuries.

We had purchased an audio app that gave us all the details of the Pantheon which was worth doing as it was only about 5 AUD.   We had purchased the app via getyourguide.com Do not know how you would find out the history, etc without such an app. It was fairly quiet when we were there and entry is free.
Very impressive monument. A must see in Rome. They have turned the inside into a church, and you can actually go in and visit for free.
Stunning. This place was truly amazing, it was an incredible feeling when we entered . Organised queuing system in place
We were lucky to have a great guide who explained all the important details - honestly, I could have missed.
Incredible structure with an audio tour very well that you can follow at your own pace. Price accessible.
Sign posting about official opening times and better queue systems were our only complaints. well worth getting a Gelato and a spritz in the afternoon and soaking up the atmosphere
This 2000 year old architectural wonder is absolutely fascinating. Visit as early as possible to avoid crowds. We went in February and there was a line but moved quickly.
the pantheon is a really beautiful temple, it's a pity that visits were closed on that day, fortunately we were able to admire it from the outside

We wanted to visit here as it always came up on places to see. It was easy to find. Scruffy from the outside with lots of people trying to sell you things and beggars everywhere but once you get inside it’s an amazing site.The architecture and pictures are fantasticThe downside is there is no limit to how many go in and no one way system etc. it was absolutely choc a block with people just banging into each other and scrambling for ovoid if things

I paid the extra euros and got the audio guide, and it was perfect. I recommend the place to everybody. The roof was something speciale. It was sunny the day I where there, but they say when it rains it's fantastic. Imagine how it was before it was i church, it must been even better.

We were on a whistle stop 2 day break in Rome and knew that we had to visit the Pantheon and all of it's rich history. We were lucky as there was no queue and could walk straight in. I don't know why but I was completely overcome with emotion when passing through the doors and completely struck by it's awe. It was by no means the first wonder which we came across in Rome but certainly the first to bring me to tears as I just hadn't realised the wonder of the place. I wish we had had more time to have had an audio guide and really spent some more time in there as it is truly a place of beauty and I there is so much history to learn about.The pillars are outstanding and just to be able to touch something that old is amazing!

We didn't book in advance, and we really should have done so.  if you're going on a Saturday, Sunday or  bank holiday Monday you need to make a reservation - no need to pay for any tour, as its a Church its free to enter (though donations are welcome)
This place is very famous and it is free access. The building is amazing and inside is great. Worth stopping by.

Beautiful building steeped in so many layers of fascinating history. We took the audio guide and it talks you through everything you need to know. The time we spent listening to the guide felt just right, approx 1 hour. Thanks again for a day I'll never forget

Stunning building and its very great that Rome has so many of these and its free to look at!   We never have time to go in and see all the buildings and monuments in Rome (and other parts of Italy) but looking from the outside is still worth our time.
Visiting such a beautiful structure is such a once in a life time experience. You are definitely living in the moment!!!
you really have to go inside to understand the marvel that is the Pantheon. What a spectacular sight
So beautiful. Visit inside during the day and stunning at night. An incredible piece of architecture. The artwork and ceiling are amazing. Not to be missed.

We booked an audio tour online and very soon afterwards realised we’d booked the wrong day. Easy you’d think to cancel and rebook another day. NO chance.This company has no flexibility and sticks exclusively to its T&C’sWe’re not talking about mega money but some flexibility would have been appreciated.Sorry to say we won’t use them again.

A nice place to see. The artwork is amazing and there is no top on the cieling. It is a church so you have to behave respectfully. It was very busy on Sunday,but during the week there is no waiting for entrance. And it is free entrance.

There was a line to get in but it moved very quickly and once inside you did not feel crowded. Take your time going around as there are so many things to read and look at - beautiful art, the history and if you are there long enough, the light changes so much with the opening in the dome - it is quite lovely.  We enjoyed exploring the entire building and getting more information on each of the individuals who are buried there. Do remember this is a church and men must remove their hats and hushed tones are used inside.
What beauty in one place. With the help of the audio guide you wander around discovering a thousand and more curiosities. An essential step

We always enjoy visiting the Pantheon when in Rome.  It's best to go early or late in the day to avoid all of the tour groups.  Read about the Pantheon in advance, and do your own self guided tour.  Rick Steves also has a free audio tour that you can download and listen to when visiting.
A must see in Rome, so went to this place. Lucky there was not a long queue. We had to wait for 15 minutes to take a look inside. You don't have to pay to get inside
One of the most recognizable and preserved ancient buildings in Rome - a temple to the Roman Gods and Goddesses.
It's free of charge to enter the Pantheon. We went in January and there were no queues. Interesting and a must do in Rome.
Recommend visiting this landmark but be careful around people trying to sell you bracelets. This doesn't take away from the beaty of the building, but it's important to note.
Free admission but you have every interest in taking an audio guide (1 for 2 is enough) because the comments are very interesting and complete the tour which otherwise is very fast.

My favorite building in Rome is the pantheon. Built in AD 120 by Emperor Hadrian it was originally a Roman temple and later a Christian church. The magnificent concrete dome was the largest until the Renaissance and was studied by Brunelleschi in Florence and Michelangelo for St Peter’s Basilica.
Looking up at the dome and sky from the inside was the best part of my experience.  I wonder how it would be when it is raining.

We have been to the Pantheon before, a long time ago, but thought we would call in again.  Amazingly it is still free to get in and there was no real queue in January.  It was quite crowded inside and seems to have much more limited access than our previous visit.  However, it is still an awesome building and well worth visiting.
We visited on a Wednesday, mid morning and didn't need to queue. Such a beautiful place with a wonderful history. Audio available but lovely to just sit and take in the surroundings.

One of Rome’s best known Attractions and not only is it relatively easy (10-15 minute walk from the Trevi Fountain) to find but there is no entry charge - probably as it is an active Church!Impressive from the outside but even better inside - not to be missed!!
Amazing! Absolutely worth going inside!!! We sat inside for ages just soaking it all in! Simply breathtaking and highly recommend visiting here!!

We didn’t book anything to visit during the day until we got to the place. I’m glad I didn’t as entering is free. There’s a QR code outside the front for you to scan, book your time (we waited outside for 30 minutes before our time slot was ready). I don’t think a guide is needed - you can pay €2 inside for an audio/video of the things your looking at and that was perfect for us. I would have been annoyed if I had payed for a tour when it’s free to enter!

Upon entering the Pantheon at the booked timeslot, you go to the left side to grab the audio guide. The people working behind the desk will ask for identification they will give you the audio guide. The audio guide itself was pretty descriptive with dretty thorough explanation of each section with history and meaning behind each of them.The architecture of the Pantheon itself was pretty amazing and especially that domed roof was totally the highlight. It's amazing how they stay durable despite all these centuries that passed.
Lovely stop after visiting some nearby landmarks. Beautiful architecture and Roman ruins. In awe of the grand structure

Do a bus tour £21/£23 a day well worth it hop on and off for 24 hours as many times as you want. It helps with getting your bearings too so you can then walk rome without missing the main attractions for the rest of your stay. Don’t ware your self out on a day time it’s as beautiful if not more on a night time. Food is very reasonable in pricing, if you need clothes or shoes plenty of shops so don’t stress before you go buy while you are there. Take comfortable footwear you will need it.  A fantastic place highlight St Peter’s basilica not to be missed. A city break to a weeks holiday both trips have not disappointed. Train fares are also reasonable if you want to do a day trip.
Over 2 centuries old and you walk through the door and your breath is taken away. Dont go to Rome and miss this.

You come across the Pantheon almost by accident, in an unassuming part of Rome and it's just magically before your eyes.  It is such a gem I almost feel there should be a fanfare to alert you to it's presence.  The Piazza in front of it has many touristy spots to eat and drink, I'm not usually inclined to do so but in the case of the Pantheon, we took a seat and ordered a drink just so we could bask in it's magic a little longer!

This is absolutely my favourite place in Rome. I love the little square in front of the pantheon, i love it inside, i love the roman columns in fron of it. It is absolutely fantastic. Try to go early in the morning in order to avoid the crowd.

This cathedral was marvelous and the structure was very well intact. This was my first time entering the Pantheon and the only words I could utter was WOW. You have to go see for yourself to see exactly what I'm talking about.

This is a relatively small experience overall. One visit is more than enough. What YOU should be prepared for is the MAN and his "workers" who control the entrance. WOMEN are not allowed to enter if the MAN running the entrance feels you have too much skin exposed. HE would like you to purchase a shawl from the children selling them who are running around the square hawking shawls. This past summer, HE refused my entry because he felt my ankle length dress that had a tie back was too exposing. While HE let in other women with strapless tops. I was body shamed and groopedby this MAN. He wanted me to purchase a shawl. A WOMAN helping run the line with HIM let me in and HE chased me down. Just be aware. I had visited the Pantheon previously several years prior. So, I knew how small it is and over hyped. Is it an ancient engineering feat? Yes. Is it worth buying an over priced shawl or being mauled by the MAN running the entrance, NO. Of note! A few hours prior, in the same outfit, I toured the Vatican without issue. Wander into the multiple free beautiful churches. Admire the obelisks. DO NOT pander to these people. Just be AWARE and INFORMED.

We saw the Pantheon in the morning before it got crowded. It was beautiful! We were able to get a sense of the history of the building and the beauty of the art. We had coffee across the piazza with a view of the Pantheon. What a thrill!
Waiting in line, we were approached to join a group tour and we are glad that we did. Tour guide was great, very funny. Inside the building is just amazing.
Awe inspiring building, really worth a visit for the sheer size and the grand dome with the opening in it. It was very crowded and we had to queue to get inside. No caps allowed.
I also visited the Pantheon internally and it was worth it very suggestive, admission is free but inside there are speakers where you can take an audio guide.

This was our second trip to the Pantheon (our first we 16 years ago so we didn't remember a lot of it) with our 14 year old daughter, for whom it was her first visit to this remarkable place. We opted to do the audio tour which was very informative and you could move at your own pace. It was busy but not crowded. The center of the Pantheon was fenced off, possibly due to the rain that had occurred in Rome over the preceding days and come through the oculus. The architecture and decoration is incredible. The pantheon is iconic and a must see if you're in Rome and haven't been inside.

This was a lovely experience. It's free, and the music was especially beautiful, but dress warmly and be prepared to wait in line a long time to get in. We arrived at 11 pm and there were already about a hundred people in front of us. We waited outside an hour an a half. Only the first couple of hundred people get seats, and the Mass lasts an hour. But we felt it was worth it. A spiritual experience in an iconic setting, even if you are not Catholic.
Very rewarding visit, free but I recommend taking the audio guide to have very interesting explanations (allow 25/30 mins). An audio guide for two is possible.

Took a guided tour (with a person), which I would recommend.  You will be in AWE that they built this place in the second century (it was completed by Emperor Hadrian) because the cupola and the interior are in such great shape.  Side note:  how did they build something like this in the 100's that is still kicking in 2022 when anything we build today crumbles after 100 years?
Kicked off Rome by visiting the Pantheon, remarkable and referenced many time throughout the stay in Rome.
No need to take tickets before because entry is free and fast. Guided audio pays for itself. Install the app and there you go...

You could say that the splendour of the Pantheon dwarfs the arrogance of its employees. For an audiogide you have to give up your passport or hand in your credit card which then disappear into a filthy bin with other papers! If you indicate that you do not agree you can go to your own country is said! Why Tourist friendly
there are no words to describe this monument, go and feel the atmosphere , its always crowded, just ignore and soak it up
It’s another church on the face of it but actually it’s so much more. The architecture is a key interest and worth a visit.
amazing visit and amazing history abound in this building.  Entry is free, but we opted for the audio tour that made it a great site to see.

it was amazing and rlly cool but extremely crowded and a long line to get in even tho the line moved fast. lots of tourists. amazing old building and the oculus was incredible to see super cool overall would recommend going early morning to avoid crowds!

The construction could have been ordered by Emperor Trajan (110s) or it could have been Emperor Hadrian (Trajan's first cousin 1x removed). I always suggest delving into the history before you reach the site. Everything is much more interesting. I should add that the lighting is beautiful and I have excellent photos.
I could not explore much as I visited Pantheon in the evening (after sun set). Nice and calm place to spend some quality and quiet time
I saw Panteon Only outside. I didn’t have time because i was in Rome Only few hours. I know that I will come here one more time because I this was amazing experience.

You can't walk more than 10 minutes in Rome without stumbling on a historic masterpiece. All of the historic places are humbling - and provide wonderful backdrops for photos. It's another well marked location so it can get pretty busy.
We went to a mass and it magical, the sound was fantastic. Well worth doing, not everyday you can hear mass in a Roman temple.
The price was right free. You go inside and look at all the beautful paintings and sculpturs it is worth your time.

Let me start by saying the venue itself was breathtakingly beautiful. The audio was provided via a smartphone with headsets, and overall very informative, however, I overheard in-person guides giving a tour, and the level of information was much wider and easier to digest. overall the trip was good and the pantheon was amazing.
Everybody needs to see the Pantheon and wonder how was it built so long ago….with rudimentary technology.
It’s monumental to be in its historic places and soak in it 😍😍😍😍😍🇮🇹🇮🇹It’s to be seen and done again beautiful
Spent time in the building and read Rick Steeves' write up of the building. Excellent explanation and made the visit much more enjoyable. The write up is available on an app.
Be prepared for long lines to get in. It is free to enter and it depends if you want to buy an audiobook. You can enjoy without it.

For how massive and impressive the Pantheon is from the outside you can actually tour the inside rather quickly— as it’s all one large space. Although it was built in 125AD, it became a Catholic Church in the 600s, and is still a functioning church with services to this day. Entry is free, but you currently need a timed-entry ticket to go inside the Pantheon. There are signs out front with QR codes you can scan to pick your window of time. We scanned in the morning and were able to get a 2pm time slot. You can also attend a service on Saturday night (5pm) or Sunday morning (10:30am.) — no tickets required. It has lovely church bells that ring on the hour. Even if you don’t go inside, walking around the building is breathtaking and unforgettable. The surrounding square and fountain are beautiful and have lots of food, coffee and gelato options. The Pantheon is lit it up beautifully at night as well, which is worth seeing in person.

You've seen it in books and in movies but NOTHING can prepare you for the first time you see the Pantheon in person. Wow. So amazing. There was a small line when we got there but within just a few moments we were inside. So impressive.

A truly beautiful, intricate monument - even though it is of course a popular tourist landmark, it was an incredibly peaceful atmosphere. Don't forget to look up at the ceiling!I recommend going early in the day - no queues (we went in December). We also didn't go for the tour, although this is quite usual for us - we just prefer to go at our own pace. Entry is free, and you're close to the Trevi Fountain too.Make it a place to visit when you're in Rome!
Yet again another thing that you should do while in Rome . Really impressive . Go and drink in the history . Visually stunning . The area around it is a typical tourist trap ..

The grandeur and ambiance, makes you disappointed at the lousy and boring architecture common today. What design, what uncompromising vision to create such a temple.I have been to Rome many times and this is always on my list to visit again and again.
I love it, even if I had to get a french tour guide for two of my friends, I did enjoy the walk and the magic from the place
So beautiful. We were in awe the entire time. Amazingly it's free to access year-round, but you need to have a reservation to enter on Sundays as they hold a mass (free too, but sells out fast!)
You’re here, check it off.! Vast and beautiful. Kinda tucked away but in the middle of everything.Architecture and history is mind blowing.
Free entrance, massive artifacts, impressive atmosphere. You should not miss it, when in Rome; you have to visit!
Absolutely breathtaking. The atmosphere inside, the structure, the details. We had seen photos of it and reconstructions but in person it is something else!
The most ancient temple in Rome. It's the unice one with it's architecture design. Wonderful the same trample Is standing more then 2500 years... Just a wow
A must see while in Rome; try to go in the off hours when it is less crowded and the lines to get inside are shorter.
very interesting place to visit - we were on a guided walking tour and it did help to have someone explain the history
There was so much information and the voice on the audio was very nice to listen to, didn’t have to que long and the experience was amazing
you should come when it's raining its beautiful. I went early morning and was 8th in the line. The Pantheon is breathtaking
It was beautiful outside, but we did not get to go in as tickets were already sold out. When we go back we will be ready!!!

We were lucky to be able to wander in one weekday evening when there were no queues.  If you have limited time in Rome and want to see this masterpiece of Ancient Roman engineering, book well in advance.
Beautiful experience visiting this majestic building. Inside you can see some tombs of the rulers of the Savoy house.

We visited at 3.30 pm and walked straight in to see this  wonderful church.  Free of charge to visit it is the most preserved monument of ancient Rome with all its history, sculptures and paintings, it is one of the most famous places in the city.  The outside architecture is impressive.  The Pantheon has a huge spectacular giant dome, with its famous hole in the top  and looking up inside it was amazing. The Pantheon should be on your to-do list in Rome.

If I could rate 0, I would. The pantheon is of course very impressive and nice to visit. But this tour is a scandal!No tour guide showed up, and no responses either. And when I applied for refund, they refused! I don’t understand… is this tour only charging money for nothing? Please stay away from this tour! It is a fraud!
Impressive piece of unreinforced concrete roof, in use since it was built and in good condition because of it, read about it before going

It’s a magnificent church with an extraordinary history. We visited at night, which gives it a more majestic look. It’s free to enter, and although there was a line to get in, it moves fast and we waited less than 5 minutes.
Staff helpful and kind. Short wait to enter and pick up the audio guides, great for those who know little about the pantheon
Provided with headphones and they are all put in the bin. A temple embossing the universe that slowly destroys it with single use waste and no recycling. A disgrace to the 21st century
After walking from our hotel to the Spanish Steps and Trevi Fountain, we opted for an easy to find taxi and got dropped off next to it.    Beautiful, and its free !

Saw this as part of a guided private tour with Marco from Eyes Of Rome. The detail and accomplishment of this building is beyond incredible. To think it's 2000 yrs old is almost incomprehensible! Highly suggest...especially if it's raining...you'll see why!
Fascinating ancient building. The structure of the building with the hole in the roof is incredible.
No visit to Rome is complete without a visit to the Pantheon.  Amazing architecture and engineering, beautiful statues and monuments inside.   An ancient wonder worth visiting.

Lucky enough to get a tour of Pantheon when we first arrived in Rome.  It can be a hard ticket to get but there are a few tour guides right at the entrance that sometimes have extra tix and you get a headset.  Must see in Rome.

The Pantheon was perhaps the biggest surprise of our Mediterranean cruise.  After weeks of touring ruins, suddenly there is the Pantheon, so gorgeously intact and ornately splendid.  An architectural marvel and incredible to have survived so unscathed for millennia.

The 'Pantheon' is one of those (many) "must-see Rome spots". Luckily for us we stumbled right into it after strutting down the Spanish Steps (and blowing by Trevi Fountain!!) It's a beautiful old building that is an easy, quick stop to whet your tourists' appetite. Located in a cool area of the city that's surrounded by great little restaurants and bars, make sure you visit here while in Rome.*Just keep your pants on!
The audio tour was easy to follow and informative. Timed entry was easy and meant the venue was not crowded.
Booked tour whilst queuing outside the Pantheon. Easy to book and admission to the Pantheon. Felt we enjoyed and learned a lot more from the audio tour than if we had not opted for the audio guide.

The Pantheon is amazing, the structure alone is impressive. It is FREE to enter and the day we went there was no line to enter. The only issue to be aware of is there are a lot of Hawkers/Sellers that harass you outside, take care of your belongings.

First of all you have to register on line at least a day before to get a ticket to go inside. Its free, just register on line. They will give you a designated time to visit and this helps control the crowd. The inside as expected is awesome. The Altar, the tomb of the King and of Raphael etc. a very good place to pray, marvel at the architecture and a lot of photo op.

This architectural wonder is definitely worth visiting. It’s impressive from outside but the inside is even more so, especially when u read about it. There were no lines outside so we went in on two different occasions.
Amazing structure to see.  Stopped in while walking in the area.  Not much time needed to visit, 15-20 minutes.  Free to walk in
Hard to imagine this being designed and built when it was.  From air flow to rain drainage to the structure itself.
standing in line outside for quite a long time (entrance is free) can be a pain when it's hot and sunny so go out of season
Obviously you have to stop.  A tour is nice, but you can go through without one, and just read the signs.  Ticket gets you to the Forum and Palatine Hill- definitely do those too.

If you have any interest in architecture or just the building techniques of ancient Rome, the Pantheon is worth a visit.  Putting aside the  lines that may form outside in the busy season (free timed entry tickets are required for weekends and holidays), a visit of 15 to 30 minutes will  suffice.  This is the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome, constructed in AD 126.  Amazing!  The interior is now part church part civic memorial, and even the interior columns, floors and artwork are worth a look.

We visited the Pantheon and its piazza every night of our stay. It is remarkably well-preserved, and the sense of being in the presence of antiquity is awe-inspiring. The lines are long during the day, so go in the evening. It is free to enter and you do not want to miss this stunning remnant of ancient Rome.

One of the history sites every tourist wil visit in Rome. It's good that they work with a queing line so inside there's always enough space to walk and see everything. Short audio guide is very clear to experience the history of the building. Great value!

Beautiful to see this building.  Worth using an audio tour to get the most out of the visit but even without it is still worth a visit to take in the beauty of this wonderful building with its magnificent history.  Remember if visiting at the weekend you need to book your visit - no charge but limited numbers allowed in at any one time.

Compared with most sites dating from Roman period, this one is in excellent condition. You can sit on the benches, listen to the audio guide, and look at what you are hearing about. Beautiful building and nice to be able to sit down.

The audio tour was thorough. I learned things I didn't know before and gave me a new perspective to the historic building. It was nice to be able to hear the guide without interruptions and be able to do it at your own pace.

Order online and was easy with audio though doesn’t take long to listen but it was very beautiful to see on our Rome tripping of two days .Go early as crowd builds up around 11:30 ,nice bars and coffee shop for resting .we also did the Spanish steps and Trevi fountain on the way to there .three in one can’t go wrong .all walkable .

The audio tour was informative and thorough, without being heavy or boring; the guided tour groups actually            were kind of in the way!  The amount of historical beauty and grandeur here is impressive, and the magnificence of the art and architecture is sometimes overwhelming, but having seen it once has only given me the desire to return time and time again.

The Pantheon itself is worth seeing.The audio tour is nothing real special. However, on weekends you will be granted entry even on short notice.To conclude, the entry in the Pantheon is worth the money - even if entry usually is free of charge.
Worth a visit. Free and steeped in history. Quite busy, so get there as it opens for better photos . One way system helped.
Paulo was great. He had good insights and was very engaging. He also could connect a lot of history and culture to today. And, he had showed us great photo spots
Pantheon is a must when you visit Rome. The building is impressive with its dome on top of the temple. The best time to visit seems to be in the morning.
Magnificent building and well kept in the original state. The choir inside made the visit well worth it.
As the title suggests, seeing this in real life was a totally unexpected surprise despite seeing it in pictures previously. A must see.
Don't miss this beautiful piece of history. Nice plaza to people watch. Lots of other sites nearby to see.
Having on,y seen photos of the inside, I thoroughly enjoyed seeing this. The haphazardly set up line moved quickly to get inside, and you can stay at your leisure.
We visited the Pantheon at night and it was impressive. We have toured the interior previously and would advise including this in your program as it is a part of essential Rome.

Stopped here when on a vespa tour and it was one of my favorite stops in Rome. This building is amazing and the history behind the building of it is very interesting. Highly recommend going here while in Rome.
An absolute magnificent church. Well worth a visit. Do not be put off by the rather long queue. It does move very quickly.

(Oct 22) Well worth visiting as you step back in history. Don't be phased when you first enter the square and see the long queue, it moves quickly. Inside are readable facts and this we did without the need for a guide.
A visit to the pantheon is a must.Booked the audio guide in advance you get your guide...duration half an hour, but absolutely to do on a Roman weekend!

Packed when we went and loads of people talking than being silent (due to being in a church). We took the audio guide which was really informative. Although there was large queues, they did move quickly.

I think The Pantheon was one of my most favourite places! The building is beautiful and amazing how it was built! We walked all around to see the sides and back of how it was built as we were recommended to do that (arches built into the brick work).The line was very quick moving even with people pushing in (noones fault, hard to see where the line was as the area is crowded).Inside was clean, just amazing and while busy inside it was not overly crowded.

I have been here about half a dozen times now and it is getting more and more intriguing for every visit due to the fact that I see more and more details so do read in about Pantheon before visiting, just amazing.

This ticket does not enable you to skip the queue.  Once inside you pick the headset up and can then listen to the commentary but they need some form of POI before they’ll let you have the headset.  Luckily, we had a bank card with us.  The commentary was interesting but I think  there were information boards inside which would have given us the basic information.

Being first a temple to the gods and then a Christian church, this building has been in use for 1900 years and so has been maintained, perhaps the best of the old buildings in Rome. It is geometrically beautiful with a few important personalities buried there. Since it is a church, it is free but closed to the public during services.

Awesome looked nothing special outside but amazing inside.Tip: we got there to be told at weekends you need to book so I stood to one side and booked through trip advisor it automatically sent booking to my phone and we went in straight away

Definitely worth going inside!! We wandered into the square at 2.55pm on a Sunday and the queue was HUGE and not moving. We were approached by a guy offering guided tours at 3pm for 15€ a person. We would normally not buy tours spontaneously but decided to give it a go and we were so glad we did. Our tour guide was fantastic and led a really interesting hour-long tour that meant we could breeze past the line and go in when we were ready. It’s definitely worth doing a tour to fully understand the building and get the most out of your visit. Our guide spoke about the full history of the Pantheon and its context within Rome’s story which was really interesting. Plus he even let us pay by PayPal when we didn’t have cash.

Walked by the Pantheon in the early afternoon. Took a few outside photos and noted that the line wrapped around the square. Then realized that the line was moving incredibly fast - joined and probably 10 minutes was in! Lots of people inside but roomy enough to get some decent photos if you can tolerate a few tourists in them! Absolutely stunning inside and doesn’t take long to visit.

There was a good sized line to get into the Pantheon (going back past the fountain in the piazza out front). But it moved pretty quickly.  There was no entrance fee, and we did not have (nor did we need) any reservation time, so I'm not sure what other reivews are referring to.  We were there on a Thursday afternoon.The architecture of the building is outstanding to see in person. The dome and oculus inside is quite amazing to behold, especially when you consider what construction methods were available back when it was built.  It is crowded, but take time to appreciate the various parts of the Pantheon such as Raphael's tomb.

This surviving example of Roman construction and design is an absolute marvel and a must see.  Luckily, we were with a guide who knew that timed tickets are required on the weekends or we would not have had the exceptional experience of seeing the inside of this amazing building.

I have heard so much about this building.  The structure was amazing and impressive.  The detail was mind blowing and the amount of people who wanted to see this building wanted to see it as much as I.  Make the Pantheon a must see but get a tour to find out much of what youtube will not say.
Even though it was very busy, we enjoyed the experience.  Ate some great food at a restaurant left of Pantheon.

It was a last minute stop as it was high on my list of things to do while in Rome but not on my husband's.  We are both so glad that we did!  It was so beautiful.  I thought the Sistine Chapel was a circular building, which it is not.  So when I walked into the Pantheon, this is what I thought the Sistine Chapel would look like.  Loved it!

The weirdest thing is that everyone almost didn't notice Raphael's grave! But it's a great church to visit. Beware! You need to book your entry tickets online and certainly at least a few days before you plan to go! A lot of people showed up and were denied entry because all entry times were booked.
A must see.  I followed the  Rick Steves audio tour and enjoyed my time there.  Wonderful historical site.

We really didn't know what to expect, but we ate dinner on the plaza one night (actually a pretty good meal and not too expensive) with the magically lit Pantheon before us.  Went back the next day and were blown away by this building which has been in use for two millennia!

Passed it at night and it looks beautiful. Went back during the day. A very long queue to get in but it moves fast. Ceiling construction is beautiful. Despite lots of people it’s very peaceful inside.

This was very misleading, beware and do not purchase unless you also have a timed entry reserved for the Pantheon. This is only an audio guide and does not get you entry to the Pantheon. We bought this first then tried to get a reservation to enter the Pantheon but could not. This was worthless to us and we will attempt to get a refund but the day no refunds after 24 hours before

Free entry and well worth reading online the history of this significant building. You may have to queue to get in, but it was fast moving and so not a problem. Take the time to really look around, because although it may only be one very large 'room' there is a lot of detail and the creation of the domed roof is an amazing architectural accomplishment!

Do not expect just to arrive and get in. Most visitation spots get filled within seconds. Access the Pantheon official website and reserve your visit time. Also, add an audio guide to make the whole experience complete. Having a pre-booked reserved time, make sure you arrive on time. You will guarantee entrance. Enjoy!

While so many churches in Rome our spectacular this is truly amazing given the time period when it was built.  It was originally built to honor Roman gods before being converted to a church.  We didn't do a tour and joined a long line but it moved very fast.  We were able to take all of the time we wanted once inside.
Beautiful churchIf you’re in the area it’s a quick visitFree with fast moving linesWorth the stop
It’s such a great monument to visit. You could stay there all day. Really recommend an audio guided tour.
One of the amazing architectural wonders of Rome. Definitely worth a visit, enter and walk around and enjoy the atmosphere.

Previously a pagan temple which has now been turned into a Christian church, this is a very beautiful building. Lots of artwork all around the circular interior, with the grave of Raphael, one of the greats.It was raining when we got there, but early enough not to queue, as it can get very busy.
Pantheon is awesome architecture. Very crowded but seating available. A must see in Rome. The ceiling is magnificent  with light beaming down? The space is sumptuous.

The oldest roman building in existence.The Pantheon is now a church after holding many guises, but still worth a visit to see the oldest, and probably most interesting building around.You need to book if you want to go inside, but this you can easily do online.

Absolutely spectacular to visit. We had no problem getting in with these pre booked tickets. We just walked straight in and we’re given headphones. We spent about an Hour inside the Pantheon with our audio tour.
A great 1-1.5 hour stop. The line moves quickly. I highly recommend the free Rick Steve's audio guide (app). The hole in the dome is pretty neat to see.
Amazing! It’s breathtaking inside and the audio guide allows you to independently listen and go at your own pace. There was comfortable seating for my mom.

Great experience! It was very crowded but regardless we had a fantastic time. We booked the audio tour and 100% recommend you to do it. You can sit in one of the benches and listen to the history of the place ! In one word amazing !!
We had this on our check list and it didn't disappoint at all. It was free to enter and worth the time to tour this architectural display. A must do when in Rome.
Another amazing site in Rome.  It is free entry but you have to queue to get in.The temple has a round hole in the roof and you would wonder how did they build this place 2000+’years ago.

Amazing structure with an amazing history.  Definitely recommend getting tickers. in advance!  Hundreds were turned away while we were there and we got to go right in because we had prepurchased our tickets.
The best place i saw in this city, in Europe, in the world. Is amazing that is still standing after 1900 years
Best to visit when the sun is shining to experience the light shining onto the inside of the dome from the central hole.

The line was long but fast moving, we sat back with a drink and joined when it was at its shortest. My tip is to download Rick Steve's audio guide for the pantheon which is free. We did this and it was great.

I have been to the Pantheon (inside and out) many times and every time I marvel at the architectural masterpiece that it is. I will never forget that our art teacher in secondary school took us there in second year and told us that the dome is built so cleverly that despite the big open oculus, it doesn't rain inside. It's history is also interesting, from temple dedicated to all the pagan gods, to christian church and now burial place of many historical figures including the first kings of Italy. It is one of the first things you should visit in Rome.
Unfortunately, it is free. It is a building to see but it takes 15 min so nothing unbelievable. It is nice to see it at night to take some pictures.

We arrived to the Pantheon and saw a very long line of people. The line moved quickly and very soon we were inside, marveling at the Pantheon's beauty, including the elegance of the engineering, the light pouring in, and the art. We were surprised and grateful that entrance is free and we could linger and enjoy for as long as we wanted. Not to be missed!
we went in the day and at night and it was just beautiful at night. kids had fun running around with other kids and listening to street performers.
This is location is worth 1 hour activity.  Walk the Square/Commons and eat and shop.  Very large crowds and worth the time and effort.

We didn't know you have to book ahead for this, unfortunately. We visited on Saturday morning, learned that we need a ticket (which is free) and they were sold out for that day and the next. Very sad for us. so learn from our mistake! :) It is worth seeing from the outside, especially at night from the restaurants nearby.
This building was our favourite site in Rome. The architecture was stunning, and it was free to visit!

Of course you are going to The Forum and St Peters and all other ruins & churches, but DO NOT MISS The Pantheon. It's a perfect building and will thrill, even if you are not an Architecture nut, as I am. GO! It's FREE, too.
The Pantheon is an amazing structure. Going with a guide helped us get in without such a long wait. It also gave us information, which for a first visit to Rome was very helpful.
Fantastic sight right in the middle of the Old Town. A church on Sundays so some times it is not accessible, but otherwise there is free entrance
It’s vast! The inside is stunning, a huge contrast to the outside. Free entry and the queue moves very quickly. Well worth a visit.
The queues look long to get on but they move quickly. Very worthwhile, it's an amazing building inside.

We chose an audio tour vs a guided tour of the Pantheon bc of time and scheduling issues , and we thoroughly enjoyed it. I don’t really know what a guide would have added bc this audio was so well done. Plus it’s only about 45 minutes. The crowds outside the Pantheon are insane, so that’s the only issue. But the Pantheon is amazing and beautiful.

We went early morning (around 8:15 a.m.) to marvel at the structure from the outside. It was great to have the place to ourselves for some photos. Around 8:30 a.m. there started to be a line-up to enter but it wasn't too bad. The building opens at 9:00 a.m. and it was free to enter. It was interesting to see the dome from the inside and to check out some of the interesting history associated with it.

Definitely worth seeing! We did this on our own (without a tour), there was a long queue but it moved quickly and it wasn't too crowded inside. It was also free to visit which was a nice surprise,  although we would happily have paid an entrance fee as it's remarkable.
Beautiful and interesting art, would recommend a visit. We had it a part of the historical tour of Rome

When we were in Rome a number of years ago we didn't have the opportunity to go inside the Pantheon. However on this trip we made a point of going inside. The line to enter went pretty quickly and the inside was well worth the visit. The ocular ceiling was amazing and the flooring with the way it was built provided drainage for when it rained. The sculptures and art work are amazing. This is a place not to be missed. Read up on it before going and you will appreciate it even more.
We were told to make sure we saw this and it was so worth it, The most amazing place and definately not to be missed!!The oculus and the dome are fantastic.
A must! Long lines that move very, very quickly. Be sure to have knees and shoulders covered or you will not be allowed in.

Really pleasant, cracking big old building with a hole in the top. For me it was my second favourite thing in rome, genuinely impressive that it’s free. Had some nutter on the organ when we went but it just added to the atmosphere.
Olga, our tour guide, is a former archeologist. She was a wealth of information on the structure and history of the Pantheon. Very informative and entertaining.
Free..A must!Amazing & huge inside!Everything is with walking distance & easy to find.One of these things it’s not what you expect inside.
Still just about complete its the most amazing place and example of the brilliance of Roman architecture...must see

This is an absolute awe inspiring monument to human achievement. Like a lot of Rome you just feel the  history all around you. I took half an hour of silent contemplation while inside. It had such moving quality to it.
Lives up to the hype. Visited this morning with my wife, wish I could  go back in time and pick this place to propose!

I always visit the Pantheon when in Rome. During this week’s visit we walked past the outside in the evening and went in during the day as it was raining and I wanted to see the rain coming through the occula. It was an amazing sight to see the rain splashing onto the floor.

Very good audio guide. We had the opportunity to see in exhibition a painting of the Last Supper version 2021. Pantheon generates incredible magnetism. On that weekend, check-in shifts had to be booked.

The line is pretty long but moves very fast. Also, I made a mistake and thought I bought a skip the line ticket. Nope. You can go online and buy an audio guide for a slotted time. But that doesn't mean you can skip the line once you get there. Love the Pantheon and history of it through.
Although there was a long line up to get in, it moved very quickly.It is an amazing 2000 year old building and well worth seeing.
During our last visit to Rome, we skipped the Pantheon beause of the long line.  This time we waited in line, and the line moved quickly.  Yes, it was crowded, but it was worth it.

Free entry into the Pantheon, and I didn't do any of the tours. Although looking back, it probably would have given a better understanding of the building if I had. Long queue to start with but it moved quite quickly. Fantastic site, that is a must see for everyone who visits Rome.
Very interesting and great to be able wander around at your own pace. Able to take some fab photos. Even better free entry. Payment only for a guide but it's not really needed.
Magnificent experience. Guided Tour with 20 people. Long queue but had “Skip the Line” pass. Several restaurants and local shops nearby. Truly amazing activity. 15-20 minutes.

Centrally located to some amazing restaurants and shops. The location itself was large than life and really incredible to see in person. It was a little crowded, and the vendors selling junk everywhere was slightly annoying. But otherwise really cool.

Really easy to book, and incredibly well organised at the venue. The tour itself is spot on if you want to find out more about the Pantheon in your own time as you can work your way around the tour at your own leisure. Highly recommended!

A wonderful journey through time. The audio tour is beautifully crafted and delivered. It's pace and detail captures the history and beauty of this ancient place. A place of worship, retains its calmness despite the numerous visitors.

A seriously big dome and church in a city centre square.......busy but queues move very quickly. The dome is spectacular and surprisingly has a big hole in the ceiling which leaves it open to the elements but that didn't affect us....every church in Rome just surprises you how beautiful they are and they are all free.
Superb and a must to do when in Rome. The building is amazing both inside and out. Well worth a visit. No need for a Tour or Guide. Just go in. It's free and should take no more than a hour

This is a must visit when in Rome. We had a tour guide, who shared his knowledge to the smallest detail, which if you hadn’t researched this magnificent building you would miss. It was mind blowing to try and understand how this now church was build and the amazing roof construction, in 125 AD, being the oldest  standing building in the world. Amazing.
I had seen a documentary and the Pantheon lived up to expectations. The audio guide is a must and enhances the experience.

Great way to get  information about what you are viewing, without it this would have been a quick visit. Don’t be put off by the queue it moves quickly as most people spend very little time inside., no skip system in place for pre-booking the audio tour.
The site was free - the lines were well organized and moved quickly.  It doesn't take much time to see it, but I would recommend that you read its history before you go to fully appreciate it.
Awe-inspiring structure. MUCH bigger than you would think! The square was very cool, too! These ancient structures should be seen by all.

This is one of the oldest building in Rome prior to Christianity. The way it was built is just amazing. The square is also nice and had a lot of good attractions and restaurants. I recommend a visit here for anyone coming to Rome.
Amazing site to see, we did it with a tour guide, Tomas, fantastic to experience and so much better with the guide.

The Pantheon is the best-preserved building from the ancient Roman times, and a marvel of engineering to boot. Its innovative construction have been studied by both Michelangelo and Brunelleschi, who designed St. Peter's and Florence domes. And it's free!
Such a beautiful space. Highly recommended. Stay as long  as you like and really soak up the atmosphere

We bought and paid for a guided tour of the Pantheon.  On the ticket it states to go an hour before your tour since the line could be that long.  When we got there, there was hardly a line so we were there about an 1 1/2 early.So, we had a choice to go back outside and wait or do the audio guide, which we opted to do.  The audio guide wasn't that bad pretty self explanatory as to which site you are seeing.Be sure to wear coverings of the shoulder and cover the knees

We went at the end of the day, and the line was much shorter, but it was a bit chaotic as it's not clear where the line starts/ends. Definitely do comeback at night to see it, and the square around it, lit up.
The only way to appreciate the pantheon is through a guided tour. The one the museum offers is outstanding

PROS- Knowledgeable tour guide.- Small group size (6 total).- Provided with a headset.CONS- Guide did not utilise the full floor space (only used about 10%).- No separate queue outside for pre-booked official guided tours.- Again no separate queue once inside, which adds to waiting time.
This ancient structure is an engineering marvel. Not happy that the Catholic Church made it its own. Would have preferred to see it in an ancient Roman theme (statues, etc.)

The Pantheon is a simply awe-inspring place to visit. The architecture, the scupltures, the artwork are all breathtaking.Word of warning though, although the Pantheon is free to enter every day, you MUST pre-book online if you wish to visit on a Saturday or Sunday. If you don't have a reservation for a weekend day, you'll be turned away.
all it is cracked up to be.  Didn't realize the interior was so ornate.  Walking tours provided interesting history.  No admission.  Large line which winds around the square but moves quickly.
The Pantheon is absolutely beautiful and rich in history as we found out on our tour. Our guide was wonderful. She gave us lots of information and readily answered all questions.
We were in love of the Pantheon. The best place to visit in Rome. It's a must see in Rome and just in the middle of the city center.
What an awesome experience to see the Pantheon.  Such history in this beautiful place. The building has been preserved and is used as a church today.
What a history and still in use! Now is a Catholic Church. A must see when in Rome . No charge just make sure your shoulders are covered to show respect for the church
Must see. Incredible building inside & out. Worth the queue which moves quickly . Very beautiful, definitely worth a visit.

As a Brit, I marvel at the Pantheon’s architecture taken in context of when it was erected, and where Britain was at the same time in history.  The building is indeed a stunning example of its time, and must be visited when in Rome.Avoid the peak times of the day, and you won’t have to queue.  Females remember to dress appropriately as it a revered place, and you won’t be permitted entry if you ignore the dress code.  Stay awhile, and absorb the beauty of this building.
In the end, admission is just free. So 8.5 euros for an audio tour is pretty pricey in my opinion

The building is beautiful and well worth visiting. The audio equipment is good and detailed. It is important to book on weekends and public holidays. Also it is not necessary to pay and an app is available.
Certainly less impressive than many other monuments, but remains a must-visit, especially as access is easy and free.

Great way to see the Pantheon & I’m glad Viator offered this option.  There was no line, everything was well sign posted & the staff there helped with any questions you had.  The Oculus is amazing :)I’d definitely recommend booking this trip.
Needed reservations to enter, but once inside, we were awestruck.  Only needed 2 hours here total. Paid the 8 Euro fee for individual headsets/walking tour.  It was fine.

As the Pantheon was free, this was just an unnecessary cost.  The bits of history and information, via the audio tour, are a bit too monotone.  This just wasn't worth the cost and could have been done in pamphlet. Save your money on this and just go in and enjoy the history of the place.
Unable to locate check in point for ticket redemption.   The entrance is free so do not waste your money here.  No way to contact anyone, felt like a scam!
You turn the corner and there is The Pantheon. It was so cool to see. Then touring the inside and seeing the oculus in the ceiling. It was a dream come true.

Pantheon on its own is amazing. Really impressive. The audio guide is not really necessary. I booked one because general (free) reservations for the Sunday were sold out, and by booking an audio tour the reservation is included (and usually still available). I only had time to visit on Sunday, but if you can visit any other day during the week, or there are still reservations available I would say the audio tour isn't a great addition to the whole experience on itself.

If you want to go to the Pentecost service, show up two or three hours early, or you won’t get in. You can always enjoy a cappuccino in the square while you wait for it to open. If you don’t get in, you can somewhat see the rose petal drop by standing outside the door right at the ropes. The singing is utterly gorgeous!

Went during the week so there wasn't much of a line. Had the chance to walk around the inside and even sit down on the benches and admire the beauty of the architecture and the sculptures. Definitely a sight to see!
This is a really misleading trip. The pantheon is a free activity and when we went was no queue at all so we were charged for £28 for free entry. Not needed. Avoid.

We visited later in the day and it was fairly fast to get in. Clothing needs to be covered shoulders and it’s well organised. The building is beautiful and you can’t stop looking up. We wandered around, it had just rained so there was quite a lot of water inside… I wonder how they get rid of that? It’s a lovely space and free to get in, even if you go for 15 mins it’s worth it.
2000 years of standing roman architecture, which looks just as good today as it always did.  Easy to access, no steps
In Rome, there is history everywhere you look.  The Pantheon is an amazing structure.  It is beautiful inside. Definitely worth a visit.

Well organized, glad there was no line which was mentioned in the description. Was able to go in an hour before our scheduled time. Loved the level of detail and that there was also a written text in the downloaded app if I wanted to refer back. Would prefer I could use this app using my own phone and ear buds. Should have a place we could dispose of the earbuds.
You must go must see this. I am so glad I had a tour guide, John. Amazing knowledge and a great sense of humour.  Fantastic
A very informative way to find out lots of history and information about this amazing structure. A must!
Around every corner in Rome you will find delights, Pantheon being one of them. Take time to sit and enjoy food or drinks, or both, and watch the world go by. Stunning!

We booked our visit last minute, which didn’t give us much choice of time slots (book early). We didn’t read the description regarding the clothing guidelines but luckily enough we had suitable attire. It was a Sunday afternoon and the queues were relatively short. Remember to bring photo ID as it is required for the audio equipment hire! Lots of space to explore the interior whilst listening to the audio commentary. The audio guide was very informative and easy to use. You can’t but marvel at the sculptures, paintings and interior. Limited numbers inside made the visit relaxed and enjoyable. Definitely worth the visit and value for money.

A very popular attraction that can result in very long queues due to a limit on the number of people allowed in at any time. Be aware that entrance at the weekend has to be booked and paid for online at least a day in advance. During the week, entrance is free and on a first come, first served basis.Once inside you realise why it is so popular, with more to see than is immediately obvious

Was an amazing place to visit, it was very busy but we had lots of chance to look at everything we wanted too, defiantly worth a visit.At busy times the ques are in the open so you will need to cover up.
When in Rome you have to visit this place hopefully you get Joe as your guide  because he will tell you The greatest story’s of Ancient Rome

Cosy hotel in the CBD. Extremely professional and friendly reception team. From their Manager miss Olivia to Yvonne and Alex.Rooms small but very well maintained. Cleanliness is top notch. High Speed internet good for business travelers.Wonderful breakfast and ambience. Everyone made me feel at home most especially the reception team. This only means their team leader is doing a wonderful job at ensuring they deliver exceptional service. Kudos miss Olivia. Best Western must be very proud to work with a person as professional as you are. Keep it up. I am most definitely recommending you guys.

The Pantheon is a beautiful piece of architectural history. In everything, the Pantheon pursues its name. It means “for all gods”. In the 2000 years that the Pantheon has been there, it has experienced numerous transformations.However, the audio tour is completely unnecessary. In the more than 2,000 years of history they decide to only go into 1600-1700. No credit to the builders of the Pantheon. Still explanation about this time of construction.The architecture is discussed, but nothing you can't already know with 1x Googling. Furthermore, the tour is full of spelling and style errors. So when you go to Rome you MUST have seen the Pantheon, but don't buy the audio tour.

Easy to follow and interesting to listen to. The provided digital audio guide (smart phone style device and headphones) had a helpful graphical system, so you knew where to stand in order to hear the guide.It was easy to collect from the desk inside the Pantheon. You will need to provide some ID, which they hold onto, until you return the devices.
To think the entry is free to such and amazing place; incredible.  A must see. The tombs of Umberto I and Victorrio Emanuele II and that of Raphael were surreal.

I've visited every time I've been to Rome and it never fails to lose it's wonder - a must see, even if busy in the summer. Just don't forget to cover your shoulders or you'll be forced to buy a scarf from one of the pavement sellers outside.
Stunning, Huge, from every angle it seems very big. Although inside there are very few things to see compared to other attractions.

The Pantheon  was built by Hadrian around  126 AD and became a Roman Catholic church in  609 which makes it a historically significant building and still has the claim to have the large un-reinfoced concrete dome .It is free to enter and usually crowded. Panthoen comes from the Greek words, Pan which means ALL and Theon, which means GODS.

We visited the pantheon and it was absolutely stunning. The outside, the inside, everything! What we were confused about was the clothing situation. Online it seems very clear that you must wear shorts and not show shoulders but most people were wearing shorts showing their knees and a lot of people were wearing tops that showed their shoulders. It seemed that you were pretty much okay as long as you weren't showing a ridiculous amount of skin.But overall, I could have sat in the pantheon for ages, it really is an amazing place.

***Bring a shawl/jacket to cover your shoulders You cannot enter the Pantheon with your shoulders/chest uncovered.  If you forget to bring something to cover up with, they will not let you in.  There were about 14 men trying to sell shawls to people in the queue.  I fell for the trap & had to pay €5 for one. Your legs/stomach (i.e. if you’re in a crop top) seem to be allowed on show. The queue looks long but it goes very quickly.  Entry is free.
A must see. line was long but moved very fast. Once you are inside you cannot believe it. You have to go if you are in Rome a few days.

Don’t pass this up!  It’s free and awe inspiring.  Grab a free audio guide podcast to make the most of your time (We used Rick Steves guide).  The line to get in was the length of the front of the Pantheon when we arrived, but moved swiftly and we were in in less than 2 minutes.
Very nice, wonderful place. But for visit inside better made reservation day and time. Inside all are interesting, beautiful. It will be deserve your hour.

Admission is free without reservation. But if you want audio guide you have to pay €8.50. There is in Spanish and it lasts about 45 minutes. Personally it wasn’t worth it, as much of the information can be found on the Internet. Of course, the monument is impressive.
My husband and I jumped on some Limes and followed our phones to this site.  There were quite a few people here but it was worth it!
Hotel Fontana, excellent....Giuseppe very friendly reception, the same facilities, breakfast very well..persolan very friendly,
On every trip to Rome we visit the Pantheon.  It is a spectacularly perfectly built monument and resting site for some of Italy's most famous.  It is a must see.

I've been here 3 times now and the "wow" factor is still very much intact. One must visit in person to experience this architectural marvel. It can be packed so choose your time wisely. To visit on weekends and public holidays, it is necessary to book admission at least one day in advance. Admission is free from Monday to Friday. A trip to Rome is incomplete without stopping at the Pantheon.

No visit to Rome is complete without visiting the 2000 year old Pantheon. Despite its age, it is incredibly well preserved. The visit is free and you don't have to reserve online unless you plan to visit on holidays or church days (like Sunday I believe). I visited this twice during my time in Rome- once I had to scan a QR code and enter my information online before entering, while the other time, they just let me in. They do offer an audioguide inside the Pantheon which talks about the architecture and the various parts of the church including Raphael's tomb.

I cannot explain how amazing is the Rome Pantheon. To see and go into a building that has been standing for almost two millennia is something very special. The architecture, the history, the people interred within it... It is just too much
You avoid queues, very good and organized Service, fast and good price. It's worth it if you're really interested, otherwise you're 5min and gone.

So we made our way to the pantheon and no clear queue at the time and went to the front with our mobile tickets to which I was told I need to cover my shoulders. I should have known as much as the vatican asks this but I've been to other churches which didn't. So was fine once I bought a scarf to cover up. We redeemed our ticket at reception to get an iPhone style audio book which guides you around the pantheon in a numbered order. The Pantheon is Rome’s great survivor. Built by the Roman emperors Agrippa and Hadrian around 2000 years ago, it has survived Barbarian invaders, wars, fire, looting and the elements and has remained in almost constant use since.Be prepared that the queue can be horribly long. We stayed just around the corner and walked past the Pantheon often. Several times the queue was so long that we skipped it, thinking that we’d easily find another time when it was not so bad. This didn’t really work. The queue was always pretty long and the only way to beat it was to be there first thing in the morning. The inside is very different to the outside. From outside, the façade has all the typical features of a classical Roman temple including a portico, Corinthian columns and pediment. Inside, the dome is the real mind-boggler. It is still the largest unreinforced concrete dome in the world. Never been beaten. That is quite an engineering feat and it required a few building tricks to stop the weight of the dome from causing the whole thing to just fall inwards. To avoid collapse, they built the foundations and lower walls out of heavy stone like travertine, then as they built upwards, they made the concrete thinner and added pumice so that it was quite lightweight. Finally, as you look up, you stare straight into the the oculus, the ‘eye’ of the dome. It looks small but that’s a trick of the eye. It’s actually 8.2m across. It just looks small because the interior is so big. The sunlight streams through but equally, so does the rain. You can spot the holes in the centre of the floor that allow the water to drain away. It’s also worth noting that the marble floor tiles that you walk on are the original ancient Roman ones. Pretty incredible to think that the ancient Romans trod the same floor.Several noteworthy tombs are here including Raphael and the first kings of Italy, Victor Emmanuel II and Umberto.TIPS: It's free to enter Monday-Friday (on weekends, you need to have booked at least 24 hours ahead). Masks are still required inside. There is no information inside so a guidebook or audioguide (€8.50) is pretty helpful here.

One of the fascinating things about the Pantheon is how the city has been built up around it. Over time new construction and necessities of modern living have impacted how it is situated. It sits below street level but originally, the Pantheon would have sat high above street level, with five steep stairs used to reach it.This sunken effect makes the Pantheon seem like a sentry amid the noise and pace of modern times. It is sturdy in appearance and seems almost stubborn in this setting. It has dug it’s heels in and has spent hundreds of years refusing to be destroyed like it’s predecessors. It is different than the other ancients in my opinion. Perhaps because it is not cordoned off like the Colosseum and the Forum are. It is just… there, right in the middle of the McDonald’s signs and it is insouciant. If the Pantheon was a movie character, it would be Clint Eastwood in “Gran Torino”… get off my lawn! Unlike a couple of hours earlier, by midday the area was now packed and we waited for 20 minutes in the queue.  We actually joined a tour along with a Scottish couple to learn a bit about the history.  The guards in the building were obsessive about face masks and mask positioning which was quite ironic as most people in the queue didn't even put them on until they went in!  The guide explained about this being one of the earliest Imperial buildings although it was then repaired and rebuilt by Hadrian at the turn of the first century. Unlike most of the pre Christian temples, this wasn't destroyed by over zealous Christians and was converted into a church.  The design was quite incredible with the dome slowly decreasing in thickness as it climbed and the building materials being swapped for lighter versions, eventually ending at pumice for the very top.  The oculus at the centre is open to the elements and represents the pathway to heaven, being where Romulus was allegedly transported to heaven after being struck by lightning.  The dome is apparently the largest unsupported dome in the world and Michealangelo built the dome of St Peters half a metre smaller as a sign of respect.

Pantheon Rome Pantheon Roma December 2021 820689077 Visited the Pantheon as one of the "must see" attractions in Rome. Current Covid rules mean you MUST have a current covid vaccination "green pass" (UK NHS online) AND a photo id. This can be a photo drivers licence but they did accept a photo of my passport. No pre-booking necessary if you go mid-week but a reservation is required on weekends and Bank Holidays. This can be done online by visiting the Pantheon website (just search for Pantheon Roma).The structure and artwork inside is very impressive but a little underwhelming if you've already seen the Vatican and its museums. For the best impression, do the Pantheon first.

It's just amazing that the Pantheon is the №1 attraction in Rome. After all, there is a Colosseum, a Forum? On the other hand, the Pantheon is the oldest building in the city and the largest domed temple in the world. Even the initiator of the construction is indicated on the pediment of the building: "M.AGRIPPA L.COS. TERTIUM FECIT" (Marcus Agrippa, son of Lucius, three times consul, did). He was the nephew of the first Emperor of Rome, Octavian Augustus. That temple was square. The pantheon became circular under Emperor Hadrian. The most difficult thing was to build a huge dome. According to legend, for this purpose, the hollow building was filled from the inside with earth, to which gold coins were mixed. After the dome was erected, Hadrian announced that anyone who found a coin could take it for himself - and overnight the Roman gold diggers took the whole earth out of the temple along with rare coins. The huge dome has a rare feature. A huge 8-meter hole is located in the middle of the 43-meter dome. Since the Pantheon is deployed not typical for ancient temples to the North, the oculus is the only opportunity to illuminate the temple. A huge puddle gathers in the middle on rainy days. The Romans did not make improvements in antiquity... Complete authenticity! The diameter of the oculus of the Pantheon (8.3 m) is not accidental. The size of the oculus allows the sun to "spend" autumn and winter in the upper hemisphere of the building, and after the vernal equinox, the sunbeam passes through the doorway. It turns out the "Axis of the world". I saw something similar on the pyramid of Kukulkan in Chichen Itza - the spectacle of the "Feathered Serpent". All this splendor had a chance to survive to this day. As you know, the whole of ancient Rome was either dismantled by the inhabitants or destroyed by Christians as part of the struggle against pagans. The Byzantine Emperor Phocas, who controlled Rome at that time, rendered a great service to world civilization by handing over the temple to Pope Boniface IV in 608. The Pope re-consecrated the Pantheon, which from the temple of all the gods became the Basilica of the Virgin Mary and the Martyrs. It was problematic to get the relics of the Madonna, but the bones of the martyrs were brought from the catacombs on 28 carts. After 14 centuries, only noble bones remained in the Pantheon. The pompous tombstones of the first kings of united Italy, Victor Emmanuel and his son Umberto, and the modest grave of Raphael meet you inside. Judging by the concentration of tourists, history has put everything in its place.

Built in 125 AD..(just think about that 125 AD) this structure was built to worship the Roman gods.  It is perhaps the best preserved  ancient Roman monument.  It was turned into a church in 609 AD Originally built in 27 BC by Emperor Augustus it burned to the ground in a great fire in 80 AD and was rebuilt by Emperor Domitian only to be hit by lightning and burned to the ground in 110.   The monument you see today was built in 12 AD by Emperor Hadrian and remains intact as one of the greatest examples of Roman engineering.  This is living history.  The dome is the largest unsupported in the world and for 1300 years was the largest as well.  It is an engineering marvel and a sight to behold.

A must see when in Rome. Don't pay for admission no matter what any website says. Admission is FREE to all. If in doubt, ask at the door. Anyone who has not given 5 (excellent) after a visit of the Pantheon was either following the wrong Rome guide or has has not read enough to understand its significance. It was built to honour the Gods of Olympus and, in particular, the legendary ancestors of the Caesars: Mars and Venus. What we see today dates from the era of Emperor Hadrian who had it reconstructed around 130AD. The bronze door aslo dates from this period. The walls are almost 7 metres thick. The dome is actually bigger than that of St. Peters. Raphael was buried here in 1520 and, while London was burning in 1666, the Pantheon had just been isolated from the surrounding buildings in the three preceding years.

The Pantheon is truly breathtaking when you first enter this magnificent structure and is something anybody should see, and particularly those interested in architecture in any way.  This building is one of the best preserved structures of the Roman Empire constructed in the 2d century. The dome and oculus are sort of mind blowing considering it has no steel inside of it to provide strength.  It is also the inspiration for rotundas around the world.  You can take self guided tours as well as connect with a few companies that offer guided tours that will are full of historic information not only about the construction but also the memorials inside.  Currently there is a temperature check, mask requirement, and one way walking traffic in order to enter. The Pantheon represents the history of Rome. I believe that The Pantheon is the oldest  operating building in Rome. It was built between the years of 27 BC to 14 AD. After the original was destroyed by fire, The Roman Temple was REBUILT by Emperor Hadrian in 126 AD. The structure is cylindrical with a portico of granite columns. despite the age of the building, it boasts of the world's largest unreinforced dome. It has the original hole in the roof which was needed to let the smoke of the fires rise out of the building. The Romans were great builders !!Then, in 609, the Emperor gave the structure to The Pope !! The Pope turned the Roman Temple into a Catholic Church.  The structure is now " The Basilica of Saint Mary and the Martyres". It still has the hole in the roof.You will  be surprised by the interior of the structure (now a Church). I did not attach any photos because you can find much better photos of the interior at any website. This building is the history of Rome.
