If you are visiting Rome, the Pantheon is a must. The sheer size of this chapel/Monument will buckle your knees. Best part about it, it is free! Unless you pay for a guided tour of course. Take pictures of this massive chapel from the outside, but trust me pictures do no justice. Once you walk inside make sure you remove any headgear. Upon looking to the ceiling, you will notice a circular opening. This is called the Oculus which is one of the most impressive features of the Pantheon. The Pantheon is host to many different symbols from different religions so you must pay attention to that. It is influenced not only by Christianity but Greek mythology as well. This building was burnt down in the great fire and reconstructed, so that might explain the different symbology that covers it today. The Pantheon is an amazing survivor. It was built by the Roman emperors Agrippa and Hadrian and has survived intact and remained in almost constant use since.From the outside, the façade has all the typical features of a Roman temple including a portico, Corinthian columns and a stately pediment. The emperor Hadrian added the rotunda around 126 AD.The queue is often long but don’t be put off by this because the inside is incredible and is completely different to the outside. You should absolutely see both. In any case, the crowd moves quite quite quickly because people don’t stay inside for long.You can pay for an audio guide inside. We didn't and in hindsight, we probably should have. It would have been much better to hear about the structure while we were actually there. If you don’t do the audioguide or tour, then I’d recommend doing some reading beforehand.As you enter, the first thing to notice are the marble floor tiles. This is the original ancient Roman floor. I was very taken with the thought that I was walking across the same floor that ancient Romans had also walked on.The next thing you notice is that the interior is perfectly proportioned. The diameter of the rotunda is 43.3m which is exactly the same as its height – a perfect space. The dome is the real jaw-dropper. It is still the largest unreinforced concrete dome in the world. Never been beaten. That is quite an engineering feat and it required a few building tricks to stop the weight of the dome from causing the whole thing to collapse. To avoid disaster, they built the foundations and lower walls out of heavy materials, then as they built upwards, they made the concrete thinner and added pumice so that it was quite lightweight. Finally, as you look up, you stare into the the oculus, the ‘eye’ of the dome. It might look small but that’s just an optical illusion because the interior is so big. The oculus is actually 8.2m across. The sunlight streams through it, stopping the interior from being gloomy and dark. Rain can also stream through but it drains away in the centre. Before you leave, do a lap around the walls because there are also several noteworthy tombs including Raphael and the first kings of Italy, Victor Emmanuel II and Umberto I.

The exact age of the pantheon remains unknown. Possibly started under Emperor Trajan around AD 114 and completed under Emperor Hadrian between AD 125 and 128. The Pantheon - the Greek adjective meaning "honor all Gods" - had for more than 1700 years, the largest dome in the world (inside diameter: 43.30m), at that time impressive and astonishing. It is one of the best-preserved structures of Roman antiquity.Even, almost 2000 years later, the design, proportions, elegance and harmony are spectecular and amazing and the buliding is a great reminder of the architecture of the great Roman Empire.There is certainly a lot of people and there are always tourists. Not far from the Piazza Navona stands this beautiful structure, which is so unique, still in excellent shape and could be voted as one of the wonders of modern civilization. We finished our Tartufo, and we were on a mission to discover this fantastic piece of architecture.Patricia remarked it is one of ancient Rome's most enduring constructions, and it is a building worthy of the Gods that defies imagination. It is one of the few places where it is possible to view Rome, just as the Romans gazed at it when first constructed. As soon as we entered this massive open place, I imagined this was no ordinary structure or temple that exhibits a timelessness where everything seems to have ceased two thousand years ago when it was dedicated to all gods. The sixty-foot granite columns at the entrance are just a sample of what we are to expect inside. Emperor Hadrian commissioned it, and most of the granite was transported from Aswan, similar to the Obelisks that were looted from the land of the Pharaohs.t One should stand outside and admire the enormous sixty-ton columns that were quarried from Egypt. Spare a thought and think of the logistics that were involved in the transportation of these massive columns across the Mediterranean Sea. The time, tools and human resources that would have gone into the preparation of this significant project would have taken many months of diligent contingency planning which would have included, procuring slave labour, food and deciphering the precise navigation route across the Mediterranean.As I gazed around, the interior has remained immaculately intact with its spectacular dome three metres high at its oculus top, through which light tickles illuminating the entire building. I was amazed at this structure, and Patricia remarked that when any rain penetrates, there are twenty-two holes that drain the water away.It is a visual feast for the eye, that is so striking, massive, well preserved, and perfectly proportioned that makes it so hard to believe that it was erected nearly two thousand years ago. Just as you think there is not much more to admire, the skylight lets in a beam of sun that makes the whole place light up. It is as good as it gets, and my imagination reverts to its early days when Emperor Hadrian entered the building in a vast, impressive procession. The music would herald in that the lord of the land was in session at this glorious temple. I was stunned and astonished, and without any hesitation, I will remark loudly, out of all the surviving structures of ancient Rome the Pantheon elicits amazement, thanks to its breathtaking construction. One last mention is that it holds the tombs of Raphael and the first kings of Italy, Victor Emmanuel, and Umberto. NB:NB: This was a part of the tour pamphlets I would hand to my clients in the 1980s who visited my agency.

This is an incredible bit of architecture and it's truly breathtaking. Created by the emperor Hadrian between 113–125 AD, it has a rectangular vestibule that links the porch to the rotunda, which is under a coffered concrete dome, with a central opening to the sky - which is insane as the evening we were there it was raining & the rain was just falling onto the marble floor below! Having been continuously used since the 7th century, it is one of the best-preserved of all Ancient Roman buildings & is now a Catholic church. A number of significant people are buried at the Pantheon, including tow Italian Kings and the Renaissance painter Raphael. The Pantheon has been built in 27-25 BC by Marcus Agrippa as a temple for the gods. It is to be admired the beauty of this building from outside and inside the architecture, with its circular structure, the immense dome, the huge colunnas, the mosaics, the interior marble, and the graves of illustrious characters such as kings, artists, such as Annibale Carracci, Baldasare Peruzzi, Taddeo Zuccari, Raffaelo Sanzio, etc.Then a walk in the surroundings will be very pleasant, full of businesses, restaurants and a good coffee in the "Tazza D'oro".

Pantheon in Rome is  a round temple on the Field of Mars, founded by the emperor Hadrian in 125 in the place from 27 BC , destroyed in a fire in 64 AD.  The construction of the building was directed by Apollodoros, who was exiled and probably later executed by the emperor. The Pantheon was dedicated to planetary deities and the reigning emperor at the time, and since the 7th century the Pantheon has been used as a Catholic church  Santa Maria ad Martyres . It is one of the best preserved buildings from the times of ancient Rome. The Pantheon is a rotunda with a diameter of 42.2 m and the same height. In front of the entrance to the temple, there is a three-row portico of 16 columns in Corinthian order. The dome of the building is cast from unreinforced, monolithic concrete with a central hole  with a diameter of about 7.9 m, the only hole in the dome illuminating the interior. Since the opening has no cover, the floor in the middle is slightly concave with an outflow to drain rainwater. Rectangular and semicircular cavities alternate in the wall . They are used for burial chapels. In one of them is the grave of Rafael, one of the greatest painters of the world. A wonderful and charming historical object on the city map. Certainly one of the most important points to visit on the map of Rome.

Once again I visit the magical Pantheon, a church in which the real kings of Italy are buried and among the many a man no one remembers, Raphael Sanzio. I want to focus on the structure of the roof, still resistant and of Roman times, built for those times with a bold technique and still existing. The central hole is the secret of the preservation of this ancient temple, through the drafts enter and exit despite the large opening rains very little inside and do not create on the walls the mold and moisture that other buildings know. Really fantastic this beautiful monument, we hope that modern man respects it as well as done by the ancients.

- Best preserved Roman building with Eight graceful granite Corinthian columns. -2,000 years old structured dome remains the world’s largest unreinforced concrete dome. Must visit place but crowded with tourists.

It was formerly a Roman temple but is now a Catholic Church, Basilica of St Mary and the Martyrs. The building is cylindrical with a portico of large granite Corinthian columns - eight in the first rank and two groups of four behind, under a pediment. A rectangular vestibule links the porch to the rotunda , which is under a coffered concrete dome, with a central opening to the sky, allowing rainfall through the ceiling and onto the floor. Thus  the interior floor is equipped with drains and has been built with an incline of about 30 cm to promote water run off. Really amazing . Circles and squares form the underlying theme of the interior design. The checkerboard floor pattern contrasts with the concentric circles  of square coffers in the dome. Enshrined on the apse above the high altar is the icon of the Virgin and child. The first niche to the right of the entrance holds Madonna of the Girdle and St Nicholas of Bari. The first Chapel on the right , the Chapel of Annunciation , has a fresco of the Annunciation.  On the left side is a canvas of St Lawrence and St Agnes. The second niche has a fresco depicting the Coronation of the Virgin. In the second Chapel is the tomb of King Victor Emmanuel II . The tomb consists of a large bronze plague surmounted by a Roman eagle and the arms of the. House of Savoy. The golden lamp above the tomb burns in honor of Victor Emmanuel II.

A visit to the Pantheon of Rome is a pilgrimage or visit in your schedule of places to visit in this beautiful city.It holds the title of the sacred place of more than 2000 thousand years, one of the oldest and best preserved in the City of Rome.Bread means: Everything (Theo) means Gods, a place that is easily accessible and that does not need reference, because it has its own brightness.Your entry is very easy, if you are in the city of Rome you only have to walk in the direction of it, you follow the entry line and that is if I recommend respecting the rules of entry , since this building is dedicated to Catholic worship and therefore must follow and abide by its already established rules.Your entry is completely free, and there is no limit time to be on site, one can enjoy the time you want or need to enjoy such a colossal structure.It has a single entrance from the Piazza de la Rotanda, on the main facade, has an inscription that says Marco Agrippa , Son of Lucius Consul for the third time did it.In this way he attributes its construction to Marco Vigzanio Agrippa. General and friend of Emperor Augustus.The third consulate of Agrippa indicates that it is the year 27, B.C. For centuries this inscription was thought to refer to the current building, but in the 19th century, it was investigated and found that the Temple of Agrippa was destroyed, and the one we see now was built in Hadrian's time, around the 2nd century, after Christ.The building is circular in shape with a portico of large Corinthian granite columns.Co a largest unarmed concrete dome in the world, with its central opening or (oculo) to the sky, 43 meters in diameter, is state-owned , visited in 2013 by 6 million people.A great cultural, architectural, faith and art experience that you cannot miss if you are in such a wonderful city.

The Pantheon, a temple in which is the best preserved Ancient Roman monument. The Pantheon, is a Greek adjective meaning “Honour all Gods” since the temple itself was first built as a temple to all Gods. The construction of The Pantheon was carried out during the reign of Hadrian, in 126 A.D. but in the 7th century it was donated to Pope Boniface IV. This donation led to The Pantheon being transformed into a church to which it still stands to this day, this can be a contributing factor as to why The Pantheon has been well preserved throughout this time. The Pantheon itself has another important function since the interior holds numerous tombs of Italian Kings as well as artworks - including Rapheal (a painter and Renaissance architect). The interior of The Pantheon is fascinating for the giant dome and the “Eye of the Pantheon” which is the hole at the top of this dome. The dome itself was the largest in the world for over 1000 years and remains the largest unsupported dome in the world. The “Eye of The Pantheon” allows natural sunlight to beam through but unfortunately allows rainwater through too, which is why you will see a slight slant and drains onto the floor. Another lighting effect, which is only viewable on April 21st, is when the midday sun strikes a metal grille located above the doorway, which in turn leads the entrance way to fill with light. In turn this illuminated the current emperor in these times, to which was perceived as their status as “God on Earth”. Many people take this stance and believed that the emperor was the same level of the Gods who had invited him to join them. Not only is the interior of The Pantheon filled with incredible visions and history but the exterior of The Pantheon is something to marvel at. The most fascinating aspect of the architecture is that the measurements from the circular aspect has the same height and diameter, the architecture was ahead of themselves in these times. The sixteen granite columns measure 14 meters in height, above these columns is a rectangular facade which encompasses the dome also has an inscription: “M.AGRIPPA.L.COS.TERTIVM. FECIT.”. The inscription when translated reads “Marcus Agrippa, son of Lucius, built this temple when he was a Consul”. Even though these letters were replaced and refurbished, it’s thought that these letters would’ve been in gilded bronze.

Easy to see since it's in one of the main piazzas. It's free, beautiful, and of historic significance, so no surprise that it is usually very crowded. Go super early in the morning to beat the crowds.

I broke my ankle on the second day of our trip to Rome and let me tell you, it is NOT a place set up well for ambulatory challenged people! I was thrilled to discover that the Pantheon had ramps for access. Both the Vatican and Pantheon are free to those in wheelchairs and one pusher. You also get to skip the lines. Traveling the streets by foot  to get there can be a tricky, as not all sidewalks have ramps to the street and most pavement in Rome is cobblestones.     From the outside you would never guess the beauty that is found inside this treasure. The artistry within and the history throughout make this a don't miss site!

Whilst on a food tour with our guide Luisa she took us on a tour of the Pantheon, wow what a site to see, Luisa gave us a quick history and left us to wander round and take it in, it is an amazing building and will leave you wondering how they built such a place thousands of years ago, loved it.
We were going to the Vatican city so made sure we went round via the Pantheon.It’s free to enter and we were offered a tour but due to lack of time before our next one we couldn’t wait for it.

A visit to the Pantheon is a must-see during your visit to Roman antiquities. A majestic work of about 2000 years ago, it has a dome that is a masterpiece of engineering. The interior is now a church, and imposes silence and recollection. There are the tombs of some Savoy, and also the tomb of Raphael: remarkable the epitaph that remembers him. It should be noted that in the past the dome was covered with bronze that was removed by the popes to make the canopy of St. Peter.
The Pantheon is a link to a very different era, well worth your time, it really took our breath away, I would recommend it as worth a visit, in one week we went twice!
The Pantheon is free to visit and enter and is another must for a trip to Rome.  Truly amazing dome and an architectural masterpiece.  Dont miss it.

Knowing the Pantheon is so ancient is pretty much all it has going for it, in my opinion.  Otherwise I’ve never found it to be that interesting of a place.  It’s cool to know that people were able to construct such a large and high dome so long ago, and it’s changed religious hands a few times, but really other than that I’ve never been that impressed.  I don’t know what it is, other people seem to think it’s great, but to me it’s a five minute walk through and check off the tourist list kind of place.

The only fully intact Roman building, built as a temple and repurposed as a church which is the reason it was not destroyed as other buildings were after The Roman Empire fell. The columns are single pieces of carved granite and the dome an engineering masterpiece. Inspiring as is the adjoining piazza. And it’s free most of the time. Contains the tomb of Raphael. The Rick Steves audio tour is free and helpful.

There is free entry to this site, which is rare in Rome for such a well known attraction. Inside is a collection of statutes, paintings and tombs. You walk around in a circular motion and, unless you stop to read, reflect or admire, you can be out in ten minutes. Surprisingly there weren't any Africans trying to sell you something there. Maybe we got lucky.

The Pantheon is a marvelous example of how structures are continuously repurposed throughout history.  The Pantheon was a former Roman temple, now a church, but on the site of an even earlier temple.  It’s a spectacular sight from both inside and outside and truly embodies its name: to honour all Gods.

I love the Pantheon, this is my second visit and I highly recommend getting there right at open, before it gets crowded. If you want a good healthy breakfast or lunch nearby, I recommend a place called Ginger Sapori!
Where in the world can you see an almost perfectly preserved building that is 2000 years old? Probably my favourite location in Rome

Wow, The best Roman building in Italy preserved in such a beautiful manner that hasn’t changed for centuries. Amazing that entrance is free yet no shortage of arrogant, disrespectful flocks of tourists. Be reminded it is a church.
I highly recommend visiting Pantheon during the day, but also in the evening. I felt it was a completely different atmosphere.
A must visit at night and during the day.  There are plenty of restaurants to sit outside and have a few drinks as you mesmerize at this wonderful structure. Best part is it's free!
We walked through here with a small group tour. What an amazing building, and still no one is exactly sure how the dome was constructed. A must see in Rome.
Beautiful architecture. Take a moment to experience this amazing and beautiful building. No charge and line goes quickly.
An important landmark of ancient Rome, this was located in a piazza near many other great sites a tourist would want to see. Beautiful!
It was very crowded on the day we visited this site for the third time. Went in and came back out for a quick stop. Still impressive and still gorgeous but crowds just don't appeal to me.
I visited this place both in the day and night, it is a must see. Such a beautiful roman building and entrance is free.

A very impressive and well preserved Roman building. The entrance is free and you can pay to get explanations inside (although I recommend to just search it before). It has a lot of history and was built a lot of time ago, and yet, it’s spotless.
You’ll have to visit in not a hurry and you’ll discover the beauty of this placeIt’s wise to learn about with the kiosk witn phonesIt’s better to look around eith daylight
I did not realise that it was free to enter, which was a very nice surprise. I recommend downloading an audio guide for this one.
This a stunning monument and it stands in all its glory, it's free to get in so take a seat and just look around and see all the amazing things, well worth a visit
Very amazing architecture and history.  This is one of our favorite places in Rome to visit. Don't miss it.

The Pantheon is on a small square, and there are touristy spots and crowded roadways leading up to it, but this site is an unforgettable must-see!  Take care to take note of the special drainage system in the floor.  We did not get a guide, which was still fine.  We read about the history and then spent our time inside taking in the views.  No tickets required.  Absolutely breathtaking.  Do not leave Rome without seeing this!
Can’t say anymore than absolutely breathtaking!! One of the finest buildings I have visited in the world .

Pantheon is Free so certainly worth the stop. It is fully preserved, has multiple alters inside the building and seating areas for prayer.  They offer a Headset tour for small fee we did not purchase as I had guide book of Rome.  The Dome is amazing; if you read about the building of this before your visit you will have much more appreciation.  The statues, plaques and pictures are not all religious; great history if that interests you.
In a city with incredible history and amazing buildings around every corner, this was the place that took my breath away.  It was awesome!
A must see while you are in Rome. It is a beautiful building and it is free to enter. The inside is not what you would expect from how the outside looks.
Impressive lookin. Quick Easy and FreeDont agree to the random people offering tours for 10€ it wont be worth it. It's a very quick visit.

We visited in early Jan and there were fewer crowds which made visiting the pantheon easier, as soon as we laid our eyes on the pantheon after emerging from one of Rome’s many side streets we were mesmerised! The iconic pillars and huge bronze doors. We hurried inside to get a look at the famous hole in the ceiling. I was shocked to see so much history actually inside the pantheon, we enjoyed it that much that whenever we were passing we quickly poked our heads through the doors for another look
This is a must see when in Rome the history of this place it's self is amazing and free entry lovely fountain monument outside for nice picturs

Was just there last week and it was nice, not too busy. Very cool historic spot to go by if you have the chance while in Rome. This building was finished around 100AD and looks great today. Look in the the background on the places you visit for nice perspective.
A building worth visiting. It is well preserved and worth a visit. It's a good photo opportunity to take some amazing photos with such an ancient building.

This is a large historic building, but you can see everything inside in about 30 minutes. The biggest draw for me was the architecture of the building and dome. Also that Raphael is buried here. I spent as much time outside the building as I did inside. It's worth stopping by on your way to something else.

We visited this in the evening. Takes about 15 mins or so to take in the building. It was free entrance. The dome is massive and is the largest non reinforced concrete dome in the world. There are holes on the floor for water that may come through the opening in the dome. Must better preserved than the Pantheon in Greece.
A must see in my opinion ceiling is amazing in itself it's free to get in here which is rare in rome.
Lovely church in a back street piazza. Very old and interesting. Got to be on your list of sites when going to Rome.
It’s quite the display of where both ancient and current pagans, historians, and those just generally curious can see how we saw the world a millennia ago - and still do.

No trip to Rome is complete without a brief visit to this site.Visited Rome over a decade ago when family were much younger; they curtail the enjoyed returning as young adults to soak up the rich history for themselves.
Such an impressive building, there is a beautiful fountain in front. Could not enter as they were performing mass.

It's amazing to think that this building has been around for 2,000 years. We were told that it's the largest unsupported cement ceiling in the world and that we don't have the technology today to recreate it. Fascinating!
This beautiful building, first a pagan temple, then a church and now a tourist site is as beautiful as it is massive.  It is famous for its skylight--built open by the Romans so that it did not leak.
Two thousand years of history, and a link between heaven and earth embodied in this building. Just look up, the vision is almost futuristic.

The dome and the Roman columns are impressive & visible from far. Entrance is free. There were long queue to get in but it moved fast. I was a bit disappointed with the interior as I was expecting very impressive interior but there were nothing much to see. Perhaps because it was converted into a church so it’s kind of strange with some christianity stuffs trying hard to fit in a Roman temple.
Si consiglia vivamente di arrivare la mattina prestoIt is highly recommended to arrive early in the morning

What an amazing temple. Free entrance all around the day. Huge temple with a lot of sculptures and am an amazing history. Highlight is the hole at the center of the roof letting the sun and of course the rain right in.
Beautiful experience. This monument monument to a great artistic and cultural wealth inside

I lived in Rome 8 years ago and made several visits to the Pantheon.  It is such a beautiful building, perfect in its dimensions and decorated inharmony with its size.  Although there were often lots of people it never felt overcrowded  A couple of times we wanted to go there on our way back from exploring other parts of this endlessly fascinating city but it was closed.  Wise to check opening times if you are on a quick visit to Rome.  Well worth the visit and spending some time there.  The When did you visit? date below is untrue as there were no options for the actual dates and I could not proceed without clicking something.  Sorry!  I hate untruth.

We walked past a couple of times and thought the Pantheon was pretty impressive. Someone suggest we go inside. We went at 9AM one morning when Romans and tourists were still having brekkie. It was unreal. The interior was unspoilt and very impressive and there were probably only 20 people inside and it was FREE!!

Just off the busy main street, leads into a square with cafes, shops etc, and is really impressive. Just sit inside and read about it, it is so steeped in history, you get a real feel of the true Rome. Hole in the roof allows rain and snow to enter, amazing marble interior with statues, mosaics and religious artifacts. Entrance is free as in all churches in Rome. This is a must see.
An amazing historical structure that I would definitely recommend seeing. And it's free to enter. And even if there's a line, it moves very quickly.

The Pantheon was a great spot for Midnight Mass on Christmas Eve!  The dimly lit interior set the tone for a personal service and the candle lit alter was the perfect stage for this occasion.A bit chilly with the doors and the oculus open, but didn't mind.Got there just after 11pm to get in line, and we were about 50th spot to get a seat.  There were a lot of spectators so the crowded interior thinned out after Mass started.
True masterpeace and it's free entrance. But be informed well about the opening hours - closed on festive days and for some event. Don't miss Raphaell's tomb just on the left after the entrance!
Amazing to see this ancient dome and contents in person. You can see most of it in 30 minutes. Awestruck knowing the events that took place here.
I have been to the Pantheon several times and just enjoy sitting taking in the unique beauty and history of it all.  Really pretty in the evening as well when everything lit up.

The hole in the roof of the Pantheon was one of the highlights, being that it was the only source of light to the interiors. When we were there, it was raining and water fell right through. It is a beautiful structure and perhaps can be made better with the audio guide. If you were visiting it for pictures, then just 10 minutes is sufficient

Another amazing sight in Rome.  It's amazing this structure has survived 2000 years.  Entry is free and we recommend you go as early as possible as it fills up with tourists as the day goes on.  An early visit allows you to get outside photos without hoards of people filling your pictures.
Free Fantastic not words that you generally associate with Rome particularly free but this is an amazing experience more than a building it’s a must see if you are in Rome
It's worth to visit the Pantheon, its very impressive. You can find al relevant information in Google.
It is good place to visit with many restaurants nearby. Daytime and nighttime are different view feeling

We went here during our trip to Rome in December because of the brilliant recommendations we've gotten but were quite underwhelmed by the Pantheon itself. It is definitely worth going to but more to be able to say you've seen it rather than to be overly impressed.

Seeing the Pantheon after reading so much about it was definitely worth it. I passed by it several times deliberately in my two week holiday in Rome, surely a must see as is everything else in Italy. But, WOW.

Beautiful symmetry in the architecture.  The stone is magnificent.  We had been told that hearing Mass at the Pantheon was special.  So as we entered, the tours and general public were still milling around but 15 minutes before Mass started, the guards began to inform the tourists that if you were not going to attend, you would have to leave.  Within 5 minutes, we were in the midst of less than 100 people in this magnificent structure.  Truly an experience!
The Pantheon is just amazing. We were awestruck from the moment we entered the building. This is a must visit when in Rome, not to be missed.
Huge buildingIn front of the impressive queue to get home but we don't stay long in because far too many people It's a Roma factory you pay you visit and you go home
A lot of history is there. It’s in a central position between other monuments. By night is even better and it’s free.

The Pantheon was a short walk from our hotel and we took a short detour to spend and hour or so to visit. It’s free entry, but unsurprisingly like most attractions quite busy! Inside it’s not that large. There’s a self guided tour (headsets available for a small charge) which provides some background on the exhibits.

The Pantheon stood unblemished through the ages - built in 118 AD - on the Piazza della Rotonda. There is an awe inspiring feeling when you walk inside. Don't miss Raphael's tomb. Everything so well preserved, even after several years.

One should be amazed to see the dome with open at center and where the interior is illuminated with natural sunlight. Oldest building and admire the bronze door and ceiling... locals visited 2000 years before and now you are also visiting.

Having missed out on being able to be inside the Pantheon a number of years ago, this was a must see, and we weren’t disappointed. From the incredible dome, to the amazing space and light, a truly remarkable place...
Amazing that this building is almost 2,000 years old. The Church has done a wonderful job of keeping it.

So underestimated—this was one of our favourite places (mostly thanks to our tour guide—business card below).  He truly was fabulous.  Very passionate about the Pantheon but also very knowledgeable in general, very funny and even kids loved it. My four year old asked me if we could get him over for a “play date” sometime, which shows you how well he can engage kids!!Definitely worth the 25 euro despite the entrance being free otherwise and he will also take you on a lovely walk and tell you so much about Rome.

The Pantheon is magnificent but the lines and crowds are ridiculous. There are far too many street vendors selling cheap rubbish. There are also scammers trying to slap “friendship bracelets” on your wrist. Although we enjoyed the Pantheon, the whole experience is overshadowed by crowds, dirt and vendors selling garbage.
Unfortunately, I wasn't able to go inside, because the line was long. It's a wonderful building, I bet everyone knows this from their History book.

I will never get sick of stopping for a look at the pantheon. It is one of the most awe-inspiring buildings I've ever been inside of. being almost 2,000 years old, it's incredible to think about how it was constructed in those times. Truly magnificent.
Incredible structure, ceiling dome, and marble floors. Well worth using the informative audio guide. An engineering marvel, especially since it was constructed over the 118 to 125 period.

I did not expect such a wonderful structure. We knew we were close but we stumbled across one of the oldest structures in Rome just by accident and we were mesmerized. It remains my favourite overall in Rome. The inside takes your breath away 😌

I just loved Pantheon. It is so amazing to listen to its history and admire it from a distance as well as admire it from inside. It continues to be one of my favourite places in Rome (more than Colosseum)
It was sunday morning and cold.I saw the assets of ancient people.Wow!! Great!! Awesome!!I couldn't help taking pictures.

The dome of the building was extremely high, but there was not much depth from the door to pulpit, but as with most older European churches, it still had quite ornate decor. Our guide stayed that the dome atop the Pantheon was the model for other domes on buildings in Rome. There are a handful of pews and chairs to sit and take in the beauty inside. However, it was still a bit chilly inside, as the doors are open and the dome is open on top.
If you can't make it to the Vatican, this was a great second choice for us.  Very spiritual experience to be in this building celebrating Christmas Eve mass
This site is centrally located, free, and easy to get and out of. If you are in the area then go see it. Even if you have 15 minutes... one of the easiest and most interesting sites to see.
I never get tired of visiting this site. Such grace and architectural design. Just go and marvel check opening and closing hours so you won’t be disappointed.
This place is amazing. It looks like it was built last year, stunning architecture.Try a coffee in the square, sit outside and soak up the atmosphere.
There is always something new to discover that the eye has missed from the last visit.  It’s more like a museum than a church, but awesome.
The Pantheon is a nice place to see on your way to a museum or any other place, its free and marvalus.
Beautiful on the inside and outside, surprising to understand it's history and it's standing after thousands of years.
This place is worth a visit while in Rome.  It has big dome. Though everything is nearby in Rome.  This place is near to all historic places in rome...

It may look old and unattractive from the outside but inside is so beautiful. Apparently Michaelangelo liked this architecture so much he also made a dome with a hole on top at St Peter's Basilica and, out of respect, made the hole a metre wider. There is so much rich history so getting a guide to explain or an audio guide will make you appreciate this Roman temple even more. The lines were actually not very long and moved fairly quickly. I was there in mid-December by the way. However, masses or other functions may be conducted inside and visitors will not be allowed to come in. May want to check the visiting times on Google.

Really enjoyed my recent trip to the Pantheon.  A guided tour gave us much more information than we would have got looking at it and we were able to go straight to the front of the line.  An amazing building and history. A must for any visit to Rome
Truly an amazing monument! You must visit this place! Lots of people, but well organized flow of the queue.

Do not miss the interior of the Pantheon, even more amazing than the exterior.  The quality of Italian Marble is impressive.  Most impressive, is the massive open roof dome (rains inside), impressed by many including Thomas Jefferson who used the design for a UVA building, the capitol, and his private residence.  Watching the shadows from the sun on the interior dome is amazing, some of the shadows look like skulls.  The Pantheon is considered a working church, so the entry is free, and the line is fast.  Do not miss this site, and the narrow streets surrounding the Pantheon with food and shopping options.
In a beautiful  city of monuments  and architecture this place disappointed  a little. Ready for a bit of TLC but never  the  less do pass by
Visited the Pantheon, self guided tour, great architecture, short walk down beautiful streets from all site seeing points of interest
Great to see well worth looking inside. Also this is one attraction in Rome that is free. Lovely at night as well.
Like elsewhere in Rome crowds can be a downer. We visited mid morning and found we were able to walk straight in avoiding the queue which can form at different times throughout the day.
Like stepping back in time. The outer view hides the incredible interior. View the amazing engineering in the structure of the Pantheon.
This is one of the easier monuments you can visit when in Rome, there is no entrance fee and you basically can walk in and out as you please.

Very cool place - first a sanctuary for gods, later a church. Has a small hole in the ceiling which is interesting both when sunny and raining, inside you will find a few pieces of interesting art.Beware of the surrounding cafes as they are far overpriced

Pantheon is one of top 5 historical attractions of Rome specially if you like to watch historical movies. The atmosphere is different and you may feel the energy of the place which for sure comes from lots of decisions which took here and we still following some of them today
You must go and see. Worth visit if you are in Rome. Don’t need pay anything. It’s huge and stunning!
I love this historical building - started as pagan  - now Christian - architectural wonder - pretty at Christmas - nice guides around

Wow! This building is absolutely beautiful. You can walk in free of charge, and just bask and enjoy this ancient building. It really is breathtaking. Also, girlfriend and I got lost at night around 1am and stumbled in front of the Pantheon, no crowds or people at all. That late at night no one is there and it’s eerily beautiful. Cheers!
Really worth while, we visited it doing a BAJA bike tour and had the best guide in town: Sarah from Belgium.  It was fun and really interesting to see Rome on a bike with a great guide. Do it!
The dome was magnificent. It allows light and water through all year around. This is a very interesting ancient building in top form.
In Rome, which is chock full of historical buildings, this was one of the best. Continually in use for 2000 yrs, great architecture. Very close to all the attractions. a must visit place.
The Pantheon was one of my favorites places in Rome. It is one of the best preserved buildings from Ancient Rome, you are not visiting ruins from the past as the building is still in use.
Take a walk around and visit the inside.  A history lesson awaits you.  We went in the winter so there were only a few panhandlers.

After seeing so many ruins today at the Coluseum, it is hard to believe this is almost 2000 years old. It doesn’t have the beautiful ceiling art of other churches but still has stunning architecture and is worth seeing.
Was part of a walking tour and learned a lot of interesting facts. The piazza was crowded in front of the building

Fabulous building and awesome to think it was built so long ago. Free to go in, but watch out for the crowds and the pickpockets in high season
I can’t imagine traveling to Rome and not seeing the Pantheon. We stopped in - it’s free - and walked around for 15-20 minutes. 2000 years old and now a church. Beautiful.

A great building with great paintings inside and free to enter! The inside is spacious and there you find the tomb of the 1st king of Italy. It's position is great and there are many restaurants around so you can combine a full walk and lunch!
Built 2000+ years ago, it is still in perfect condition. Try visiting it early in the morning to avoid the huge crowds.
Mostly kidding. Who am I to review one of the most significant buildings in Western history? Beautiful. 20 minutes is enough. Put down the camera and TAKE IT ALL IN, as in LOOK!

One of the oldest buildings of Rome used for many reasons throughout history, no lines, free, nice restaurants nearby

This is my second time to visit the pantheon.. I Love it still.. from my first to my second visit..sitting on the pews and just Looking up at the ceiling and the occulus. Easy to find, no entrance fees. This can actually be grouped together in one day with the Spanish steps, Trevi fountain, Piazza Navona,il Corso.
Stunning on both the outside and inside! One of the most beautiful must-see architectural treasures in Rome.
Amazing!!! Stay away when busy times. Breath taking! A definite must see!!Rome has so so much but this is it!!!

One of the best examples of Ancient Roman monuments. Helps to give an idea of how the Colosseum etc looked at their best. Didn't do an audio guide but should have for a bit of extra information. We went on Wednesday & entrance was free. Worth checking on website (tourist info) as  italy often has free entry to places of interest. They get booked up very quickly

On Sunday they close the church [ yes it is a church] at about 1000 hrs and mass starts at 1030.  If you go in the winter months bundle up as there is no heat in the building[ see the 29' hole in the roof] After mass you get to see the tombs and altars w/o the maddening crowd at lease until they open the flood gates and let the crowd in!

The Pantheon is a mesmerizing architectural marvel structure in the heart of Rome that was dedicated around 126 AD by the emperor Hadrian. The Pantheon is one of the best preserved structures from the ancient world mainly due to continued use of the building since dedication. At present and since the seventh century, the building is a church dedicated to Saint Mary and the Martyrs (Sancta Maria ad Martyres). The dome is still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome. An absolute must see on an adventure to Rome.

It is a place of wonders. You can get in for free and just look around and marvel at what you see, get a guide of hire a headset. Whatever you do there is a chance you will step into the timeless mystery of the Pantheon, look up and see the sky and think about all who have been there before you.

This building is one of the best preserved ancient monuments in the world, and it still has an important function and status today as within it are the tombs of the Italian monarchy from 1870-1946 CE and another notable tomb is that of Raphael (1483-1520 CE). ... I copied that, but it says it all.  An architectural and engineering masterpiece.  Hadn't realized how enormous the oculus in the ceiling was; and it is amazing that with such a large gaping hole in the ceiling that the interior is so perfectly preserved. Still categorized as a church so it is free to visit.  Definitely see this beautiful piece of history.
If you go to Rome - you have to see this site.  It is spectacular.  The engineering of the building alone is a marvel.
You must visit the Pantheon, which also holds Raphael’s tomb. This interior of the church is amazing. We were awed by the oculus in the center of the giant dome which provided light to the church.
Amazing building. Just to sit and look around and marvel at the people who designed and built this remarkable building. It is magnificent and worth a visit.

An architectural feat and free.  You can spend as much time in here as you wish, however it is recommended that you observe silence, and if you don't then an official will remind you as occurred with a large group who were quite talkative when we were there.

There are just a handful of places in the world where you'll feel calm even when you are surrounded by hordes of people. The pantheon is one such place. Forget everything else and take a moment ot appreciate God, nature and man's resilience when yo uare here.
Amazingly beautiful structure ! Free entry ! Was not much crowded, spend less than one hour in side.
The Pantheon is a must see while in Rome, Italy. The good news is that it is one of the few sites that you will Most likely have more expeditious access to.
This place is a must see while touring Rome. It’s free to get inside and the architecture is gorgeous. Small souvenir shop inside with fantastic prices.

Amazing structure, and built from concrete....It's said rain doesn't fall in through the open roof, but it does, and if you are lucky enough to be in Rome when it snows, as I have been albeit not this year, the sight of snowflakes falling and swirling into the building is magical!
One of the sites you can visit for free in the centre of Rome, you can purchase an audio guide but don’t think necessary as descriptions of everything written in English. Thoroughly enjoyed our visit
Amazing place in the heart of the city. It was a Temple and lately a Roman Church. Pantheon is worth a visit for its history.

So walked past one night and it was closed. Ended up closing a tour here and it was impressive. Not overly crowded and easy to appreciate the place. Interesting as our tour guide was so on top of things able to point out fine details and even the construction process. Off season from everything we heard is the time to see Rome.

Very busy, great location with plenty of restaurants nearby. Since its a free attraction expect large crowds, close to Trevi fountain and Spanish steps.

This I would say is not a visit only for Christians given the aspects of the construction, the dome, the central hole and the indication of the seasons of the year. Very interesting visit. He drew my attention to his destination: "All the gods" and then the need to be taken over by the Holy See. Worth a visit!!

Maybe the most famous church of Rome, nobody can skip it. It is a constructial wonder with the lack on the roof. You can see the tomb of Raffaello Santi, one of the most famous artists of Italy. The church is monument. Do not worry if you see a lot of people in front of it, inside there is much bigger place. There is no entrance fee so once you are on Rome, go and visit this wonder.

Pantheon Rome :- Even today, almost 2000 years after its construction, the breathtaking pantheon is a remarkable building to see. The spectacular design, proportions, elegance and harmony are a striking reminder of the architecture of the great Roman Empire. When Michelangelo saw this wonder for the first time he said that it looks more like the work of angels, not humans.The word Pantheon is a Greek adjective meaning “honor all Gods”. In fact the pantheon was first built as a temple to all gods. The Pantheon as we know it today was built in 120 AD by Emperor Hadrian  It is the best preserved Ancient Roman   monument. The most wonderful part of the Pantheon is its giant dome, with its famous hole in the top (The eye of the Pantheon, or oculus). The dome was the largest in the world for 1300 years and until today it remains the largest unsupported dome in the world! You will never forget the Rome, if you visit this place.

This structure, while converted to a catholic church around 608 AD, was originally a Roman temple dedicated to the Gods of Rome. It is one of the truly monumental spaces ever built and the oldest continually occupied building in the western world. It has a pleasing proportion to the space as the height of the dome is equal to the diameter of the space. This structure was erected around 128 AD by the emperor Hadrian after the previous temple (built by Agrippa) had been destroyed by fire. The inscription over the door still reads in Latin that it was built by Agrippa, which some describe to a very magnanimous gesture by Hadrian. The dome is amazing, using a variety of different materials and engineering techniques it was the largest dome in the world until the 1400's when the cathedral in Florence was constructed and it still the largest reinforced concrete dome anywhere. The oculus (hole in the center of the roof) was one of the methods that the Romans used to lighten the dome as well as provided amazing light into the space (when it rains they rope off that section of the floor since it does get wet). Free to get in just remember to dress appropriately as it is still officially a church though I'm not sure that masses are still held there.

This place is beautiful. Inside has some exhibits of old artifacts, very interesting. It's free entry. I went there in the end of november and was drizzling all day.

The Pantheon is spectacular. It has enjoyed many careers over the century and enjoys beautifully preserved architecture. Open long hours and accessible outside by night it is not to be missed. There's lots of good shopping around this precinct too, as well as some nice restaurants. It's also only 450m from the Trevi fountain so it's easy to combine the two!

I love this building. have been to Rome five times and still love to come there and just take it all in. The Pantheon is a marvel of engineering. Although built around AD124, it still holds the record as the largest unreinforced concrete dome in the world. The interior is all open below the dome (except for some small side chapels), and its "openness" belies how incredibly large this structure is the oculus (the opening to the air at the top of the dome) is thirty feet across, yet when you are down below it does not seem so large. The wonderful proportions of this building help give it an intimacy not found in similar-sized buildings. But more than the architecture, the Pantheon contains the tomb of Raphael and two of Italy's kings. The Pantheon was dedicated as a church in the 7th century (and features some side chapels). This helped it retain much of its original finishes and columns, unlike many of the Roman buildings which were looted for their marble, columns, etc. The Pantheon should be on everyone's list when coming to Rome.
The outside and inside of the pantheon is just amazing. The detail that has gone into it was just unreal. Definitely worth the time to visit if you can.
We arrived here at twilight so it was hard to see all the sights but this is a beautiful place and one we would like to return to.  Even better...no lines and it was free!
Really we only did one coin each. It was a beautiful place.  The place had huge crowds.  We were there both days and night.  It is especially great at night.
This is one of the most wonderful architectural sights in Italy. There is nothing like it. If in Rome it is an absolute must, and you don’t have to stay long to appreciate it, and it’s free!!

Not to be missed when you visit Rome. Free entry here. To step into a two thousand year old Roman temple so well preserved is very special.
still standing and in use. Marvelous dome with open air oculus. Truly an engineering marvel and inspirational space.

The Pantheon, among many other building, was on our bucket list to see. The incredible engineering gives us the most preserved building of ancient Rome.  Built between 113-125AD, it is awe inspiring as you enter and look up to the dome. It is the biggest brick dome in architecture history.

Simply one of the most magnificent monuments of Rome which has stood for almost 2 Millennia. The Pantheon was the blueprint for all other domed churches and is an architectural marvel, especially considering the dome is non reinforced concrete. The best part is that it is free to visit and the queue to get in moves quite quickly!

Enter through the 2 enormous doors and you will immediately be astounded.  If you have the time, buy the audio tour.  It's just one large room so you will not be overwhelmed here. First look upwards and spend some time viewing the incredible ceiling.   It is an engineering marvel that they would be able to construct something like that in 26 AD (give or take a few years).   Then walk around and view all of the history inside this temple (now a church).  The square outside has some great restaurants, as well.  Recommended.

It's difficult to think of anything to say about the Pantheon that has not already been said. It is important to remember that this ancient temple was converted into a Catholic church, and remains so today. There is the possibility that Mass is being said inside and there is a permanent altar and seating area for this purpose. Overall, the ancient interior scheme is fairly well preserved, though the pagan statues have been removed (along with the bronze covering from the outside of the dome). When you go, marvel at the fact that this structure remains the largest unreinforced concrete dome in the world. Why is this impressive? This building from the Hadrianic era (the previous version built by Marcus Agrippa burned, so don't be fooled by the pediment inscription) stands intact due to expert engineering that we cannot easily achieve today! Whilst you are in the Pantheon, you can visit the tombs of Raphael (yes, THE Raphael as in of the School of Athens, etc), as well as Italian kings Vittorio Emanuele II and Umberto I.
A beautiful structure on the outside; breathtaking beautiful on the inside --- did not realize that it is now a church.
Worth seeing would go again. Some very interesting artefacts, statues and roof to pursues over. The marble floors can be sloppy so take care.

The Pantheon is a church in the centre of Rome that was formerly a Roman temple that was thought to have been the private temple of Marcus Agrippa, the son-in-law of Augusta. The building is a chapel in the shape of a rotunda, but with a front entrance built in the form of a Neoclassical Portico. The interior is an enormous rounded room covered by a giant dome, with niches around the room for chapels.

One of the best-preserved Roman buildings, (it is very old). The Pantheon was built in 126 AD as a temple for all the Roman gods. Can you believe it, the temple has served as a Roman Catholic Church since the 7th century. Columns in back, considered to be 2,000 years old.Fact: (I love this part) - the Pantheon’s famous dome remains the world’s largest unreinforced concrete dome. The supreme achievement of Roman interior architecture hangs suspended without visible supports - these are well hidden inside the walls - and its nine-meter central opening is the building's only light source.Famous painter Raphael, as well as Italian kings Victor Emmanuel II and Umberto I are all buried there. Don’t forget to check their tomb. There is Dress code, cover your shoulders, shorts are not allowed. Also, check the timings before goingIMP: IT’S FREE..!! Originally built as a temple dedicated to all gods by Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa in 27 BC and then rebuilt by Hadrian, the Pantheon is a masterpiece of architecture and engineering. The imposing pronaos, with its sixteen monolithic Corinthian columns of grey and pink granite, overlooks the square directly, creating a spectacular visual impact. The distance from the floor to the top of the dome is identical to the diameter: practically the interior space consists of a perfect sphere. The vault is decorated with 5 orders of drawers and has in the center the bronze-edged eye, the only source of natural light in the building. The marble floor, slightly convex and with an almost invisible slope, conveys water (in case of rain) to 22 hidden drainage holes, preventing the formation of puddles. The niches are overflowing with works of art, including the fresco of the Annunciation by Melozzo da Forlì, the tomb of the sovereigns Vittorio Emanuele II and Umberto I, the tomb of Raphael Sanzio (obtained from an ancient Greek marble sarcophagus and surmounted by the statue of the Madonna del Sasso by Lorenzetto). Leaning against the back of the “Rotunda” are, instead, the conspicuous remains of the Basilica Neptuni (raised by Agrippa, at the same time as the Pantheon, but later rebuilt by Hadrian. Wonderful!!

Entombed inside are the Renaissance master Raphael and the first kings of the united Italy, Victor Emmanuel II and Umberto I. The star of the show is the incredible cupola with its occulus, still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome. To visit Rome and not see it would be a crime of omission.

It is one of the best preserved ancient Roman buildings in the world and a masterpiece in the history of human architecture. Its name comes from the Greek meaning place dedicated to all gods. Known for its huge domes and magnificent porticoes, the building remains one of Rome's most popular tourist destinations.HistoryInitial construction (27 BC)Originally built under the auspices of Octavian's son-in-law, consul Marcus Agrippa, the Pantheon was dedicated to the Roman gods around 27 BC. Above the main entrance, there is still an inscription “M.AGRIPPA·L.F.COS·TERTIUM·FECIT” (which was built during Agrippa's three reigns), which has long led scholars to believe that the existing building was built by Agrippa.Fire and reconstructionAgrippa's original building was destroyed in a fire in 80 AD, which was repaired by Emperor Tumisan, but soon burned by lightning.Extant buildings (c. 118–125 AD)The existing Pantheon was rebuilt by order of Emperor Hadrian and completed around 125 AD. Hadrian kept the original inscription without signing himself out of respect for Agrippa, a practice that was rare in ancient times.Medieval: changed to churchIn 609 AD, Byzantine Emperor Phocas gave the Pantheon to Pope Boniface IV, who converted it into a Christian church dedicated to the Virgin Mary and the martyrs. It was this “conversion” that allowed the building to survive the massive demolition and destruction of pagan temples in the Middle Ages.Renaissance to modern timesThe Pantheon became an important royal mausoleum, where Vittorio Emmanuel II, King of the Italian Reunification, and his son Umberto I are buried. The famous painter Raphael also slept there (1520).Architectural featuresThe Pantheon's dome is 43.3 metres in diameter and indoor net height, and is perfectly proportioned - if a complete sphere is placed under the dome, it can reach the ground and the dome. There is a circular sky-eye about 8.9 meters in diameter in the center of the dome; it is the only source of light indoors and serves as a vent. Rainwater falls from the eye of the sky to the ground, channelled by an elaborate drainage system.The dome remained the largest reinforced concrete-free dome in the world for more than 1,300 years, until it was surpassed during the Renaissance (Cathedral of Our Lady of the Flowers, Florence, 1436).AnecdotesRaphael's last wish: Raphael, who wished to be buried in the Pantheon, is said to have said with affection: "As long as I rest here after I die, I have no regrets in this life. “His epitaph was written by Cardinal Peterbo Bembo and reads in Latin to the effect that: “The natural fear of living was transcended by him, and when he died, he feared his own demise.”Copper was misappropriated: in the 17th century, Pope Urban VIII ordered the removal of bronze materials from the Pantheon portico for the construction of the Huagai (Bernini design) of St. Peter's Basilica and the canons of the Vatican Castle. The move drew strong criticism, with the Romans mocking: “The Barbarynis (i.e. the barbarians) did what even the barbarians did.”The magical light effect of the heavenly eye: every year at noon on April 21 (the anniversary of Rome's founding), sunlight shines directly from the heavenly eye on the entrance gate, illuminating the porch and creating spectacular columns of light, which many scholars believe Hadrian designed on purpose.Concrete Mystery: The ash concrete formula used by the ancient Romans was lost in the Middle Ages. The study found that the concrete is not only extremely strong, but even strengthens over time - a property that stuns modern engineers when it comes into contact with seawater to create new mineral crystals.The Pantheon spans nearly 2,000 years, from pagan temples to Christian churches, from royal mausoleums to tourist resorts, and is almost the best symbol of the vitality of Roman civilization.

The Pantheon is one of Rome's most extraordinary and impressive buildings, both from the outside and inside. From the outside it exudes peace and strength with its huge columns and classic facade, but as soon as you step inside you are truly overwhelmed.The interior is phenomenal: the huge dome is perfectly proportioned and still feels modern, despite the building being almost 2,000 years old. The oculus – the open opening in the middle of the dome – creates a magical play of light that changes throughout the day. When it rains, the water simply falls in and is cleverly drained through small holes in the floor, a technical masterpiece from Roman times.Fun facts:The Pantheon is the best preserved antique building in Rome.The dome was the largest in the world for over 1,300 years.The famous painter Raphael is buried here.The building is still used as a church.Tips:Go early in the morning or later in the afternoon for less crowds.Take a moment to stop and look up – the effect of the dome is truly unique.

Creative aspects of the design of the Pantheon include:* Concrete Innovation: The Pantheon's dome is the largest unreinforced concrete dome ever built. The Romans developed high-quality concrete and used it to create the dome, arches, and vaults. * Differential Concrete Mix: The concrete mix wasn't uniform throughout the dome. Heavier materials like brick and tufa were used at the base, while lighter materials like pumice were incorporated above. * The Oculus: The oculus, a circular opening at the top of the dome, reduced the overall weight and provided a source of natural light. This also allowed for a unique lighting effect on certain days of the year, with the sun illuminating the interior in a dramatic way. * Step-Rings: Step-rings were incorporated into the construction, which helped to distribute the weight of the dome and counteract hoop stressSome prominent examples of buildings that are considered copies or are heavily influenced by the Pantheon's construction include: * The Jefferson Memorial in Washington, D.C., designed by John Russell Pope, is a notable example, often considered a direct copy of the Pantheon's dome and portico.* The University of Virginia Rotunda, designed by Thomas Jefferson, is a direct homage to the Pantheon and served as the university's library.* The U.S. Capitol Building in Washington, D.C. incorporates a massive dome and a grand entrance influenced by the Pantheon's design.

The Pantheon oculus is a circular opening at the top of the Pantheon's dome in Rome that serves as the building's sole source of natural light. This 25-foot-wide aperture, meaning "eye" in Latin, is an architectural marvel that acts as a giant sundial, casting shifting beams of sunlight inside the rotunda. The oculus also allows for ventilation, lets rain in, and its design helps distribute the dome's weight.

Visiting the Pantheon in Rome felt like stepping into a space where time refuses to move forward. I had seen countless photographs of the building before arriving, but nothing compared to standing in front of it on a warm afternoon with the noise of the city swirling around its ancient columns. The moment I walked through the massive bronze doors, the sound softened and the atmosphere changed. Light poured through the oculus at the top of the dome, forming a bright circle on the marble floor that slowly shifted as the sun moved. Watching that gentle movement made the entire structure feel alive.The interior felt both vast and intimate. Even with other visitors standing around me, the space had a quiet presence that encouraged you to slow down and absorb every detail. The marble walls, the intricate chapels, and the perfect symmetry of the dome created a sense of balance that is hard to describe until you are inside it. I found myself looking up repeatedly, drawn to the way the light shaped the dome’s coffers and how the sky seemed close enough to reach. At one point a bird flew across the opening, and its silhouette against the sunlight became one of those unexpected moments that stay with you long after you leave.What struck me the most was how the Pantheon is still used as a living building. It is not just an ancient monument but also a church, so the quiet hum of devotion mixes with the curious energy of travelers. I liked lingering near the tombs of Italian kings and listening to a local guide explain how the structure has remained standing for nearly two thousand years without losing its harmony. There was something reassuring in that longevity, as if the building itself were asserting that beauty and purpose can endure through centuries of change.Stepping back outside felt like emerging from another world. The plaza was lively, filled with people taking photos or sitting on the edge of the fountain while musicians played nearby. The contrast between the calm of the interior and the buzz of the square made me appreciate the experience even more. The Pantheon left me with a sense of awe that was both peaceful and energizing, and it became one of those rare places where history feels immediate and personal.

The dome of the Pantheon isn't even the largest dome in Rome. But the building that was constructed in 27-25 BC is the only ancient Roman building that has remained practically intact through the centuries and is described as "the most beautiful relic of ancient Rome" and an"unrivaled artifact of Roman antiquity." Built on the site of an earlier temple by Marcus Agrippa during the reign of Augustus to honor their military victory at Actium in 31 BC, the present building is a result of a radical reconstruction by the Emperor Hadrian between 118 and 125 AD. It includes a Greek-style portico with 40-foot-tall Corinthian columns made of Egyptian granite, an enormous portal with bronze doors, walls made of brick-faced concrete and a rotunda with statues of Roman emperors occupying niches in the walls The open window at the top of the dome was the interior's only source of direct light. The dome is an architectural masterpiece, a true stroke of genius by Hadrian. It is almost a perfect sphere because it's height is equal to its diameter, 43 meters and 44 centimeters by 43 meters and 44 centimeters. Go figure. The dome is made entirely of concrete that doesn't have a frame to stand on. To build it, Hadrian invented a grandiose solution because he had to built it in such a way that it became lighter as it rose in height, thus reducing the thickness of the wall, which goes from six meters at the base to two meters near the central hole. Lighter materials were mixed with the concrete from travertine marble at the base to pumice stone at the top. And so it stands today.

The Pantheon is a must see in Rome.  We visited on our first day in the city after walking from our hotel to Piazza Navona and the Trevi Fountain.   It was a hot July day but we did not wait long in line to purchase the 5 euro entry ticket.  We did not bother with the timed ticket because it was afternoon and the lines were short.  The building is a marvel of engineering.  How could it withstand the elements and still be standing?   Built almost two thousand years ago, it has been a temple to pagan Gods and is currently a church.  In previous visits (this was my third) you could stand in the center of the building directly under the round opening in the ceiling.  Today there is a large sculpture in that space.  Visit to appreciate the building’s longevity and history, but you don’t need more than 45 minutes.  Other sites are a short walk away, making this a great stop on a walking tour of the old city.

The inside actually felt small and simple compared to many of the other churches in Rome. I'm glad I visited inside once, but if I were in Rome again, I take a selfie outside and skip the inside visit.

This structure reveals architectural ingenuity, which has made it stand the test of time. The last time I visited it, there was no entrance fee. Now you'll have to pay €5 for it. Inside you'll marvel at its beauty from the ceiling (with an open hole on it, called the "oculus") to its marble flooring. The Pantheon, Trevi Fountain and Spanish Steps are all within a reasonable walk of each other. My ranking is 1 Pantheon, 2 Trevi Fountain, 3 Spanish steps.  I will review them in descending order starting with the Spanish Steps. The Spanish Steps are --- steps. Perhaps there is an elegance to the curve and the balustrades but you will be hard pressed to see it because of the crowds. To me this was the Kardashians of landmarks, famous for being famous, a picture of it, the real thing, neither is really going to make that much impact on anyone who has half a life. People wax poetic about the sound the fountain makes. Well, maybe at 6AM but you will not be able to hear or fully see the fountain around all the people posing for selfies. The streets around the steps are filled with high end lux brand stores that were crawling with private security outside. I guess they were supposed to be anonymous in their tailored suits but they really need some Mod Squad training if they are going to fit in with all the tourists. Admittedly there was a heightened security threat announced that day and I am sure every one in Italy immediately yelled 'Protect the Gucci bags at all costs!' So, The Spanish Steps. they make a scenic backdrop for shopping in stores that don't have clearance racks.The Trevi Fountain is a magnificent and massive thing. It looks like it is growing out of the building or that the building is growing out of it. It is incredibly dramatic in all its baroque style. There was a barricade around it when we were there while a crew used very specific vacuum cleaners to suck all the coins out that people throw in daily. The vacuuming was supposed to be finished by 10AM and people could get closer to the fountain, so keep that in mind if you interested in throwing money your around. It is actually easier get a senes of the entire fountain from above. This is another site famous for all the films it has been featured in so it was quite crowded, even before 10AM on a weekday, and while people are encouraged to reenact Three Coins in the Fountain, you cannot reenact La Dolce Vita. Saving the best for last the Pantheon is an other worldly experience. It is a space impossible to get a sense of from a photograph. We walked by it on a Saturday around noon and the crowds were so dense we decided to try again early on a weekday. You can get a timed ticket online, but this is the one time we didn't do it in case I drank the entire bottle of wine the night before and we had to delay it another day. We managed to get there around 8:45 and oddly the timed ticket line was significant while there was no one in the line to buy tickets. It was 5 euro (cash only) and we walked right in. The building is singular. It starts with the approach. There are massive doors and you can see a coffered dome with a mystical light that is revealed slowly as you approach the doorway. Inside, words, like pictures, cannot describe the experience of this colossal volume with an opening to the sky in the center. James Turrell has nothing on the ancients. A perfect circle of dense blue, occasionally a cloud drifts by, the sense of immense space, it literally transports you. This building is old and there is nothing that compares to it. Even with the amount of people in it, you can still feel alone with it and that is a rare thing. I am not going to go into the history of the Pantheon, that's what Wikipedia is for, but it has been in use since the 2nd century so it has been through a lot. While the inside has been pasted with images of catholicism the outside really shows the changes and abuse it has withstood in its life, so it is worth looking at more than just the front facade. Walking around the building past the back left corner there is a small plaza with a fantastic monument of an elephant with an Egyptian obelisk on its back. The easter egg hunt of finding Egyptian obelisks in Rome is its own thing, but this one, it turns out, was designed by Bernini. The elephant is so alive you can tell it is by a major artist, but who would expect a Bernini that has been out in the elements for almost 400 years virtually overshadowed by the Pantheon. If you are taking a leisurely walk and continue down that road you will come to the Theatre of Pompey, where Julius Caesar was murdered, which is how that democracy ended.

Visiting the Pantheon in Rome with my family was one of the most memorable experiences of our trip to Italy. Walking through the cobbled streets of the Eternal City, we knew we were approaching something special—but nothing prepared us for the moment we stepped into the Piazza della Rotonda and saw the Pantheon in all its grandeur.First ImpressionsThe Pantheon is absolutely breathtaking from the outside. Its massive Corinthian columns and imposing facade are instantly awe-inspiring. Even my kids, who can usually only be impressed by gelato and gladiators, stopped in their tracks. The sense of history was palpable—you can feel the centuries in the worn stone and the quiet reverence of the place.Stepping InsideAs we walked through the enormous bronze doors into the rotunda, a collective “wow” escaped from all of us. The interior is a perfect circle, with marble floors, statues of saints, and beautiful niches lining the walls. But the true highlight? The oculus—a circular opening in the center of the dome that lets sunlight pour in. It was around midday, and the sunbeam shining through the oculus felt almost spiritual. The light moved across the walls like a sundial, and it was mesmerizing to watch.Even our youngest was intrigued by the idea that this hole in the ceiling has been open to the sky for nearly 2,000 years—and that it’s the only source of natural light in the building.A Living MonumentWhat struck me most was how alive the Pantheon still feels. It's not just a relic—it's a working church, known as the Basilica of St. Mary and the Martyrs. There were people praying, lighting candles, and taking in the moment in silence. We explained to our kids that we were standing in a building that had been a Roman temple before becoming a Christian church, and that it has been continuously used since around 126 AD. That timeline was mind-blowing.Family-Friendly ExperienceThe visit was surprisingly family-friendly. There’s no entrance fee (which is great for a family group), and the site isn’t overwhelmingly large, so you can take your time without feeling rushed or exhausted. There are benches where you can sit and take it all in, and just outside are plenty of cafes and gelaterias where we grabbed a treat afterwards.We also appreciated the subtle educational opportunity—history, architecture, and art all in one spot, without it feeling like a museum.Final ThoughtsThe Pantheon is more than just a must-see site—it's a powerful experience that bridges the ancient and modern world. Whether you’re a history buff or just want to feel a deeper connection to human civilization, it delivers in a quiet, awe-inspiring way.Our family left feeling humbled, inspired, and surprisingly peaceful. If you're in Rome with your loved ones, don’t miss it—it’s not just a monument, it's a moment you’ll remember together.

The Pantheon is not only one of the most iconic monuments in Rome: it is an experience that leaves you speechless, even if you thought you already knew everything. As soon as you enter, time seems to stand still. The light filtering through the oculus – a perfect 9-metre opening in the centre of the dome – creates an almost mystical atmosphere. There are no windows, just that circle of sky that illuminates the interior as if it had been designed by a Renaissance artist with today’s technology. And instead it is there, intact for over 1900 years. The dome is the largest in the world in unreinforced concrete. Even today, engineers and architects study it to understand how it is possible. And then there is that sense of harmony: everything is proportionate, simple and perfect. You walk and you realize that you are stepping on the same floor that the Romans of the second century stepped on. Inside, in addition to the imposing architecture, there are also the tombs of great Italians, including Raphael Sanzio and the kings Vittorio Emanuele II and Umberto I. An ancient temple turned church, became a mausoleum, became a legend. The Pantheon is one of those places that never disappoints you. It's simple, solemn, great. A monument that combines technique and spirituality in an absolutely unique way.
