Timeless Wonder of Rome: PantheonI was really captivated when I visited the Pantheon, which is high on my attraction list in Rome! Rising right in the heart of the city, in the heart of Piazza della Rotonda, this structure impresses with its architecture and atmosphere.First Impression & IntroductionWhen you approach the Pantheon, you see a majestic structure welcoming you with its massive columns. It’s simple but just as impressive from the outside. Entry was completely free, which is a big advantage over other sights in Rome. The moment you step in, you’re entering a different world.Architecture & AtmosphereThe massive dome inside is truly breathtaking. The natural light flows in, especially through the oculus (open round window) in its middle, adding a mystical touch. This structure has been here for centuries, so people feel like they’re on a journey through history. Inside, there''s a quiet atmosphere, so visitors often spend time looking up in awe.Dates and DetailsThe history of the Pantheon, M.S. It dates back to 126 and is most impressive because it’s largely preserved from the Roman Empire to the present day. There’s also the tomb of the famous Italian artist, Raffaello. Strolling around historic details makes the structure more meaningful.Square and EnvironmentPiazza della Rotonda square in front of the Pantheon is also special beauty. There are plenty of cafes to sit around for a coffee. We sat in a cafe after a visit, savored an Italian coffee, and savored the moment with repeat views of this amazing structure.As ResultOne of the most impressive structures to see in Rome is definitely the Pantheon! With both architecture and history, it takes you on a journey through time. If you’re on your way to Rome, be sure to stop by and be enchanted by that massive dome!👉 Tip: Visit early in the morning or in the evening, and you’ll find fewer crowds and a more peaceful atmosphere.

The Pantheon is the best-preserved structure from ancient Rome and is a marvel of engineering considering it was built nearly 2000 years ago in AD 120. It was a temple dedicated to all of the Roman gods. It remains intact to this day as it was transformed into a Christian church after the Fall of Rome guaranteeing its preservation. Inside is found the tombs of King Victor Emmanuel II, the first king of Italy after unification and of his son Umberto I who was assassinated in 1900 along with the great Renaissance artist Raphael. In truth, there really isn't a lot to see inside. However, the Pantheon is a must-see highlight of Rome. Unlike the Roman Forum which requires much imagination to picture what the ruins of the great temples must have looked like, the Pantheon remains intact to this day. Visiting the Pantheon is no longer free, and it is best to book in advance online to get a specific time slot for entry. The 15-euro charge includes an audio guide. It is best to visit as early in the day as possible as it can get quite crowded. Opening time is 9am. It is also worthwhile to stop by at night to get photos as the piazza in front of the Pantheon is quite beautiful when lit after dark and it is more serene when less crowds are around.

One of the most famous monuments of the capital as well as one of the most significant and best preserved in ancient Rome. Made by Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa, son-in-law of Octavian Augustus, what we see today is the reconstruction made under the emperor Hadrian, and subsequently restored by both Septimius Severus and Caracalla. The magnificent state of preservation is probably due to the fact that since 609 AD it has been used as a Christian church (and still is!), dedicated to Santa Maria ad Martyres. Externally, the majestic pronaos of 16 granite columns 14 meters high will already impress, but the greatest charm is inside for the famous dome, a masterpiece of Roman engineering. Made entirely of Roman concrete it is the largest dome in the world made with this technique and probably the widest ever. From the bottom you can not understand the real enormous dimensions: the central hole, the only point of light of the interior, is 9 meters wide and the entire diameter is 43 meters and 44 cm (as well as the height from the floor, so that the interior of the Pantheon could contain a perfect sphere of 43.44 meters in diameter); to support its enormous weight (thousands of tons) expedients have been adopted, such as the decrease in the thickness of the wall (from 6 meters of the base to 2 meters near the oculus) and the use of lighter and lighter concrete as you climb in height (from the pieces of travertine and bricks at the base, to then continue with tufa and finish with lapilli and pumice). The interior has obviously been modified over the centuries since, as mentioned, from a Roman temple it was transformed into a church, so today the perimeter of the cylinder has chapels, niches, aedicules and works from various eras, but like the dome, the large bronze door and the floor in polychrome marble is from the Roman period, a floor that, although not noticeable, is slightly concave in the center to collect water that falls from the oculus on rainy days and that through some holes is then collected in underground pipes. Among the works present you can see valuable sculptures of the sixteenth century and two remarkable paintings from the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, as well as the tomb of Raphael and the tombs of the first two kings of Italy: Victor Emmanuel II and Umberto I (the latter together with his wife, Queen Margherita). I will not dwell on other aspects, curiosities and legends concerning this extraordinary monument, which I invite you to discover in advance to make the visit even more interesting.

The Pantheon is one of the iconic landmarks of Rome and with good reason. In simple terms described as a temple dedicated to all of the gods, the first structure to appear on this site was built during the reign of the Emperor Augustus. It was subsequently destroyed by two different fires over the next 100 years until the Emperor Hadrian commissioned the current structure, which is widely renowned for having the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome, making it an architectural marvel. The Pantheon has been in continuous use since its construction, initially as a Roman temple and subsequently as a Christian church dedicated to St. Mary and the Martyrs. It currently contains the tombs of the renowned Renaissance artist Raphael and the first King of unified Italy, Victor Emmanuel II. One of the oldest artefacts in the building is a 7th-century Byzantine icon given by Emperor Phocas II to then Pope Boniface IV.The Pantheon is a truly spectacular structure and it should be on anyone's top 5 list of sites to see in Rome. You can easily combine a visit with a walk to the beautiful Piazza Navona, which is less than 5 minutes away.

Inside there is room to sit because it is a church. You need to dress with knees and shoulders covered.If you need to go to the bathroom, buy a coffee. One of the best gelatories we tried in Italy is on the side. The coffee "La Casa del Caffe Tazza D'oro" we liked, they have a great variety, good quality in coffee and has bathrooms. They also have coffee grounds or beans to go. METER: Children under 10 years old do not pay. If you buy tickets you have a certain number of hours to use them. If they expire DO NOT THROW THEM AWAY because it is cheaper to "reload" them on the same machine than to buy a new one.  Learn this word: USCITA, it means "exit."  Subway doors won't open if you stick your hand or foot in, be careful. There's a yellow line on the floor, stay back.STRIKES: We stayed 4 days in Rome and we got 2 strikes and a march in front of the hotel. There are many strikes all over Italy. Strikes are announced a day or two in advance and there is always a schedule or some line that does guarantee service, but you have to be aware. Check the news a day before your walk or ask at your hotel.TAXIS: Taxis in Rome can only be taken from certain places and there are huge queues. You find them on google maps, they are usually close to very tourist places like the Pantheon or the Colosseum. There is no UBER but limousine service, very expensive. Don't trust me to be able to take a taxi.

The oculus at the top of the dome creates a magical light effect that has left us speechless. Geometric perfection and the millennia-old history that permeates every stone have transported us back in time.

For me all of the Rome attractions are rated as 5   It is overwhelming experience in the presence of history. From the practical point of view: we were lucky as out visit came at the first Sunday of the month when all or some of the attractions are for free. Waiting time to enter Pantheon was short

Head out Pantheon skip the line with English guide booked for 11:25. We booked this because we are not able to stand in line for long periods of time due to mobility issues.  However we still had to, we paid a premium even though 1st Sunday of the month is free admission we wanted the skip the line and guide. We got the guide but had to wait until 12:10 to enter!!!So not sure the advantage of paying $52.50 each.   We had to leave the tour early because there is a limit to standing in one place.  So not worth it at all, we would have gotten in at the same time on our own and could have gotten the info provided from google, the guide even said that!!!!!
Super super crowded with way too many tour groups. Whole area unable to walk through without walking into people. As I’d been there before we just left. Heavens know what it is like in peak season.
What a fabulous building! Being able to skip the line was great! It was crowded but we were able to move around and stay as long as we wanted.
Fascinating building. Able to avoid the long queues by booking the tour. There is seating inside, so you can listen to the audio guide in comfort.
Take your tickets on the website of the Municipality of Rome and for 5 euros you will love it!The largest unbuilt concrete dome A must-see in Rome!

One of the oldest Roman temple buildings it’s a Christian church The Pantheon dome remains the single largest, unreinforced concrete dome in the entire world The square in front of the Pantheon is called Piazza della Rotonda

Although there isn't much to see at the Pantheon, there is a lot of history to discover, so it's good to be able to take your time and explore all the stories the structure has to offer on your own. Given that we could move at our own pace, this was a truly pleasant and soothing experience. The tour itself is really easy; simply play the appropriate audio pieces and follow the numbers. Fantastic complement to our trip!

The Pantheon is an ancient Roman building that was a temple, and it is one of the world's most ancient, civilized sites. The Pantheon is in the center of the city of Rome, it's a good place to take a tour. The temple dates initially from 27 to 25 BC, and was upgraded by the Emperor Hadrian in 125 AD. The Pantheon is a favorite destination for visitors to Rome.

The Pantheon certainly does not need to be "sponsored", being one of the most famous attractions in Rome and beyond. However, it was a long time since I visited it and I must say that, despite the ticket for a few years for a fee, it has not lost tourists. It's a truly magnificent work and one that lives up to its fame.

The monument is exceptional.  The ticket price is twice as expensive online than purchased on-site. There was no queue.Although the ticket indicates that the guide would meet with us at the venue to distribute the tickets, this did not happen. I had to call the operator "Love Rome" to explain the process, and they sent me the tickets via WhatsApp.The App is not worth the difference value spent.The audio does not explain anything unusual that adds value to the experience.

We visited the Pantheon with 9 am tickets that we purchased online through museiitaliani.it. The entry was smooth and efficient, and although there was a bit of a crowd, the atmosphere remained enjoyable. For those planning a visit, entry is also free on the first Sunday of each month, making it a great option for budget-conscious travelers.For the best experience, I recommend visiting on a sunny day. The sunlight streaming in through the dome creates beautiful shadows on the walls, adding a magical ambiance to the already awe-inspiring structure. Audio guides are available for purchase just inside to the left, or you can use Rick Steves Audio Europe for a self-guided tour.The Pantheon is an incredible example of ancient engineering and architecture, and its massive, perfectly symmetrical dome is truly something to behold. Highly recommend adding this to your Rome itinerary!
Beautiful place of worship and history. Admission 5 euros from 18 years old. Well described in the internal tables and audio guide present inside to be taken independently. You cannot not visit

We toured at about 4:00 pm. there were still lots of people around, but the line entered quickly.  Its hard to believe that a structure over 2000 years old is so well preserved, and impressive from the inside and out.  We admired the huge bronze doors and the dome, sort of wishing it was raining so we could see how the water entered and drained.  We should have done the audio tour to learn more, but it was the end of the day and we were dead tired.  Definitely put this on your list when in Rome.

We went to the Pantheon at 4pm. There was a long queue but the queue kept moving. Within 35 minutes, we reached the side entrance of the Pantheon. Each of us were given a ticket. We scanned the ticket at the gantry and we had a bag security check and we were allowed into the Pantheon. No need to fret if you cannot get a pre-booked ticket, just make a trip to the Pantheon on the first Sunday of the month and get your free ticket to access.

They didn't buy tickets in advance. On the sides of the pantheon there are two queues to the tills-- cash on the left, bank cards on the right. You approach first in a lady, and then she already lets you into a small room-- entrance 5 euros. And then with the ticket, you walk through the main doors of the pantheon. The line of 12 days was fast, 15 minutes maximum. Raphael and his fiancée are buried in the temple. There are benches in the hall and there's a place to sit.

Easy to redeem tickets.  Skipped the line. Collected audio easily.  Looked around according to own pace while listened relevant information through audio.  Magnificent exterior building and the interior was beautiful also.  Amazing dome.  Memorable visit and great experience.

Great experience. No queue as we had pre booked. Collected audio from the desk on the left. You do need to leave formal id as security. Spent a few hours looking around at our own pace while we listened to a very informative and easy to listen guide.

A wonder without a doubt. It is highly recommended to book entry through the official website of the Italian Ministry of Culture and save yourself the management costs and queues to take it out on site. It's usually crowded, both inside and outside.

The frontage is great, 2000yrs history. But when we stepped into the portico we were in awe. To walk through the doors and catch our first glimpse of the interior of the round basilica was mind blowing. We walked all around then walked round again. The domed roof is open to the sky (no glass) and is spectacular. The floor and walls are all different types of marble. Raphael Santi's tomb attracted a lot of attention but there are other areas to look at which are equally important. Not the biggest of places, but bigger than expected from the outside. Not to be missed.

The Phantheon is a magnificient place with so much history. The queue was not bad straight in. The only issue was trying to find the book shop to get the tickets not easy at all could be better sign posted.

Book this myself instead of going through one of the middlemen. The outside of the building is awesome and the inside is just as awesome! The history behind the pantheon and how it was built very interesting.well worth visiting if you go toRome.Considered one of the first temples to worship in RomePlan on spending approximately one hour

This is just to skip the long line.  The guide just lets you into pantheon with a special ticket.   The guide also was informative about pantheon and other places in Rome.  If you want to skip the line, this is worth it.  We had time to visit at 5pm.  The pantheon closes at 6:30pm.  So, to skip, we booked at 3pm online and were able to make it.   We downloaded the pantheon app for 2 euros.  Which is a must.  Or audited for 10 euros inside.

It has recently become paid (5€), but it is worth it.Inside there are many bilingual Italian and English signs that explain a lot about the history of the monument and the works it contains (such as the tombs of Raphael Sanzio, Vittorio Emanuele II and Umberto I).Impressive is the concrete dome, over 43 m in diameter, larger than that of St. Peter although erected more than 1900 years ago.
One of the most impressive monuments in Rome. From the Corinthian granite columns to its impressive vault with the central eye. The Pantheon of Agrippa is essential.
Very nice place (although I expected much bigger), nice frescoes, you have to go imperatively it is beautiful
The place is not so big, but the architecture is beautiful.It's a lesson in Italian history.The opening in the ceiling is a demonstration of the quality of ancient engineering.

Definitely worth visiting. One of the benefits of the self-audio tour being that with so many other people also visiting, you can replay any steps, and also change the order of the sequence. There were a number of queues to get in so would recommend booking  tour. Also give yourself time to collect the ticket from the museum shop (it’s a few minutes from the Pantheon).
For cash, there is no line. It’s an amazing work of architecture. Even though it was crowded, we were able to see everything at our own pace.
Coliseum , following the recommended tripadvisor fast track link, its   a bit of a ripe off . Avoid , try to find other pre ordered tickets to visit sites . I will not be trusting Trip Advisor again
It's not worth it in the off-season, just come early and there's little line. Audio guide is interesting but no need to go through the platform

No one answer the phone number and there was no directions to meet the person directly at the Pantheon location. I need up getting sick and had to return to my hotel. It felt like I was scammed =( I’m expecting a refund but will see what happens. If you’re in Rome, better go to Touristation very professional and safe.

The agency where the tickets are picked is a few blocks from the place, access facilitated because it is well moved the place, the only bad thing is that to pick up the cellphone where the audio Guide comes has a relatively time-consuming queue and deliver a document to be redeemed when returning the cellphone with audio Guide but the return is not long. Audio Guide very good and very quiet to use, for those who know little about the history of the monument helps quite to understand and know!
Stephan was very knowledgeable, professional and personableHe seemed to make sure that we saw everything that was relevant and we didn’t feel rushed through the tour

One of the best preserved monuments in Rome and a must see. A few years ago their entrance was free, now they pay €5 for access. Inside you can see the magnificent architecture, with the dome and the oculus; as well as the tombs of Raphael or Vittorio Emmanuelle II.

I understand that going there on the first Sunday of the month is not advisable, but during the mass is closed and ok.But that no one says anything, the staff that does not make a nod is zero communication after draining area I find it really bad.Inside it is worth visiting if you manage to get inside....

If you're visiting Rome, this should be on your list. You now have to pay to visit the Pantheon, but it's worth it. Go early morning or early evening, as the queues are big. There's a really nice chocolate and gelato place 2 min walk from here called Venchi which I highly recommend.
We simply did the skip the line and used headsets.  Great history and well-preserved.  Recommend.  Go in morning when less crowded.

Beautiful suggestive place and a beautiful dome that deserves to be seen from the inside. A lot of people queuing for the entrance, you find yourself inside tossed by the flow of tourists. The cost of admission is 5€, until a few years ago it was free, I understand that it is still a low figure, but in reality I do not find it right to charge the ticket.Inside although the atmosphere should be collected and prayerful, there are several shouts and shouts, in short I did not fully enjoy the visit.Management can be improved.

Knowing its architecture, we went to see it from close.. the words are unnecessary!! There was also a shopping mall nearby when we went with the witch that the Italians celebrate.. very nice experience all..

An emblematic place of Rome, not to be missed absolutely. You can combine a visit with Piazza Navona, the Church of San LUigi dei Francesi, Piazza Campo de Fiori and Piazza Farnese (all a few minutes away). the duration of the visit to the PIgna district lasts about 4 hours.

Well, one can’t go to Rome and not see the Pantheon. We went early to beat the crowds… can’t imagine what it’s like at peak visiting hours. It’s very interesting, and worth the visit. But be prepared to have your personal space invaded. It must be incredible when it’s empty.
What a shock when you arrive in front of the pantheon, it is imposing, beautiful, what to say about the beautiful interior, but many people.

Don’t put it on your list of to dos,The shorter queue is the credit card one ,choose this one ,I thought we would have more to see move maybe if I did the audio it may have been better .The seating was good give my legs a rest looks nice and seen it but definitely not worth paying in the church up. The road was a lot better.
This was one One of our favorite spots to visit in all of Rome The admission was five euros. It is absolutely fantastic. Its varied history and its architecture make it a fantastic stop
Monument to see, but not the most memorable for me, the entrance is not free, arrived in front of the entrance you will have to download an app and pay the entrance, 2€ if I remember correctly
Definitely worth doing the audio guide when visiting the pantheon, was really interesting to walk round and listen to everything!
A momentary show with almost 2200 years of history from Agrippa to the present day, indicating the great construction in the time of Emperor Hadrian. I recommend the acquisition of an audio guide.

The Pantheon is a must see! We paid $5 euro and the line moved us in quickly. The Pantheon's dome is still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome. At the top of the dome sits an opening, or oculus, 27 feet in width. The oculus, which has no covering, lets light—as well as rain and other weather—into the Pantheon. The bronze doors are 24.7 feet high, the oldest in Rome. It also houses the tombs of Vittorio Emanuele II, the first king of Italy; his son, Umberto I, and Umberto’s wife, Queen Margherita. The Pantheon is a catholic church and services are still held so dress appropriately with knees and shoulders covered.
Speedy entry to the venuEasy to follow and interesting audio explained many of the facets of the pantheon.Well worth the trip.
Surely there are a million reviews of the Parthenon and little else I could add...Just to say that I found the degree of conservation of this building incredible. Oblong visit in Rome!

So the Pantheon is really impressive. Architecture gives you a direct slap when you arrive, and inside, it’s even more ephemeral. Between the tombs of the great names of history and Foucault’s clock that makes you doubt gravity after a few drinks the day before, there is reason to ask existential questions.We did the tour in quiet mode, and it was really cool to see the names we studied in class (but maybe we forgot a bit 😅). And then the view from the dome is the icing on the cake. To do, even if you're not a big fan of museums!"

The Pantheon is one of the best preserved buildings of antiquity. For us it was a highlight of our trip to Rome. The massive dome, measuring around 43 metres in diameter, is a masterpiece of Roman architecture and has been considered the largest dome ever made of unreinforced concrete for almost 2,000 years. For this reason alone, you should definitely visit the Pantheon from the inside. In addition, there is the magnificent marble interior decoration, the beautiful altarpieces (as it is now a church) and the tombs of world-famous personalities (among others Raphael, two Italian kings). The official audio guide is highly recommended for interesting background information. Time required: 45 - 60 minutes.
The Pantheon is one of the most fascinating buildings in the city, especially at night. Its size and grandeur will make you feel tiny.
The faster lane to get in was definitely worth it. The regular line is very long. Everything was super easy to figure out. However, the audio guided tour isn’t very special.
Clearly an impressive, imposing and important site. The audio guide was alright but the included digital map was poor and made it too hard to follow any sensible directions inside

Lovely , historic & busy site.  The only reason this ancient site wasn’t destroyed with time was that it was turned in a Christian church centuries ago.  Go the Rome municipal website for buying tickets for this , (if you have local data on your mobile. ). This can be done on day or hour of your visit.Then you can  buy tickets that day   so there’s no wait of any kind. 🙂

Easy to book last minute. I highly recommend the audio option because it’s in a storytelling format which was enjoyable to listen to as you strolled about. I was even able to sit in the pews as I listened. It’s a beautiful place, worth a visit. Spent about 45 minutes.

An incredibly rare example of a building that has survived nearly 2000 years of history, the Pantheon is magnificent inside and out. I cannot recommend a visit enough. It only costs €5 to enter and is easy to book via the official website. If you book in advance, join the central queue to enter. I went at opening time, 9am, and it only took a couple minutes to get in
What about...wonderful...for me one of the most beautiful monuments...inside rich in history. There are no words to describe her beauty and perfection
Wonderful! It's worth paying to get in! On the outside it is already a wonder, but the interior is wonderful, full of history. It is very worth paying (5 euros) to enter!
Audio is worth having. It tells the history and gives perspective. The building itself is magnificent. Skip the line and chose your time is the way to go especially during busy season

Buy tickets on line. We had to set up a new PayPal. Saved lots of time (5pm). Watch UTube video for tips. Men remove hats as respect inside.  It's 2,000 yrs old, unbelievable building techniques to last this long!

Alessandro was excellent! He started this off with an explanation of the incredible structure and really helped us appreciate the amount of difficulty it took to produce this structural phenomenon. He explained everything from A-to-Z and give us very good historical context about every inch of the place inside and out. Even though a regular entrance ticket is five euro it is definitely worth paying for the private fast track ticket with a guide!
A great tour at a great price. The ticket office was easy to find to collect our tickets, and the pantheon is only a short walk away. Great audio tour, learned a lot.

This is without doubt  the best building in Rome.It was built in 129 AD and it is in amazing condition, From the colossal bronze doors to the beautiful well colour co ordinated interior it smacks of class.The dome itself with its central hole is breathtaking and worth the entrance fee alone.If ever you’re in Rome this is a must see.
A wonderful experience to see a magnificent,The self guided tour is very well presented and informative. The information provided ensures an understanding of the Pantheon.Highly recommended.

We booked this tour and the "meet up address" given, when entered in the Maps App was impossible to find. We went around in circles following instructions, it was hot and finally stopped at the city Tourist Info Booth. Unable to find. Lost Tour $$. No refunds after explanation.

Ticket office to collect your passes was really close by. Quick access to the Pantheon which was absolutely stunning, a must see. Audio programme was really informative. Make sure you have id to leave for collection of your listening device.
Not sure what all these tours are, every time I've been there is no charge 🤷Anyhow, a must! It's a beautiful building. Looks very modern. Ancient!
A beautiful place worth seeing not only from the outside but also inside. There are two queues for tickets, one to pay by card and the other by cash
A wonderful structure absolutely to see but above all to live.  You cannot go to Rome without visiting such a structure. Recommendation to everyone!!!
Truly beautiful a truly imposing architectural work if you come to Rome you can not come here '.... leaves you speechless

We were my boyfriend and me, he is a minor me just of age so we paid only 2 € on site (it is free for under 18s) and we enjoyed it a lot, there were not many people, it must have been around 9:30 and it was very nice:)

You cannot visit the center of the capital without stopping at the Pantheon and paying homage to an architectural miracle and the illustrious figures who are buried there from Raphael Sanzio to Vittorio Emanuele II and Queen Margherita.

A majestic, imposing work (its dome is the largest in concrete in the world) and of inestimable value: it is in fact the only Roman monument that has survived intact. Originally built by Marco Vipsanio Agrippa as a temple in honor of all the deities (hence the name Pan-Theon), in the seventh century AD it was converted into a Christian basilica under the name of Santa Maria della Rotonda, or Santa Maria ad Martyres and houses the tombs of kings and illustrious people: Umberto I, Vittorio Emanuele II, Raffaello Sanzio and Baldassarre Peruzzi. It 'a place full of charm, magical, magnetic, so that once you enter you would not want to go out. Inconceivable, however, is the mindlessness of the security personnel who allow too many visitors to speak out so loud that it seems to be at the Point of Sale and not in a church, too bad! The property is wheelchair accessible thanks to a ramp.
A must-see when in Rome. Nevertheless, you should aim to arrive quite early because there will be a minimum one-hour wait if you don't.

I was at the Pantheon on August 13, 2024, very hot day, hundreds of people lined up to roast in the sun because the ticket machines had the screen that could not be read well, you had to shade and enter a lot of data, 3/5 minutes a ticket At one point we notice a small sign that says that with cash ~ A C another entrance, said done no line, time zero and we enter. At the exit I tell a policeman that all those people do not enjoy burning in the sun, that they put a prominent sign that you can enter with cash, Response is not up to us but the ministry. They should be ashamed to treat tourists like this, they like it too well..

This church was once an important roman temple and is around 2,000 years of age. The architecture and engineering was hundreds of years ahead of that time and is one of the engineering masterpieces of Ancient Rome. The dome is a real wonder. The interior is also marvelous and has luckily survived the Christian take over more or less intact. While this gen was free of charge until recently, they unfortunately rip you off now. But it is worth it. In my eyes, this building even surpasses the Colloseum.

This ancient temple, dedicated to all the deities of the Roman Empire, is one of the best preserved buildings of antiquity and every time I return to Rome and visit it continues to amaze me for its majesty and its ingenious architectural design.Famous for its dome, which remains the largest unreinforced concrete dome in the world. The central oculus, a circular opening 9 meters in diameter, is the only source of natural light and creates a spectacular effect inside.Visiting the Pantheon in Rome is a breathtaking experience. To enter you have to buy a ticket (Recommendation directly on the spot that costs less than the apps) and the visit takes about half an hour.
It is always worth visiting Pantheon when you go to Rome. There is a line of tourists, but inside it is always a spectacle especially on sunny days

One of the best preserved and most beautiful monuments of Imperial Rome.originally built in 27 BC at the behest of Agrippa and rebuilt between 112 and 125 AD at the initiative of Hadrian.The Corinthian granite columns are apotheosic.It is currently a temple, Basilica of St. Mary and the Martyrs.One of the icons of the Imperial city.

Long queues to get in the Pantheon and entry fee for adults were only 5 Euros each with children free in.  Beautiful building and good to see. However  if you were short on time and places to see, you can miss the Pantheon as there are other more worthwhile places to visit that cannot be missed in Rome.

Funny story, bought the tickets for 50€ only to go on a Sunday to find out it is free on Sunday just no audio.All good, I know the history, explained it to the wife and enjoyed our day.P.SGo super early!

After days of walking tours this was just the experience we needed. It was a great way to hear about the Pantheon while sitting in one of the pews. No need to move around, you can see everything from your seat. Also, you can take as much time as you need. Make sure to bring an ID to get the audio devices. Wonderful tour, the site is so well preserved.  Our tour guide was outstanding, providing insights that we  unlikely would have gained.
Was very difficult to find where to collect the tickets from. The office did not have decent signage out the front to locate the office.

2000 years old and still a sight to behold. Beautiful interior and the dome is an amazing site. As they charge admission, be sure to get your timed entry ticket ahead of time through the Italian state site for 5 Euros instead of the over priced church sponsored ticket. We were supposed to go in at 1800 hrs after the 5:00 Mass, but it went 30 mins over and there was a huge line by then. Recommended that you don’t book short after a Mass.

Come scriveva Stendhal: "The Pantheon is the most perfect thing we have left of Roman architecture." It was originally a temple dedicated to all gods, built by urban planner Agrippa in 27 BC. It became a church thanks to Pope Boniface IV, who received it as a gift in 608 AD from an emperor of Byzantium and took the name of S. Maria ad Martyres. Inside is the tomb of Raphael Sanzio, between the fifth and sixth chapels. Noteworthy is the dome, which has a huge opening in the center, the only source of light. Admission costs € 5 for adults, minors enter for free.
The Pantheon seen from the outside is spectacular and majestic pity the great crowd of tourists to enter. However it is always worth it, it is still one of the great monuments of Rome.

The views and inside are just amazing we took a lot of photos. It still stands today as one of the most well-preserved structure from Ancient Rome, having survived floods and earthquakes, and staying relatively untouched for > 2000 years – it is still made up of the original marble and granite from thousands of years ago, salute to those ancient architecture

The Pantheon is one of the most fascinating wonders of Rome, a place that enchants with its architectural beauty and its spiritual atmosphere. Built as a temple dedicated to all gods, the Pantheon is now a Catholic basilica and a place of great historical significance.

An essential visit of a trip to Rome You are absolutely overwhelmed by the majesty and power of this monument One cannot remain unimpressed by this work that has two millennia of history behind it Book only in advance to avoid the endless queues

Wasn't really on my list of things to do, but happened to be passing, as it was really hot we thought it would be a good break.Didn't pre-book anything & the 'cash' queue was really short and cheap to enter (5€per person) so we decided to go in.Was more interesting inside than expected and not to busy, so I would say worth a detour if you're in the area and the queues aren't too long.Possibly wouldn't bother with a 'guide' but maybe an audio guide if you have time.
Another popular site in Rome. The dome is the largest unreinforced dome in the world. The lines can be long. There is sitting inside. Entry is not free.

We used the skip the line audio tour for our visit to the Pantheon. Collecting the tickets was very easy and the staff was very helpful. The audio tour was excellent and very informative as well as entertaining. However, I would say that you need to bring ID with your name on it to exchange for and collect your audio devise. This was not mentioned before arrival and we were lucky we had something that fit the criteria. Overall, amazing place and a memorable visit to a wonderful landmark.I would definitely go back again one day.
A real wonder to enjoy and see up close an engineering work like this. It seems incredible that it is preserved so well, which evidences the constructive skill of the time.

Loved my visit here. In awe of the whole place and the atmosphere as soon as you walk in is one of silent wonder. Was given a recommendation to come here before I came to Rome and to be honest it wasn't on my radar of places to visit....but I can hands down say it was my favourite place I visited in Rome

Having studied it in History, I wanted to visit it and I was impressed by the beauty of the dome! It is amazing how the Romans managed to build such a solemn building! I also managed to see the tombs of Raphael Sanzio, Umberto I and Vittorio Emanuele II. Recommended for those who love Roman art!

Finally revealed the mystery of the hole on top of the great dome. Interesting story of the burials of our kings of Italy. Excellent system of online purchase of tickets (even just before the entrance) that allows you to do not queue.

I highly recommend getting the audio guide tour and the fast track ticket.  It is self-guided and we were there over an hour.  Lots of history and very informative.  I suggest not going in July - way too hot, but we enjoyed our visit.  I would also purchase tickets in the morning.  This tour is worth it for the price and history.  We took an Uber to get here from our hotel.  We could have taken the Metro, but we were tired.

Simple self guided tour. For $20, you get ticket inside, audio device which is an iPhone like device with earbuds. You visit different spots inside the pantheon while listening to audio about each one. It’s a bit cheesy but is more interesting than just hearing facts. You can enter the Pantheon and read about each spot for 5 euros. We went on a Sunday and got there as the doors opened at noon. Place to pick up tickets is about a 10 min walk to the Pantheon. From there you go straight inside and then to a line to pick up audio device. Amazing place to visit. Highly recommend going inside.

The Pantheon is beautiful! So amazing that it has survived all these years in tact! You do want to see this in person! I’m not sure I’d “skip the line” again, though I’m sure the fee goes to maintenance of the church. You pick up tix at a side entrance to the nearby church, walk a short distance to the Pantheon, then wait in the center line. On our day, this was the longest line. Not sure if that’s always the case. We were not told where to pick up the included audio guides. In fact, we had to ask the young woman where to go etc. There were audio guides for purchase as you walked in for EU2 and a sign about a smart phone app, which we downloaded. We later saw the desk where perhaps the included guides could be picked up?Note  that admission is free and you can download an app for your phone for EU1.99 in advance. I suggest doing this on your own and downloading the app when you have a strong signal.
The audio guide is perfect it allows to project thanks for this excellent work.despite the influence it is easy to make a fiction.

Mandatory monument to visit if you pass by Rome and you have some time to lose in line the Pantheon for me has never been anything exciting (in fact I have never rushed to go and see it when in the eternal city), but surely it is necessary to make it known to the Family to give a fuller picture of Rome and its history.Outside the line, as in these times, it is really stressful: up to two hours at certain times just to view a circular building with little inside to see (apart from the tomb of Raphael, some Kings and little more).I noticed instead that many tourists, forgetting that this is more like a real church, enter to protect themselves from the weather or the warm weather, and bivouac sitting on the pews for hours thanks to the very low entrance fee.Even in unsuitable clothes (and in fact the guardians inside have a lot to do to dress semi-naked women and little men with panzone in plain sight!).When we went, late afternoon, but with over 30 degrees temperature, for example tourists noticed inside more than interested in any things to see were competing to sit and rest a little '.And maybe nibble and drink the things bought from the stupid stalls and dingy little bars around.That said, however, for those who have not had a visit to the Pantheon must be done, if only to observe the curious roof "with the hole" ("oculus"), a hole that nowadays does not really serve any purpose, but that at the time was believed to allow the gods to "spy" on visitors and astronomers of that time to better scrutinize the sky.Nice to observe when it rains: inside, in fact, the water does not enter even in the presence of heavy rainfall, except in very modest quantities (something that the gratings inside can very well handle, as they have done for centuries and centuries).

Very easy to follow. A guide not needed. Only issue was picking up your hard copy ticket at a different location but too was relatively easy. Once in the self guided tour with the audio was perfect. Go at your own pace.

The architecture and structural engineering of this amazing building never fails to impress and once you walk inside….the art and history….Wow! Easily missed but incredibly important.Here is a tip….Skip the tours and the audio devices you have to pick up and return at the entrance to the Pantheon. You are going to wait in a line. Best of all no waiting in line to pick up/drop off the audio devices. Purchase the ticket and download the app before going to the Pantheon.

When in Rome you have to visit the Pantheon. Earlier last year (Feb. 2023) it was still free, now they charge entry fees...Join a good tour, you will get more out of it, especially if you can avoid long chaotic tourist lines.The Pantheon ( from Greek Πάνθειον Pantheion, "[temple] of all the gods") is a former Roman temple and, since AD 609, a Catholic church (Basilica Santa Maria ad Martyres or Basilica of St. Mary and the Martyrs) in Rome, Italy. It was built on the site of an earlier temple commissioned by Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa during the reign of Augustus (27 BC – AD 14); then, after the original burnt down, the present building was ordered by the emperor Hadrian and probably dedicated c. AD 126. Its date of construction is uncertain, because Hadrian chose not to inscribe the new temple but rather to retain the inscription of Agrippa's older temple.The building is round in plan, except for the portico with large granite Corinthian columns (eight in the first rank and two groups of four behind) under a pediment. A rectangular vestibule links the porch to the rotunda, which is under a coffered concrete dome, with a central opening (oculus) to the sky. Almost two thousand years after it was built, the Pantheon's dome is still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome !The height to the oculus and the diameter of the interior circle are the same, 43 metres (142 ft).It is one of the best-preserved of all Ancient Roman buildings, in large part because it has been in continuous use throughout its history. Since the 7th century, it has been a church dedicated to St. Mary and the Martyrs (Latin: Sancta Maria ad Martyres), known as "Santa Maria Rotonda". The square in front of the Pantheon is called Piazza della Rotonda. The Pantheon is a state property, managed by Italy's Ministry of Cultural Heritage and Activities and Tourism through the Polo Museale del Lazio. In 2013, it was visited by over six million people.The Pantheon's large circular domed cella, with a conventional temple portico front, was unique in Roman architecture. Nevertheless, it became a standard exemplar when classical styles were revived, and has been copied many times by later architects

Built by the Roman emperors Agrippa and Hadrian around 2000 years ago, it’s incredible that the Pantheon has survived intact for so long. It’s beaten barbarian invaders, wars, fire, looting and the elements. You can’t help but be struck by a sense of awe. Having been inside before, we were put off by the very long queues that snaked across the piazza and we didn’t want to wait in the sun so we didn’t go inside this time. That was probably a mistake. The dome is quite amazing - the largest unreinforced concrete dome in the world. The record still stands. Never been beaten. To pull off this incredible engineering feat, they built the flower walls out of heavy stone, then as they built upwards, they made the concrete thinner and added pumice so that it was quite lightweight. Sunlight streams through the ‘eye’ of the dome. It looks small but it’s actually 8.2m across. I’d recommend going inside and in hindsight, I’m sorry that we didn’t bite the bullet and join the queue.If you have the chance to walk past the Pantheon at night, do it. The facade is beautifully illuminated and it just looks magical.

The Pantheon was built by Agrippa, friend and son-in-law of Augustus, in 27 BC, damaged in 80 AD. and in 110 AD. from a fire, it was restored by the emperor Hadrian around 123 AD, it is a temple dedicated to past, present and future divinities. Temple with the largest Roman-era dome in the world, there is a central hole, oculus, where on April 21st (Rome's birthday) at 12 the sun hits the main door from where the emperor ritually entered. The bronze door dates back to that era. The kings of Italy are buried here. Take a look at the floor, they were like this in the great Roman buildings. Now it is a church and it is precisely due to the fact that it almost immediately became a church with the advent of Christianity as the religion of the empire that it did not suffer spoliation as happened to all the other Roman monuments. Here too, under 18s don't pay, there are totems where you have to get tickets for both adults and minors, the cost per adult is only 5 euros, it closes at 6.30pm. Fabulous.

This is one of the iconic landmark buildings in Rome. The Pantheon that you see today was built between 118-125 AD during the time of Emperor Hadrian and is located at Piazza della Rotonda in the heart of the city centre. It is a masterpiece of architectural skill from Ancient Rome and one of the most popular attractions in the city. Opening hours are now 8.30am to 7.30pm (Mon to Sat) and 9am to 6pm (Sun). Note there is now an entry fee of €5 per person if you want to visit the inside. Highlights of the Pantheon start with the dome structure which was the largest in the world until finally being surpassed by Brunelleschi's Dome at Basilica Santa Maria del Fiore in Florence in the 15th-century. This is an enormous concrete roof with no support that has remained standing for nearly two millennium. It is best viewed from inside where you can look directly upward and appreciate its width and scale. Originally constructed to be a temple, the Pantheon was converted into a Christian Church in 609. Today, you will find small altars and tombs lining the interior circular layout of the building. The marble flooring is impressive as well. Outside, it is worth a few minutes viewing the front portico structure and large Corinthian style columns that support it. You will walk through here to enter and exit the building. The portico actually belongs to an earlier Augustian period Pantheon which was mostly destroyed by the Great Fire of Rome in 64 AD. There is lastly an interesting fountain (1575) and obelisk (added in 1711) that are worth seeing at the Pantheon as well. In the end, this is must-do sightseeing for first time visitors to Rome. You can purchase entry tickets at the Pantheon directly or online prior to your visit. Allow 15-30 minutes to view the interior and a bit more time to view the exterior as well.

Our visit to the Pantheon with my family was a transcendent journey through time and space, immersing us in the awe-inspiring beauty and timeless elegance of one of Rome's most iconic monuments. From its majestic dome to its ancient mysteries, the Pantheon captivated our senses and left an indelible mark on our hearts.A Marvel of Ancient Engineering:As we approached the Pantheon, we were struck by its imposing facade and grandeur. Built over 2,000 years ago, this architectural marvel stands as a testament to the ingenuity and craftsmanship of ancient Rome. The sheer scale of the structure, with its towering columns and monumental portico, evoked a sense of wonder and reverence.Stepping Inside History:Entering the Pantheon's hallowed halls, we were transported back in time to the glory days of ancient Rome. The vast interior space, bathed in soft light filtering through the oculus, felt like a sanctuary of serenity and contemplation. We gazed in awe at the majestic dome, marveling at its perfect proportions and elegant design.Discovering Ancient Mysteries:Our visit to the Pantheon was a journey of discovery, as we unraveled the mysteries and legends surrounding this ancient monument. We learned about the history of the Pantheon, from its origins as a temple dedicated to all the gods to its transformation into a Christian church in the Middle Ages.We marveled at the engineering feats that enabled the construction of the dome, with its innovative use of concrete and cleverly designed coffers. Each detail, from the intricate marble floors to the celestial motifs adorning the interior, spoke to the genius of the ancient architects who created this masterpiece.Awe-Inspiring Moments and Shared Memories:As we explored the Pantheon together, we shared moments of wonder and reflection that will stay with us forever. We stood in silent awe beneath the oculus, watching sunlight stream through the opening like a divine spotlight illuminating the sacred space below. We whispered secrets to each other, marveling at the acoustic marvels of the dome that carried our voices to the heavens.Outside, we lingered in the Piazza della Rotonda, soaking in the vibrant atmosphere and basking in the beauty of the Pantheon's majestic presence. We snapped photos to capture the memories, but no camera could fully capture the magic of our shared experience.Our visit to the Pantheon was a journey of discovery, enlightenment, and shared wonder that transcended time and space. As we bid farewell to this ancient marvel, we carried with us not only memories of its beauty and grandeur but also a profound appreciation for the enduring legacy of ancient Rome.

The Pantheon is truly a structure that has stood the test of time. It is one of the best preserved monuments of Ancient Rome, in large part because it has been in continuous use since its completion around 128 AD, first as a temple for Roman gods before being converted to a church. The rotunda building is connected to the granite-columned portico via a rectangular vestibule. The star attraction is undoubtedly the coffered concrete dome ceiling with an oculus (central opening) to the sky. Amazingly, it was still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome 2,000 years after it was built. As the expression of Rome's glorious past which has gone on to influence countless buildings across Europe and the Americas throughout history, the Pantheon is worth every Euro (only €5) and every waiting time that you have to go through just to enter the building and appreciate its magnificence and beauty.

Built in 27 BC as a temple dedicated to all the Olympian gods, the Pantheon is an extraordinary example of Roman architecture. The Pantheon's dome is one of the largest ever built in antiquity, and its central oculus, a large circular opening, allowed natural light and, according to some, symbolizes the connection between the temple and the sky. After initially being a pagan temple, it was transformed into a Christian church in the 7th century. This change in function helped preserve the structure for future generations. The interior houses the tombs of important historical personalities, including Raffaello Sanzio, the famous Renaissance painter. From July this year the entrance fee is 5 euros for adults and 3 euros for those aged 18 to 25, previously it was free

We visited the Pantheon mid-week around mid-afternoon.  From my advance reading, I was under the impression that this was free to enter. However, at some point they have begun charging 5 Euros to get in.  We had a city pass that purported to cover the Pantheon, so I asked a staff member about it. She said I would need to exchange the voucher for a ticket between two shops in the same plaza.  I walked over there but all that existed between the shops was an alley with plywood over the entrance.  It reminded me of a scene from one of the later Harry Potter movies, where there is a row house that is enchanted so that it is invisible to most people. I was still puzzling over this when my wife showed up, having read some fine print on the pass' site description. It referenced a company rep with an orange umbrella. We found her and got our tickets for the Pantheon (and a reservation for Castel Sant'Angelo, which we planned to visit afterwards). With that, we were able to enter the Pantheon at no additional charge. The interior is a bit dark, lit mostly by the doorway plus light from the oculus in the roof.  Architecturally, it was stunning.  We spent a while strolling the perimeter and seeing who was buried here (the artist Raphael, Queen Margherita for whom the pizza is named, the first King of reunified Italy, etc.). We also took a lot of photos.  It was also crowded inside.  I'm sure it would be even more so in the summertime. They had the center part roped off for some reason (it would likely be wet if there had been recent rain, given the oculus). We also had to dodge people trying odd photo techniques (putting cameras on the floor to take a vertical selfie with the roof, etc.). Also, this is a functioning church, so there could be times when worship services are being conducted.I was very glad we made time to see the Pantheon.  There are so few structures that remain basically intact from ~ 2000 years ago.  Marveling at the dome's architecture and the overall sense of history was great.

The Pantheon was praised by Michelangelo as "the design of an angel". In 609 AD, the Byzantine Emperor dedicated the Pantheon to Pope Boniface IV, who renamed it the Church of Our Lady and Martyrs. Santa Maria ad Martyres﹞, which is also the official name of the Pantheon today. The Pantheon, the "Temple of the Gods" in Rome, is the crystallization of ancient Roman architectural art. Its majesty and dome interior have become a symbol of Rome itself. So you have to visit inside to appreciate its true size and beauty. The hole above the dome is the only source of light. This hole is also the only lighting point of the Pantheon, so that the light leaks from the top and moves with the position of the sun. And changing the angle of the light gives people a sacred and solemn feeling.

The Pantheon makes the strongest impression if you approach it from behind or from the side. And then, instead of a neat facade that closes the picture area, you will see powerful walls that seem to be rooted in the ground, covered with holes and scars. It is here that you begin to believe in the two-thousand-year age of this stocky structure. Augustus's son-in-law Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa founded the temple of all the gods here in 27 BC. True, the first building was square. And the Pantheon became round already under Hadrian (118-126). Since no one fully understood how to build a roof over such a huge structure, the inside of the building was filled with earth, to which the emperor ordered gold coins to be mixed. When the work was completed and the inscription “M.” remaining from the original version was installed on the façade. AGRIPPA I.F. COS. TERTIUM FECIT” (“Marcus Agrippa, son of Lucius, three times consul, made”), Hadrian announced that everyone who found the coin could take it for themselves - and in a matter of minutes the inside of the temple became empty and clean. However, this legend could have been put into circulation by Renaissance architects, who spent weeks on end taking measurements in the hope of understanding the secret of the dome. The Pantheon managed to survive until the beginning of the Renaissance in almost intact form thanks to Emperor Phocas, who in 609 donated the building to Pope Boniface IV. The Pope immediately rededicated the temple of all the gods to the Madonna and all the martyrs, transporting 28 carts with the remains of the first Christians from the catacombs, and in gratitude to the emperor he placed a column in the Forum. Later pontiffs tried in every possible way to tame the pagan building - Urban VIII even ordered Bernini to add a pair of bell towers along the edges of the pediment, but the idea turned out to be extremely unsuccessful, the structure was called “donkey ears” and was scolded until it was finally demolished at the end of the 19th century. The same Urban ordered bronze decorations of the pediment to be placed on the canopy in San Pietro. The church is still active here. The cool thing is that, if you wish, you can even get married here with a view of the modest grave of Raphael and the magnificent tombstones of the first Italian kings - Victor Emmanuel and his son Umberto. Previously, there were more monuments; almost all the artists of Rome wanted to rest next to Raphael. At some point, Pope Pius VII even had to clear the place, sending some of the busts to the museum on the Capitol (they left Baldassare Peruzzi, Annibale Caracci and the composer Corelli). But the most interesting thing here is not the monuments, no matter how many there are, but the dome.☝️ Its height is equal to the diameter - 43.44 meters and you can fit an ideal sphere into the interior of the temple. From the inside you can see that the dome does not have a lantern, like in later Christian churches. In the very center there is an uncovered hole, under which a rather impressive puddle accumulates when it rains. But at any other time, the lighting effects are simply amazing. The spectacle of snowflakes spinning in a whirlpool of light is especially mesmerizing - if you are suddenly caught in the snow in Rome❄️❄️❄️, grab your camera and head straight to the Pantheon. An interesting fact, there is a legend that in the original version there was no hole in the dome and it was allegedly pierced by evil spirits who were rushing around in search of a way out at the sounds of the first mass. And lastly, the most reliable way to get to the Pantheon without wandering through the alleys for too long, this is to turn off Via Corso, 305 behind Palazzo Doria Pamphilj, and then straight ahead, you won’t miss it.☝️

The Pantheon is my number 3 in Rome after the Colosseum and the Roman Forum. This is a must see. The 2nd century building commissioned by Emperor Hadrian is one of the best-preserved buildings from ancient Rome. Its size is impressive. The portico with columns seems to be glued to the round building and does not fit in well with it. The Pantheon was once the largest structure covered with a concrete dome in the world. The building is simple and made of good materials, giving the entire interior a unique character. You have to queue for tickets but it doesn't take too long. While standing in line, we admire the huge columns supporting the portico, which was commissioned by Marcus Agrippa. The dome with a hole in the middle makes an amazing impression. The light comes in. The interior is a mix of architectural styles. There are grave chapels in semicircular niches, including: kings Victor Emmanuel II and Humbert I and Raphael. I recommend this facility

An undisputed masterpiece of architecture and engineering, the circular Roman-style building, built with bricks and concrete, is composed of a circular hall covered by a hemispherical dome. The oculus (the eye), a circular hole with a diameter of 9 meters, located at the top of the large dome, is the central opening from which the light radiates and expands the space inside in an engaging and intriguing way. The visit to the Pantheon, to discover the hidden mysteries of its history, attracts visitors from all over the world for the magnificence of its proportions, the majesty of the dome and the architectural peculiarity. Entering the Pantheon is an emotion every time... and not just for the eyes.
