We met with Elaine today  She  is very experienced, knowledgeable, and fun who delivered an amazing experience at all three main venues at the Vatican, Vatican Museums, Sistine Chapel, and St Peters. She obviously studied the history of so many works of art and side facts that enriched the tour. She kept checking with our small group for affirmation to move to other levels of our interest. Truly a great tour! Highly recommend Elaine! David

Buy the tickets online and breeze past the crowd.. it’s not worth standing in the sun in summer.. we Googled it and found a great “ how to buy” site that talked us through it.. the payment system can be tricky as it’s in Italian but persist as we literally walked right in past all the tours and the people waiting … the site in my option is THE place to see for Roman antiquity, even though it has been slightly hijacked by being made into a church. The sheer size and materials used, the mathematical genius to build it.. unmatched in my option.. I like the collosuem but I will always love the pantheon
Definitely a must while in Rome.very amazing and magical place.very well maintained.they only charge a small fee of 5 euro

I am so glad to have visited the Pantheon. It is a true historical and architectural marvel of antiquity. The audio tour is well worthwhile and gives wonderful background information on this site.The only issue was that this is a site requiring modest dress and no photography and yet there were plenty af people waking around with bare shoulders and short shorts taking photos and video everywhere! The supposed guards who should be enforcing this seemed oblivious!

The well preserved Pantheon, in the center of Rome, is essentially a 2 component circular building featuring a rotunda with a colossal domed ceiling rising from it's walls and a front porch with supporting Corinthian columns that dramatically greets visitors and gives access to the main building through the monster doors.I found the structure of the Pantheon remarkable: for it's size, design and engineering construction. One of the architectural feats revealed in it's interior is dome. The dome mathematics are: a 142' diameter, a rise of 71' above the base and yet there is no evidence of arch support .....free form.It is thought that the quality of the mortar used in the concrete for the dome contributed to the success of it's stability (still around to-day). At the top of the dome sits an opening (oculus) referred to as the "Eye of the Pantheon" which has no cover....it lets light in.....as well as the weather.Presently the Pantheon generally exists in its original form and is today a source of architectural inspiration, know-how and is still a modern working church.
Pantheon is good from history point of view but nothing spectacular inside. It was too crowded when we visited - during the second half the day.

You now have to pay (free under 18’s) it is only €5 but I wouldn’t queue when you get there you can buy a ticket online on the website, wait a few minutes and then go straight through. The queue to buy tickets was pretty big! There are also lots of touts saying you need to buy shawls and can’t go in if your shoulders are uncovered. THIS IS A SCAM! You can go in with bare shoulders so don’t buy any scarfs unless you actually want one.It’s really pretty inside and although popular and busy it’s not too crowded. A must see when in Rome

Planning to visit Rome for few days then the Pantheon is a place to visit. The beauty of the hall is worth the visit. Seeing the largest unsupported dome in the world is enough to queue  outside in the heat.
We arrived early. There were no queues but it was very busy later in day. Arrive early! We also got a guided tour so we could appreciate the building and its history

Tickets now required as of the beginning of July 2023. The website was not working to buy direct but may be possible to buy through a broker with up charge. We had to arrange through the hotel and then at the door they made us buy new tickets. If I went back, I'd investigate further and make sure you have tickets in addition to a tour!

Worth a visit as the inside of the dome is spectacular and because of its influence on ancient and modern architecture. However, there is not much to see inside other than that. Pre-book timed tickets online or you will spend more time queuing to get in than you will actually inside. You can book tickets on the Musei Italiani website (search for musei italiani pantheon).

Alas, admission to the Pantheon is no longer free as from 3 July 2023. Even if there was a queue, you were inside very quickly.Now there is an admission fee, it had made the queue system worse, so unless you are able to get there early or buy a skip the line ticket, please avoid during the rest of the day. Therefore my images are only external shots of the building.

Highly recommend weekdays. The line moves quickly and entry is free. However, please remember to wear clothes that covers knees and shoulders, we saw quite a few people denied entry purchasing scarves from street vendors to be able to get in.

I recommend seeing it, but it might be better to skip entering. The line to get in can be quite long. By the time you finally get to buy tickets, you might realize that you've already seen everything there is to see.

Just being there in the Pantheon amidst the history and architecture was amazing. Booked on the website directly and had no waiting time outside. Arrived an hour earlier anticipating waiting in the line (queue) but was allowed inside upon showing the print out of the ticket. The audio guide was definitely worth it. The staff makes an effort to make visitors feel welcome and valued. They also appreciate the sanctity of the place and will advise maintaining a quiet ambiance when necessary (which is a good thing in my opinion).

The last time I was in Rome I found the Pantheon by accident but that was 35 years ago. So we followed our map and found the Pantheon but this time you had to queue to go in there were hundreds of people queuing groups and individuals. I was not prepared to waste an hour queuing as I had seen it before.

Who in their right mind would implement a new entrance fee and procedure in peak season? Only in Rome. What was the hurry? This monument has been there over two thousand years, I can’t believe a few months delay would have made much of a difference. The Cultural Minister should address these concerns as his website, the one which issues tickets to the Pantheon , was down all day long yesterday. When it finally came back up it said there were no more tickets. Totally disappointing as I had already purchased a tour for 10 on the official Pantheon site. then realized entrance tickets weren’t included. Total disorganization. Dear Cultural Minister, if you’re going to turn Rome into Disney World , at least do it right .
What an absolute waste of money. Not worth it at all. We waited over an hour to get inside and then had to wait in another line to get the audio equipment. Very disorganized!

We ordered the entrance (free) online, about a week or two before arrival, we didn't want any surprises.When we arrived there, it looked like the most popular club in Rome - plenty of people (without tickets) trying to get in.The Pantheon itself is stunning piece of ancient genius architecture with later art, including paintings, sculptures, and tombs of some of Italy's most famed persons.

Took less than 2 minutes to get in during peak tourist season. It's free. Takes around 10-15 min inside. Incredible to think this building has been standing for that long! Make sure you dress modestly or the security will not let you in.

When we visited my niece who was studying abroad in Rome, the first place she showed us was the Pantheon.  This ancient temple/church is a sacred space and an architectural marvel.  Touring this peaceful and inspiring site was a grand way to begin our Roman adventure in February 2014.

We visited Pantheon independantly(Booked online) not with a tour Guide. It was Fabulous but if you have booked an Audio Tour online do not stand in the queue show your tickets at the middle door and be admitted imediately
Amazing historic place, worth a quick stop; interesting that sometimes in the morning there are no lines at all but typically in the afternoon the line to enter can get quite long

The 3 star rating is not of the Pantheon itself but the buying of a ticket, or in our case not the buying of a ticket.Went online to buy, searched for Pantheon tickets, bought an audio tour which did not state was not including the entrance ticket.  HD to select a specific time.  What lunatic would sell an audio tour without the entrance ticket!You have to turn up at least 90 minutes before your audio tour booking to get an entrance ticket.Shambolic process which someone has not thought through.Hopefully the process changes so when you go to buy the audio tour it prompts you to also buy an entrance ticket.Guys this is not rocket science!

They led you to believe during online booking that audio guide include the entrance ticket. It doesn't. The 10 euro audio guide is way overpriced. And we had to wait in the sun for about 45 minutes. The stuff was rude and unpleasant.

You do not want to miss going inside. On my last trip to Rome many years ago I decided not to go in and only saw it from the outside. Major mistake on my part. The Pantheon needs to be on your list of places to visit in Rome.

I am fully aware that The Pantheon has only this week started ticketing, but it should be noted that the edition of tickets made the experience a bit worse for wear. We stood in a queue for almost 45 minutes and upon reaching the front, got overtaken by three groups of people being ushered to the front because they had booked the audio guide and didn’t need to queue. Despite asking directly if I needed to queue, I was directed to join the queue and wait despite also having pre-booked the audio guide. Staff were sharp, rude and generally projecting stress onto visitors. It’s not advised to shout in someone’s face that they’re blocking the line, after they’ve queued for no reason, and have paid to be there!Ruined what would have been a lovely experience really.
This was great - definitely a must if you are going to Rome. I would suggest the audio tour, it was great.

We visited the Pantheon today (5th July 2023), which is just the third day that you now have to buy a ticket to enter (previously it was free). Beforehand I bought tickets to the guided tour on the official pantheon website (not the audio guide, an actual tour with a person) and also bought entry tickets online. These were provided to me in two separate ticket PDFs. However even if you buy tickets online, we were still told to wait in the line for the tickets. Please note when facing the pantheon there are two lines. At the time we were there, the line on the right was for buying tickets with a credit card. The one on the left was for paying with cash.We started waiting in line at 2:05pm ahead of our 3pm guided tour time. When we were close to the entry doors a staff member checked our tickets and then escorted us to the front of the line, saving us maybe 10 minutes more waiting.  Another staff member scanned our entry tickets again and then gave us a paper ticket that a guard then ripped off. Not sure what the point of that was. The system seemed a bit chaotic and they clearly are still ironing out issues. We were directed to the audio guide counter to show our ticket for the guided tour. At this point it was 2:45pm.After all that we were told to wait by the audio guide counter and then our guide met us at exactly 3pm. We were the only ones in the tour so we had a private tour! Our guide was fantastic and clearly very passionate. She also had great historical diagrams and photos on her ipad that were very interesting to me as an engineer.Overall the experience was great and the Pantheon is well worth visiting. However I've taken a star off because of the hectic ticketing system and that we still had to wait 40 minutes in the line even though we'd bought 'reserved' tickets online.

Beautiful building, which is a must to visit. We went on Friday evening, not long before it closed. Queue was not too long and we were in within a few minutes. They were quite strict on the dress code so glad we researched before we went. Knees and shoulders must be covered. Lots of lovely bards and restauarnts in the area too and prices were very reasonable!
With the switch to paying for entry things are very disorganized. They purposely make it difficult to purchase tickets on line so you pay more for a visit.
No one to tell us where to go so very confusing. Get in the general admission line then get your audio equipment. Not sure we really needed to pay for a reservation.

The Pantheon is always impressive with its carved ceiling, thinking about the craftsmanship to build such a structure 2000 years ago is just mind blowing. We enjoyed looking around both the inside and the exterior, though the interior was incredibly crowded. There are now metal corrals for tourists to queue in (weren't there when we visited 10 years ago) which help somewhat but also detract from the front steps of the building where you used to be able to stand or sit between the pillars while admiring the giant front doors.

The building is stunning, but the organisation is a complete shambles. Having just changed their booking system, it appeared that no one had a clue what was going on.  Having queued for an hour for my allotted time, I had my pass scanned on my phone to be made to wait until they printed me a paper ticket which was immediately taken by a man standing next to where they were printing tickets - the purpose the entirely unclear. I was then told to go to queue for the audio guide, which they wouldn’t give me to unless I left my identity documents with them. In no way would I ever leave my identity documents with a random person…. I went without, so all in all, the worst experience in Rome which was a shame as I’m sure it’s an interesting place.

We visited today, which was the day after the paid ticketing system was introduced. It is a complete mess. Obviously the Pantheon itself is incredible, but I have given it a 1 star review because as of today, this new system is a shambles. We queued for an hour & then got told we were in the line for the audio guide. I told them numerous times we didn’t want the audio guide & that I had bought tickets online, even showing them the tickets. They laughed at us (& others in the queue in the same situation) & told us we had the audio guide tickets (we didn’t) & to stay in the line. Eventually we just walked in because no one had a clue what they were doing & people who hadn’t bought tickets were walking past us, buying tickets & gaining entry. They really need to sort it out, the queue was ridiculous by the time we left on account of the disorganization.
Booking the audio tour in advance made it much quicker on the day. Good info, the narrators were kind of dull.  Amazing building, every nook and cranny.
The Pantheon is an amazing, ancient, architectural structure that is still wholly intact. I recommend the audio tour. It’s very informative. The Pantheon is a definite must-see.

The Pantheon was free to enter and was absolutely amazing. Huge cathedral highlighting Romes transition from Paganism to Christianity. Highlights;The resting place for Rafael do Santi. Beautiful church with enormous dome.

Incredible feeling sitting in the oldest building in the world which is still in use! Few tips for visiting this place:1. Avoid visiting on weekends or public holidays. While there is no ticket but there is pre-booking required on weekends or public holidays. This can be done on their website at no charge.2. Be prepared to stand in the queue for upto an hour to enter, but the wait is worth it!3. Do sit there inside for getting a complete perspective and the environment to soak in.The place where Julius Caesar was killed is nearby too, don't miss visiting that.
The Pantheon is not to be missed! It was amazing and awe inspiring! The self guided audio tour was informative and engaging to an adult. I highly recommend!

The Pantheon in Rome is truly a sight to behold! From the grandeur of its impressive architecture to the ancient artifacts and breathtaking views, it is a must-see for all visitors to the Eternal City. Its grandeur and splendor will leave you in awe, and its ancient artifacts will give you a glimpse into the past. The Pantheon is an incredible testament to the grandeur of Rome and its history, and a visit to this incredible site is sure to be an unforgettable experience.

I have to first admit, being in Rome for the first time, I was unaware that the Pantheon would have limited accessibility on Christian Mass days, so anyone looking to go in should plan to go on a day other than Sunday or Wednesday. My fiancé and I booked a tour because we wanted to see the inside of the Pantheon during our short stay in the city and as I mentioned before, access was unavailable on Sunday when we first went. We booked the tour through viator for about 25 euros a person and it was well worth it. Our guide Giulia (hopefully I'm spelling it right) was informative and provided a lot of information we wouldn't have gotten touring on our own. She even let me take a picture of one of her iPad photos! I'm so glad we were able to fit in this experience during our short stay in Rome.

Friendly staff and easy to understand audio tour. I didn't give it 5 stars because the audio tour didn't follow a path around the building. So we heard info on one area then had to turn around to another area.

My husband I walked over from our hotel one evening. Not far from Trevi Fountain which was like a zoo it was so crowded. Lines were short, free entry. Just get in the free line. It moves quickly. Quiet and contemplative. Cool relief from the heat. Not overly crowded in the evening.

We went on our own.  Don't miss the opportunity to walk into this incredible building that was build/re-built a good 1900 years ago!  It's crazy how stunning it is and how people that long ago created such a beautiful and amazing place.

We visited on our own during the week and were able to get in to walk around for free after about a 10 minute wait in a fast moving line.  We returned on Saturday 6/10 and were told at the front of the line all tickets had been sold.  A guide offered for us to join his group, which we did and they provided a headset for each and shawls for ladies with bare shoulders. The tour was amazing, and our guide was very informative, showing pictures on her phone of solar events on certain dates, and reviewing the architecture, history, constructions theories, materials and more.  I highly recommend but don’t have the contact information as we paid cash euro afterwards (significantly cheaper than the Venmo price).

Nicely to walk around is the spacious & grand circular floor which is likely "one of marble" (although not to walk through the floor for some reason) but really not much time is needed I would say with just "pretty plain" altar & small alcoves in the wall among which is the tomb of Victor Emmanuel II the "1st king" of Italy (all plain with only statues of saints & such and with nothing else of additional decorations).  And apart from looking around is also to look up to the interior of the "sizeable dome" above with some elaborate designs & such, and actually also pretty impressive to look up not inside but just before entering this temple are these "great pillars" of nice lengths & sizes (with "some time" to view those pillars in my case since there was a line-up to enter starting from the small square outside then through the pillars to get to the temple entrance).Located at just about a 15-minute walk north from the Victor Emmanuel II Monument building (with "2 chariot sculptures" on its top & a number of statues around the building), the visit to this temple was followed by those to: the Loyola Church (with a beautiful "ceiling mural" which can also be viewed in the "slanted mirror" on the floor); the Trevi Fountain (with a set of great statues at a little higher up and water flowing from under them to the pool a little below); and the Spanish Steps (a "pretty wide" staircase between buildings along both sides and with a small church all the way at the top).  And yes, the area overall is also "quite a crowded one" with many narrow streets which are also lined with plenty of souvenir shops (kind of an "old town" area I would say with many colorful heritage buildings but among them also some grand-stately ones with front pillars & such).

This is so worth it! Even though the Pantheon is free (for now) there is a good chance you'll be stuck in line in the hot sun for a hour or so just to get in. Because this tour is also affiliated with the Pantheon, if there's a long line to get in (like there was when we arrived), you can just walk past all those suckers and go right on in. There were a lot of people walking around with their ears to their phones listening to self-guides, but I could tell they were jelly that we had an actual historian as our guide. Our guide was very enthusiastic and full of so much information - we asked so many questions and she had an answer for all of them. You could tell how much passion she has for the Pantheon and Rome in general.

The visit was great. Brief, concise, enlightened. Here the audio guide is enough, unlike the Vatican where the audio is long and boring, favor a guide. And just remember to book your visits to avoid queuing in full sun.

What appears as merely a boring building on the exterior transforms into a scintillating masterpiece on the inside. Entry is free, and history/religion buffs will be delighted right away. I learned so much from my visit that I never knew I would know. Dress appropriately and use a quiet voice to maintain a pleasant atmosphere for everyone.A top-three attraction in Rome!

A decent audio tour of the Pantheon. On arrival you take your booking details to the audio tour desk and receive your audio device and earphones.  Just to note that we were asked for some ID to act as security to make sure you hand back the device at the end. We were fine with this and used a driver's license, but just a note to anyone considering the tour to make sure you have some form of ID on you.The audio tour was informative with a number of points of interest together with a map to show you where you should be looking.  There was a lot of detail on the Pantheon itself and its history as well as a number of sculptures and the interesting design points of the building, particularly the dome and the oculus. The only disappointing element was that 2 of the points of interest were outside the main doors, however we were told that we couldn't exit with the audio devices so you just have to try and get a decent view from inside by the doors (which isn't easy with the number of people entering and exiting) or commit the detail to memory and try to look at it on the way out.  The information provided ranges from 2 or 3 minutes on an item to 6 or 7 minutes on some of the more detailed points of interest. Personally I would have liked there to be a few more points of interest on the tour (if I recall there were around 15 in total) and there were a number of elements that could have had more detail included, but there seemed to be just enough information and detail to make the tour worthwhile, particularly if you've not been to the Pantheon before or know much about its history. The tour does allow you to take things in at your own pace rather than follow a guide so you can take in the superb detail and design slowly and quietly and give the Pantheon the reverence it deserves.
It's amazing, but I didn't realize we needed tickets to get in, unlike previous years when I have visitied!

Amazing architecture, this iconic building should not be missed. It is located in a beautiful piazza with a lovely fountain. Check out their website for when you need reservations.  We stood in line one afternoon - it moved quickly. Lots of neat restaurants surrounding the Pantheon that have great views of it.

The Pantheon is an architectural marvel, definitely worth standing in line to see. It is magnificent and, as the largest freestanding dome in the world,  something you will enjoy visiting. Don’t be deterred by the extremely long line — it moves very quickly.  You can spend as little as 5 minutes or as long as an hour in the dome. Absolutely beautiful!
The queue was not too bad on a Saturday. The audio guide made it much more interesting.  The building is magnificent and well worth the visit.

Unfortunately, my daughter and I could only see the outside. We were waiting in line and a woman told us that reservations were needed on Saturdays and Sundays.  It was also Pentecoste that added to the lines. I'm glad that she mentioned it, since we would have been turned away after waiting in line.  Had I known reservations were needed, I would have made one before we left the US.  I had read it was free and there was no mention of weekend reservations.

Took a private tour with a local guide.  It was spectacular, not only did we see inside, we felt like our guide brought us back in time to fully understand the pantheon.  No cost to enter, so worth the visit

Where else can you step inside a massive and beautiful building that is nearly 2000 years old for free (I say this as there is talk about charging entrance – but who knows how long that may take)?  Probably not many options.  The lines can be long, but step in anyway.  They move very fast since there are no tickets, etc. to worry about.  In its original design, it would have been covered in marble in many other places but that was removed a few centuries ago to build St. Peter’s Basilica.  It is still is phenomenal sight to see and is fantastically well preserved based on its age.

An example of exquisite ancient engineering built 2000 years ago. A marvelous dome.  This is a must-see site for any visitor to Rome. Admission is free but there is a line to get in which moves quickly. Though previously  this was a temple to the ancient pagan gods, it is currently a working Catholic Church and the visitor’s dress code is required and enforced. This cannot be missed.
Love this building but unfortunately so too do lots of tourists. Never disappoints particularly the ocular

BEWARE…This tour was falsely advertised as “Skip the Line”. That was NOT TRUE as you must join the one line for everyone until you enter the building and check in at the inside  Audio Tour Desk. We tried to visit on Sunday June 4th which unfortunately was a holiday with everyone out. The line was many blocks longs and we just skipped the tour and forfeited out ticket cost as a bad experience. I would like to get a refund as it totally false to show this as skip the line, but doubt that ever happens.
Don't miss this.  It surprised me that the inside is so well preserved and beautiful.  Great staff.  Good audio guide.  The organisers did everything right.  Best part of my trip to Rome.

This is one of the oldest buildings in the world & a MUST visit for every visitor to Rome; we were no exception. The first time we went was on a Sunday afternoon, to find out that at weekends, you have to book a time slot on line, and everything was full. We went back the following day early afternoon, bit of a queue outside, but well organised and soon inside. The place is breathtaking & a true wonder. The final resting place of 2 former Kings of Italy, and the painter Raphael. Take your time going round and enjoy
Just walk in.  It is AMAZING.  I've been there many times.  Right in the heart of town.  Look up to the ceiling and in every crevice.  Take amazing photos.

Go early in the morning to minimize the time waiting in queues.  We went in mid-May at around 10am and the wait was only around 15 minutes (or less).  Well worth seeing (but be wary of pickpocketing).

Pantheon is very interesting building and very nicely managing tourist as compare to other tourist area. In my experience in Italy, people who managing visitors in Pantheon is doing great job. And it is free to visit.
We went on the first Sunday of the month and it was free. Only had to queue for about 15 minutes. Don’t forget to cover shoulders.

We didn’t get to go in so this review is only in regards to the outside. I thought it was so cool to see. You’re walking through a city and suddenly walk right next to this beautiful ancient structure. There’s so much history to it! This is what I think of when I think of Ancient Rome. Definitely great to stop by and see!
It was very busy but the staff allowed my husband to bypass the queue as he has walking difficulties.  Incredibly helpful
The electronic guide was very extensive and easy to handle.Everything was easy to find,

Invisible. Also get informed right before arrival that they require a passport as a deposit, which is completely out of date when this is prepaid.Did not get anything out of this as we did not find out audio guide based on the description.

On a beautiful square of Rome not far from the fountain of Trevi that the Pantheon stands. There is always a queue at the entrance but quite fast because it is a fairly quick visit because the goal is the ceiling:sumptuous of this difficulty

I love the Pantheon and love the area. It is breathtaking! If you are visiting on the weekends (as of May 2023) you have to make a reservation, unless it the first Sunday of the month! Otherwise you just line up to go in!
A must do if you are in Rome.  Audio guide definitely enhanced the visit.   No long queues and easy access.  Nice vibe outside in the square.
Cheat pack: Audio guide directly available at a lower price. No skip-the-line included (not possible at church)
This was an incredible building. Very well preserved and free to enter. Whilst the queues look long, they move rapidly.Only issue is that the information is incredibly dense, so rook a while to read.
Perfect for a quick trip through.  I can't imagine spending any more time than this in that building unless you are in love with facts and historical information.

Pantheon Rome is a must-see for anyone looking to explore the rich history of Italy. Its grandeur and awe-inspiring interior have made it a popular tourist destination for centuries. The ancient artifacts and architecture of this magnificent structure are a testament to the grandeur of the Roman Empire. From the impressive dome to the intricate marble floors, visitors can't help but be in awe of this ancient wonder. Not only does it offer a unique glimpse into the past, but its interior is filled with ancient artifacts, including statues, frescoes, and paintings. It's no wonder why Pantheon Rome has been a popular destination for centuries. Its rich history and grandeur make it a must-see for anyone looking to explore the history and culture of Italy.

Despite the long queue it only took a few minutes to get in. The exterior of the building is impressive.  Inside is fascinating. Despite the number of people inside it was fairly  easy to get around. We arrived after midday  so missed the sun shining  vertically  through the roof. It would have been a better experience if people showed a little more respect as it is a religious site. Otherwise very recommended.
Beautiful, nothing more to add!!!! Difficult to give an opinion because everything is beautiful inside.
One of the wonders of the world. It is free and beautiful.  So much history.  Feels peaceful.  Good along with Roman Forum, etc.

This is an amazing ancient place. The beauty, history and amazing architectural accomplishment make this something we continue to enjoy visiting. I've been to it at least 5 times and my wife has seen it twice. It never disappoints.

A definite must! The line was long- but went quickly. It's a stunning piece of history. We didn't take a tour- but it was free to walk in. You can spend as much time as you want. It's amazing when you walk up to it.
A must see experience. Tonya was very knowledgeable and was eager to share extra interesting tidbits.
This is a must see. Very rich in history and the tour was very informative. Purchasing tickets ahead saved us 3 hours of waiting in line!
We had a very great time at Pantheon. The taffs r extremely friendly. The architecture is stunning! Much visit attraction!
Beautiful, the best preserved monument in Rome!! Beautiful frescoes, and currently houses the remains of King Vittorio Emanuele II, King Umberto I and Queen Margherita of Savoy.

Before you go to all those fountains, piazzas and cathedrals, go visit Pantheon first.  We went to Pantheon at least twice every time when we visit Rome.  Make sure you visit Raphael's tomb as well inside.
The line goes fast - don't miss it.  Very beautiful and interesting to see!  The concrete dome was made of a single piece of concrete.
Made a reservation on line. It is free but if you want to learn about the church they have different language audio guide for about €8.5.  Easy to do. Previously a Roman temple now a Catholic Church.

There was a very large queue that went around the square but it passed very quickly so don’t stop entering the queue. It was built by Adrian in 126 AD. C where there was previously a temple built by Agrippa.Inside you even have benches where you can sit and admire the largest mass concrete dome in history. It's even bigger than the one built in the Vatican that was based on this one. It has a diameter of 43.44 meters.In the center it opens to the sky where rainwater can enter, called OCULO and is 9 meters in diameter.Buried are Kings Vitorio Emmanuel II and his son Humberto I as well as the painter Raphael.

Wow! The architecture and the art of this ancient temple turned church is breathtaking. The lines are long but move very fast (about 15 minutes to get in) and although there are large crowds inside, it does not feel like it thanks to the massive size of the building. I have always wanted to see the pantheon and am so glad that I was able to. When we visited it was still free entry, although they are going to start charging a fee soon. You need to make reservations on weekdays and holidays, but during the week you do not need reservations.

The Pantheon is a beautiful building because it has been in continuous use. So, though ancient it is in great repair. A very interesting tour.  There was a long line to get in when we arrived but it went very quickly.

A must see, and they didn't charge the fee yet. If you go on Saturdays or Sundays, you need to have reservations. We went on a Tuesday, and it was packed but only waited in line, maybe 10 minutes. Beautiful inside.

We toured the Pantheon on a self-guided tour using the Rick Steves free audio tour through Rick Steves Audio Europe app.  The experience was more impressive than I thought it would be. The dome is incredible and the overall space is awe inspiring, making it easy to imagine ancient people worshiping pagan gods in the space. Highly recommend viewing.  It was also free.  Go early in the am to avoid some of the crowds.

Built by (or more accurately “ordered to be built by”) the Emperor Hadrian, and preceded by a similar temple commissioned over a century earlier during the rule of Augustus, the Pantheon was finished around 126 AD and has impressed millions of people ever since.  While it was roughly meant to pay homage to the pagan gods, it was subsequently given to Pope Boniface IV in 609 by the Byzantine emperor Phocas and consecrated as a church soon thereafter.  It remains an active place of Christian worship as well as an even-more-active magnet for tourists from around the world.  Numerous dignitaries are buried inside, and the structure is simply and visually ethereal.  Entry is free but ticketed on the weekends as of this review, although I did hear murmurings of fees being charged in the future.I visited twice during my week in the city.  The first was on a Saturday with a timed ticket and walking tour previously purchased for 3:00PM.  I was allowed to enter early although unfortunately the tour never materialized, and I wasn’t given a reason why.  I spent maybe an hour inside viewing everything in a clockwise fashion, jockeying around static storyboards (the line for audio guides was prohibitively long) and trying to make sense out of the details of the interior.  I returned two days later on a Monday at opening; there were surprisingly even more people present, the audio guide counter was closed, the app didn’t download, and my time on site was even more brief.  I walked around the exterior of the building, admired the fountain and portico, and departed.In summary, I was equally impressed and frustrated by my two visits to the Pantheon.  I’m certainly realistic about organization in one of the Europe’s most frequented buildings in one of the world’s most visited cities, but this was just plain messy.  Disorganization in this case led to a subpar overall experience and I learned more about the structure on the flight home while I was typing this review.  Worth a visit?  Of course, it is a stunning structure.  Room for improvement?  Most definitely… here’s hoping that the powers-that-be make some adjustments as the world opens back up to tourism to make this a more fulfilling experience.

We had planned to just turn up and queue but weekends are booked tours only. Luckily, an English guy saw us look a bit lost and within 9 mins we were on the back of an English speaking tour for €15 each. Well worth the money, absolutely superb to be guided through, I think this might be my favourite place in Rome out of many favourites, just go.

One of the "musts" for any U.VA alum visiting Rome is to "check out" the Pantheon - after all, it so impressed Mr. Jefferson that he used its design as a model for The Rotunda, the centerpiece of his "academical village".  So, I was expecting to see an older version of The Rotunda.WRONG!  The volume of the open space of the Pantheon is nearly six times that of The Rotunda.  So, I was stunned by its sheer size.  No wonder Mr. Jefferson, like me, got a little weak in the knees when he first beheld it.At the time of its construction the Pantheon embodied advances in architecture and construction techniques that would not be matched for a millennium.  As it stands today, nearly 2,000 years after its construction, it is the only building from its era still serving its basic function.  Simply amazing; an eternal part of The Eternal City.

This is a former Roman temple and, since 609 AD, a Catholic church (Basilica di Santa Maria ad Martyres or Basilica of St. Mary and the Martyrs) in Rome. Originally  on the site of an earlier temple commissioned by Marcus Agrippa during the reign of Augustus, It was rebuilt by the emperor Hadrian and probably dedicated c. 126 AD. The building is cylindrical with a portico of large impressive granite Corinthian columns.  It is regarded as probably the best-preserved of all Ancient Roman buildings, mainly due to it being kept in continuous use. The Pantheon is free to visit, but you may have to stand in line as they control the amount of people inside at any one time. You can hire an audio guide for about 8.5 euro's or have the full guided tour for about 25 euro's. It is open every day 9.00 am – 7 pm (last entrance at 6.45 pm).

Arguably the engineering marvel of ancient Rome, this Roman temple is now a Catholic church and attracts tourists throughout the day. ...in construction but is the oldest in continuous use... always long lines during dayhours finally after 4 days (late afternoon) we saw a short line so we queue to see this wonder...Built as a pagan temple when converted into a Christian church in the early Medieval period saved the building from complete destruction and spoliation, but still some materials were taken from it to build elsewhere (external marble and bronze from the ceiling portico were recycled as cannons for St. Angelo castle). A nice place to see. The artwork is amazing and there is no top on the cieling. It is a church so you have to behave respectfully. It was very busy on Sunday,but during the week there is no waiting for entrance. And it is free entrance.

There was a line to get in but it moved very quickly and once inside you did not feel crowded. Take your time going around as there are so many things to read and look at - beautiful art, the history and if you are there long enough, the light changes so much with the opening in the dome - it is quite lovely.  We enjoyed exploring the entire building and getting more information on each of the individuals who are buried there. Do remember this is a church and men must remove their hats and hushed tones are used inside.
What beauty in one place. With the help of the audio guide you wander around discovering a thousand and more curiosities. An essential step

We always enjoy visiting the Pantheon when in Rome.  It's best to go early or late in the day to avoid all of the tour groups.  Read about the Pantheon in advance, and do your own self guided tour.  Rick Steves also has a free audio tour that you can download and listen to when visiting.
A must see in Rome, so went to this place. Lucky there was not a long queue. We had to wait for 15 minutes to take a look inside. You don't have to pay to get inside
One of the most recognizable and preserved ancient buildings in Rome - a temple to the Roman Gods and Goddesses.
It's free of charge to enter the Pantheon. We went in January and there were no queues. Interesting and a must do in Rome.
Recommend visiting this landmark but be careful around people trying to sell you bracelets. This doesn't take away from the beaty of the building, but it's important to note.
Free admission but you have every interest in taking an audio guide (1 for 2 is enough) because the comments are very interesting and complete the tour which otherwise is very fast.

My favorite building in Rome is the pantheon. Built in AD 120 by Emperor Hadrian it was originally a Roman temple and later a Christian church. The magnificent concrete dome was the largest until the Renaissance and was studied by Brunelleschi in Florence and Michelangelo for St Peter’s Basilica.
Looking up at the dome and sky from the inside was the best part of my experience.  I wonder how it would be when it is raining.

We have been to the Pantheon before, a long time ago, but thought we would call in again.  Amazingly it is still free to get in and there was no real queue in January.  It was quite crowded inside and seems to have much more limited access than our previous visit.  However, it is still an awesome building and well worth visiting.
We visited on a Wednesday, mid morning and didn't need to queue. Such a beautiful place with a wonderful history. Audio available but lovely to just sit and take in the surroundings.

One of Rome’s best known Attractions and not only is it relatively easy (10-15 minute walk from the Trevi Fountain) to find but there is no entry charge - probably as it is an active Church!Impressive from the outside but even better inside - not to be missed!!
Amazing! Absolutely worth going inside!!! We sat inside for ages just soaking it all in! Simply breathtaking and highly recommend visiting here!!

We didn’t book anything to visit during the day until we got to the place. I’m glad I didn’t as entering is free. There’s a QR code outside the front for you to scan, book your time (we waited outside for 30 minutes before our time slot was ready). I don’t think a guide is needed - you can pay €2 inside for an audio/video of the things your looking at and that was perfect for us. I would have been annoyed if I had payed for a tour when it’s free to enter!

Upon entering the Pantheon at the booked timeslot, you go to the left side to grab the audio guide. The people working behind the desk will ask for identification they will give you the audio guide. The audio guide itself was pretty descriptive with dretty thorough explanation of each section with history and meaning behind each of them.The architecture of the Pantheon itself was pretty amazing and especially that domed roof was totally the highlight. It's amazing how they stay durable despite all these centuries that passed.
Lovely stop after visiting some nearby landmarks. Beautiful architecture and Roman ruins. In awe of the grand structure

Do a bus tour £21/£23 a day well worth it hop on and off for 24 hours as many times as you want. It helps with getting your bearings too so you can then walk rome without missing the main attractions for the rest of your stay. Don’t ware your self out on a day time it’s as beautiful if not more on a night time. Food is very reasonable in pricing, if you need clothes or shoes plenty of shops so don’t stress before you go buy while you are there. Take comfortable footwear you will need it.  A fantastic place highlight St Peter’s basilica not to be missed. A city break to a weeks holiday both trips have not disappointed. Train fares are also reasonable if you want to do a day trip.
Over 2 centuries old and you walk through the door and your breath is taken away. Dont go to Rome and miss this.

You come across the Pantheon almost by accident, in an unassuming part of Rome and it's just magically before your eyes.  It is such a gem I almost feel there should be a fanfare to alert you to it's presence.  The Piazza in front of it has many touristy spots to eat and drink, I'm not usually inclined to do so but in the case of the Pantheon, we took a seat and ordered a drink just so we could bask in it's magic a little longer!

This is absolutely my favourite place in Rome. I love the little square in front of the pantheon, i love it inside, i love the roman columns in fron of it. It is absolutely fantastic. Try to go early in the morning in order to avoid the crowd.

This cathedral was marvelous and the structure was very well intact. This was my first time entering the Pantheon and the only words I could utter was WOW. You have to go see for yourself to see exactly what I'm talking about.

This is a relatively small experience overall. One visit is more than enough. What YOU should be prepared for is the MAN and his "workers" who control the entrance. WOMEN are not allowed to enter if the MAN running the entrance feels you have too much skin exposed. HE would like you to purchase a shawl from the children selling them who are running around the square hawking shawls. This past summer, HE refused my entry because he felt my ankle length dress that had a tie back was too exposing. While HE let in other women with strapless tops. I was body shamed and groopedby this MAN. He wanted me to purchase a shawl. A WOMAN helping run the line with HIM let me in and HE chased me down. Just be aware. I had visited the Pantheon previously several years prior. So, I knew how small it is and over hyped. Is it an ancient engineering feat? Yes. Is it worth buying an over priced shawl or being mauled by the MAN running the entrance, NO. Of note! A few hours prior, in the same outfit, I toured the Vatican without issue. Wander into the multiple free beautiful churches. Admire the obelisks. DO NOT pander to these people. Just be AWARE and INFORMED.

We saw the Pantheon in the morning before it got crowded. It was beautiful! We were able to get a sense of the history of the building and the beauty of the art. We had coffee across the piazza with a view of the Pantheon. What a thrill!
Waiting in line, we were approached to join a group tour and we are glad that we did. Tour guide was great, very funny. Inside the building is just amazing.
Awe inspiring building, really worth a visit for the sheer size and the grand dome with the opening in it. It was very crowded and we had to queue to get inside. No caps allowed.
I also visited the Pantheon internally and it was worth it very suggestive, admission is free but inside there are speakers where you can take an audio guide.

This was our second trip to the Pantheon (our first we 16 years ago so we didn't remember a lot of it) with our 14 year old daughter, for whom it was her first visit to this remarkable place. We opted to do the audio tour which was very informative and you could move at your own pace. It was busy but not crowded. The center of the Pantheon was fenced off, possibly due to the rain that had occurred in Rome over the preceding days and come through the oculus. The architecture and decoration is incredible. The pantheon is iconic and a must see if you're in Rome and haven't been inside.

This was a lovely experience. It's free, and the music was especially beautiful, but dress warmly and be prepared to wait in line a long time to get in. We arrived at 11 pm and there were already about a hundred people in front of us. We waited outside an hour an a half. Only the first couple of hundred people get seats, and the Mass lasts an hour. But we felt it was worth it. A spiritual experience in an iconic setting, even if you are not Catholic.
Very rewarding visit, free but I recommend taking the audio guide to have very interesting explanations (allow 25/30 mins). An audio guide for two is possible.

Took a guided tour (with a person), which I would recommend.  You will be in AWE that they built this place in the second century (it was completed by Emperor Hadrian) because the cupola and the interior are in such great shape.  Side note:  how did they build something like this in the 100's that is still kicking in 2022 when anything we build today crumbles after 100 years?
Kicked off Rome by visiting the Pantheon, remarkable and referenced many time throughout the stay in Rome.
No need to take tickets before because entry is free and fast. Guided audio pays for itself. Install the app and there you go...

You could say that the splendour of the Pantheon dwarfs the arrogance of its employees. For an audiogide you have to give up your passport or hand in your credit card which then disappear into a filthy bin with other papers! If you indicate that you do not agree you can go to your own country is said! Why Tourist friendly
there are no words to describe this monument, go and feel the atmosphere , its always crowded, just ignore and soak it up
It’s another church on the face of it but actually it’s so much more. The architecture is a key interest and worth a visit.
amazing visit and amazing history abound in this building.  Entry is free, but we opted for the audio tour that made it a great site to see.

it was amazing and rlly cool but extremely crowded and a long line to get in even tho the line moved fast. lots of tourists. amazing old building and the oculus was incredible to see super cool overall would recommend going early morning to avoid crowds!

The construction could have been ordered by Emperor Trajan (110s) or it could have been Emperor Hadrian (Trajan's first cousin 1x removed). I always suggest delving into the history before you reach the site. Everything is much more interesting. I should add that the lighting is beautiful and I have excellent photos.
I could not explore much as I visited Pantheon in the evening (after sun set). Nice and calm place to spend some quality and quiet time
I saw Panteon Only outside. I didn’t have time because i was in Rome Only few hours. I know that I will come here one more time because I this was amazing experience.

You can't walk more than 10 minutes in Rome without stumbling on a historic masterpiece. All of the historic places are humbling - and provide wonderful backdrops for photos. It's another well marked location so it can get pretty busy.
We went to a mass and it magical, the sound was fantastic. Well worth doing, not everyday you can hear mass in a Roman temple.
The price was right free. You go inside and look at all the beautful paintings and sculpturs it is worth your time.

Let me start by saying the venue itself was breathtakingly beautiful. The audio was provided via a smartphone with headsets, and overall very informative, however, I overheard in-person guides giving a tour, and the level of information was much wider and easier to digest. overall the trip was good and the pantheon was amazing.
Everybody needs to see the Pantheon and wonder how was it built so long ago….with rudimentary technology.
It’s monumental to be in its historic places and soak in it 😍😍😍😍😍🇮🇹🇮🇹It’s to be seen and done again beautiful
Spent time in the building and read Rick Steeves' write up of the building. Excellent explanation and made the visit much more enjoyable. The write up is available on an app.
Be prepared for long lines to get in. It is free to enter and it depends if you want to buy an audiobook. You can enjoy without it.

For how massive and impressive the Pantheon is from the outside you can actually tour the inside rather quickly— as it’s all one large space. Although it was built in 125AD, it became a Catholic Church in the 600s, and is still a functioning church with services to this day. Entry is free, but you currently need a timed-entry ticket to go inside the Pantheon. There are signs out front with QR codes you can scan to pick your window of time. We scanned in the morning and were able to get a 2pm time slot. You can also attend a service on Saturday night (5pm) or Sunday morning (10:30am.) — no tickets required. It has lovely church bells that ring on the hour. Even if you don’t go inside, walking around the building is breathtaking and unforgettable. The surrounding square and fountain are beautiful and have lots of food, coffee and gelato options. The Pantheon is lit it up beautifully at night as well, which is worth seeing in person.

You've seen it in books and in movies but NOTHING can prepare you for the first time you see the Pantheon in person. Wow. So amazing. There was a small line when we got there but within just a few moments we were inside. So impressive.

A truly beautiful, intricate monument - even though it is of course a popular tourist landmark, it was an incredibly peaceful atmosphere. Don't forget to look up at the ceiling!I recommend going early in the day - no queues (we went in December). We also didn't go for the tour, although this is quite usual for us - we just prefer to go at our own pace. Entry is free, and you're close to the Trevi Fountain too.Make it a place to visit when you're in Rome!
Yet again another thing that you should do while in Rome . Really impressive . Go and drink in the history . Visually stunning . The area around it is a typical tourist trap ..

The grandeur and ambiance, makes you disappointed at the lousy and boring architecture common today. What design, what uncompromising vision to create such a temple.I have been to Rome many times and this is always on my list to visit again and again.
I love it, even if I had to get a french tour guide for two of my friends, I did enjoy the walk and the magic from the place
So beautiful. We were in awe the entire time. Amazingly it's free to access year-round, but you need to have a reservation to enter on Sundays as they hold a mass (free too, but sells out fast!)
You’re here, check it off.! Vast and beautiful. Kinda tucked away but in the middle of everything.Architecture and history is mind blowing.
Free entrance, massive artifacts, impressive atmosphere. You should not miss it, when in Rome; you have to visit!
Absolutely breathtaking. The atmosphere inside, the structure, the details. We had seen photos of it and reconstructions but in person it is something else!
The most ancient temple in Rome. It's the unice one with it's architecture design. Wonderful the same trample Is standing more then 2500 years... Just a wow
A must see while in Rome; try to go in the off hours when it is less crowded and the lines to get inside are shorter.
very interesting place to visit - we were on a guided walking tour and it did help to have someone explain the history
There was so much information and the voice on the audio was very nice to listen to, didn’t have to que long and the experience was amazing
you should come when it's raining its beautiful. I went early morning and was 8th in the line. The Pantheon is breathtaking
It was beautiful outside, but we did not get to go in as tickets were already sold out. When we go back we will be ready!!!

We were lucky to be able to wander in one weekday evening when there were no queues.  If you have limited time in Rome and want to see this masterpiece of Ancient Roman engineering, book well in advance.
Beautiful experience visiting this majestic building. Inside you can see some tombs of the rulers of the Savoy house.

We visited at 3.30 pm and walked straight in to see this  wonderful church.  Free of charge to visit it is the most preserved monument of ancient Rome with all its history, sculptures and paintings, it is one of the most famous places in the city.  The outside architecture is impressive.  The Pantheon has a huge spectacular giant dome, with its famous hole in the top  and looking up inside it was amazing. The Pantheon should be on your to-do list in Rome.

If I could rate 0, I would. The pantheon is of course very impressive and nice to visit. But this tour is a scandal!No tour guide showed up, and no responses either. And when I applied for refund, they refused! I don’t understand… is this tour only charging money for nothing? Please stay away from this tour! It is a fraud!
Impressive piece of unreinforced concrete roof, in use since it was built and in good condition because of it, read about it before going

It’s a magnificent church with an extraordinary history. We visited at night, which gives it a more majestic look. It’s free to enter, and although there was a line to get in, it moves fast and we waited less than 5 minutes.
Staff helpful and kind. Short wait to enter and pick up the audio guides, great for those who know little about the pantheon
Provided with headphones and they are all put in the bin. A temple embossing the universe that slowly destroys it with single use waste and no recycling. A disgrace to the 21st century
After walking from our hotel to the Spanish Steps and Trevi Fountain, we opted for an easy to find taxi and got dropped off next to it.    Beautiful, and its free !

Saw this as part of a guided private tour with Marco from Eyes Of Rome. The detail and accomplishment of this building is beyond incredible. To think it's 2000 yrs old is almost incomprehensible! Highly suggest...especially if it's raining...you'll see why!
Fascinating ancient building. The structure of the building with the hole in the roof is incredible.
No visit to Rome is complete without a visit to the Pantheon.  Amazing architecture and engineering, beautiful statues and monuments inside.   An ancient wonder worth visiting.

Lucky enough to get a tour of Pantheon when we first arrived in Rome.  It can be a hard ticket to get but there are a few tour guides right at the entrance that sometimes have extra tix and you get a headset.  Must see in Rome.

The Pantheon was perhaps the biggest surprise of our Mediterranean cruise.  After weeks of touring ruins, suddenly there is the Pantheon, so gorgeously intact and ornately splendid.  An architectural marvel and incredible to have survived so unscathed for millennia.

The 'Pantheon' is one of those (many) "must-see Rome spots". Luckily for us we stumbled right into it after strutting down the Spanish Steps (and blowing by Trevi Fountain!!) It's a beautiful old building that is an easy, quick stop to whet your tourists' appetite. Located in a cool area of the city that's surrounded by great little restaurants and bars, make sure you visit here while in Rome.*Just keep your pants on!
The audio tour was easy to follow and informative. Timed entry was easy and meant the venue was not crowded.
Booked tour whilst queuing outside the Pantheon. Easy to book and admission to the Pantheon. Felt we enjoyed and learned a lot more from the audio tour than if we had not opted for the audio guide.

The Pantheon is amazing, the structure alone is impressive. It is FREE to enter and the day we went there was no line to enter. The only issue to be aware of is there are a lot of Hawkers/Sellers that harass you outside, take care of your belongings.

First of all you have to register on line at least a day before to get a ticket to go inside. Its free, just register on line. They will give you a designated time to visit and this helps control the crowd. The inside as expected is awesome. The Altar, the tomb of the King and of Raphael etc. a very good place to pray, marvel at the architecture and a lot of photo op.

This architectural wonder is definitely worth visiting. It’s impressive from outside but the inside is even more so, especially when u read about it. There were no lines outside so we went in on two different occasions.
Amazing structure to see.  Stopped in while walking in the area.  Not much time needed to visit, 15-20 minutes.  Free to walk in
Hard to imagine this being designed and built when it was.  From air flow to rain drainage to the structure itself.
standing in line outside for quite a long time (entrance is free) can be a pain when it's hot and sunny so go out of season
Obviously you have to stop.  A tour is nice, but you can go through without one, and just read the signs.  Ticket gets you to the Forum and Palatine Hill- definitely do those too.

If you have any interest in architecture or just the building techniques of ancient Rome, the Pantheon is worth a visit.  Putting aside the  lines that may form outside in the busy season (free timed entry tickets are required for weekends and holidays), a visit of 15 to 30 minutes will  suffice.  This is the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome, constructed in AD 126.  Amazing!  The interior is now part church part civic memorial, and even the interior columns, floors and artwork are worth a look.

We visited the Pantheon and its piazza every night of our stay. It is remarkably well-preserved, and the sense of being in the presence of antiquity is awe-inspiring. The lines are long during the day, so go in the evening. It is free to enter and you do not want to miss this stunning remnant of ancient Rome.

One of the history sites every tourist wil visit in Rome. It's good that they work with a queing line so inside there's always enough space to walk and see everything. Short audio guide is very clear to experience the history of the building. Great value!

Beautiful to see this building.  Worth using an audio tour to get the most out of the visit but even without it is still worth a visit to take in the beauty of this wonderful building with its magnificent history.  Remember if visiting at the weekend you need to book your visit - no charge but limited numbers allowed in at any one time.

Compared with most sites dating from Roman period, this one is in excellent condition. You can sit on the benches, listen to the audio guide, and look at what you are hearing about. Beautiful building and nice to be able to sit down.

The audio tour was thorough. I learned things I didn't know before and gave me a new perspective to the historic building. It was nice to be able to hear the guide without interruptions and be able to do it at your own pace.

Order online and was easy with audio though doesn’t take long to listen but it was very beautiful to see on our Rome tripping of two days .Go early as crowd builds up around 11:30 ,nice bars and coffee shop for resting .we also did the Spanish steps and Trevi fountain on the way to there .three in one can’t go wrong .all walkable .

The audio tour was informative and thorough, without being heavy or boring; the guided tour groups actually            were kind of in the way!  The amount of historical beauty and grandeur here is impressive, and the magnificence of the art and architecture is sometimes overwhelming, but having seen it once has only given me the desire to return time and time again.

The Pantheon itself is worth seeing.The audio tour is nothing real special. However, on weekends you will be granted entry even on short notice.To conclude, the entry in the Pantheon is worth the money - even if entry usually is free of charge.
Worth a visit. Free and steeped in history. Quite busy, so get there as it opens for better photos . One way system helped.
Paulo was great. He had good insights and was very engaging. He also could connect a lot of history and culture to today. And, he had showed us great photo spots
Pantheon is a must when you visit Rome. The building is impressive with its dome on top of the temple. The best time to visit seems to be in the morning.
Magnificent building and well kept in the original state. The choir inside made the visit well worth it.
As the title suggests, seeing this in real life was a totally unexpected surprise despite seeing it in pictures previously. A must see.
Don't miss this beautiful piece of history. Nice plaza to people watch. Lots of other sites nearby to see.
Having on,y seen photos of the inside, I thoroughly enjoyed seeing this. The haphazardly set up line moved quickly to get inside, and you can stay at your leisure.
We visited the Pantheon at night and it was impressive. We have toured the interior previously and would advise including this in your program as it is a part of essential Rome.

Stopped here when on a vespa tour and it was one of my favorite stops in Rome. This building is amazing and the history behind the building of it is very interesting. Highly recommend going here while in Rome.
An absolute magnificent church. Well worth a visit. Do not be put off by the rather long queue. It does move very quickly.

(Oct 22) Well worth visiting as you step back in history. Don't be phased when you first enter the square and see the long queue, it moves quickly. Inside are readable facts and this we did without the need for a guide.
A visit to the pantheon is a must.Booked the audio guide in advance you get your guide...duration half an hour, but absolutely to do on a Roman weekend!

Packed when we went and loads of people talking than being silent (due to being in a church). We took the audio guide which was really informative. Although there was large queues, they did move quickly.

I think The Pantheon was one of my most favourite places! The building is beautiful and amazing how it was built! We walked all around to see the sides and back of how it was built as we were recommended to do that (arches built into the brick work).The line was very quick moving even with people pushing in (noones fault, hard to see where the line was as the area is crowded).Inside was clean, just amazing and while busy inside it was not overly crowded.

I have been here about half a dozen times now and it is getting more and more intriguing for every visit due to the fact that I see more and more details so do read in about Pantheon before visiting, just amazing.

This ticket does not enable you to skip the queue.  Once inside you pick the headset up and can then listen to the commentary but they need some form of POI before they’ll let you have the headset.  Luckily, we had a bank card with us.  The commentary was interesting but I think  there were information boards inside which would have given us the basic information.

Being first a temple to the gods and then a Christian church, this building has been in use for 1900 years and so has been maintained, perhaps the best of the old buildings in Rome. It is geometrically beautiful with a few important personalities buried there. Since it is a church, it is free but closed to the public during services.

Awesome looked nothing special outside but amazing inside.Tip: we got there to be told at weekends you need to book so I stood to one side and booked through trip advisor it automatically sent booking to my phone and we went in straight away

Definitely worth going inside!! We wandered into the square at 2.55pm on a Sunday and the queue was HUGE and not moving. We were approached by a guy offering guided tours at 3pm for 15€ a person. We would normally not buy tours spontaneously but decided to give it a go and we were so glad we did. Our tour guide was fantastic and led a really interesting hour-long tour that meant we could breeze past the line and go in when we were ready. It’s definitely worth doing a tour to fully understand the building and get the most out of your visit. Our guide spoke about the full history of the Pantheon and its context within Rome’s story which was really interesting. Plus he even let us pay by PayPal when we didn’t have cash.

Walked by the Pantheon in the early afternoon. Took a few outside photos and noted that the line wrapped around the square. Then realized that the line was moving incredibly fast - joined and probably 10 minutes was in! Lots of people inside but roomy enough to get some decent photos if you can tolerate a few tourists in them! Absolutely stunning inside and doesn’t take long to visit.

There was a good sized line to get into the Pantheon (going back past the fountain in the piazza out front). But it moved pretty quickly.  There was no entrance fee, and we did not have (nor did we need) any reservation time, so I'm not sure what other reivews are referring to.  We were there on a Thursday afternoon.The architecture of the building is outstanding to see in person. The dome and oculus inside is quite amazing to behold, especially when you consider what construction methods were available back when it was built.  It is crowded, but take time to appreciate the various parts of the Pantheon such as Raphael's tomb.

This surviving example of Roman construction and design is an absolute marvel and a must see.  Luckily, we were with a guide who knew that timed tickets are required on the weekends or we would not have had the exceptional experience of seeing the inside of this amazing building.

I have heard so much about this building.  The structure was amazing and impressive.  The detail was mind blowing and the amount of people who wanted to see this building wanted to see it as much as I.  Make the Pantheon a must see but get a tour to find out much of what youtube will not say.
Even though it was very busy, we enjoyed the experience.  Ate some great food at a restaurant left of Pantheon.

It was a last minute stop as it was high on my list of things to do while in Rome but not on my husband's.  We are both so glad that we did!  It was so beautiful.  I thought the Sistine Chapel was a circular building, which it is not.  So when I walked into the Pantheon, this is what I thought the Sistine Chapel would look like.  Loved it!

The weirdest thing is that everyone almost didn't notice Raphael's grave! But it's a great church to visit. Beware! You need to book your entry tickets online and certainly at least a few days before you plan to go! A lot of people showed up and were denied entry because all entry times were booked.
A must see.  I followed the  Rick Steves audio tour and enjoyed my time there.  Wonderful historical site.

We really didn't know what to expect, but we ate dinner on the plaza one night (actually a pretty good meal and not too expensive) with the magically lit Pantheon before us.  Went back the next day and were blown away by this building which has been in use for two millennia!

Passed it at night and it looks beautiful. Went back during the day. A very long queue to get in but it moves fast. Ceiling construction is beautiful. Despite lots of people it’s very peaceful inside.

This was very misleading, beware and do not purchase unless you also have a timed entry reserved for the Pantheon. This is only an audio guide and does not get you entry to the Pantheon. We bought this first then tried to get a reservation to enter the Pantheon but could not. This was worthless to us and we will attempt to get a refund but the day no refunds after 24 hours before

Free entry and well worth reading online the history of this significant building. You may have to queue to get in, but it was fast moving and so not a problem. Take the time to really look around, because although it may only be one very large 'room' there is a lot of detail and the creation of the domed roof is an amazing architectural accomplishment!

Do not expect just to arrive and get in. Most visitation spots get filled within seconds. Access the Pantheon official website and reserve your visit time. Also, add an audio guide to make the whole experience complete. Having a pre-booked reserved time, make sure you arrive on time. You will guarantee entrance. Enjoy!

While so many churches in Rome our spectacular this is truly amazing given the time period when it was built.  It was originally built to honor Roman gods before being converted to a church.  We didn't do a tour and joined a long line but it moved very fast.  We were able to take all of the time we wanted once inside.
Beautiful churchIf you’re in the area it’s a quick visitFree with fast moving linesWorth the stop
It’s such a great monument to visit. You could stay there all day. Really recommend an audio guided tour.
One of the amazing architectural wonders of Rome. Definitely worth a visit, enter and walk around and enjoy the atmosphere.

Previously a pagan temple which has now been turned into a Christian church, this is a very beautiful building. Lots of artwork all around the circular interior, with the grave of Raphael, one of the greats.It was raining when we got there, but early enough not to queue, as it can get very busy.
Pantheon is awesome architecture. Very crowded but seating available. A must see in Rome. The ceiling is magnificent  with light beaming down? The space is sumptuous.

The oldest roman building in existence.The Pantheon is now a church after holding many guises, but still worth a visit to see the oldest, and probably most interesting building around.You need to book if you want to go inside, but this you can easily do online.

Absolutely spectacular to visit. We had no problem getting in with these pre booked tickets. We just walked straight in and we’re given headphones. We spent about an Hour inside the Pantheon with our audio tour.
A great 1-1.5 hour stop. The line moves quickly. I highly recommend the free Rick Steve's audio guide (app). The hole in the dome is pretty neat to see.
Amazing! It’s breathtaking inside and the audio guide allows you to independently listen and go at your own pace. There was comfortable seating for my mom.

The best place i saw in this city, in Europe, in the world. Is amazing that is still standing after 1900 years

we went in the day and at night and it was just beautiful at night. kids had fun running around with other kids and listening to street performers.

Very sad for us. so learn from our mistake! :) It is worth seeing from the outside, especially at night from the restaurants nearby.

Definitely worth seeing! We did this on our own (without a tour), there was a long queue but it moved quickly and it wasn't too crowded inside. It was also free to visit which was a nice surprise,  although we would happily have paid an entrance fee as it's remarkable.
