The Pantheon is Rome’s great survivor. Built by the Roman emperors Agrippa and Hadrian around 2000 years ago, it has survived Barbarian invaders, wars, fire, looting and the elements and has remained in almost constant use since.Be prepared that the queue can be horribly long. We stayed just around the corner and walked past the Pantheon often. Several times the queue was so long that we skipped it, thinking that we’d easily find another time when it was not so bad. This didn’t really work. The queue was always pretty long and the only way to beat it was to be there first thing in the morning. The inside is very different to the outside. From outside, the façade has all the typical features of a classical Roman temple including a portico, Corinthian columns and pediment. Inside, the dome is the real mind-boggler. It is still the largest unreinforced concrete dome in the world. Never been beaten. That is quite an engineering feat and it required a few building tricks to stop the weight of the dome from causing the whole thing to just fall inwards. To avoid collapse, they built the foundations and lower walls out of heavy stone like travertine, then as they built upwards, they made the concrete thinner and added pumice so that it was quite lightweight. Finally, as you look up, you stare straight into the the oculus, the ‘eye’ of the dome. It looks small but that’s a trick of the eye. It’s actually 8.2m across. It just looks small because the interior is so big. The sunlight streams through but equally, so does the rain. You can spot the holes in the centre of the floor that allow the water to drain away. It’s also worth noting that the marble floor tiles that you walk on are the original ancient Roman ones. Pretty incredible to think that the ancient Romans trod the same floor.Several noteworthy tombs are here including Raphael and the first kings of Italy, Victor Emmanuel II and Umberto.TIPS: It's free to enter Monday-Friday (on weekends, you need to have booked at least 24 hours ahead). Masks are still required inside. There is no information inside so a guidebook or audioguide (€8.50) is pretty helpful here.

A bit of a long wait to get in but well worth it.  We had visited before and didn't do any official tour or use the audio guide.  As a geologist visiting with my engineer student son it was a nerd temple as well.  Picking a time during the day to get good sunlight through the roof can make for a better experience if you want good photos.
Free entry, doesn't take too long to go round the building but definitely worthy to see/visit will provide some beautiful photos for you with the ceiling a big wow!

This was an unforgettable experience. We came from a very small street, turned the corner and boom. There is was. So old looking and utterly beautiful. I would recommend that you book before like we did. On the day there was no more tickets left for you to go in and have a look. The audio guide was pretty short but really informative. You would not have wanted it any longer or you would have got bored. Most of it you could spend sitting down in front of the alter and still see what they was talking about. The opening in the roof was great o see and the audio guide explained it all. You could spend hours in here looking at the features you will literally get lost. I was beautiful. Just looked so old and wonderful. I am upset that I am only able to upload 10 photos. GO HERE!

The Pantheon and surrounding area is wonderful.   Water fountains right in front to refill your bottles.   The line to get in goes very very fast.  You DO NOT need to pay anyone to get in.   So full of history and the feeling of being part of something that magnificent, priceless.

A truly amazing piece of architecture! The ceiling is a structural masterpiece and shows the skill and craftsmanship of the Ancient Romans. A very peaceful place which is still a place of worship today. The interiors are stunning. Be aware as it is an active church men are asked to remove headwear and ladies need to cover their shoulders to enter. Admission is free of charge. A must see if you visit this beautiful city
Its free to get in, so why wouldnt you? The queues can be horrendous, but its interesting inside and is in a lovely little piazza

Makesure to arrive early when the open it opens at 8:30 am and it's free to enter this is a must visit , but makesure to arrive early to avoid big  lines . Makesure to cover your shoulders or won't let you in
I mean whatever. Was boring we just walked right past it boiling hot in rome in July go when it’s cooler
It is an incredible piece of art. Quite easy to get in, we booked in the morning and had visited in the afternoon.
We did this somewhat spontaneously without the guided tour and were lucky to be up early and arrive without it being too full. If you like history and architecture, you have to see this place.

Visited here in July 2022! A must visit whilst in  Rome! The inside of it is stunning and so decorative! Well worth a visit and free to visit!I didn’t realise you had to cover your shoulders for this one, if you want to go inside it, so don’t forget to take a shawl or wear a top covering shoulders, there are stall dotted you can buy them at but are up from €6 for a shawl as I imagine a lot of people don’t realise this!
walking into history is a wonderful experience. loads and loads of history! The oculus- the central dome is simply stunning
This is so moving to see in person. The size of the pillars and the door. It projected a feeling to be in its presence. Best time to go is weekday morning.

On our previous trip to Rome we didn’t get to visit here but this time we did. We went late afternoon and we didn’t have to wait very long in the queue. It is free to visit but please note as this is a church shoulders need to be covered up. It was busy but not massively so. There was controls on how many people were allowed in. I enjoyed the visit and would recommend it to all. Fascinated by the fact that Raphael has his crypt there. An audio guide can be downloaded by not your phone for more info. Beautiful building.

One of the fascinating things about the Pantheon is how the city has been built up around it. Over time new construction and necessities of modern living have impacted how it is situated. It sits below street level but originally, the Pantheon would have sat high above street level, with five steep stairs used to reach it.This sunken effect makes the Pantheon seem like a sentry amid the noise and pace of modern times. It is sturdy in appearance and seems almost stubborn in this setting. It has dug it’s heels in and has spent hundreds of years refusing to be destroyed like it’s predecessors. It is different than the other ancients in my opinion. Perhaps because it is not cordoned off like the Colosseum and the Forum are. It is just… there, right in the middle of the McDonald’s signs and it is insouciant. If the Pantheon was a movie character, it would be Clint Eastwood in “Gran Torino”… get off my lawn! You have to at least stop by and view the exterior if you aren't up for witing in line to go inside.

A very interesting place steeped in history, there are some very interesting facts during the tour related the structure of the place, a lot of theories as to the purpose of the building and why it has a hole in the centre of the domed roof. Amazing architecture, art, burial places and statues to see. Definitely worth the cost of the guided tour to get the facts, you can’t go to Rome without visiting the unusually shaped Pantheon.

This was a beautiful place to visit. Walking in through the big huge doors and the beauty upon entering was wow. The paintings and stories of how it was built was so interesting. The Open doom, the paintings the tombs you have to see to believe. Worth the visit

The Pantheon is an architectural marvel: the huge dome remains the largest in the world made from unreinforced concrete, and it's wonderful to stand below it and stare at the open sky through the oculus.  And oh, those doors... the oldest original Roman doors in this city.   Don't miss it.
Look go to Rome, go see this ancient building.  Its amazing.  No need for tickets just go on in.     I had to write a few more words to complete this review...love it!!

Very overcrowded but it is worth seing. It’s an art masterpiece and you don’t want to miss out. You can’t get to all of sculptures but you can see them. I believe there is a mass every Sunday if you are looking for more spiritual experience.
Very impressive visit. We had audio guide tour. Is very historical, very antique place. Has to be on must to do list.

Ticketed at weekends but I think tour operators take all the tickets to sell to unsuspecting tourists! No need to book during the week so we waited until then to visit as we had 5 days, would have been disappointing if we had only had the weekend.  The building itself is very impressive and a joy to see inside.
Pre-booked tickets required for weekends.Early & late seem to be the best times. A fantastic buildig that is a must see.

You must cover your shoulders but there is a dude there ready to see you a shawl to cover your shoulders.  We paid 10E which seemed fair but I bet if you walk for a minute you can find one for less.This building is crazy.  We listened to the Rick Steves Audiocast which is on a free app.  It was good.  Probably the best one I’ve heard so far as it was easy to know you were in the right spot for each section of the audiocast. I would highly recommend visiting the Pantheon.(For some reason they weren’t charging us, so it was also free today.)
The most amazing in RomeIt was the most impressive for me and my children in Rome.Stunning , when you enter it.So if you have to choose . Then don't miss this
A freak of roman culture and history, this is the place to go. The building is older than the colosseum, and the most preservative building from ancient Rome, 2 thousand years ago. 😊

The Pantheon is a unique building in Rome. More than 2000 years old it is an architectural marvel. We spent about 40 minutes standing inside. A historical note: the artist Raphael is buried inside. A worthwhile visit.

The Pantheon is very central in Rome, and should not be much of a detour on most routes through the city, It is an impressive building from the outside as well as from within. There is no entrance fee, and even if you are short on time, it is worth your while to step in for at least a few minutes to experience the wonderful atmosphere of a building erected 2000 years ago.

Whilst this is an imposing building the interior, in my opinion it wasn’t as grand as some other places of worship. If you really want to know what you are looking at I would suggest a headset guide, can be hired inside. We didn’t which I think was a mistake. There is a lot of fuss outside not knowing where to queue, turns out there wasn’t a queue you only had to have made a reservation for the day. We had thankfully as all allocations had been booked….. worth booking.

There are no lights in this building.  There is an opening in the roof that lets light in.  When it rains, the rain just falls onto the floor of the building.  There are pictures and artifacts to view inside.  So much history!I watched a TV show about the building before we went.  I'm glad I did because I knew a little more about the architecture of the building.

A must see.  Go early before they open to take pictures with no crowds/people (before 7;30AM).  To visit the inside go late in the evening, the line moves quickly.  Save your money for other guided tours.  You can do this one for free.

The history of the place and how important it was in Roman time was something I did not know before visiting.   Explanation of the guides was key. That the romans where able to create a dome of this size,  with the huge occulus in the center is amazing.   Still today the building is very imposing.
Glad we took the time….. truly beautiful !  W should have done the listening tour…… but enjoyable anyways

My fiancé is a huge history nerd so we were really looking foreword to this. We turned up on a Saturday to find you need a reservation, so we couldn’t go in. We then went back the following Tuesday, queued up to be then told by one of the guards that because my fiancé was wearing a vest top we couldn’t go in. The guard was very patronising and unprofessional . So we went to a nearby shop and my fiancé bought a T-shirt to put on. We queued up again and finally got in. When we got inside we were a little underwhelmed. The outside of the pantheon is more impressive than the inside. Inside it’s just a church, and there’s more grand and beautiful churches than that in Rome. To top it all off there were more grumpy guards walking around inside telling us and other people to take our caps off, but other hats were allowed apparently. Don’t bother with a paid tour as it’s small inside. There were no signs outside stating the dress code, the guards were rude and kinda ruined the experience. We obeyed the rules when told to, it wasn’t clear there were any rules and there was a lot of people being shown away because they weren’t dressed accordingly. The rules were more enforced here than when we went to the Vatican. We had a better time eating ice-cream and sitting by the fountain outside and admiring the pantheon building.

Since we went on a weekend when you need to schedule entry, having the audio tour assured us of a guaranteed entry. Although VERY crowded, the staff dispensing audio tour equipment were friendly and efficient. The audio equipment worked well and really enhanced the experience. The Pantheon is breathtaking and I learned so much about it through this tour.
It was a very special experience to visit this miracle. It really worth it. I really had a good time in Rome and in her attractions site. Love Rome ❤

Worth a visit for the history and magnificence of the architecture. I went early morning before it opened and was able to get in within 2 mins. Highly recommend trying this else there are longgg queues in peak season. Used the official audio guide app of just 2 euros. The audio did not work but u can read it all and worth it to understand the place and it’s significance.

Great place to visit and was impressed it was free of charge. Lots to see and amazing to be in such an old church. Just don’t forget to take something to cover bare shoulders or you can’t go in. There are people in the street outside who will charge extortionate amounts for them.
A really must-see when visiting Rome. Absolutely amazing. You can really feel the ancient vibes! Nice shops to eat around the alleys.
This is a must do activity on your Rome’s bucket list. The place is so beautiful and it also takes little effort to get there.It is right in the city center and also near to other attractions.
Amazing building, definitely worth the visit. Beware of the guys selling scarfs outside of the building, they almost ruined the visit by following you around offering their merch .
We did the really exciting guided tour I think one of the most beautiful we've ever done

We were there in the morning during the peak of summer tourist season and the line was long - however it moved fast! Don't bother with a paid tour ... the Pantheon is small once inside and each piece has a great description. Save the tour $$ for other stops!

OK, so there are enormous crowds of people waiting to get in, and also packed inside the place. But there's an ethereal, otherworldly quality to the building. My wife said "it's alive, almost like a spider." It's a strange, wondrous place. Worth the wait.

Panteon is a must see when visiting Rome. It is free of charge and you can spend inside from 10 to 50 minutes so it can be easy part of walking Tour. Keep in mind the cue outside, but it moves pretty quickly. Also remember it is sacred place so cover yourselves to the knees and cover shoulders.
The Roman Pantheon is the most preserved and influential building of ancient Rome. It is a Roman temple dedicated to all the gods of pagan Rome

There is always a line to get in but it moves quickly and there is no entry fee or reservation required. You may stay for as long as you wish, or until the kids get bored.
That temple is overrated. Here just a million tourists but here nothing for watching. Just a gigantic temple with nothing inside.
The pantheon, the one. What an amazing building. One of the oldest churches in all of Rome. Vittorio Emanuelle and Raphael are buried here

A landmark full of history that is a must in Rome. I would recommend having the audio guide because it provides great insights not only about the history of the Pantheon but also its architectural brilliance.  It s free so be prepared to wait in line  The waiting time can go above 1h but we "only" waited for 25 minutes, A must see in Rome
Free entry but no signs so get the audio tour.  Its on an iphone and very easy to use. Get as much or as little informations as you like

There is now a line to get it but do NOT be discouraged.  It moves fast!!!  It is worth the short wait to get inside.  THe line DOES help curtail the amount of people inside which makes for a better experience once inside.
we saw this on the same day as the vatacan sisteen chapel and st peters basillica the whole experience is amazing from start to finish
Absolutely stunning…And that is pretty much it - just go…. probably the most impressive thing we’ve visited in Rome, to be honest (and it’s free! 😊)
Augustus had a great idea but it took Hadrian to make it happen.  Amazing structure even today.  Tickets needed on weekends but walk right in during the week.
It is hard to believe this building is 2000 years old. The engineering skill necessary to construct this concrete dome is impressive.
you could easily walk by this building and not go in..GO IN!  its incredible inside.   We just walked thru and looked around - didn't do the audio tour - didn't feel the need but its beautiful inside
Pantheon was amazing.  We had an audio tour.  Make it as long or as short as you want.Recommend having a ticket to avoid lines.
Shoulders must be covered- please bring something with you if you are wearing a tank top - bring a scarf, jacket or something to cover up your shoulders; they are strict.
in our opinion this is a must see in Rome our advice would be to book a tour guide ours was very knowledgeable and enthusiastic
Magical place.Worth visiting. Rafaelo,s rest place is there.  Admission is free, it is used as church, so beware, cover yourself, don't flash flesh.
Absolutely beautiful and incredible place. It is a must see whilst in Rome. Entry is free. Would recommend going outside of peak hours. Long lines but moves fast.
Be prepared as this is a church.   to have  ladies cover shoulders  and  below  knees. Shawls are  sold on site by vendors for $20 Eu's .  amazing  structure  for  the time build
Stunning interior. Very historically significant church, former temple. This is where is all started!
Go during off peak hours (before 10 and after 7), be careful of scammers trying to 'give' you bracelets and jewellery.
Long lines but move fast. Very crowded.... not mask required You cannot miss the pantheon.  Thank en time and wait for you turn in line you will not regret it
Absolutely beautiful place to visit in Rome. It truly is mind blowing how huge this place is and how well maintained it is.

A large, ancient temple with a hole in the ceiling is just about what the place is. It’s rather nice though and makes a great backdrop for some dramatic photography. The gentlemen are required to remove any headwear and the ladies are required to cover their shoulders. There is a persistent lot of local salesmen pushing a variety of scarfs on the ladies outside and guards at the entrance to make sure everybody gets with the program. The place is basically a church and thus free of charge, so travellers on a budget and people doing church-spoting might want to check the place out... and of course there is lots and lots of history here as well, so for history nerds and especially theological history nerds the place is a no-brainer. It’s also rather quickly seen so that’s a plus for anyone on a tight schedule.
Get to the pantheon  early to avoid the queues and ladies make sure  your shoulders are covered, take a shawl!
Came across this outstanding building while walking through the side streets, wasn’t looking for it but glad we found it.One of the most important sites to visit in Rome
This is not as ornate as many of the churches around it but is definitely a must-do when you're in Rome.
This was an amazing structure to see. The dome is almost perfectly round. Advise going at opening to beat the crowd. It is a very religious site and very beautiful.

What a building! Stunning both inside and out. The absolute best backdrop to have an evening drink/meal with the Pantheon lit up as darkness descends. And the interior isn’t too bad either! Beautiful.

Great tour which took about 45 mins and which we did literally last minute a lady approached us and offered a place for reduced price, very interesting. The Pantheon is one of the many iconic sights of Rome which you’ll pass many times on your walking trips around the city as it leads onto the Piazza Navona in one direction and Trevi Fountain in the other direction.

A suggestive place rich in history, the guided tour allowed us to skip the long queue. We particularly enjoyed the guide (her name is Laura) who made the visit even more special thanks to her preparation, passion and ability to involve the group.
Take the time to look up!  Try to figure out how such precise work could be accomplished with out heavy equipoment and power tools!
You simply must see this building.  Pictures don't do it justice.   It is free, but sometimes there is a long line.
I wonder what the point of booking if you have to wait in a very long line and bake in the sun. The place is nice, too bad it's mismanaged. Staff rude and very slow.
Atmosphere of Inside of Pantheon is  fascinating. There was also an exhibition when we visit. There is always queue in front but goes very fast.Women should cover shoulders when entering.

This is one of those buildings that take your breath away. I was in awe of it for a bit, I sent a few pictures to friends and family but they don't do it justice at all. You have to see the thing up close. There are restaurants extremely close to it so you can sit outside and swoon over it.It is far more impressive outside than inside.
Amazing sight.  In ways more stunning than the Colosseum.  If you are able to walk to places like this do.

...I had been to Rome before and saw the Pantheon but didn't go inside.  One of the locals urged me to visit it and when I did, I found a line wrapped around the entire plaza around 10:30 AM.  I thought "OK, just go for it" and I stood in line.  It moved rather quickly and I don't think it took more than 25 minutes before I reached the front entrance.  The architecture is amazing and the inside is pristine and gorgeous.  The center area is roped off so that you can't stand directly under the open roof (what a traffic jam that would be).  It was definitely worth the wait!
Don't be put off by the long lines/crowd, the line moves quickly and the magnificence of this treasure should not be bypassed.

We did the audio guided tour, had to leave my passport as collateral for the audio tour equipment.  The audio tour was very informative.  The Pantheon is an amazing piece of engineering, built nearly 2000 years ago.  They even considered the drainage of the water that come in through the eye of the Pantheon or oculus when it rains.  Mind blowing!
Go early if you want to see this amazing structure. By 9:30 am, the line up wound around the square 3 times
Only visited outside during the night.. It worth the stop, the Pantheon is stunning. Probably my favorite piece of Rome.
Very impressive landmark, much larger than expected. Very busy area, so be prepared to be surrounded by tons of tourists.
Another must see in Rome, in remarkable condition for it's age.  Be prepared to queue, equally if not more impressive from the outside rather than inside.
There was along queue to get in to the Pantheon and you do have to be aware as in most busy areas of pickpockets but it is well with visit
Without a guide of any kind, a visit will probably not give much. The audio guide is quite nice to follow on your own, interesting details!
VERY busy get there early and the cue moves super fast, later in the day you are in line in the sun.
Another gem of Roman genius. The queue to get in was quite long, but it moved quickly and was worth the wait.

This is one of the oldest buildings in Rome. It survives because it became a church. Weekdays are free admission, but weekends and holidays need reservations and have a fee. We did the audio tour which included admission. Be prepared to stand in line in the sun, The dome is incredible, a true work of art. It’s too bad all the bronze was taken off the building and repurposed for use in the Vatican.

My husband and I went here with a guide, who gave great explanations.  We walked right in, even though there were lots of people, but we weren’t squashed together unbearably, you were still able to move.  Very worthwhile.
Another must-see place in Rome: it's really impressive and it deserves the waiting queues. Try to be here early in the morning.
This sight was about 4 mins walk from our hotel. Suggest you arrive by opening time to get in and enjoy. It’s free and amazing.
I just can't get enough of that amazing art work, every time I go back to Rome. This building is nearly 2,000 years old, and still looks like the day it was constructed! An absolutely masterpiece!

Absolutely stunning - the pantheon is a must-see if you're visiting Rome! We booked the audio tour which was OK but the building itself was of course the main attraction. In theory you don't need to pay to see the Pantheon, but on the dates we went we couldn't see how you could go without paying for the audioguide.

We spent only 1.5 days in Rome and the visit of the Pantheon was one of my personal highlights. Its actually quite difficult to find it (or at least for us it was quite difficult), but its totally worth it. The visit is free, and when you arrive there and the line to get in is very long, dont walk away - it moves very quickly.Inside, its so impressive. Difficult to describe, you have to go there yourself to know what I mean.
Overpriced. Google the info as it is free. Admission is free. Just remember that on the weekends you need to sign up the day before for a time slot.
A must see place in Rome.The line looks long but it does not take long to get in and then you can view it in your own speed.

The Pantheon is a must-visit in Rome. Went there during the weekend without knowing that an online reservation is required. All (free) tickets were no longer available of course. Therefore, booked an audio tour, which could not impress me much: I lost interest at the end. The building and architecture are indeed very special.

We didn't do a proper visit, we just viewed from the outside but it was worth seeing and a very impressive structure. Would recommend a proper visit. Large tour groups congregate in the square and make viewing/taking photos quite difficult so arrive early if you intend to take photos.

The pantheon is the best conserved Roman building in Rome, because during history it becomes a church. Nowadays the visit inside is free (there is always a big queue to enter) and inside there are the tombs of Italian kings, queens and artists. Very beautiful is the big Dome, the biggest in the ancient Roman architecture, and the square in front of it is really nice too
The Pantheon is a former Roman temple and now a working Catholic Church. Tickets are required but can be purchased on-site. Go early to avoid the crowds.

Classic sight to see in Rome. Long line but it moves quickly. Beautiful to see the inside with so much detail in the artwork and architecture. No cost to go inside. Great piazza to hang around and have a gelato afterwards.

We went to the  Pantheon and negotiated a tour guide to 10E each rather than 15.  Well worth it in my view  - learned loads about the Pantheon we had missed last time we went.  So beautiful inside.  Worth the visit for sure

We planned to visit the Pantheon on the Saturday of our trip.I had read ahead of visiting, that although entering the Pantheon is free (as it is a church), bookings are needed for the weekend. And thank god I read this first!I booked ahead, and we were able to visit the Pantheon with no crowds and without queuing - as only bookings are accepted on the Saturday. Was so satisfying seeing pompous tourists being rejected at the door.The audioguide at the Pantheon was great - superbly packed with interesting facts about the history of the site.
We booked the self tour in advance, most people walk in and out, but the recorded self tour was priced well and informative, th line moved rather quick and we were not rushed.

Our 17 year old grandson was less than enthusiastic from the outside because you can't see what you are going into, but was then blown away.  He rated it his favourite place in Rome.  Sensationally designed, just beautiful.

As a model for so many other iconic structures over the years, it is always a thrill for me to spend time in the piazza and simply think of all that has happened at this spot since it was constructed so many years ago. And then consider all the other "pantheons" that have sprung from this one structure. And, yes, have a meal at Fortunato, which may not be as good as it once was, but it is still my "go to" restaurant in Rome. Great sommelier these days!

If you have an opportunity to visit, don’t miss it!   What an impressive and enduring accomplishment of design, engineering and construction.  Integrated beauty form and function at an elevated level many centuries ahead of its time.
This architectural building is such a surprise. A must see. Book your ticket before going as everything is ticketed at the moment.Anne Dublin
We tagged onto a tour as a couple hadn't turned up. Had a great time and learnt a lot.A place like this needs a guide so you can get a real insight

Stunning Roman architecture, both inside and out. Despite being transformed into a place of Christian veneration, the interior is largely original to the period in which the building was created, and it is unbelievably gorgeous.

After seeing all the ruins of the Roman empire in the rest of Rome, we were amazed to see the Pantheon still standing in all its splendor. Impressive outside already, the inside and it's concrete coupola is even more!

Magnificent, the audio guide is the perfect complement to enjoy the visit. The building is imposing, we enjoyed it a lot. There were not many people so we could take our time and admire everything. Highly recommended the visit.

This was one of the few "must-sees" during our trip to Rome. The entrace is free (however over the weekend and Bank Holidays you must book a visit). Don't get discourgaded by the long looking queue - it moves very fast. If there is a queue while waiting to go in you can admire the marvellous and imposing columns at the entrance. It is a magnificant building that has stood the test of time.

The Pantheon is a must see when you're in Rome.  Wonderful and historic architectural masterpiece.  It's busy, but easy to get in to.  We had a short, quick moving line to get in and then had a chance to take it all in.  Absolutely beautiful!

Come early and you can get in line pretty easily. Once inside you sort of make your way through on your own. We just brought a guide book to read about a few specific elements. There isn't a lot inside so definitely less than hour is needed even if you are very interested in history & like taking pictures. Really the enjoyment is being in the enormous space and having a feel for what it would have been like.
Very nice, yet we skipped the audio tour being 15 minutes early. Very impressive but for me didn’t need any explanatory tour
My boyfriend and I saw the pantheon from the outside and it was a wonderful piece of architecture. I'd have loved to go inside but we were only in Rome for 24 hours...maybe next time!
This place is amazing everyone should at least visit while in Rome the ques go down really quickly and it free to enter amazing
Really worth a look, but thought that we benefited greatly by having a guide help to explain all that was going on.

Wow what an amazing structure, when you consider when it was built.It looks old from the outside,when you walk right round the building.When you get inside,it is stunning. Absolutely loved this this one and its free to enter Please remember its a church, so its respectful to observe the dress code.
We went there early in the morning and there was no cue. One of the most impressive buildings we have visited in Rome.
Beautiful, impressive structure that is a must -see. Lovely painting and interesting history behind this monument.  Definitely go with a tour guide.
One of the few places that are free to get into, what a wonder of engineering into that ceiling, it gets very busy so unless you go early morning be prepared to que.
Very beautiful in the middle of the city.  Line although long moved very fast.  Wish there were more plaques and descriptors.
Full of history and beauty. A treasure in the middle or Rome.The waiting line might seem endless but it usually moves quickly.Definitely a must go.
Visiting the Pantheon without a guided visit would not be the same. The insight gotten from the guides is a necessity to fully enjoy the experience. 10/10!

I didn’t do an audio tour but wish I would have, as I came a bit ill prepared about whom was buried inside and had to google some stuff while walking around…especially because the tombs are marked but weren’t marked in an incredibly obvious way to my American eyes. Unlike a couple of hours earlier, by midday the area was now packed and we waited for 20 minutes in the queue.  We actually joined a tour along with a Scottish couple to learn a bit about the history.  The guards in the building were obsessive about face masks and mask positioning which was quite ironic as most people in the queue didn't even put them on until they went in!  The guide explained about this being one of the earliest Imperial buildings although it was then repaired and rebuilt by Hadrian at the turn of the first century. Unlike most of the pre Christian temples, this wasn't destroyed by over zealous Christians and was converted into a church.  The design was quite incredible with the dome slowly decreasing in thickness as it climbed and the building materials being swapped for lighter versions, eventually ending at pumice for the very top.  The oculus at the centre is open to the elements and represents the pathway to heaven, being where Romulus was allegedly transported to heaven after being struck by lightning.  The dome is apparently the largest unsupported dome in the world and Michealangelo built the dome of St Peters half a metre smaller as a sign of respect.

Pantheon Rome Pantheon Roma December 2021 820689077 Visited the Pantheon as one of the "must see" attractions in Rome. Current Covid rules mean you MUST have a current covid vaccination "green pass" (UK NHS online) AND a photo id. This can be a photo drivers licence but they did accept a photo of my passport. No pre-booking necessary if you go mid-week but a reservation is required on weekends and Bank Holidays. This can be done online by visiting the Pantheon website (just search for Pantheon Roma).The structure and artwork inside is very impressive but a little underwhelming if you've already seen the Vatican and its museums. For the best impression, do the Pantheon first.

It's just amazing that the Pantheon is the №1 attraction in Rome. After all, there is a Colosseum, a Forum? On the other hand, the Pantheon is the oldest building in the city and the largest domed temple in the world. Even the initiator of the construction is indicated on the pediment of the building: "M.AGRIPPA L.COS. TERTIUM FECIT" (Marcus Agrippa, son of Lucius, three times consul, did). He was the nephew of the first Emperor of Rome, Octavian Augustus. That temple was square. The pantheon became circular under Emperor Hadrian. The most difficult thing was to build a huge dome. According to legend, for this purpose, the hollow building was filled from the inside with earth, to which gold coins were mixed. After the dome was erected, Hadrian announced that anyone who found a coin could take it for himself - and overnight the Roman gold diggers took the whole earth out of the temple along with rare coins. The huge dome has a rare feature. A huge 8-meter hole is located in the middle of the 43-meter dome. Since the Pantheon is deployed not typical for ancient temples to the North, the oculus is the only opportunity to illuminate the temple. A huge puddle gathers in the middle on rainy days. The Romans did not make improvements in antiquity... Complete authenticity! The diameter of the oculus of the Pantheon (8.3 m) is not accidental. The size of the oculus allows the sun to "spend" autumn and winter in the upper hemisphere of the building, and after the vernal equinox, the sunbeam passes through the doorway. It turns out the "Axis of the world". I saw something similar on the pyramid of Kukulkan in Chichen Itza - the spectacle of the "Feathered Serpent". All this splendor had a chance to survive to this day. As you know, the whole of ancient Rome was either dismantled by the inhabitants or destroyed by Christians as part of the struggle against pagans. The Byzantine Emperor Phocas, who controlled Rome at that time, rendered a great service to world civilization by handing over the temple to Pope Boniface IV in 608. The Pope re-consecrated the Pantheon, which from the temple of all the gods became the Basilica of the Virgin Mary and the Martyrs. It was problematic to get the relics of the Madonna, but the bones of the martyrs were brought from the catacombs on 28 carts. After 14 centuries, only noble bones remained in the Pantheon. The pompous tombstones of the first kings of united Italy, Victor Emmanuel and his son Umberto, and the modest grave of Raphael meet you inside. Judging by the concentration of tourists, history has put everything in its place.

Built in 125 AD..(just think about that 125 AD) this structure was built to worship the Roman gods.  It is perhaps the best preserved  ancient Roman monument.  It was turned into a church in 609 AD Originally built in 27 BC by Emperor Augustus it burned to the ground in a great fire in 80 AD and was rebuilt by Emperor Domitian only to be hit by lightning and burned to the ground in 110.   The monument you see today was built in 12 AD by Emperor Hadrian and remains intact as one of the greatest examples of Roman engineering.  This is living history.  The dome is the largest unsupported in the world and for 1300 years was the largest as well.  It is an engineering marvel and a sight to behold.

A must see when in Rome. Don't pay for admission no matter what any website says. Admission is FREE to all. If in doubt, ask at the door. Raffaello Sanzio (AKA Raphael) is buried there along with a couple of Kings (Vittorio Emanuele II and Umberto I) and a Queen (Margherita) of Italy. It is believed that Margherita Pizza was named after Queen Margherita as it was her favorite. My suggestion is to get thee early (around 8:30AM) and get in line. Opening times vary between warm and cold months, so check at the door if in doubt. I've seen several different schedules in the different websites. Avoid visiting in late July, August, and early September as this is the hottest time in Rome and the sun and humidity are brutal. Anyone who has not given 5 (excellent) after a visit of the Pantheon was either following the wrong Rome guide or has has not read enough to understand its significance. It was built to honour the Gods of Olympus and, in particular, the legendary ancestors of the Caesars: Mars and Venus. What we see today dates from the era of Emperor Hadrian who had it reconstructed around 130AD. The bronze door aslo dates from this period. The walls are almost 7 metres thick. The dome is actually bigger than that of St. Peters. Raphael was buried here in 1520 and, while London was burning in 1666, the Pantheon had just been isolated from the surrounding buildings in the three preceding years.

The Pantheon is truly breathtaking when you first enter this magnificent structure and is something anybody should see, and particularly those interested in architecture in any way.  This building is one of the best preserved structures of the Roman Empire constructed in the 2d century. The dome and oculus are sort of mind blowing considering it has no steel inside of it to provide strength.  It is also the inspiration for rotundas around the world.  You can take self guided tours as well as connect with a few companies that offer guided tours that will are full of historic information not only about the construction but also the memorials inside.  Currently there is a temperature check, mask requirement, and one way walking traffic in order to enter.  There are plenty of restaurants within a block in every direction so it’s a perfect place to visit around lunch time.

The Pantheon represents the history of Rome. I believe that The Pantheon is the oldest  operating building in Rome. It was built between the years of 27 BC to 14 AD. After the original was destroyed by fire, The Roman Temple was REBUILT by Emperor Hadrian in 126 AD. The structure is cylindrical with a portico of granite columns. despite the age of the building, it boasts of the world's largest unreinforced dome. It has the original hole in the roof which was needed to let the smoke of the fires rise out of the building. The Romans were great builders !!Then, in 609, the Emperor gave the structure to The Pope !! The Pope turned the Roman Temple into a Catholic Church.  The structure is now " The Basilica of Saint Mary and the Martyres". It still has the hole in the roof.You will  be surprised by the interior of the structure (now a Church). I did not attach any photos because you can find much better photos of the interior at any website. This building is the history of Rome.

If you are visiting Rome, the Pantheon is a must. The sheer size of this chapel/Monument will buckle your knees. Best part about it, it is free! Unless you pay for a guided tour of course. Take pictures of this massive chapel from the outside, but trust me pictures do no justice. Once you walk inside make sure you remove any headgear. Upon looking to the ceiling, you will notice a circular opening. This is called the Oculus which is one of the most impressive features of the Pantheon. The Pantheon is host to many different symbols from different religions so you must pay attention to that. It is influenced not only by Christianity but Greek mythology as well. This building was burnt down in the great fire and reconstructed, so that might explain the different symbology that covers it today. The Pantheon is an amazing survivor. It was built by the Roman emperors Agrippa and Hadrian and has survived intact and remained in almost constant use since.From the outside, the façade has all the typical features of a Roman temple including a portico, Corinthian columns and a stately pediment. The emperor Hadrian added the rotunda around 126 AD.The queue is often long but don’t be put off by this because the inside is incredible and is completely different to the outside. You should absolutely see both. In any case, the crowd moves quite quite quickly because people don’t stay inside for long.You can pay for an audio guide inside. We didn't and in hindsight, we probably should have. It would have been much better to hear about the structure while we were actually there. If you don’t do the audioguide or tour, then I’d recommend doing some reading beforehand.As you enter, the first thing to notice are the marble floor tiles. This is the original ancient Roman floor. I was very taken with the thought that I was walking across the same floor that ancient Romans had also walked on.The next thing you notice is that the interior is perfectly proportioned. The diameter of the rotunda is 43.3m which is exactly the same as its height – a perfect space. The dome is the real jaw-dropper. It is still the largest unreinforced concrete dome in the world. Never been beaten. That is quite an engineering feat and it required a few building tricks to stop the weight of the dome from causing the whole thing to collapse. To avoid disaster, they built the foundations and lower walls out of heavy materials, then as they built upwards, they made the concrete thinner and added pumice so that it was quite lightweight. Finally, as you look up, you stare into the the oculus, the ‘eye’ of the dome. It might look small but that’s just an optical illusion because the interior is so big. The oculus is actually 8.2m across. The sunlight streams through it, stopping the interior from being gloomy and dark. Rain can also stream through but it drains away in the centre. Before you leave, do a lap around the walls because there are also several noteworthy tombs including Raphael and the first kings of Italy, Victor Emmanuel II and Umberto I.

The exact age of the pantheon remains unknown. Possibly started under Emperor Trajan around AD 114 and completed under Emperor Hadrian between AD 125 and 128. The Pantheon - the Greek adjective meaning "honor all Gods" - had for more than 1700 years, the largest dome in the world (inside diameter: 43.30m), at that time impressive and astonishing. It is one of the best-preserved structures of Roman antiquity.Even, almost 2000 years later, the design, proportions, elegance and harmony are spectecular and amazing and the buliding is a great reminder of the architecture of the great Roman Empire.There is certainly a lot of people and there are always tourists. Not far from the Piazza Navona stands this beautiful structure, which is so unique, still in excellent shape and could be voted as one of the wonders of modern civilization. We finished our Tartufo, and we were on a mission to discover this fantastic piece of architecture.Patricia remarked it is one of ancient Rome's most enduring constructions, and it is a building worthy of the Gods that defies imagination. It is one of the few places where it is possible to view Rome, just as the Romans gazed at it when first constructed. As soon as we entered this massive open place, I imagined this was no ordinary structure or temple that exhibits a timelessness where everything seems to have ceased two thousand years ago when it was dedicated to all gods. The sixty-foot granite columns at the entrance are just a sample of what we are to expect inside. Emperor Hadrian commissioned it, and most of the granite was transported from Aswan, similar to the Obelisks that were looted from the land of the Pharaohs.t One should stand outside and admire the enormous sixty-ton columns that were quarried from Egypt. Spare a thought and think of the logistics that were involved in the transportation of these massive columns across the Mediterranean Sea. The time, tools and human resources that would have gone into the preparation of this significant project would have taken many months of diligent contingency planning which would have included, procuring slave labour, food and deciphering the precise navigation route across the Mediterranean.As I gazed around, the interior has remained immaculately intact with its spectacular dome three metres high at its oculus top, through which light tickles illuminating the entire building. I was amazed at this structure, and Patricia remarked that when any rain penetrates, there are twenty-two holes that drain the water away.It is a visual feast for the eye, that is so striking, massive, well preserved, and perfectly proportioned that makes it so hard to believe that it was erected nearly two thousand years ago. Just as you think there is not much more to admire, the skylight lets in a beam of sun that makes the whole place light up. It is as good as it gets, and my imagination reverts to its early days when Emperor Hadrian entered the building in a vast, impressive procession. The music would herald in that the lord of the land was in session at this glorious temple. I was stunned and astonished, and without any hesitation, I will remark loudly, out of all the surviving structures of ancient Rome the Pantheon elicits amazement, thanks to its breathtaking construction. One last mention is that it holds the tombs of Raphael and the first kings of Italy, Victor Emmanuel, and Umberto. NB:NB: This was a part of the tour pamphlets I would hand to my clients in the 1980s who visited my agency.

This is an incredible bit of architecture and it's truly breathtaking. Created by the emperor Hadrian between 113–125 AD, it has a rectangular vestibule that links the porch to the rotunda, which is under a coffered concrete dome, with a central opening to the sky - which is insane as the evening we were there it was raining & the rain was just falling onto the marble floor below!  Almost two thousand years after it was built, the Pantheon's dome is still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome & it is a sight to be seen. Having been continuously used since the 7th century, it is one of the best-preserved of all Ancient Roman buildings & is now a Catholic church. A number of significant people are buried at the Pantheon, including tow Italian Kings and the Renaissance painter Raphael. This is a must on your visit to Rome, then walk back outside to one of the many restaurants surrounding the church and just take in your surroundings. Just lovely!

The Pantheon has been built in 27-25 BC by Marcus Agrippa as a temple for the gods.  It was saved from destruction thanks to the fact that in the Middle Ages, in 609 it became a Catholic church called "Sancta Maria ad Martyres", then in 1000, it was changed to the name of basilica "Santa Maria della Rotonda".  It is to be admired the beauty of this building from outside and inside the architecture, with its circular structure, the immense dome, the huge colunnas, the mosaics, the interior marble, and the graves of illustrious characters such as kings, artists, such as Annibale Carracci, Baldasare Peruzzi, Taddeo Zuccari, Raffaelo Sanzio, etc.Then a walk in the surroundings will be very pleasant, full of businesses, restaurants and a good coffee in the "Tazza D'oro".

Pantheon in Rome is  a round temple on the Field of Mars, founded by the emperor Hadrian in 125 in the place from 27 BC , destroyed in a fire in 64 AD.  The construction of the building was directed by Apollodoros, who was exiled and probably later executed by the emperor. The Pantheon was dedicated to planetary deities and the reigning emperor at the time, and since the 7th century the Pantheon has been used as a Catholic church  Santa Maria ad Martyres . It is one of the best preserved buildings from the times of ancient Rome. The Pantheon is a rotunda with a diameter of 42.2 m and the same height. In front of the entrance to the temple, there is a three-row portico of 16 columns in Corinthian order. The dome of the building is cast from unreinforced, monolithic concrete with a central hole  with a diameter of about 7.9 m, the only hole in the dome illuminating the interior. Since the opening has no cover, the floor in the middle is slightly concave with an outflow to drain rainwater. Rectangular and semicircular cavities alternate in the wall . They are used for burial chapels. In one of them is the grave of Rafael, one of the greatest painters of the world. A wonderful and charming historical object on the city map. Certainly one of the most important points to visit on the map of Rome.

Once again I visit the magical Pantheon, a church in which the real kings of Italy are buried and among the many a man no one remembers, Raphael Sanzio. I want to focus on the structure of the roof, still resistant and of Roman times, built for those times with a bold technique and still existing. The central hole is the secret of the preservation of this ancient temple, through the drafts enter and exit despite the large opening rains very little inside and do not create on the walls the mold and moisture that other buildings know. Really fantastic this beautiful monument, we hope that modern man respects it as well as done by the ancients.

- Best preserved Roman building with Eight graceful granite Corinthian columns. -2,000 years old structured dome remains the world’s largest unreinforced concrete dome. Must visit place but crowded with tourists.

It was formerly a Roman temple but is now a Catholic Church, Basilica of St Mary and the Martyrs. The building is cylindrical with a portico of large granite Corinthian columns - eight in the first rank and two groups of four behind, under a pediment. A rectangular vestibule links the porch to the rotunda , which is under a coffered concrete dome, with a central opening to the sky, allowing rainfall through the ceiling and onto the floor. Thus  the interior floor is equipped with drains and has been built with an incline of about 30 cm to promote water run off. Really amazing . Circles and squares form the underlying theme of the interior design. The checkerboard floor pattern contrasts with the concentric circles  of square coffers in the dome. Enshrined on the apse above the high altar is the icon of the Virgin and child. The first niche to the right of the entrance holds Madonna of the Girdle and St Nicholas of Bari. The first Chapel on the right , the Chapel of Annunciation , has a fresco of the Annunciation.  On the left side is a canvas of St Lawrence and St Agnes. The second niche has a fresco depicting the Coronation of the Virgin. In the second Chapel is the tomb of King Victor Emmanuel II . The tomb consists of a large bronze plague surmounted by a Roman eagle and the arms of the. House of Savoy. The golden lamp above the tomb burns in honor of Victor Emmanuel II.

A visit to the Pantheon of Rome is a pilgrimage or visit in your schedule of places to visit in this beautiful city.It holds the title of the sacred place of more than 2000 thousand years, one of the oldest and best preserved in the City of Rome.Bread means: Everything (Theo) means Gods, a place that is easily accessible and that does not need reference, because it has its own brightness.Your entry is very easy, if you are in the city of Rome you only have to walk in the direction of it, you follow the entry line and that is if I recommend respecting the rules of entry , since this building is dedicated to Catholic worship and therefore must follow and abide by its already established rules.Your entry is completely free, and there is no limit time to be on site, one can enjoy the time you want or need to enjoy such a colossal structure.It has a single entrance from the Piazza de la Rotanda, on the main facade, has an inscription that says Marco Agrippa , Son of Lucius Consul for the third time did it.In this way he attributes its construction to Marco Vigzanio Agrippa. General and friend of Emperor Augustus.The third consulate of Agrippa indicates that it is the year 27, B.C. For centuries this inscription was thought to refer to the current building, but in the 19th century, it was investigated and found that the Temple of Agrippa was destroyed, and the one we see now was built in Hadrian's time, around the 2nd century, after Christ.The building is circular in shape with a portico of large Corinthian granite columns.Co a largest unarmed concrete dome in the world, with its central opening or (oculo) to the sky, 43 meters in diameter, is state-owned , visited in 2013 by 6 million people.A great cultural, architectural, faith and art experience that you cannot miss if you are in such a wonderful city.

Constructed between 112AD and 126AD, The Pantheon is a former Roman temple, now a church of immense size in central Rome. The Pantheon, a temple in which is the best preserved Ancient Roman monument. The Pantheon, is a Greek adjective meaning “Honour all Gods” since the temple itself was first built as a temple to all Gods. The construction of The Pantheon was carried out during the reign of Hadrian, in 126 A.D. but in the 7th century it was donated to Pope Boniface IV. This donation led to The Pantheon being transformed into a church to which it still stands to this day, this can be a contributing factor as to why The Pantheon has been well preserved throughout this time. The Pantheon itself has another important function since the interior holds numerous tombs of Italian Kings as well as artworks - including Rapheal (a painter and Renaissance architect). The interior of The Pantheon is fascinating for the giant dome and the “Eye of the Pantheon” which is the hole at the top of this dome. The dome itself was the largest in the world for over 1000 years and remains the largest unsupported dome in the world. The “Eye of The Pantheon” allows natural sunlight to beam through but unfortunately allows rainwater through too, which is why you will see a slight slant and drains onto the floor. Another lighting effect, which is only viewable on April 21st, is when the midday sun strikes a metal grille located above the doorway, which in turn leads the entrance way to fill with light. In turn this illuminated the current emperor in these times, to which was perceived as their status as “God on Earth”. Many people take this stance and believed that the emperor was the same level of the Gods who had invited him to join them. Not only is the interior of The Pantheon filled with incredible visions and history but the exterior of The Pantheon is something to marvel at. The most fascinating aspect of the architecture is that the measurements from the circular aspect has the same height and diameter, the architecture was ahead of themselves in these times. The sixteen granite columns measure 14 meters in height, above these columns is a rectangular facade which encompasses the dome also has an inscription: “M.AGRIPPA.L.COS.TERTIVM. FECIT.”. The inscription when translated reads “Marcus Agrippa, son of Lucius, built this temple when he was a Consul”. Even though these letters were replaced and refurbished, it’s thought that these letters would’ve been in gilded bronze.

The dome is still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome. An absolute must see on an adventure to Rome.

The Pantheon is always one of my first stops when in the Eternal City. Pantheon Rome :- Even today, almost 2000 years after its construction, the breathtaking pantheon is a remarkable building to see. The spectacular design, proportions, elegance and harmony are a striking reminder of the architecture of the great Roman Empire. When Michelangelo saw this wonder for the first time he said that it looks more like the work of angels, not humans.The word Pantheon is a Greek adjective meaning “honor all Gods”. In fact the pantheon was first built as a temple to all gods. The Pantheon as we know it today was built in 120 AD by Emperor Hadrian  It is the best preserved Ancient Roman   monument. The most wonderful part of the Pantheon is its giant dome, with its famous hole in the top (The eye of the Pantheon, or oculus). The dome was the largest in the world for 1300 years and until today it remains the largest unsupported dome in the world! You will never forget the Rome, if you visit this place.

This structure, while converted to a catholic church around 608 AD, was originally a Roman temple dedicated to the Gods of Rome. It is one of the truly monumental spaces ever built and the oldest continually occupied building in the western world. It has a pleasing proportion to the space as the height of the dome is equal to the diameter of the space. This structure was erected around 128 AD by the emperor Hadrian after the previous temple (built by Agrippa) had been destroyed by fire. The inscription over the door still reads in Latin that it was built by Agrippa, which some describe to a very magnanimous gesture by Hadrian. The dome is amazing, using a variety of different materials and engineering techniques it was the largest dome in the world until the 1400's when the cathedral in Florence was constructed and it still the largest reinforced concrete dome anywhere. The oculus (hole in the center of the roof) was one of the methods that the Romans used to lighten the dome as well as provided amazing light into the space (when it rains they rope off that section of the floor since it does get wet). Free to get in just remember to dress appropriately as it is still officially a church though I'm not sure that masses are still held there.

I love this building. have been to Rome five times and still love to come there and just take it all in. The Pantheon is a marvel of engineering. Although built around AD124, it still holds the record as the largest unreinforced concrete dome in the world. The interior is all open below the dome (except for some small side chapels), and its "openness" belies how incredibly large this structure is the oculus (the opening to the air at the top of the dome) is thirty feet across, yet when you are down below it does not seem so large. The wonderful proportions of this building help give it an intimacy not found in similar-sized buildings. But more than the architecture, the Pantheon contains the tomb of Raphael and two of Italy's kings. The Pantheon was dedicated as a church in the 7th century (and features some side chapels). This helped it retain much of its original finishes and columns, unlike many of the Roman buildings which were looted for their marble, columns, etc. The Pantheon should be on everyone's list when coming to Rome.

One of the free entry attractions in Rome, just join the queue, Wasn't worried about doing an Audio Tour, wait time 5-7 mins, quite busy but comfortable.Definitely a must see in Rome, beautiful piece of history,

Top of the must do things when visiting Rome! This ancient roman temple , converted church is a stunning piece of engineering and stands beautiful in all its glory after 2000 years. You should take time inside appreciating it’s true splendour and the fabulously designed ceiling which is its only source of natural light. The entry into this iconic monument is free too.

If you don’t have a tour guide that talks about this, download the Rick Steve’s app. He has a 30 min episode just on the Pantheon. We were there in November. For nearly empty pictures, go during the week and when it’s misting rain. You’ll have it all to yourselves.
After seeing so many beautiful uncrowded churches the pantheon seemed crowded and unimpressive. My wife thought it was great. She is more interested in the history and architecture than me.
Not to be missed when you visit Rome. Free entry here. To step into a two thousand year old Roman temple so well preserved is very special.
still standing and in use. Marvelous dome with open air oculus. Truly an engineering marvel and inspirational space.

The Pantheon, among many other building, was on our bucket list to see. The incredible engineering gives us the most preserved building of ancient Rome.  Built between 113-125AD, it is awe inspiring as you enter and look up to the dome. It is the biggest brick dome in architecture history.

Simply one of the most magnificent monuments of Rome which has stood for almost 2 Millennia. The Pantheon was the blueprint for all other domed churches and is an architectural marvel, especially considering the dome is non reinforced concrete. The best part is that it is free to visit and the queue to get in moves quite quickly!
Attended here as night fell on Rome. Beautiful converted church. You wouldnt believe that its almost 2000 years old.

Impressive building which is of course a must-see in Rome. Free entrance. Found within a lovely little market area which offers load of restaurants and gelato shops. As always with crowded area's: Be wary of pickpocketers.A quick visit of about 30 minutes is usually enough to see the Pantheon.

Enter through the 2 enormous doors and you will immediately be astounded.  If you have the time, buy the audio tour.  It's just one large room so you will not be overwhelmed here. First look upwards and spend some time viewing the incredible ceiling.   It is an engineering marvel that they would be able to construct something like that in 26 AD (give or take a few years).   Then walk around and view all of the history inside this temple (now a church).  The square outside has some great restaurants, as well.  Recommended.

It's difficult to think of anything to say about the Pantheon that has not already been said. It is important to remember that this ancient temple was converted into a Catholic church, and remains so today. There is the possibility that Mass is being said inside and there is a permanent altar and seating area for this purpose. Overall, the ancient interior scheme is fairly well preserved, though the pagan statues have been removed (along with the bronze covering from the outside of the dome). When you go, marvel at the fact that this structure remains the largest unreinforced concrete dome in the world. Why is this impressive? This building from the Hadrianic era (the previous version built by Marcus Agrippa burned, so don't be fooled by the pediment inscription) stands intact due to expert engineering that we cannot easily achieve today! Whilst you are in the Pantheon, you can visit the tombs of Raphael (yes, THE Raphael as in of the School of Athens, etc), as well as Italian kings Vittorio Emanuele II and Umberto I.
A beautiful structure on the outside; breathtaking beautiful on the inside --- did not realize that it is now a church.
Worth seeing would go again. Some very interesting artefacts, statues and roof to pursues over. The marble floors can be sloppy so take care.
Another amazing old building, truly beautiful. At night it's all lit up. I sadly didnt get to go inside, next time if possible!
Try to visit when the crowd is not there yet, with the sunlight falling through the oculus in the ceiling. History becomes almost tangible.
Absolutely worth to see. Free visit inside and enjoy see the tall floor and old thinges in this old tample.
Basically you are paying for the audio explanation, the entrance to the place is free, so unless you are very interested with the architecture it is not worthy.

One of my favourite memories from Rome is walking through the alleys of the city then turning the corner to see the great Pantheon sitting in front of you, coming almost out of nowhere. It is just so impressive to see. Take a moment to sit back and take it in, and think about how on earth did they build that dome, and bring those pylons at the front all the way from Egypt, almost 2000 years ago...

We went to see the fountains @ night and obviously passed the Pantheon. Also the Pantheon is spectacular after sunset! Of course - also beautiful during the day - but the atmosphere during after sunset, with the lights on I certainly prefer!
This was our favorite place in Rome - why? There is a strange inner quiet here, though it is packed with tourists, it still holds a stillness.
Like most things in Rome this is a must see. It doesn’t take much time but it is amazing on the inside! I would only budget an hour for this place.

Looking at the Pantheon from the outside, you'd never realize the way in which the domed roof and circular design influence the beauty within.  We didn't do a specific tour or use an audio guide but simply walked throughout the Pantheon admiring the art and architecture.  It was quite crowded the day that we were there and we had to wait outside for quite awhile to get in because it was closed to visitors for a mass, so you may wish to investigate whether any special services are being held when planning your visit.

I had seen the Pantheon on TV and wasn’t in a hurry to visit.On our first evening however, we walked over to it and it went inside and i found it to be amazing.Just the sheer size is impressive.It can only be appreciated in person so not to be missed if visiting Rome.Also, it’s free admission and in the center and easy to walk to so there’s no reason not to visit.

If you get the opportunity to make it to the Pantheon, give it a look.it is a  interesting, beautiful church, and has the world's largest poured concrete dome. The art is phenomenal and it is free entry. Please remember it is a church so be respectful as people are praying.
This is a fantastically magnificent  historical site in downtwn Rome and situated in a great place for walking.
What a treat to visit the Pantheon again now under the guidance of an Architecture Professors from Rome. Getting the inside scoop and seeing this architectural gem is not to be missed.
The outside does not do this place justice. Go inside & marvel at the setting.You can just feel the history. A pity tourists don't observe silence in this sacred space.
This is a majestic building set in a larger than expected place, once again this structure pops out from nowhere

The Pantheon area is one of my favourites when I come to Rome.   Always a lively part of the city, day and night.  This stunning monument is a temple to ‘all gods’ hence the name ‘Pantheon’.   A stunning example of Augustan Rome.   Built by Agrippa and Hadrian it’s the worlds biggest unsupported concrete dome.   Go the middle when inside and look up and see the sky.   Home to Vittorio Emanuele II mausoleum amongst others.   It’s free to enter but be mindful no sandals, flip flops or sleeveless tops else you will be turned away as shoulders must be covered up.  Come and night too as it’s stunning then with the lights/laser.

We purchased the audio which was facilitated by a mobile phone held up to our ear. This allowed us to walk to each separate area and be able to understand the significant aspects what we were looking at. Much better than just looking with no explanation.
This place is a must see WOW.You are in awe of the grandeur and the build of this place.A little touristy so can get extremely busy at certain times.EnjoyMonaro
have a walk through the small streets and alleys to the Pantheon, book your ticket in advance to skip the line
Do not miss this when you go to Rome.  Get in the middle and take a picture of the oculus and take in all of the wonderful sites.

The Parthenon is a site not to be missed as it is a beautiful, ancient and sacred building with gorgeous religious icons and statues. I loved the Pantheon and would highly recommend it to someone visiting Rome.

This wonderful historic church was converted from a temple . The dome is a perfect circle . if you had sphere the size of the dome it would fit perfectly Within the building. No charge to enter. Though they do hold chargeable concerts in it , which has wonderful acoustics. It’s also an active church .

It’s free to get in but like with all of the sightseeing locations in Rome, if you want to know the history of the place you can purchase an app for your phone, I didn’t do this and it was still interesting to walk around and see the magnificence of the place.

Unfortunately it was repurposed as a church along the way,  disturbing the purity of the ancient design and replacing the classical statues with Christian statues.

What a gem.  The architecture still is astonishing and the art is stunning.  It's hard to believe that it is open to the public.
Well worth the visit. Free to get into and you didn’t have to queue. Worth the visit. Some lovely shops and restaurants near by too

Even though it’s now a church it’s easy to imagine this as a fully realized pagan temple 2000 yrs ago, and seeing it before the Forum makes it easier to imagine all those buildings and what they must have been. Elegant simple and powerful architecture.

We walked by the Pantheon when there was quite a line, so we returned the next day at an earlier time and we were able to walk right in and enjoy this marvelous architectural wonder.  To think that it has been in continuous use for 2,000 years is incredible!

this was a great location to visit. we visited late at night and it was safe to visit with kids and family. even though we could not go in, the structure just holds you in awe from outside. and it was a great time to take pictures w/o worrying about others getting in the frame.

Take time to stroll the side streets, walk along the river, and linger over lunch at an open air cafe.  Don't be afraid to get lost. The Italian people are warm, helpful, generous people, and we had some of our most memorable experiences from being lost!

The Pantheon is a church in the centre of Rome that was formerly a Roman temple that was thought to have been the private temple of Marcus Agrippa, the son-in-law of Augusta. The building is a chapel in the shape of a rotunda, but with a front entrance built in the form of a Neoclassical Portico. The interior is an enormous rounded room covered by a giant dome, with niches around the room for chapels.
The Pantheon is breathtaking. It is in excellent condition. Hard to believe it is 2000 years old. Amazing
Sometimes could be crowded (yet civilized), nonetheless this is such a masterpiece of architecture that one cannot ignore.
no words, just go.  Take your time and enjoy the history.  Friday at 5 pm is a mass, so the doors will closed to tourist

Seeing the pictures cannot even come close to the experience of seeing and entering the Pantheon. Now a church, it has a lot of history and apparently its a huge sundial, so if you are passing by try and stop in a different times of the day.

This is our favorite spot in Rome, inside and out. But the illumination was minimal the two nights we were there on this visit.

Visited the pantheon and was impressed by the history behind it and the view. The large Dome on top was extremely impressive and what happens when it rains is also impressive.

Sorry to miss such a place, maybe on our next trip.still mazes at the crowds even with a closed building. I guess a lot of folks like us were surprised it was closed.

text: One has to realize the AGE of this structure and it's history. Pantheon - temple to all gods. As different religions occupied Rome, the Pantheon changed the gods. Also, the architecture of the dome and oculus - the fact it is a perfect sphere if the dome was continued to ground level. Be sure to do a bit of research BEFORE you to there. You will appreciate it much more.
Impressive for an historical point of view. But to took at less so. Access was relatively easy and the place not too crowded unlike other spots in Rome.
This place was just amazing, absolutely incredible architecture and very well kept remains of this wonderful masterpiece! It took our breath away, would highly recommend!

The entire structure is another site that you need to experience.   The oculus is an architectural wonder by itself.  The adjacent court yard is festive and full of life.   Colorful street vendors and musicians dot the immediate area.   Not as crowded as some of the major attractions in Rome.  Worth the time to go in the late afternoon to gain inside access (free), then eat in one of the nearby restaurants, and then stroll the area after dark, maybe some gelato for a late dessert?

One of the best-preserved Roman buildings, (it is very old). The Pantheon was built in 126 AD as a temple for all the Roman gods. Can you believe it, the temple has served as a Roman Catholic Church since the 7th century. Columns in back, considered to be 2,000 years old.Fact: (I love this part) - the Pantheon’s famous dome remains the world’s largest unreinforced concrete dome. The supreme achievement of Roman interior architecture hangs suspended without visible supports - these are well hidden inside the walls - and its nine-meter central opening is the building's only light source.Famous painter Raphael, as well as Italian kings Victor Emmanuel II and Umberto I are all buried there. Don’t forget to check their tomb. There is Dress code, cover your shoulders, shorts are not allowed. Also, check the timings before goingIMP: IT’S FREE..!! Square Piazza della Rotonda where pantheon is located, is filled up with cafes, pizza shops and yes, Gelato.

The Pantheon was built around the birth of Christ, and is considered to be a “perfect” space because the diameter of the rotunda is equal to that of its height. In my opinion, it's worth a trip to Rome just to see this structure.  Unbelievable!
the most impressive church in Rome. The age of this church requires respect from the first moment. Must see !
This size of this temple is breathtaking as you first see it, It seems much smaller if photos. This is something that cannot be missed during your stay in Rome and going inside is a must!

No visit to Rome is complete until you visit the Pantheon.  It is an amazing structure almost 2000 years old and still looks wonderful inside and out. A little crowded but everything in Rome is crowded.  A must see when you visit Rome.

Really enjoyed the history and architectural experience of the Acropolis, however, it was so crowded that our party of three got separated from our tour guide and two of us ended up visiting the sites on our own, hence no guided tour that we had paid for. Unfortunately I don't believe that there is ever an uncrowded time at this area. My only suggestion is to take the tour with a small group, with provided earphones to hear your guide. The sensation of walking down a narrow street, turning a corner, and suddenly being directly in front of one of the most iconic buildings of the ancient world (it is nearly 2000 years old) is incredible!
