It is a significant skyline sight.Remember it is a sacred space and be respectful.

When taking into consideration the history of the Pantheon and all of it’s happening this is an amazing building with an even better history that we are fortunate enough to still have here for us to visit in great condition!And it’s even free to enter!
My favorite site in Rome. So well preserved and awe inspiring. Visit at night and during the day if possible to get the full effect and really view the magnificent architecture.

Nothing really prepares you for turning the corner into the square and seeing the Pantheon. Stunning and huge! After seeing the ruined temples in the forum the day before it was wonderful to see one still standing. There was a longish queue but it moved really quickly. Once inside it’s incredible- the dome is amazing and with the sunlight streaming through the hole in the roof was really moving. Astonishing engineering - best place we visited in Rome.
A visit to the Pantheon is a must for anyone travelling to Rome, with the bonus that it is free to enter!! It can get busy at times but the queue's tend to move quickly.

One of my favorite sights in Rome time and again. This is not to be missed. Occasionally there is a line in front to get in but it moves quickly and is well worth the wait to see inside and gaze up at the beautiful roof.

This is another stop on the four hour Tome bike tour. We spent about 20 min there...very interesting and the info they give you prior to going in is awesome! I highly recommend the four hour Rome bike tour...you get TONS of info, ride around Rome and see ALL the Roman highlights and numerous places!!!
It was a Sunday & as we walked in the choir was just ending the most beautiful song...it was an incredibly touching moment!

Compared to the Roman Forum, this structure was very well preserved, since it was converted into a church. The dome was the largest in the world for a long time and a miracle of ancient engineering. Highly recommend this.

Structure was completed ~126AD by the emperor Hadrian but it was started under the reign of Augustus (27BC -17AD).   The building is cylindrical with a portico of large granite Corinthian columns supporting a pediment.  An architectural design copied still today. This is a must see site.  The dome is coffered concrete (inset squares) with a central opening to the sky.  It is still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome. And the drain still works because rain does fall through the opening. It is so well preserved because it has been actively used since it was built. It was dedicated as a church in the 7th century and still in use today.

Ok worth a visit for sure and you can combine it with Fontana do Trevi and the Colosseum easily. I absolutely loved the paintings on the ceiling and the monumental building style in general. It's fee too so going in the morning you almost have the place for yourself. Don't forget to head downstairs into the crypt.
Much larger than I thought. Lovely inside. Very easy to find. Well worth a visit. Truly an amazing structure.
Went day and evening.  Beautifully lit up. Used the app for an audio tour off our own phone. Bring your headphones.

We thought this was the perfect way to learn about the Pantheon!!! No need to pay for a tour guide.  Plus it gets quite loud and crowded and I think a tour group would be more difficult than doing on your own.

This was our first stop in Rome and such an amazing piece of old architecture. Crazy to imagine how they built that dome 2000 years ago. The place was not crowded when we were there and could take plenty of time looking at everything. A big plus is the free entrance.

We visited the Pantheon early in the morning best time if you dont want to wait forever,the inside of the building is unbelievable,you have to see it to believe it,photos do it no justice,and the dome you look and wonder hoe did they do it,it is incredible the work that was done so many thousands of years ago.You must see this when you are in Rome.
We went there in early morning..entry was free. We didn't take any tour guides...u can take the audio tours for a small charge inside. We loved the architecture and inside artwork.
My favorite piazza day and night.  Day for the inside of the historical pantheon.  Night for sitting outside with lemoncello and watching the live entertainment.
An Amazing piece of history, the concrete dome is amazing and it is free to go in and look inside. It is a must see place in Rome. Great architecture and historic pieces inside.

Try to go in the afternoon when it's  not as busy. We went 11am and noon and there was a line to go in so we skipped it.   Went at 6pm and there was no line, just went right in. Plan to stay no longer than 15 to 20 minutes.  It's about a 10 -15 minute walk from the Spanish Steps Metro.  I think that's the closest Metro station. The other two, Barberini and Republica weren't open when we were there.  Must see site in Rome. It's truly amazing how it's been around since 126 AD.  There is a portion of the Pantheon where there is a makeshift chapel to  say a prayer.  I did notice a lot of people just sitting down looking at their phones and not really praying.  Pretty sad to see how people can be disrespectful.  Don't be one of them.
We went to visit the Pantheon later in the afternoon and it was great no lines and not that many people. this is a must see.
Fascinating! A real masterpiece! Roman Genius at the Service of Human History and Memory! And that orifice at the very top, open to the stars, from where the gods call us.
Rome in May is full of people,  I enjoyed the city much more in January.  Watch out for pickpockets and pigeons.

I have seen pictures of the Pantheon, even climbed around in it during Assassin's Creed, but those are nothing like the real thing. It really is a sight to behold.That said, aside from the massive size and of course the dome, there is very little to it.
former Roman temple, now a church. The Dome is huge and it has  no column support. Must place to visit to admire the construction capabilities of ancient rome

Free to get in to and the queue to enter was less than 5 minutes, visited around 11am. Very impressive decor/ architecture and lots of history around it. I loved the way the sun shone through the open hole in the ceiling. It's a working church so it's recommended you dress appropriately (no low cut tops, cover your shoulders) - though I was let in and I was wearing a vest top and shorts so I don't think they're strict on dress code. We spent 30mins max inside, definitely worth a visit.
A quick visit, entrance is for free, no queue. Could be done with Trevi Fountain which is at a walking distance

A big, open space with a lovely dome with a thirty foot wide hole in it to let light and air in. You can see lovely artwork and the tomb of Raphael. We were told by our guide that if they closed the doors when it rained, it would stop raining inside.

We just walked in one afternoon and had a look around. The “silence” rule is not enforced which means that it is fairly loud and there were even children running around. It was crowded. Nevertheless, it was stunning. I really enjoyed taking pictures here.
Probably one of our longer walks but was worth it in the end, a amazing piece of architecture considering it was built so long ago, scholars lrish pub is just round the corner and has a good pint
I just love the Pantheon. No long lines, no admission, great location, amazing architecture and monument to Emmanuel.
The Pantheon was one of the first  ‘must see’ sights we visited and it was breathtaking. To walk in and look up at that extraordinary dome was just incredible. Beautiful.

Do a walking tour with this included and it brings a lot of the building and structures to life. This is one! Free entry, spend about 15 mins looking around is enough. Lots of people.... as you would expect

This was unexpectedly my favourite of the sites I visited whilst in Rome. It’s a beautiful building, full of history and the fact that it’s free to enter is wonderful. We paid a small fee (a few Euros) to listen to the built in audio guides which are inside which was worth it to get some background history on what we were looking at. The square around the Pantheon has many restaurants and cafes which provided a brilliant view of the architecture. Our only disappointment was that the weather was so lovely we didn’t get to witness the rain draining away through the cleverly designed floors.
We came here 2 years ago and didn't realise it was free to enter then. Didn't have to queue and there was enough room inside to move around and take photos.
We really enjoyed here. Kids also liked to watch and the contrast of rusty outside and beautiful inside really was written into memories.
2000 year old engineering and construction is an incredible feat.  The history from pre-Christian to post Christian purpose is also interesting.

We visited around 1pm in the hottest of afternoons and were greeted by the sun spilling in through the opening in the roof..it was breathtaking.The alter and  artworks around the walls and ceilings are some of the finest you will see.We really were shocked at the unexpected fabulousness of the Pantheon and we sat and just drank in its majesty There was a long and off putting queue but persevere as it moves very quickly and you will be thrilled by the interior. The rotunda and area around the Pantheon are buzzing and you can sit on the surrounding walls or fountain and just take it all in.

Second time visit and still amazed at how magnificent this building is. From the Piazza it is just so incredible.  During the day it is overly crowded, so have dinner and come past around 10pm and take some amazing photos without the people.  Only issue is you cannot go inside at night.

There are so many amazing things to see in Rome and you do so much walking it’s tempting to pass on something, but make sure you stop here. This is truly  an amazing work of engineering and so different from most of the other Roman buildings!   And it’s totally free. Stop, rest your tired feet and try to fathom how this was designed without computers and machinery.

Like many similar structures in Rome, the Pantheon is a must. The artwork is superb, and the story of its construction, the change of its religious significance, and the phenomen of the light penetration through the opening in the dome at a specific date and time underlines the degree of engineering skills that were available at the time of its construction. Be prepared to see just how little respect is shown by by tourists to the fact that this place is an active church.

Fellow tourists - there are too many of us!  The Pantheon along with the Vatican and Sistine Chapel were horrendously crowded and people take no notice of the requests for silence and no photography.  If you care about reverence in these places you will be disappointed. Try hard to concentrate on what you really want to see.

Not to be missed when visiting Rome. Amazing architecture and visually stunning inside. Take time to marvel at the wonder of what the ancient Romans achieved. Sit in the square and take in the whole experience.
This Cathedral is so amazingly big....its free to go inside..but try to get in in the morning..while I was inside I listened to the Rick Steve's app, it was full of great info

the pantheon is a cool piece of roman history. it is free to get in. if the line is long don't be discouraged as the line moves pretty fast. it is in walking distance of many other attractions. souvenir shops and restaurants all around for you to choose from

Awesome place to visit.  Easy to get into and though crowded, not that hard to move around inside surprisingly.  Altars inside are beautiful and with it being an active church, there are still services inside.  One of my favorite sites in Rome.
What a beautiful old building! It is magnificent but I wouldn’t go in the busy months as it is so crowded. However, I would not have wanted to miss this gem.

The Pantheon is a "don't miss" while in Rome. This longest lived church (pre Christ) in the world, is incredible.  It serves today as a Catholic church, St. Mary and the Martyrs. The building itself is beautiful, be sure to look up when inside to see the 9 meter occulus in the center of the dome.  It is open to the sky, not covered.  The floor beneath this occulus is slightly dished to collect rain water (it did rain that day!) and allow it drain away.  You could easily spend several hours here, taking in the beautiful mosaics, statuary and paintings.
If you haven’t seen this, you must when in Rome.  If you’re not part of a tour, read up on it in Wikipedia or online somewhere to fully understand and appreciate what you’re seeing.

We had a beautiful tour of this incredible, historical building with Rolling Rome Golf Cart Tours. Our guide, Alice made sure we came to this must see building and we learned all about its rich history. A must see !
The Pantheon takes my breath away every time. Sometimes if you are lucky you can get a choir inside. The acoustics are wonderful!
It’s an amazing structure as being the biggest self supporting dome ever built. To think it’s over 1800 years old and still looks structurally sound. Ancient times are amazing.
Really impressive place. Must to visit. You have to be dressed properly. There is no entrance fee its free. Its close to piazza Navona where you can have a break. Spot on the open roof!!!
Great Piazza to vist and enjoy the Pantheon, suggest you enjoy breakfast in one of the restraunts on an early sunny morning and enjoy

Arrived early as this was my last stop before heading to the airport. Spent about 30  minutes admiring the beauty and history. There was a tour guide offering his services as I waited, but since I had downloaded Rick Steves audio guide there was no need. I wish that I had a little more time to stop by the nearby La Casa Del Caffè Tazza D'oro, for one more espresso.Definitely a must do!

A must see site in Rome.  Remember it is a church and so be respectful but it is an impressive piece of architecture and engineering.  From the impressive dome and if I remember rightly the 9m hole in the centre to the marble floors it is awe inspiring to see such a building still intact today.  We were there and the sun was streaming through the opening in the dome sending an angelic shaft of light onto one of the walls illuminating in a beautiful glow.  A short walk from the Trevi Fountain put it on your walking list.  No entrance fee.

Another really terrific ancient and historical monument in the heart of Rome! No tickets necessary either. Short walking distance from Piazza Navona and only takes but a few minutes to visit and view the entire spectacle. A must see!
You cant miss thos beautifulPlace and if tou are lucky it will begin to rain. Younwill see why. Impressive work of emgineering
Fantastic place to see. The history and storyline is so rich. It was closed while there so could not have a  tour through this great building

Stopped in while wandering around Rome - no fee to get in. Beautiful building to see, spectacular marble and of course, the dome. Use Rick Steve's guide while you go through. It was a bit crowded, tho.
Choose early in the morning for visiting, take a map, light shoes, take water and be patient!many people!

We had the pleasure and good fortune to have David Battaglino as our Pantheon tour guide while in Rome.   David was one of our tour guides on the City Tour Guide at the Colosseum, Palatine Hill & the Roman Forum.  His part was his great explanation, wit & humor of Palatine Hill and the Roman Forum.   He is so entertaining, while giving a great dialogue of the history behind these places.   After he finished up at the Forum, he explained that he gives a personal tour of the Pantheon.  We met him and his wife that evening at the Forum with others and we were not disappointed.   I would recommend him very highly and give him 5 STARS!    - Roger Mohn, June 2019
Very interesting piece of architecture. I would recommend being there in the early morning if you want to take pictures without being too crowded.
We toured this on a 2 hour guided walking tour to this and a few other must-see places. Well worth it, very impressive place.
What can I say another must see in Rome.  This is a temple to the 12 most important classical gods.  The art work is beautiful.  and the structure amazing. This was another sight we did on our own.

We've been here before (see our previous Reviews), but we can't resist coming back again, to enjoy what we regard as a truly outstanding "Attraction" in a City which has many, many wonderful venues.Bear in mind that what you experience to-day, is essentially the imaginative work of the 2nd century Emperor, Hadrian, who redesigned and rebuilt the earlier Marcus Agrippa Temple, from around 27 B.C.(the generous inscription on the Porch Facade still says "Marcus Agrippa, son of Lucius, three time Consul, built this" - for all that he actually didn't).What's absolutely stunning, to our eyes, is the vast hemispherical Dome, whose height & diameter are both 43.3 metres, and which provides the only light to this magnificently preserved, ancient building."The Pantheon" (originally a Roman Temple to "All of the Gods"), became a Christian Church in 609 A.D., and remains so to this day.Aside from being an absolute marvel of engineering, the interior is also highly-attractive, with interesting features (such as the Tomb of the superbly-talented Raphael : 1483-1520), and the burial places of Italian Kings from far more recent times."The Pantheon" is on the Piazza della Rotunda, a quite picturesque Square, with relatively expensive Cafes/Bars on its fringes, surrounding the throngs who join the line to visit this Temple/Church. Access is handled smoothly and sensitively, while inside, not all visitors behave with equal sensitivity to the reality that this is still, to-day, a functioning place of worship.There's a small "Souvenir Shop/Store", over on the right-hand side - this year, we purchased a quite tasteful "Coin", showing the Building itself on one side, and the unarguably stunning Dome on the other.And, by the way, if, having experienced the absolute delight of visiting this unique, magnificent "Attraction", you are interested in light sandwich snacks & coffees, at extremely reasonable prices, we highly recommend "Pane Pane" Cafe, on Via del Gesu, which is just a few streets away from the over-priced Piazza della Rotunda.
For us it was a quiet and beautiful, peaceful religious experience. For this one has to be lucky with the timing...

A must see in Rome - we were very lucky today and were able to get in easily. This is a holy place but you can make pictures and it is free. Really gorgeous!! We also visited at night and it was lit up beautifully.

Yes, it is crowded, but go anyway.   The Pantheon is worth seeing.   Be sure to do a little study beforehand -- Rick Steves has good information in his Rome or Italy guidebooks -- so you'll know what you are looking at.  It is a marvel.
Any time you get to enter a piece of ancient history you should do it.  As you would imagine, crowded!  Otherwise the usual great history of Rome on display.
Long entry queue but it moved quickly and we only had to wait 5 minutes to gain entry.  Once inside you could stay as long as you wanted.

The Pantheon is another place you should check out. It is decked out with columns and the inside is cylindrical. it has an unreinforced concrete dome. It is also one of the best preserved building. It has stones from all over the world and it very beautiful. It also helped that we had a guide to tell us all about it.
We had a great experience, our guide was excellent. He was easy to understand, and the tour gave just enough information.
Fantastic structure, as many are in Rome. Hard to believe it's as old as it is and is still so beautiful.
Beautiful structure where the dome is the highlight.  Best seen at night. Great use of marble as part of the design. No entry fee.
If you want to see Pantheon and not crowd of tourists - you should come here at early morning (i guess from 9 am)

Let’s start with it’s free! This is one of the few places in Rome where you can see something this old that still looks as beautiful as it has for centuries. The line to get in takes only minutes and you can explore to your heart’s content. A trip to the Pantheon is a must while in Rome!

The Pantheon is a spectacular building in the middle of historic Rome.  It's free to the public, and filled with in credible art and light. Lines grow as the day progresses, if you want more space and less people go early in the morning. If you have any interest in history and art this is a MUST see.
Free to enter so that's a plus, was a lot of people there but a constant line exiting so easy to see everything, it was a loverly place
This is an incredible piece of roman architechture in almost its complete original form. Amazing, with free entry.
Line was long but moves super quickly! Plus it’s free to enter!  If you’re interested in art, architecture, or history i would highly recommend putting the pantheon at the top of your to-do list!
Could you believe that this is almost two millennia old? Extraordinary engineering, so impressive inside. Free entry.

The line to enter the Pantheon was long, but it didn't take long to be granted entry. Originally built to honor the Roman gods, the structure was turned into a Catholic Church, which is one reason it survives to this date. The floor and religious decorations are quite beautiful. The building also houses the crypts of Italian Kings Vittorio Emanuale II and Umberto I, as well as the artist Raphael. Well worth taking the time to visit. Don't forget to look up - the oculus is 142 feet above the floor. The dome is still the largest (at 142 feet in diameter) unsupported concrete dome in the world and it is nearly 2000 years old.

Walked to the Pantheon from Piazza Navarro. Great architecture... was once a Roman temple and now a church. It is well preserved and the central huge dome has a huge hole in it, the occulus, which is supposed to be the main source of natural light. Although crowded, it is definitely a must see.
It looks old on the outside but It's amazing on the inside. But as with everything in Rome it's super busy but free entryGotta be don't whlie in Rome
We arrived very late, an hour before it close, not that crowded, sitting inside for a while, not too bad.

Worth a walk around the outside of the building first, to see the architecture.  We only queued about 5 mins this was around 11am.Once inside the dome is beautiful and their are information points around the Pantheon written in English.worth while visit.

The Pantheon is a true testimony  to Romain architecture. It looks to be as grand as the day it was built. The art within is from floor to ceiling.The surrounding area is full of shops and cafes as well.
Such a neat experience to walk inside.  We only spent a very short time in here and would have loved to explore more.  An easy stop for a tour of Rome though.
Interesting place to visit, entrance free, special light-atmosphere. If you pass by you should visit. A detour isn't worth it.
A must see if in Rome! Its free to enter and very fast to go through. Don't let long lines intimidate you, it moves so fast, but if you don't like waiting go once they open or after 6pm.
We found the Pantheon by accident and boy was it worth it. Beautiful statues and architectural design.
We visited Rome several times but this was the first occasion when I could go inside.The entrance was free, hardly any queue.Beautiful and unique building .I am so happy I could see it.

Beautiful, both inside and out, with explanations of everything so you're not lost even if youre not part of a tour. Lots of people outside but the flow into the building is controlled by guards at the entrance so you have room to appreciate the place without been too cramped.

A place that my husband and I wanted to see, and it didn’t disappoint. Free to enter, we had to queue for a short while but it wasn’t that bad. What a breathtaking building, over 2,000 years old and dedicated to Marcus Vispanius Agrippa. We were able to sit down inside and take in the beauty and age of the building. Beautiful paintings and vibrant colours, with the oldest atrium in the modern world, a real must to see, situated amongst buildings you would never expect so don’t be put off when trying to find it. Would have hated to miss this amazing place.

Well this is the place as I turned the corner took my breath away I didn't expect it to be in such an unusual place we stayed and sat in there for over half an hour it was so inspiring statues paintings this is a must see place
Free entrance and no line. Not the biggest of attractions but it's an impressive sight from inside and you can look at the tomb of Rafael if you're into that sort of thing.

Simple, quick attraction but it sure is a sight to see. Quite literally one of the most stunning sights in Rome. What makes it so crazy is the fact that it’s in the middle of regular buildings!! You never see it coming.

We visited the Pantheon as it opened at 8. 30am, definitely the best time of day to visit. No queues, walked straight in and wandered around in awe at our leisure. Do not pay for a tour, the incredible building and the information inside is enough. Plenty of bars and restaurants in the Piazza to sit and admire this spectacular temple. Definitely a personal highlight for us!
One more exciting monument in Rome. Interesting and beautiful. Best to visit with a guide, to get the best out of the visit

The issue with the hole in the middle came to fore when the rain came when we visited. Even though it's pouring, you can barely hear the raon drops from the corners of the place. The crowd can be overwhelming but don't let it deter you from taking in the sight.

The Pantheon is by far my favorite place to visit in Rome. The building is simply incredible and it brings a feeling of joy. The internal dome is unsupported and open to the rain, so being inside when it's raining is a magical experience.  It is a church currently, so be aware of dress codes and a need to be silent if at all possible.  There are guided tours or great informational tours with headsets around the edge of the church. This place is beautiful and if you visit one church - make this the one.

The Pantheon is a must see for any geometry or engineering enthusiast. The dome is 43m wide and 43m off the floor. It’s oculus is 30 ft wide and to think it was built almost 2000 years ago is mind boggling.
Went early and there were no lines, as this is a free "attraction".  Words do NOT do it justice.  Was a 3 minute walk from our hotel. Historically amazing.
Free tour. Downside: it has been turned into a Basilica so it lacks some of the Roman era charm. Instead of statues of roman gods you have tombs of famous people that leaved much later.

Can’t that we’ve not visited on previous trips to Rome. Do not miss the Pantheon it’s absolutely stunning feet of Roman engineering. The hole in the roof is uncovered when it rains the rainwater drains through holes in the floor in the centre of the building. Stunning! We visited on first Sunday of the month and it was National Republic day for Italy. The entry was free, bonus as they recently started charging to enter. The queue was fast flowing, took around 15minutes instead of most queues at other attractions in Rome. Don’t miss the Pantheon, definitely worth a visit!  There is an amazing gelato shop Grom nearby, very good!
This place is amazing. It is beautiful and breathtaking. It is so hard to believe how old it is but easy to imagine it being a place of worship for many centuries.
Astonishing architecture. Demonstrates all the might of Roman Empire. But interior is so-so. Let's say not for the art lovers.
My daughter planned a walk around Rome and this was on the route - well worth a visit and queue was really quick
Tips: go to visit this Pantheon (Church) just before the mess because they make the people away and if you leave the last one you can take a good picture without anybody! :-)

Stunning architecture and beautiful marble. Everyone wants to come here and it is easy to see why. The dome is exquisite and is often compared to the one in Mosta which we have also visited. Ladies, be prepared to cover up out of respect.  If it's hot, take a wrap or pashmina so you can slip it round your shoulders and you should be fine to get in even if you are wearing a strappy sundress. If you can manage to squeeze a seat by the fountain opposite, take a few minutes to enjoy and take it all in from the outside too.
I have always really liked this building because of the scale and classical design. Go inside if you an opportunity.
Excellent structure with combination of cylindrical shell and spherical shells along with long and thin age-old bricks.

Easy to navigate to.. nice things to see in the surrounding area too, shops and churches, also great restaurants. The building though, full of folks but who cares when the place is so cool, it’s so big and beautiful you have to keep looking up. I was in there for about an hour, mesmerising architecture. From the rear of the building as you approach, it looks ugly. But wow, get in front of it and then inside. It’s amazing and we’ll worth a look.
How they made that ceiling all those years ago by hand, and into position without electronic machinery and lifting equipment - just wow!

Cleverly built building, that is now used as a church. The whole in the dome fascinated me, it can thunderstorm outside and not one drop off rain will touch the inside of the building. Very clever architecture. A must visit while in Roma.
it was raining and it looked magical from inside the pantheon. The girls and boys choir was singing as well. Truly amazing experience, definitely need to see if from the inside if you're visiting.
An easy freebee, but oh so impressive.  Easy to get to, and no tickets needed.  We went here after a food tour, so glad that we did :-)

We went there after walking the city.  Another amazing building from Ancient Rome and it's still in use today.  Who else but the Romans could build something that lasts for 2,000 years and looks like it could last another 2,000 years.
Easy access from center.  Nice to visit, fun area in general.  Free entrance, no line when we went in late May.
Amazing building. Worth the walk from our hotel. Close to Trevi Fountain. Interesting to see contrast of Roman architecture and later use as a church.

What can be said that hasn’t already been. This is simply an amazing depiction of Roman art and history. A visit to Rome would not be complete without seeing this magnificent structure and the artifacts and art collection within.

The Pantheon is FREE to visit. It has the tombs of Victor Emmanuel II and Umberto I. The dome is pretty amazing. I would recommend for you to visit during the week as the queue will be inexistent compared to the weekend ones that can be quite long.
We were in awe of the building, inside and out.  It is an amazing structure and it wasn't too crowded when we went.

The Pantheon is undoubtedly the most beautiful remnant of Roman antiquity. This temple is very well preserved in centuries and appears to us as the Romans must have seen it in their time! It's a must visit even if you have little time to see Rome, but if you are on long vacation here, take some time to study the Pantheon's history and spend a couple of hours to admire this special building
What a fantastic, amazing place!Take the that the church stole it out of the equation and see this wonderful building for what it was meant to be. Ahhhhhh those Roman's.

What a beautiful place.   It's free to enter.  It's hard to believe it has survived for so many years.  Converted from a pagan temple to a Christian church you can see both styles.  Well worth seeing.

Don't be put off by the long queue outside, it is very fast moving. The Pantheon is FREE to visit and go inside and is usually not that busy. The place itself is lovely inside, however like most places in Rome, be prepared to arrive very early (before 8am) if you wish to capture photographs when it is slightly quieter.

Wonderful building that amazed me.  Although there were crowds queuing to get in they moved very quickly and once inside this amazing building there was plenty of room. To think that this building has been standing for nearly 2000 year. it is well worth a visit if you are in Rome.
Iconic facade. Less impressive inside than other monuments in Rome. No fee or security so minimal wait times.

Imposing, impressive and incredible. How did they build these monuments in ancient times...it boggles the mind. Inside, you can spend hours marveling on the artisanship and the religious significance of every angle, tile and statue. This place whets the appetite for cultural knowledge.

The Pantheon is an increadible piece of roman history and now an interesting church.  On a rainy day you can watch the rain fall through the occulus.  Worth seeing on your own, but crowded as all attractions are.
Free to enter... no lines... and absolutely beautiful and fascinating.  A must go for sure, and ask about the dome and how it rains!
Very well preserved place. Beauty and grandeur combined.Outside and inside both are impressive. Worth visiting

We saw this amazing space twice--once during the day and then another evening just at dusk.  It was less crowded in the evening.  Can be a bit noisy, but still a highlight you do not want to miss.  And don't forget to look up!

This isn't really much to look at from the outside in contrast to loads of buildings in Rome, however when you get inside you will see what all the fuss is about. The dome is an incredible piece of architecture and structural engineering. Well worth going to see

We took 2 goes to get in to the Pantheon. Advice would be check the opening and closing times to save disappointment. Well worth a visit. We went on a wet day which was even better as you could see the unique effect in the centre. The entry is free so can get busy.

A visit to the Pantheon is a must see while in Rome!  Considering the age of the structure it is amazing to think of how this structure was built without the aid of modern technology.  From the Portico to the Rotunda to the holes in the floor of the structure to help with drainage, it is amazing to think of the architectural genius that went into the design of this ancient structure!
I loved the Pantheon and the history behind it. We used Rick Steve's audio tour, it was vey helpful!
The ceiling and dome were incredible.  Definitely take the time to visit.  It's free to enter- but check the open hours.  Beautiful!
Well worth a visit when in Rome, great to walk around and soak up all the history of the area. It does get busy here.
The Pantheon is not a very large Church but  very interesting, with a circular hole in the dome.It  also contains the tomb of Raphael , the renaissance  artist.

A beautiful example of Roman architecture. we took a guided tour. It was worth taking the tour as we got to know about amazing facts about the building and Romans. This was former Roman temple, which is now a church. Ceiling is amazingly engineered with ancient engineering skills (approx 125AD). Must Vist place.
Free to enter and no queues which was a bonus. Visited on a bright, sunny day and the light shining through the roof is something to be seen along with all the other art.

By our 3rd evening in Rome, we were full of history and information. This evening the four of us strolled the Pantheon, Spanish Steps & Trevi Fountain route. We started by walking through the Pantheon, which was gorgeous and just full of enough people to have energy. We found a cafe in the Piazza della Rotunda and shared appetizers and wine. The sense of balance was perfect! By the time we walked to Trevi Fountain, it was too crowded and touristy feeling; we far preferred this piazza and the Pantheon to our other stops this evening. Our college-aged sons Loved it!
It's a moving place, cool and calm compared with outside, even with loads if people withinTombs of Kings and Raphael add a serious touch as sun streams through the oculaWell worth a walk

This ancient temple turned church is definitely worth a visit. The outside is impressive given how old it is, but the inside will wow you. Expect a line, but it moves very quickly. Stay as long as you want.  We spent about 30 minutes because we had a free audio guide preloaded on our mobile phones. The tomb of Rafaelo (Raphael) is inside as well as several others including the first constitutional monarchs of the republic of Italy.The piazza outside is crowded with people. Watch for the annoying peddlers illegally selling junk made in China and illegal fake handbags. Watch for pickpockets since this is a prime tourist destination. If you have time, walk by at night while enjoying some tasty gelato( the inside closes at 1800). The pantheon is beautiful lit up at night.
lovely place to visit. i loved sitting there and taking in the interior. best to get an app such as rick steves to help you understand what you are looking at

Pantheon is a pagan church in Rome near Piazza Navona. It's s on a square with lots of restaurant in the heart of Rome and one of my favourite place. I always take my overseas friend there to show them around!:-)
Amazing building, well worth a visit to see this building, its free to get in and you can take plenty of photos without being rushed

One of the most remarkable thing about this monument is that it is placed in a very small (compared with its "grandeur") square accessible by six/seven narrow roads so that you can really discover it in a unexpected way. That is to say you'll never expect to have an such an impact with a big and wonderful thing this one at the end of road that is difficult for one small car to travel down. And finally, when you are inside of this monument there is that incredible hole on top of the dome through wich sun rays penetrate lighting up the interior that contains, among many other things the grave of a couple of kings. Think about this: once in a year, on april 21st (the day of Rome's birthday, it was 753 BC) the sun ray lights up the entrance of the monument so that the ancient roman emperors could cros the threshold at that very moment and show to the people that they were discending directly from the sun and actually deserve the title, usually awarded only to the Gods, of "sol invictus" (invicible sun)

Wow! What a place! We stumbled across this beautiful building whilst walking to find lunch. I had no read up on this place so it was a huge surprise. And it definitely was a highlight. The inside is stunning and amazing how much light goes in through the hole in the ceiling. It’s free entry without a tour and you’re welcome to take photos (no flash) If you get there at the right time you won’t have to que either. We just walked in. But when we came out there was a huge que to get in.

As a builder I must say this place is a marvel. It’s the largest in reinforced concrete dome in the world. When you walk inside the sheer size of it will amaze you. Entrance is free but if you want a audio guide that will set you back about €8. My advice is find an audio guide online and download it to your phone beforehand.
Beautiful and peacefull - even with a ton of tourists.Come at mid day and see the Sun come down from the top of the dome and shine up the whole church.
This is a truly amazing place. I wish we had spent more time there. Make sure you do not miss this on your tours.

Missed visiting the first time in Rome so was an objective this time around.  Listened to Rick Steve's podcast while roaming the entry and inside the building.  Fascinating history and architecture.  You can see how this building influenced architecture and design to this day.
The building is almost 2000 years old, and still magnificent.  Just to walk in, and you are struck with wonder.

This former Roman temple, which is now a church, is a must see not only for the historical aspect of the building (approximately 125 AD), but for its architectural magnificence.  The columns and outside structural elements are only the initial "wow" to the interior dome, which is open. Admission is free.  Don't be concerned about the long lines as the limited wait provides time to appreciate the exterior as you move  into the interior.  It is amazing!

Have come here twice, once recently and once about 10 years ago during restoration works. A beautiful example of Roman architecture. The square outside is usually filled with a wonderful bustle of people and entertainers, making for a great atmosphere for lunch. Well worth a visit.
The most stunning engineered ceiling, the pantheon is for fans of building and ancient engineering skills, and not as busy as the rest of Rome

The Church has been built keeping the place for a beautiful big square all around embracing the Pantheon . The ouside is really imponent with all tall columns and the interior round and unbelievable special. The hool in the middle of the roof allows rain to fall on the gorgeous floor and makes this church unique.Even if you see line ouside it is very fast. No ticket to visit.Worthwhile!!!!
I guess the building sits on top of many buried bodies. It looks so modern it is hard to believe it is as old as it is. It shouldn't be missed while in Rome.
Never tire of coming here.Still a magical place after 14 visitsAwe inspiring building over 2000 years old

An amazing piece of history, I was surprised it was free.  There are many free audio tours you can download to listen to as you tour the pantheon.  I would highly recommend downloading one before you get there. I arrived at 6:00pm and there was not wait to get in.   A must see if you are visiting Rome.

Roma's Pantheon has one incredible example of dome, with incredible light coming in. The interior is also very interesting for the burial of Corelli, the father of classical concertos, and Raffaello. There is also a beautiful painting by Melozzo da Forli'. It is one of the best preserved examples of ancient Roman architecture. The square in front of it, Piazza della Rotonda, is also very beautiful and a nice place to stroll around.
Wonderful place to visit. A definite must for any visitor to the eternal city. It’s free to visit and an architectural masterpiece.

Very impressive structure considering its age. And the marble floors are similarly wonderous. Yet one can only imagine what the original inside contained since those original elements have long since been removed or rather appropriated.
This was my first historical sight I saw in Rome and was amazed upon entering the Pantheon I recommend it.
Do your research before you go so you know what you are looking at. Eg: God’s eye.  Get there early as line can be big although once it starts moving you move pretty quickly.
This can be a quick addition to any tour. Relativly small but extremely beautiful and worth the stop.
An wonderful place to visit with more history than can be imagined.  The building itself is incredible and the artwork amazing.

Ok, if this was any other city in the world (outside Greece), I would have rated this higher. The reality is that Rome has so many places to see that the Pantheon was just a bit over rated in my book. However, I was happy to have seen it!
very beautiful and does not take very long to tour so you could visit this site and many others on the same day.

Had NO idea on the grandeur of this place.  It was raining when we went in yet it was SO very bright inside but it has NO windows.....on the very top of the is an open dome, which lets the light in (and the rain :) ) .  What a spectacular site.   Line ups may seem long - but they do move fast.
One of the best preserved buildings of ancient Rome. Really beautiful and impressive. Free entrance. So there is actually nothing to criticise.
One of the highlights of our trip in Roma, this is such a wonderful attraction. It's free and even in the middle of the day didn't require any lining up. Absolutely breathtaking.

Was there on a Sunday, was a long line but got in after waiting maybe 10 minutes. Free entry so that was nice. The middle is blocked off so don't plan on that pic right under the dome. Awesome to see.

I came, I saw, I was a bit disappointed.  It was interesting, but compared to the Parthenon, the Roman Forum, St. Peter's Square and Basilica, and the Vatican Museum, this was a bit of a disappointment.

After hitting the Colosseum and the Sistine Chapel I walked from Piazza Navona to the Pantheon and several other sites! The Pantheon is amazing as all of the ancient buildings are. The Pantheon, The Spanish Steps, and The Trevi Fountain were all very busy so expect crowds (during May of 2019 anyway). Several suspicious people walking around the square very pushy about giving away little free trinkets..be careful, not sure what they are up to. There are plenty of restaurants and shops in the area.
Worth a visit, the queues move quickly so don't be put off. Beautiful building with ornate features.
This architectural gem never fails to excite with the added advantage of costing nothing to enter. This should be on the must-see list of every tourist.
Amazing condition considering how old it is, absolutely beautiful inside!  Free to enter, you won't be disappointed!

We had a brief visit and meant to come back at a less busy time - although we walked around the outside, we didn't make it back inside. I learned later that it's possible to stay for evening Mass, which is now on our list for a future visit.
Free.  Pantheon is not very large to tour but large in structure.  Amazing history of Pantheon.   They do stop the line when mass is going to be held.
Had lunch in the area before singing in the church. Visited Pantheon inside, alot of people but not impossible.

We walked around a corner and there was the Pantheon! We were trying to find a restaurant and had the lovely surprise of seeing the Pantheon. It's hard to get a good photo of it, because there are so many people around, but it's still beautiful to see. We didn't see any guides around to talk about it, but I know it was on the hop on/hop off bus tour.

If you are in Rome, Pantheon is a must see, unbelievable frescos and the architecture of this building is majestic and truly admirable.  There are two churches very close to the pantheon and you can literally walk to them - Santa Maria Minerva, San Iglesias Loyola.  If you have a couple of hours free I would suggest covering these 3 places.  If you admire art, architecture and culture, you are in for a treat here!!!
one of my favorite places to visit in Rome, Definitely will visit in the future . very busy be aware of pickpockets Italy

This is an ancient Temple in the Piazza di Spagna area.   The Pantheon is a short walk from the well-known Trevi Fountains.  Very nice temple with quite a few ancient collections of marble statuettes, wooden carved boxes,  a skylight ceiling dome and last but not least huge double entry door.

The Pantheon is spectacular.  We came at 5:30 on a Sunday and there was a kind to get in, but it moved quickly.   There is no cost to enter.  We downloaded the free audio guide from Rick Steves   The audio gave great historical context to the beautiful things we saw inside.   Highly recommend visiting the Pantheon

We went to see the Pantheon as a must see on our trip to Rome but found it mediocre really. We went in and was very busy inside and quite nosy. Had a good look around and there was some information to read but were in and out quickly as not much so see but very impressive architecture. There were many many touts once again outside pestering us to buy stuff and can be pretty persistent which ruins the atmosphere when just wanted to sit and take in our surroundings.
Free to enter but more to the point interesting. The dome was amazing and the inside was so well kept. Worth a visit.
Built 1700 years ago with stressed concrete .... how?  Beautiful inside and towering impressive on the outside.Watch the rain fall through the huge open roof.
Historical landmark, a must-see for those interested in Roman culture. Entrance is free. Can be crowded in daytime.
The first time inside is something spectacular. Long que outside can be daunting but moves exceptionally quickly so don't let this put you off.
This is an amazing place with its architecture, it’s beauty, and I’m inspired by those who built it so long ago. The line is very long to get in but it moves quickly.
You can enter the Pantheon free of charge, so it is crowded as all the monuments, but it is one you should visit anyway. The church has a hole in the roof and that makes it very special.

Not one of the places I'd have gone to see on my own, but our tour guide thought it was important enough to take us to.  Lovely statues, great history and NOT that crowded. Now that I've been there, I don't think it would warrant a return trip

When you arrive it takes a second to take it all in. Its tremendous in size and has so many characteristics to it. Its free entry so you hop in a line and go in, yes its crowded so you have to shuffle your way around but you make do. There is a lot of things to look at around the side so i recommend taking the time to go around.

The Pantheon is so important it's hard to appreciate on one visit so go back for another if you can.   This classic beautiful building is a magical experience even if you are not interested in architecture.
While the Pantheon is remarkable to see, there is so much more there that would be missed without a guide to give the history and to point out specifics.  Explanations added many more "wow"s.
Was not sure what to expect here but it was an awesome experience so very clever not to be missed on a trip

Difficult to appreciate how old this building is. It is crowded  (especially when it is raining) but it's history, spanning two millenia, is fascinating. Free entry so a visit is a must when getting to know Rome.
This was on our list of things to see whilst in Rome.  Quite easy to fine and plenty of bars and cafes near by.  Free entry and not too much of a queue.  Well worth a stop.
This is a stunning building and well worth a visit, although if it is raining it does get rather crowded. The architecture is amazing.
FIrst time in Rome and you must see it. Once a temple of Gods and now a church, the architecture is amazing. And when it rains you see water drops from the oculus, WOW!

Free Entry !!  Looks pretty ordinary from the outside - but once inside wow the size of the roof and yes it has hole in the middle. How on earht did the Romans construct such a huge structure. Awe inspiring , and plenty of room ot move around and inspect the many artefacts on display. It is still a working church, so you might be asked to leave if thiey ahvea serivce scheduled.

We decided to see the Pantheon as one of our top 10 to do list in Rome. We combined it with a visit to Piazza Navona and Im so glad that we did. The facade of the Pantheon looks as if it could do with some spring cleaning. It is in a very small square with a fountain and it can feel stifling in the heat, especially with so many tourists. It is free to get into, which is unusual in Rome, and you dont have to queue.  It is a round shaped building with usual historical adornments, I wont go into it, there is enough tourist information on it. But compared to other sites we visited we found it underwhelming and dare I say it a tad boring. I dont thinkw e spent more than half an hour in it. Thankfully the Piazza Navona is 5 minutes walk away and that is worth a visit
Incredible history and architectural fest in central Rome.  Wile there are lines they move very quickly. Must see!
The sheer size is amazing  do the skip the line if you can. If you just want photos outside, go early as it gets super busy!@

No visit to Rome would be complete until you have visited the Pantheon and it's a beautiful place to go. The whole place is stunning and so worth the time to find it as it's located in what appears to be a more modern part of the city.

The Pantheon is located in the midst of modern buildings in Rome.  It is easy to spot, however.  We visited in early May about noon, and there was a line of about 100 people.  The line slowly walked into the Pantheon; we were inside in 5 minutes.  It was awesome inside.  We were drawn to the oculus (as is everyone).  We were so happy that his building is intact and is an example of ancient Roman architecture.HINT #1: Pay the small fee and take the audio tour.  It guides you though all 12 stations of the Pantheon and really widens your understanding of the building.HINT # 2: Look down!  The floors are amazing.  To think that you are walking where so many famous Romans have walked...pretty cool.
Be ready do expect a crowd - pretty much as the for all the other major tourist attractions in Rome. Worth the visit nonetheless!
This church is so different to others do to its form. Loved it. My only complaint:I would like to know more about the different art and sculptures - there was not a lot to go by once you were inside.
2 years ago I had the opportunity to visit during daylight hours. Although amazing it is nothing like the view in the dark with lights.
Always greatWas there for second time Was raining which came down though oculus Nice to see it In great shape for its age No problem driving in Rome if anyone worried to do so

Went with a guide, David Battaglino, and must say it was one of the best experiences from the whole of Rome. David was absolutely amazing, told us many unknown information about the Pantheon and its surroundings and even made sure that we saw the Pantheon in the way its supposed to be seen - without all the tourists. Amazing experience and can only recommend David to go on a tour with!
The Pantheon should not be missed. It is amazing what was accomplished so long ago and it is still standing and fascinating to see!
Our first trip to Rome and we’re so glad we went to the Pantheon. The dome is famous for being as tall as it is wide. And it’s open at the top! Great location and beautiful
Missed this on our first visit to Rome so we made sure to see it this time. Standing in such a famous old spot was so cool, especially for history lovers!
Certainly catches the eye , it’s amazing how this building was constructed all those years ago with what is now rudimentary instruments , yet its a masterpiece of engineering

Don't just stand outsite looking at this masterpiece, take the trouble to go inside and marvel at this marvel built almost 2,000 years ago during the reign of Augustus Caesar.  I wonder if any 20th Century buinings will still be standing in 2,000 years!!!
Very beautiful bulding, but too many tourists around to be able to really enjoy it. Beware of pickpockets in the area.

I was personally quite disappointed with my visit to the Pantheon. With the exception of the exterior and the great dome there was little to reflect the original purpose of this amazing Roman temple built in honor of the pagan goddesses worshipped by the Romans. Entirely consumed by the Catholic church, from the Papal Seal at the front door to the altar and side chapels, there is nothing left from Roman times. In a city where there's a church every 100 yards or so you'd hope that at least acknowledging the history of this building,  much less returning it to its roots,  wouldn't be so hard to do.

Beautiful and amazing architecture - imagine that the dome has been build more than 2,000 years ago! Impressive ...Definitely worth a visit - it's easy to get through, despite the fact that we are many tourists ... ;-)

One of the quieter sites of Roman history, the Pantheon drips with hidden history. The resting place of such luminaries as Raphael and the Kings Vittor Emanuel and Umberto the church although bustling and busy exudes a peace and weird tranquility, unusual in this hectic city. Perhaps due to the 'sacred' nature of the place, this is one site not plagues with the lucky lucky men or street sellers.

This place is so amazing! You can just feel energy flowing here. It was a little hard to find, but after getting a little lost we found it. It is just so amazing how this place is located in the middle of so many regular buildings.

First port of call on our first day and it did not disappoint.  A structure of wonderful proportion, amazing history behind it and quite, quite beautiful.  The amazing dome is outstanding and when the sun streams through, it brings a lovely spiritual feel. Well worth the visit.
Beautiful sight. Amazing what was possible to build 2000 years ago.  A very special atmosphere inside.
We had a wonderful tour guide.   So knowledgeable and passionate about teaching us about the Panthenon.  This tour is a must !   Grazie !

We were staying close to the Pantheon and when were first walked past it during the day it had a huge line to get in. We decided to wait and we were so glad we did! They had a special night opening that day (Saturday) and it stayed open until 10pm. It was beautiful inside and I think even better in the evening.  It is free to go in and worth a visit!

It is one of those mystical places where one can feel being transported to different times, despite the crowds inside. Free entry and easy to locate. That should be on everyone's "to see" list while visiting Rome.
Extremely crowded. Pouring rain, so very slippery floor, everyone was carrying an umbrella. Children's choir was performing and it was very beautiful and added to the experience!
The Pantheon is free and it is very interesting, just to know that the 1st Kings tombs are inside and Raphael as well.  The dome is something else, the artchitect alone of the builidng will awe you.
Such a beautiful place to visit,  going in May worked it perfect.  No line, a but crowded but manageable and you still get to enjoy it and appreciate everything about it.  Highly recommend to visit.

Was not really on my radar of things to visit in Rome but our guide took us here and we were amazed .... this magnificent building is literally in the centre of Rome surrounded by all shops and cafes ... not as busy as the other tourist areas but equally impressive.

This site is free so you won't need a guided tour to see it.  It can get crowded during the morning hours so we visited the Pantheon late afternoon.  This former Roman temple is now a church.  Almost two thousand years after it was built, the Pantheon's dome is still the world's largest unreinforced concrete dome!!!
Fantastic to find this treasure amid the bustling back streets of Rome.It feels as if stepping into the past when you feel the aura of age envelope you

Visited early at a time when we were lucky to find the Pantheon as quite as I can ever remember it being. A simple building in many respects but a beautiful and complex one in others. No need to rush yourself here, take time to sit.

Do research and find out about this amazing place before you go- don't worry about the crowds- in May, even with a crowd/line,we did not have to wait long to go in and once you're in (it's spacious), you can hang out a bit and not impede others.  It flows smoothly and is so impressive of a place- one of my favorites in Rome!  Just breath-taking!  Esp. as you walk in under that famous Oculus.

A must-see and it’s completely free to visit. Make sure you sign the guest book. There’s really no need to do a guided tour. We hopped in what seemed to be a long line, but we were inside and enjoying the views in no time. Also, it’s massive so even visiting on a Sunday, it was easy to see everything and not feel like a sardine.

It was raining cats & dogs when we reached Pantheon. There was no proper queue at the entrance, no information in English for non-Italian visitors. No commentary available. One has to fend for himself. Once inside, it is grand as are many other Roman structures.One of the must-dos  when in Rome, Italy.

The Pantheon is a true wonder.  Its beauty and grace are perfectly balanced by its colossal size.  It feels massive yet weightless.  The light coming in from the huge oculus brings a mystic and sacred aura to the rotunda.  I can't help being moved by such an architectural and engineering tour de force.  It is also moving to be able to stand next to Raphael's grave.  Every time I am in Rome, I make a point of visiting the Pantheon.

Always busy but we were not rushed and took our time looking around. At the time of our visit it was raining heavily so the floor was flooded with the rain coming in the central opening but we spent ages gazing up. They do rope off an area to avoid people slipping. The architecture is amazing and a rarity that it is free to enter! Definitely worth a visit.
Multi-faith Basilica turned Museum - impressive open dome, if you're every nearby when it's raining be sure to head inside for a unique display.

Centrally located and amazing that the Pantheon has stood the test of time.  No entry fee, this is a church.  The central opening in the ceiling and round walls are an engineering feat to be seen, as with many of the ancient structures in Rome.
Trip round the pantheon - free to enter - must be quiet - heavily guarded and kept moving to ensure queues move quickly - a must when in rome - stacked with history
Beautiful building and definitely worth a visit. It always seems busy but it's free so that's understandable. We came on a Saturday so maybe it was busier because of that too. Must see when in Rome.
The Pantheon is the most amazing place.  Its actually hard to describe this ancient, iconic wonder.  To me, this is what Rome is all about and should be experienced by everyone who visits the city.

How these structures were made thousands of years ago, from aerated concrete no less - just awe inspiring.  The travertine pillars are massive, transported from Egypt... how the hell did they do it?!  I am still curious about how people worship here when it rains, as the roof is open to the elements - guess that's why the mosaic floor slopes gently towards the entrance.

Our family of four all loved this place. It’s free and such a surprise when you walk inside. Such an amazing dome. Construction from the outside is also amazing and worth the slow look. Make sure to walk all the way around and keep an eye on the bricks.Very cool place.

We didn’t get a chance to stop here for long, but yet again this was another Roman building basked in history.A former Temple completed by Hadrian (formerly commissioned by Agrippa), it protrudes from the Circus Flaminius region in a very well preserved state (due to it’s constant use).The queue for the entrance appeared to be long but it soon filters down. There was no fee for getting inside but it was overcrowded once you were in. Please note a couple of things to look out for - the glorious dome (which is still the world’s largest unreinforced concrete dome) and the tomb of King Victor Emmanuel II.Afterwards, take your time in exploring the area around the Church as it is filled with bars and restaurants.

Everybody goes to the Pantheon, but it's best in pouring rain, pouring in through the oculus above and draining perfectly after 2,000 years. The sun makes a sun-disk on the walls as the Earth rotates below it, like a giant pinhole camera  -- i'd like to see it at a winter full moon...
The Pantheon visit was a great stop on our tour.  The guide that we had was great and very knowledgable.
Beautiful inside and out! So full of interesting history. The square in front is always crowded with people. Fun and fascinating!
Amazing building. And the history is unsurpassed. The piazza is lively but the building is truely something special and it never disappoints. Crowds detract but you must look past that.

The Pantheon should not be missed by a traveler to Rome. From the outside, it is well preserved. Walking under the portico,  to see the detail in the stonework is just amazing. The interior is interesting for the tombs. The Pantheon is the oldest church in a city full of old churches, and there is a sense of history to this building.  Entry is free and i definitely recommend

The Pantheon is a really interesting building as it is the first circular, domed and self-suspended place of worship to be built in the world, although it still has the original design for the outside, with the columns and the rectangular shape.  It has lasted so long because it’s made of marble which hasn’t been taken for use in other places or buildings, unlike the Colosseum. It can still be used for worshipping as well as a place of interest where we can see tombs, art and monuments, which are all very pretty.  It is also free to enter.

This is turely a gem.  It's still free although you can now purchase an audio tour.  You will also encounter folks outside selling a guided tour.  We just took Rick Stevens book in and toured the site ourselves. They have a lot of signage as well.  So much history and cool stuff here.  We went fiarly early on a Monday morning (around 9) in mid May.  We walked right in.  later in the day it was crwoded with alot of school children.  If you are going to Duomo in florence, this was the dome that they copied.
A two thousand year old building that never ceases to amaze and is always spotless with entry well controlled and free. Not a rip off like some of the other attractions.
Dont get a tour for this place its free to get in and their is plenty of notice boards and information about.Plus their are some lovely restaurants in the square - a super way to lose a few hours
This is a must visit stop while in Rome.  The architecture was beautiful but they had so many people inside that the noise made my head feel like it was going to explode.
Although extremely crowded, this is an important part of history to see. It is incredible what human hands could do.
When in Rome...the Pantheon is a massive building with history, splendor and a view that doesn't quit. This is a must see historic site when visiting Rome
Worth doing and it’s free. A nice square also with food options and good statues and lots of history and religion inside. It can be very busy like the whole of Rome.
Incredible building full of history. We used Rick Steves' (free) audioguide on our phones and that allowed us to get a lot of information about the building while we were visiting.
A simply amazing place, on a scale you can't imagine until you are standing inside. Add to that it is free admission and the queues are relatively short when compared to other sites.

When in Rome it’s an absolute must that you visit the Pantheon. It’s usually crowded, but it seemed like people thinned out a little near closing time. The domed ceiling steals the show, with its ingenious oculus. Just be on the lookout for pickpockets.

We stopped here along our private tour of Rome. It is truly an incredible site to see and almost unbelievable that humans were able to build such a marvel that long ago. A must see if you are only in Rome a few days don't pass this site up.
They said that is a gods landmark, and it really looks like. Amazing structure, that attract many tourists at any hours.

This building is a wonderful structure that makes you appreciate the architecture of Rome. The more you read up on it the impressive it is. Not a lot to do on the inside except have a look around and appreciating it further. No need to spend more then 20-30 mins in here.
Amazing space..audio tour is worth the price...no admission, which is unusual for Italy! Open ceiling dome is a must see.

Beautiful, stunning architecture. What spoils a lot of Rome’s beautiful buildings is that touts selling tat are permitted to park their carts directly in front of the steps leading to these buildings. It spoils the photo opportunities and I feel detracts from the meaning of some of these historical buildings.

This relic has survived a very long time.  You do not need to go inside to appreciate it either.  It is in a large piazza and will often be crowded for entry.  As I indicated, it is not necessary to go insides, but if you have the time, you should consider doing so too, though there can often be long waits to do so.
Breathtaking, history right there in front of you. It is free to enter, although people do not seem to respect the request to keep the silence of this sacred space.

This is a massive piece of art, first glimpse of which will astonish and mesmerize you. A place of god, it has free entry and is best enjoyed early in morning when there is minimal crowd. Look out for oculus hole.
Pantheon has a Wonderful Architecture  and it is very famous Rome attraction. Its Pillar and Dome are very attractive.
Take your time everything is massive and naturally very popular with tourists.  Great time of year for weather making it easier to get around in the heat
So much history, walked everywhere and saw as much a possible.These incredible buildings just take your breath away. Rome is history on steriods.
I went early evening before dinner around 8:00 pm, and went right in without waiting in line. The inside had pleanty of room to enjoy without feeling like I was stuck in a crowd

This was one of our very favourite destinations in Rome. I would go back again. The quality of the light, the feeling of peace, even though it is generally crowded with tourists and the age and almost perfect condition of the pantheon is truly incredible. I thought the light made people inside look like they were in an old masters painting.  This si a must see in Rome.... and it is free too!
What an amazing building. Not a whole lot to view inside, but go and stand in awe of the architecture.
Unlike other interesting buildings, this one is free to enter. Best time to visit is early morning or early evening.
Centrally located in Rome, walkable distance from other popular sights in Rome, lovely to just sit around and get amazed by the beautiful built.
Very lovely Temple, now turned into a Church, very impressive from both outside and inside, definitely a must see while in Rome. Entry was free.
What an amazing building, go and see it! It is better if you can get there before the crowds, morning seems to be good.

The Pantheon is a magnificent structure built by Marcus Agrippa, the great friend of Augustus. It is entirely original, intact and an architectural masterpiece which is now a church or huge dimension with an open cupola. It is one of the 'must see' attractions of the city and is well-worth a visit. You will be impressed!
It is one of the best-preserved of all Ancient Roman buildings. I advise you to visit early and get it empty.
This building constantly surprises me and is staggering regardless of the time of year. Don't forget to look at the coins on the front door.

The cool thing about the pantheon is that the queue is extremely fast, you get inside this huuuge circular building thay became a church, and churches in Italy never disappoint. Good to spend 10 to 15 minutes there and move on.

The Pantheon is perhaps the best preserved ancient Rome building, it is impressive in particular considering the vault and the size of the whole building. It is incredible that in many countries you have to pay a not-cheap ticket to see basically nothing or a quite negligible museum, and there is no fee to enter this incredible monument. I like this free entry, but some way to create value out of this marvelous Roman heritage should be devised.
A must on the Rome trail. free in (think that is because it was a Sunday so please check)- big crowds but we didn't queue for long at all- at its most atmospheric in the evening. A must see.
Free entry to an intriguing structure full of history. Don’t forget to sit in one of the cafes nearby and gaze and it and people watch

The Pantheon is definitely not to be missed on any trip to Rome.  This magnificent building was constructed by Emperor Hadrian in 120 AD, and 2,000 years later this imposing structure continues to impress.  During the centuries following its construction, the architectural knowledge was lost.  But it was the inspiration for the Renaissance domes-- St. Peter's Basilica by Michelangelo in Rome and Brunelleschi's Duomo in Florence.  Incredible!

Absolutely stunning structure! The design, precision, and interior is truly incredible considering the age! This is a must see as part of Ancient Rome. It’s hard to believe how old this building is but how well taken care of it is. The interior dome where it was open to allow rain water to come in and the drain in the floor is remarkable!!

If you only see this from the outside, you must come back to see the inside.  And make sure you have audio guide with you.  You can easily spend couple hours in this place to very appreciate the its history, architecture, and its content

As is the case with many Ancient Roman structures, it’s difficult to believe that people, in around the time of Jesus, could build such magnificent structures.  Interestingly, the Pantheon is one of the few Ancient Roman structures that has survived the many invasions from the Barbaric German tribes.  You will be impressed!
We just walked in off the street and went straight inside. No cue and no charge. Stay as long or as short as you want to.

Amazing to think how this was built almost 2000 years ago! It is impressive from both the inside and outside, and it is very convenient to many other sites. Take the time to visit. TIP: It is ~5 minutes from both the Trevi fountain and the Piazza Navona
Another have-to on our Rome list, this one 2as easy. We walked right in, no line. Very impressive Church.

The plaza itself where the iconic facia of the building is definitely can become crowded, try to visit during off hours to avoid the mass amounts of people.There is lots of space and such to take great pictures though. Amazing how these buildings still look great today.

I love stopping into this place as it is so beautiful and amazing that it was built so long ago. Only reason for four stars is that it is no longer a quiet peaceful place you can pop into like it once was. There is a line out the door and you have to wait until enough people leave to be ushered in. Place is packed!

We by passed standing in the long line one morning and pretty much decide not to see the inside.  The next day when we were with our tour guide he insisted we see the inside.  It's so  beautiful and massive.  Words cannot describe. Glad we changed our minds.

The rotunda is incredible, you have to go in and see it for yourself!  We visited on May Day and the line was long but it moved relatively quickly.  Men must remove hats before entering, as the armed soldier informed everyone.  Inside, there was a hushed tone as the admiration began.  I was extremely happy to finally get to this wonder.

The place is amazing and a must see when in Rome. It is full of statues and paintings specifically dedicated to the Gods. We visited on our first day in Rome and it really started off our holiday. There is quite a long queue to get in but we got in pretty quick.
Head over late afternoon. Crowds dropped away. Some great eateries close. What a wonderful preserved building > a man st see site in Rome.
Former Roman temple dedicated to all gods, amazingly well preserved and remarkable from an architectural standpoint.
Free to enter it's a church and they ask for silence when you are in there fantastic ceiling , worth a visit .

The Pantheon was by far my favourite landmark in Rome!! Definitely best to go early in the morning and beat the crowds. I was able to walk straight in but later in the day the question were massive to even get inside to have a look.

We weren't going to go to the Pantheon but on a tour of Palatine Hill, David Battaglino convinced us to go. The Pantheon was amazing but it was David who brought it to life and showed us how fascinating it was. He also engineered it so that we had time in the Pantheon completely alone! Would recommend contacting him before your trip to Rome if you want the best experience possible. His email is david@davidsrome.com

Entering the Pantheon is free and don't let the long line across the piazza frighten you because it moves quickly.  We walked in and were left speechless by the size and grandeur of the space and the   ceiling.

We spent the afternoon seeing the Pantheon, along with the nearby Trevi Fountain and Spanish Steps. The entry is free, and very quick to enter, with no long queues inside. We used the Rick Steves App which I highly recommend for free audioguides that explain what you are seeing. The size of the building was awe inspiring.

If you go to Rome don't miss the Pantheon! It's the best preserved monument of ancient Rome. Impressive! Most of the structure is the original one, coming back to the 1th cent AD. And its dome is the biggest in the world! Breathtaking!

I hadn't quite expected the sheer scale and wonder of the Pantheon.  It's utterly enormous and breathtakingly beautiful.  The dome is an architectural wonder and the religious aspects are wonderfully ornate and moving.  The only downfall is that in spite of many signs and audio signals requesting silence, it's a bit of a noisy place which ever so slightly detracted from the awe of wandering around
The Pantheon is a wonderful mix of the Roman and later Christian ages. Beautiful interior and fascinating to see the burial places of renowned Italians.
A bucket list item that was just like walking into an art history textbook. Beautiful space but a bit crowded - we went back at different times in the day and found the crowds varied.
Nice to be able to go inside without having to pay . Amazing building inside and out .Worth a visit . Crowds were also ok .Did not have to wait long to go inside 👍
Like many buildings in Rome, the exterior belies what you'll find inside. Very easy to visit and best of all there were no lines to get in and it is free.

The Pantheon is a must for any visit to Rome. Try to visit early in the morning, when you are likely to find it nearly empty. But even at high points in the day it is generally easy to enter (no lineup), unlike Trevi Fountain or the Vatican, which get crazy. The architecture is amazing, and it is a great tribute to the brilliance of early Roman architects and builders.

24 years ago, we walked straight into the interior and were able to contemplate in peace the incredible unsupported dome and the many shrines around the walls. Not so this time, the floor was packed, the hubbub was disturbing and the chances of that exclusive shot free of tourist bodies nonexistent. For all that, the dome is a marvel, just don't stand directly under the central opening on a rainy day!
What can I say?  It's a must.  Easy access. Free.  Giagantic scale.  A lasagne of Roman architecture.  Look out for the 20 drainage holes for run off rain water.

We went here for both historical and religious reasons and it is IMPRESSIVE!  It is hidden by many buildings so navigate by going one block west of Via del Corso and look for signs.  It will jump up on you to be sure.
Inspiring outside but Awesome interior and history, that this was hand built 2,000 years ago a truly incredible achievement

I didn't actually know we could go inside, nor that it is free to do so. Pantheon is an impressive building with all its pillars and artworks inside. As always with popular landmarks like this, please be careful of your belongings (pickpockets).
Another spot in Rome that's a must to visit but again, very touristy outside, but nonetheless worth it.
